<story>
Halfway down the Rue Saint-Denis, near the Rue du Petit-Lion, a weathered house helped historians reconstruct old Paris. Dilapidated walls revealed timber patterns and cracked plaster; a warped triangular roof overhung the street, sheltering the entrance and dormer. The upper story’s overlapping planks lightened the fragile structure.On a rainy March morning a young man, cloaked like an antiquary, stood across from the house studying its peculiarities. He ignored the narrow boarded windows of the first floor and inspected the third floor’s modest sash windows, crudely made and clouded with green-tinted glass, occasionally glancing at the shop below where small comic details amused him.A large wooden beam, supported by four pillars, bore many paint layers and a whimsical painting of a cat with an oversized racket and an absurd ball. A faded sign read “Guillaume, Successor to Master Chevrel,” a remnant of the shop’s old charm.The young man, in a stylish cloak and white silk stockings, looked as if from a wedding or ball; his white gloves and disheveled hair hinted at recent revelry. Market gardeners hurried by, but the street was unusually still. His striking features—pale face, dazzling cravat, expressive eyes—drew shopkeepers’ attention.Three apprentices then appeared at the dormer, laughing and sprinkling him with shaving cream, which he brushed off indifferently. A young girl’s face—recalling a Raphael Virgin—briefly appeared and fled as the shutter slammed, leaving him disappointed.At that moment the “Cat and Racket” shutters opened and a manservant hung the cloth sign. Behind iron bars the shop revealed many packages wrapped in brown linen. Though old-fashioned, Monsieur Guillaume was reputed Paris’s best-stocked cloth merchant: honest and profitable without flattery, while fellow traders grumbled about his notary’s heavy discounts.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The old merchant stood at his shop threshold on the Rue Saint-Denis, awakening from a long sleep. Monsieur Guillaume, in traditional dress, watched a stranger loitering by the shop. His shrewd, weathered face marked him a staunch traditionalist who lamented lost customs and imposed a strict routine on his three fearful apprentices.At work Guillaume noticed the stranger’s anxious glances and suspected ulterior motives. The eldest apprentice, seeing Mademoiselle Augustine hasten away, hailed a cab and thus averted a prank aimed at her.He reproved the youths for idleness, recalling his own youthful industry. Though the apprentices came from comfortable homes and paid board, he demanded rigorous labor, unlike newer, softer shops. The shop was meticulous; Madame Guillaume’s frugality even excluded apprentices from the family dessert.They kept strict routine—attending Mass together—and Joseph Lebas, the eldest apprentice, after years of service earned a modest wage. Guillaume considered Joseph a possible husband for his elder daughter Virginie, though Joseph secretly loved the younger Augustine.Household life remained traditional. Virginie was devout and severe; Augustine, eighteen, was delicate and charming but smothered by maternal expectations. Augustine’s appetite for passion, fed by romantic novels, deepened Joseph’s affection, and Virginie, lacking suitors, felt drawn to him too. Amid the shop’s monotony quiet feelings grew, and Guillaume worried about his daughters’ prospects in changing times.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day the old merchant, grieving his eldest daughter’s decline, remembered marrying Mademoiselle Chevrel under circumstances like those of Joseph Lebas and Virginie. Marrying off his daughter would repay a sacred debt to an orphan benefactor. Joseph Lebas, thirty-three, aware of a fifteen-year gap with Augustine and Monsieur Guillaume’s rule that the elder sister marry first, suffered in silence. Warm-hearted and reserved, he kept his love hidden.A few months earlier a young painter paused outside the darkened Cat and Racket, transfixed by a scene any artist would envy. The shop, cave-like, revealed a dining room lit by a hanging lamp that recalled Dutch interiors. The family—Augustine among them, with a chubby maid—formed a peaceful, modest tableau. Fresh from Rome and tired of grand art, the painter craved genuine nature. Augustine, pensive and scarcely touched by food, seemed almost angelic in the lamp’s glow. Overwhelmed, he left unable to eat or sleep.Determined to capture the moment, he retreated to his studio and kept returning to the Cat and Racket, even disguising himself to study Augustine. For eight months he lived in love and art, neglecting friends and routine. One morning his friend Girodet broke his isolation to ask about his Salon submission. The artist revealed two masterpieces; Girodet, sensing deep feeling, embraced him and suggested love had made the work great. Despite warnings that the public might not understand them, the paintings were exhibited and created a sensation. The Interior launched a new genre, and the portrait became unforgettable, winning acclaim and offers the artist declined.These public successes contrasted with Augustine’s monastic life in Rue Saint-Denis until Madame Guillaume mentioned the exhibition during a visit. With her cousin’s help, Augustine obtained permission to see the Louvre. Trembling before the portrait, she recognized herself and felt terror and excitement. The artist, nearby, whispered, “You see how love has inspired me,” leaving her stunned.Fleeing the crowd, Augustine tried to leave but was drawn back to the celebrated painting. Overwhelmed, she wept and, in a quiet gesture, exchanged a nod with the artist. That brief connection ignited guilt and exhilaration; the Salon’s chaos and the painter’s gaze revealed unfamiliar emotions. She feigned a headache to avoid discussing the exhibition, dreading her mother’s planned visit. That night she felt a void and longed for the elegance she had always admired; the idea of being a genius’s wife filled her with hope. The next day her parents returned disappointed: the painter had removed the two pictures, and Madame Guillaume had lost her shawl. The paintings’ disappearance, however, had awakened in Augustine a woman’s instinctive sensitivity.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The morning after a ball, Theodore de Sommervieux, who had captured Augustine's heart, found it difficult to declare his love because her household constrained them. Augustine sat in the counting-house between Mademoiselle Virginie and Madame Guillaume under her mother's watch, while Theodore, imagining rivals among the assistants, felt hemmed in. He bribed a maid to arrange discreet exchanges and they agreed to meet during Mass at Saint-Leu.At the Cat and Racket the family concentrated on stock-taking under Monsieur Guillaume’s supervision, tallying inventory and balancing accounts, indifferent to Augustine’s longing. When the work ended, they relaxed: attended a performance of Cendrillon and the apprentices enjoyed a boisterous night out.The next morning Guillaume resumed routine and summoned his assistant Joseph Lebas to review the profitable results, offering to make him a partner in recognition of his work. Overwhelmed, Joseph blurted that he loved Augustine; Guillaume at first misheard and thought he meant Virginie. Once clarified, Guillaume agreed to consider Joseph’s feelings and suggested he accompany Augustine to Mass.At breakfast tension rose as Madame Guillaume and Virginie exchanged glances at a nervous Joseph. Madame later chastised Augustine for inattentiveness in church and sent her to her room, prompting a heated discussion about their daughter’s future. Augustine confessed her love for Theodore, insisting on his respectable background. Monsieur, wary of artists, protested until Madame Roguin arrived with news of Theodore’s honors and fortune and advocated the match. Her report swayed the parents. By evening Guillaume informed Joseph, the family celebrated, Theodore presented a painting of the Cat and Racket, and, though cautious about money, Monsieur consented: the lovers and household embraced a joyful future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few months after that memorable Sunday, Saint-Leu’s high altar saw two contrasting weddings. Augustine and Theodore shone with happiness while Virginie, simply dressed, followed her sister. Monsieur Guillaume had tried to have Virginie married first, but the priests attended the more elegant bride. Neighbors praised Virginie’s sensible match and envied Augustine for marrying an artist of rank. An old fan-maker warned that such extravagance would bring ruin, and Monsieur Guillaume felt vindicated in his cautious approach to settlements. After a splendid ball and supper, the Guillaumes returned home and the Lebas family resumed their old residence. The ecstatic artist carried Augustine to their richly adorned apartment.Their first year was blissful: Theodore lavished affection, and Augustine, lost in love, believed her devotion would always enchant him. Naïve at times, she remained happily unaware of much around her. As pregnancy approached, Theodore felt the pull of work. He reconnected with friends and began frequenting the Duchesse de Carigliano’s salons, enjoying the social proof of being seen with a beautiful wife.Augustine liked society but soon felt out of place among Theodore’s artistic circle. Her admiration for his work was sincere but superficial, and an intellectual disconnect grew between them. Theodore hoped studio life would draw her in, but she could not adapt; at gatherings her innocence clashed with the freer manners of artists and her discomfort showed.Gradually Theodore’s warmth cooled and Augustine keenly felt his indifference. Despite trying to fulfill her duties, she could not bridge the widening gap. Three years into marriage, the once radiant Augustine sank into sorrow as Theodore’s involvement with the Duchesse intensified her fear of being supplanted. Determined, she tried to educate herself in poetry and art to reconnect, but her efforts faltered and only highlighted her inadequacy. Seeking solace, she returned briefly to her childhood home, where Virginie and Joseph Lebas ran their drapery with steady, ordinary happiness. Joseph’s practical, commercial view offered no comfort. Augustine ultimately returned to her husband’s home, burdened by uncertainty and longing, trying every remedy to heal a fractured marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The elderly couple welcomed their daughter warmly; her visit broke their years of monotonous reminiscence about past business successes and failures—most notably the Lecocq bankruptcy. Monsieur Guillaume habitually checked on his shop, the Cat and Racket, and chatted with former competitors, while Madame Guillaume—appointed to the Army’s clothing consulting board through her son-in-law’s influence—filled their home with extravagant, tasteless decor. Their nostalgic stagnation struck Augustine as a sharp contrast to her own turbulent life, leaving her both proud and appalled.Despite her turmoil, Augustine displayed her achievements and aired marital grievances, which her parents received with keen interest. Madame Guillaume was especially curious about Augustine’s husband, Theodore, a painter. When Augustine described his late nights and eccentric habits, her mother disapproved of his lifestyle and profession. Augustine defended him, and the family’s incredulity grew into a debate about genius versus marital responsibility.At the mention of divorce her parents were shocked; Monsieur Guillaume offered practical support. Overwhelmed by their concern and unable to convey the depth of her suffering, Augustine recoiled from separation, realizing she was isolated in a way only those who had endured similar trials could understand.Determined to change course, Augustine sought the Duchess de Carigliano—not to force Theodore back but to learn how to captivate him. Approaching the Duchess’s opulent home, she felt envy and sensed rivalry. The Duchess listened sympathetically and counseled shrewdly: with men of genius, one must project strength and self-control, cultivate mystery, and never reveal the whole measure of one’s affection. Though uneasy about such manipulation, Augustine began to accept these lessons, convinced her love required a new approach.The Duchess offered a portrait of Augustine painted by Theodore to rekindle his interest. Hopeful, Augustine returned determined to embody the image. Theodore’s fury and jealousy led to a violent quarrel that shattered her hopes. The next morning her mother found Augustine in despair among the wreckage; overwhelmed by her tumultuous life, she died young. Her tragic end remains a poignant reminder of the struggle women face pursuing love and fulfillment amid the complexities of artistic genius; the inscription on her grave reflects this sorrow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Comte de Fontaine, a loyal royalist from Poitou who had fought for the Bourbons in La Vendée, was ruined when his estate was confiscated. He married Mademoiselle de Kergarouet—noble but penniless—and moved to Paris with his large family seeking relief. Though initially disheartened, he unexpectedly received promotion and royal decorations, which revived his hopes. Soon, however, his sacrifices seemed unacknowledged as new aristocrats rose; about to return to his estate, he reentered royal service during the 1815 upheavals. His loyalty was rewarded: he became a trusted adviser to Louis XVIII, valued for his wit and political sense. The family prospered—his sons won important posts and his daughters made advantageous matches, generally with lesser nobility. Emilie, the youngest, became the spoiled center of Parisian society; beautiful and exacting, she drew many suitors but remained indifferent, snubbing those she deemed inferior and rejecting matches for trivial faults. As the political climate shifted and the King’s health declined, the Comte pressed harder to find Emilie a husband to secure the family’s status, but her continued disdain through the winter left their future uncertain.Nature had given Emilie de Fontaine every quality to play the part of Célimène. Tall and slender, she could shift from dignity to playfulness; her long neck and arched brows suggested scorn and impertinence, her fine black hair and melodious voice could charm or silence. Her alabaster complexion remained composed except for fleeting emotions. Many young men she spurned accused her of acting; she replied that she merely inspired desire to subject it to her whims.Monsieur de Fontaine realized too late that home adoration had spoiled his daughter. Constant admiration had inflated her pride and selfishness, hidden by youthful charm but obvious to a father who tried in vain to teach her wisdom. His gentle counsels slid off her like water from marble; her affection often took a condescending tone, like a child demanding attention and returning to play.Though Emilie sometimes showed fondness for her parents, she often withdrew, jealous of the shared love given others, including her siblings. She blamed fate for her solitude, failing to see that happiness is inward. Determined to resist her sisters’ marriages, she nevertheless felt pangs of envy at their contentment; her unpredictability made her mother suspect she might be a little mad.This pride was common among beautiful young women of affluent families: feeling misunderstood by mothers, suspecting maternal envy, shedding secret tears, and rebelling against perceived tyranny. They built elaborate ideals of the perfect partner, only to learn by experience that reality rarely matches fantasy.Emilie’s standards were precise and unyielding: her husband must be a peer of France, a soldier wearing an Order so they could be respected; he must be slim, for she despised corpulence and believed only the slender could be romantic. Her disdain for failing suitors was plain, and Monsieur de Fontaine feared her eccentricities would invite ridicule and that delay in the marriage market might bring consequences.As winter under Charles X progressed, he redoubled efforts to find matches, hosting gatherings to attract suitors despite his reputation as a corrupter of legislative integrity. After a season of introductions he hoped Emilie would choose, but her whims and standards remained unchanged.One morning he summoned her to discuss her future, arranging his study to look dignified; she greeted him with a light, chilly kiss. He pressed the importance of a wise choice for happiness. Emilie interrupted, reminded him of their prior agreement about suitors, and mocked his suggestions for appearance or background. Exasperated, he declared he had fulfilled his duty and that the choice now rested with her, warning that marital happiness depends on mutual respect rather than mere wealth or rank. Touched by his sincerity, Emilie feigned affection and coaxed him, yet her pride stayed intact.At a family gathering she refused to consider marriage, insisting she would only wed a peer; her uncle jokingly suggested she wait for the Duc de Bordeaux, and she remained resolute.At the country house that summer Emilie asked to attend the Bal de Sceaux, drawn by the crowd’s anonymity. There she noticed a striking young man apart from the throng whose elegance and dignity sparked immediate attraction. Aloof, he watched another dancer, a pale girl named Clara; curious and jealous, Emilie followed them. Days later, learning he was not the noble she’d hoped, she pressed her uncle for an introduction. The promise intensified her interest; her infatuation grew, fed by imagination and youthful ideals, still unaware of the complexities love and marriage would bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning, before Emilie awoke, her uncle hurried to Chevreuse. Recognizing the young man he’d insulted the day before, he approached with the civility of an old courtier. “I’m a vice-admiral,” he said, “and I think as little of dueling as of smoking a cigar. I had too much grog and ran you down. Let’s shake hands; I’d rather face a hundred rebuffs from a Longueville than give his family cause for regret.” The young man accepted the apology, and they rode into the woods. The Count, playful about dueling, fired a pistol into a birch and encouraged the young man to shoot near his mark; the man obliged and won the admiral’s approval.During the ride the Count probed the young man on gentlemanly codes. “Do you have debts?” he asked. “No,” came the reply. “What, you pay for everything?” “Punctually; otherwise, we’d lose credit and respect.” The Count teased about mistresses and youthful extravagance, confessed his own past recklessness, and promised to introduce the young man at the Pavillon Planat, urging him to dinner to meet his nephew, the Comte de Fontaine. The young man—Longueville—found the old man odd but affable and accepted.When the house party gathered the next afternoon and a servant announced “Monsieur de Longueville,” everyone rushed to appraise the newcomer. Simply and elegantly dressed, Longueville seemed at ease in the Receiver-General’s luxury. His manner and polished education suggested a man of the world; when the admiral mentioned naval architecture he answered aptly, prompting a lady to suspect he had attended the École Polytechnique. Longueville allowed himself to be flattered but courteously declined dinner, saying he was “Hippocrates” to his delicate young sister—an explanation that invited ironic speculation about his profession.Mademoiselle de Fontaine, who had dressed to attract him, felt slighted when he did not give her the attention she expected. Emilie, usually charming with newcomers, became unusually simple and natural, prompting family comment and varied interpretations—some thinking she was withholding to dazzle later. Longueville’s visit was neither too long nor too short; he left having pleased everyone and intensified their curiosity. The Count called him “a cunning rascal.” Mademoiselle de Fontaine’s disappointment and Emilie’s restraint puzzled the family. During dinner, a sarcastic remark and Mademoiselle de Fontaine’s warning—that universal perfection must hide a defect, “the worst fault is to have none”—kept speculation alive.Despite efforts at indifference, Emilie’s feelings soon betrayed her. Longueville returned, and by his third visit everyone saw she was affected. Accustomed to being the center of attention, she was unsettled by someone who attracted her in turn; she tried to resist but could not dismiss him from her heart. Anxiety grew when his modesty and discretion clashed with her curiosity: he never spoke of himself or his family and deftly evaded hints. If she discussed painting, he answered like a connoisseur; if she played music, he proved a good pianist. Once they sang a duet, a moment of intimate harmony, yet he preserved the mystery of his social status.Her uncle and the family schemed to learn more, keen to meet Longueville’s sister, Clara. They proposed an informal dance to entertain her. Emilie felt joy at the thought of another person to care for and grew gentler and more indulgent; the change pleased her family, who read it as love transforming her selfishness. She believed Longueville’s visits were chiefly for her, and though they never spoke openly of passion, she sensed his affection. She led him into revealing small personal details and realized he, too, was studying her and encouraging her improvement.On the day of the dance Clara and her brother were principal guests. For the first time Emilie took pleasure in another girl’s triumph and lavished Clara with sincere attention. But Clara proved more deft in conversation, steering away from personal topics while charming Emilie—who felt both envious and measured. “Mademoiselle,” Clara said, “I’ve heard so much about you from Maximilien that I wanted to know you out of affection for him; isn’t wanting to know you a wish to love you?” Emilie admitted worry about speaking of those not of noble birth; Clara dismissed such concerns with graceful ease, putting Emilie at ease, and they danced with renewed warmth.Believing him well born and seeing his grace surpass her ideal, Emilie felt satisfaction and sparkle. As autumn deepened, the couple surrendered to the sweet tide of feeling. Small shared incidents—aimless conversations masking deeper affections, admiring sunsets, gathering daisies, singing passionate duets—knitted them closer. Each casual talk, musical exchange, and stroll deepened their intimacy and confirmed the mutual affection they had carefully concealed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################There is a particular twilight when day yields to night that draws reflective artists into their work. In that hour a young painter, finishing a large canvas, falls from his ladder, strikes his head, and loses consciousness.He is awakened by a young woman and her mother who rush to his aid. As he recovers he notices the girl’s beauty—matching an ideal he had imagined—and the older woman’s refined, marquise-like air. The mother tends him with gentle advice; both are reserved and withdraw without pressing for acquaintance.After they leave, the porter’s wife identifies them as Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, long-time residents of the building. The painter, Hippolyte Schinner, has just moved into the attic studio and begun to gain recognition. Determined to secure a better life for his own mother, who sacrificed much for him, he keeps his private affairs guarded despite modest success.During convalescence Hippolyte replays the encounter, especially the image of the girl, which lingers. Returning to his studio he is eager to visit them, drawn by a kinship born of imagined shared misfortune.In their modest home he sees signs of poverty but feels only empathy, noting the contrast between their humble surroundings and the elegance they maintain. The room holds faded traces of past luxury—worn finery kept meticulously clean. Conversation starts awkwardly but soon turns to art, allowing Hippolyte to speak openly about his work and feelings. Madame Leseigneur reveals a cherished portrait of her late husband, a naval officer, is decaying. Hippolyte offers to paint a new portrait, hoping the gesture will deepen their acquaintance; they accept with grace, and he senses the emotional weight behind the request.Adelaide briefly leaves and returns with candles to brighten the room. The mood shifts when a visitor arrives—someone who clearly knows her. A quick jealousy stirs in Hippolyte. The newcomer, an older gentleman in a coat of bygone fashion, carries an air of nobility mixed with libertinism; their exchange hints at layered history and social distinctions. Throughout the evening Hippolyte wrestles with admiration and uncertainty, drawn to her spirit while aware of the fragile boundary between their worlds.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A remarkable, faded figure stood beside the “Louis XIV’s light infantry,” Bonapartist sarcasm for the ragged remnants of the Monarchy: lean, aged, powdered hair dulled, intellect shadowed. Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz seemed apt. The silent old man puzzled the painter Chevalier—no one addressed him. His relation to the gallant man—friend, relative, or companion—was unclear; his presence hinted at a possible past link to the gallant’s deceased wife but offered no answers.One of the least dilapidated men approached Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat beside her; another bowed and took a nearby seat. Adelaide leaned over the old gentleman’s chair in a posture recalling a famous painting, though his familiarity appeared to irritate her. “Are you sulky with me?” he asked, casting a shrewd glance at the painter Schinner as if measuring him.Madame de Rouville introduced Hippolyte, the celebrated painter. The old man noticed Hippolyte’s red ribbon and contrasted it with his own hard-won honors. Hippolyte, captivated by Adelaide’s beauty, was briefly blind to the squalor around them; enthralled, he spoke little while the old gentleman lamented his losing streak at cards. Adelaide offered help; he insisted she stay where he could see her. When the game ended and the gentleman complained of losses, the silent figure only echoed the hour, prompting Hippolyte to take his leave, still under the spell of budding affection.The next day Hippolyte longed to see Adelaide again. He waited until afternoon before visiting Madame de Rouville and awkwardly asked for the Baron’s portrait. Adelaide, alone in the drawing-room, sent him to fetch it, revealing modesty with a charged glance. Hippolyte took the portrait and promised to return it soon.Their relationship blossomed over the following days with laughter and shared confidences. Schinner, eager to learn piquet, often lost, while the unspoken bond between Hippolyte and Adelaide deepened. They became inseparable; Adelaide’s affection grew and Hippolyte’s jealousy flared when the old Count showed familiarity with her. He worried their social life harmed his health and expected more from her. Their love unfolded without grand gestures, founded on mutual trust and subtle exchanges. Within two months Hippolyte visited daily, the old gentleman’s visits faded, and Hippolyte took his place at the card table, sharing equally in the losses.Still, Hippolyte fretted about Madame de Rouville’s finances. He spent two months on the Baron’s portrait—considering it among his finest—but was puzzled by the Baroness’s silence about payment. One day Adelaide visited his studio and was awestruck by the portrait; moved, Hippolyte declared his desire to marry her. Their moment was interrupted by the old vice-admiral, who praised the work and hinted at financial help.That evening Hippolyte noticed Madame de Rouville’s unusual excitement during cards. After a night of turmoil he found his purse missing and, in pain, suspected Adelaide and her mother of theft. A friend’s mention of rumors unsettled him further. Torn between love and doubt, he confronted Adelaide; struck by her pallor and sadness, he felt ashamed. During a tender evening of cards, Adelaide discreetly placed a new purse before him to replace the lost one. Overwhelmed, he declared his intention to marry her.The old vice-admiral soon returned with news of a promotion and offered support to the young couple, praising the portrait and dispelling lingering doubts. The evening closed with renewed unity. Ultimately the tale follows characters navigating love, trust, and misunderstanding, finding solace through faith, reconciliation, and shared bonds.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In October 1800 Bartolomeo di Piombo, a distraught foreigner in his sixties, stood outside the Tuileries with his wife and daughter. He handed his dagger to his wife and declared he would confront the Bonapartes. A soldier barred him for lacking a written request until Lucien Bonaparte recognized him and brought him to Napoleon.Napoleon dismissed his aides and listened as Bartolomeo sought asylum, admitting he had killed the Portas — the family that burned his vineyard and murdered his son. He confessed to avenging his son by killing all seven Portas, shocking Napoleon. Bitter at perceived Bonaparte indifference, Bartolomeo reminded them he had protected their family.Napoleon, unable to condone private vengeance, acknowledged the Corsican vendetta tradition, offered lodging in Paris and financial aid, but warned the city would not tolerate private justice. Bartolomeo accepted and pledged loyalty. Lucien found they had arrived with nothing, gave them money and promised help, noting their Corsican property could not sustain them in Paris. Fifteen years later, events would make Bartolomeo’s choices significant.Servin, a distinguished artist of about forty, was the first to open a studio specifically for young girls who wished to paint. Morally strict and devoted to art, he was married to a general’s daughter who brought no dowry. At first mothers stayed with their daughters; reassured by Servin’s principles they soon left. To avoid social disputes and because professional training required greater rigor, he limited pupils to young ladies from wealthy families and refused those intent on becoming professional artists. His careful method won him a stellar reputation among the aristocracy: Servin became to feminine art what Herbault was to bonnets and Leroy to gowns. Any mother who suspected talent in her daughter recommended him.The studio—an attic above his apartment reached by a small spiral stair and sealed from below—was arranged with care: a large, light-filled room with big panes shaded by green linen, dark-gray walls hung with caricatures and plaster models, a curious blend of disciplined disorder and harmony.On a bright July day the attic hummed with girls at their easels, each absorbed in different styles. Two cliques had formed: daughters of wealthy merchants and aristocrats led by the vain, pretentious Amelie Thirion, and the rest. Among them sat Ginevra Piombo, modestly dressed and anxious. Her talent and beauty provoked the aristocrats’ jealousy; Amelie, intent on ostracizing her, pushed Ginevra’s easel away amid whispers. The Bourbons’ second return had sharpened political divides: Ginevra was a devoted Bonapartist and drew disdain from royalist families, so Amelie’s slight was both personal and political.A faint sound behind a partition revealed Servin was sheltering a fugitive, an officer of the Imperial Guard. Concerned and resolute, Ginevra chose proximity to protect him rather than risk exposure. Madame Servin’s arrival—hinting her husband’s absence but seeming aware of the hidden man—heightened the danger. To mask the officer’s breathing, Ginevra quietly hummed a Corsican song while sketching his head with surprising fervor.Servin praised her talent; the likeness drew the other girls, inflaming Amelie’s envy when her probing failed. The shared awareness of the fugitive forged a tacit bond: Ginevra helped protect the officer and deflect suspicion. By day’s end her resolve to keep the secret and aid his escape had strengthened. Outside, political turmoil loomed; inside, rivalries and friendships negotiated themselves around their art.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the painter Servin and Ginevra thought themselves alone, Servin admitted a young Imperial officer with his arm in a sling. He recoiled at Ginevra until Servin soothed him, introducing her as the daughter of a loyal friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo; recognizing her patriotism, the soldier relaxed.Ginevra noticed his wound. He dismissed it, but cries from the street announced a death sentence: "Condemned to death!" The officer went pale at the name "Labedoyere" and collapsed. In despair he lamented that while Labedoyere faced death for their cause, he was hiding. When he tried to leave, Ginevra stopped him and asked if he truly believed he could restore the Emperor. Feeling helpless after losing his protector, he spoke of being willing to die, convinced an honest life might outweigh traitors’ lives.Alarmed, Ginevra offered her savings, saying her father was wealthy and would not blame her for helping a soldier. She urged him to wait out the storm, suggesting escape and service elsewhere. Her maternal grace and hope moved him deeply; he admired her and called her “an angel of goodness,” though his thoughts kept returning to Labedoyere. In silence an immediate bond formed: acquaintance deepened into understanding. Servin asked if the officer could save Labedoyere; the officer said he could only avenge him. Ginevra felt a surge at the thought of vengeance and his devotion to Napoleon.As the sun set, illuminating his face, Ginevra felt a near-magical connection, recalling childhood memories and leaving smiling, ignited with hope.The next day the officer—now called Luigi—shared stories of the retreat from Moscow and Waterloo. They spoke Corsican, which deepened their closeness. Ginevra listened, admiration growing.Days passed and Ginevra’s absence from social gatherings spurred rumors linking her to Luigi. Mademoiselle Thirion, worried, withdrew from the class; attendance declined. Ginevra, lost in thoughts of Luigi, remained oblivious. Laure, a fellow student, confided that her mother insisted she leave the studio because of the scandal; Ginevra was shocked but reassured Laure they would meet again. Laure left sad, and Ginevra felt the loss.Their bond strengthened into unspoken love. One evening Luigi signaled to Ginevra but retreated on seeing Laure. Ginevra then learned her classmates had left because of the rumors and confronted Servin, revealing the falsehoods. Servin dismissed the gossip, focused on his exhibition. Luigi entered, jokingly claiming responsibility for the missing students; the painter playfully suggested they marry. Their silence acknowledged their love. Servin encouraged their union and celebrated love’s joy. Ginevra proposed helping Luigi obtain a pardon, confident in her father’s influence.Meanwhile Ginevra’s parents grew anxious about her late returns. Bartolomeo, her father, paced, concerned though used to her hours. When she finally arrived, he embraced her with relief; the family’s warmth was evident. In the studio, amid challenges, Ginevra and Luigi’s relationship blossomed: their love, forged in adversity, became a source of strength that united them and embodied the resilience of their shared ideals.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Here she is, my Ginevra… mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old baron, embracing his daughter. Ginevra protested that he hurt her and laughed it off to ease the moment. The baroness revived; Piombo rubbed his hands with courtly satisfaction and the parents seemed renewed.At dinner Piombo teased Ginevra about her studio hours and hinted at jealousy of painting’s hold on her. The tone shifted when Ginevra, eyes downcast, confessed she loved a young man. Her parents were stunned. He was no prince but a man without fortune, a comrade of Labedoyere who had been proscribed and hidden by Servin. Piombo, offended, declared she should love only her father; Ginevra answered that love was not a matter of will.Piombo lamented his right to expect her fidelity; she retorted that she had not reproached him for devotion to Napoleon despite long absences and that she wanted a husband to protect her. Accusations followed—Piombo’s possessive pride versus Ginevra’s plea for the right to choose. She insisted she would not break her word: she loved this man truly. The baroness tried to soften things by saying daughters were meant to marry, but Piombo could not accept obedience to what he called an unjust order.The argument escalated into threats. Piombo raged that if this man loved Ginevra he would kill him; if not, Piombo himself would. Ginevra maintained one cannot compare filial and romantic love; she felt both but admitted the latter might be stronger. He thundered he would not allow the marriage; she calmly replied she would marry him. The baroness entreated peace. Shaken, Piombo asked to think. Ginevra implored him to love her for herself and promised never to abandon him. Their tenderness cooled his anger; he forgave and they played as in earlier days.In the following days Ginevra was more affectionate, as if thanking her parents for an ungranted consent. She sang and said their nocturnes needed a man’s voice. A week later her mother signaled triumph: she had persuaded Piombo to receive the suitor. That same day Ginevra arrived at home on Luigi’s arm.Luigi, an officer who had reappeared after hiding, had been restored to the officer’s list thanks to Ginevra’s appeals to the Duc de Feltre. Warned of Piombo’s possible disapproval, Luigi was anxious to meet him; his pallor deepened Ginevra’s love. Inside, Piombo sat cold and stern. Ginevra presented Luigi as a soldier who had fought beside the Emperor at Mont-Saint-Jean. Piombo inspected him and sarcastically noted he wore no decoration. Luigi said simply he no longer bore the Legion of Honor.The baroness, trying to bridge the moment, remarked that Luigi resembled someone they knew in Corsica—Nina Porta. Luigi answered that Nina had been his sister and then, when directly asked, confirmed he was Luigi Porta.At this name Piombo reeled. He and the baroness left the room in stunned silence; Ginevra stood pale and speechless. The revelation plunged the household into dread. The baroness had once said the Portas had killed Ginevra’s brother and burned their house; Piombo had then massacred the Portas in revenge. Luigi, bewildered, asked how he had survived. He recounted being bound to a bed before the house was set alight, then taken to Genoa at six and raised by an old man named Colonna, who warned of enemies and advised he assume the name Luigi to hide his identity. How he escaped death remained unclear.Ginevra, horrified at the vendetta revealed in a single word, urged Luigi to stay for the moment—he was safe in her father’s house—but warned that once he left he would be hunted by Corsicans in Piombo’s service. Luigi, shaken, asked if the vendetta existed between them; she smiled sadly and nodded. Their love, she said, must be pure and resolute to endure what lay ahead. He squeezed her hand and smiled; they resolved their destiny was sealed. Ginevra escorted him to his modest lodging herself, determined rather than cowed.Back home the atmosphere turned chill. Piombo fumbled for words but could not speak; he rang for a servant and ordered a fire kindled, though his voice trembled. Both father and daughter paid for their pride—her reproachful eyes, his sorrowful ones—each nursing private torment the household could not bridge.On Ginevra’s birthday, hoping to reconcile them, her mother gathered father and daughter in Piombo’s study. Ginevra read her mother’s intent and smiled sadly. At that moment two notaries arrived with witnesses to fulfill the law regarding Ginevra’s marriage to Luigi Porta. Piombo bristled, furious at what he saw as an assault on paternal authority. He demanded whether French laws could tear a daughter from her father and deprive an old man of his last consolation. The notary tried to speak, but Piombo’s agitation grew; he thrice interrupted with theatrical outrage.When the notary continued calmly, Piombo suddenly seized a dagger from the wall and rushed toward Ginevra. Witnesses lunged to restrain him, but his fury pushed them aside. Seeing him advance, Ginevra knelt before her father and begged him to be merciful and give his consent. The sight unnerved him; he flung the dagger away and, struck by his own weakness, pushed her aside as if in shame.Then Piombo pronounced the cruelest judgment: “Go. The wife of Luigi Porta cannot be a Piombo. I have no daughter. My Ginevra Piombo is buried here.” He pressed his hand to his heart and bade her leave his house. The baroness, stunned, could not prevent him; he had renounced Ginevra publicly and irreversibly.Ginevra fled to Luigi’s humble abode. She confessed the loss of parental blessing and their lack of fortune, but Luigi flung aside material concerns. “Then I am richer than kings!” he exclaimed. He vowed to love her in her parents’ place; she answered they would be happy, though joy mingled with dread at the long ordeal ahead.Thus remained the hard facts: Ginevra’s candid declaration of love, Piombo’s possessive claim and violent renunciation, Luigi’s return thanks to Ginevra, and the catastrophic discovery that Luigi was a Porta—tied to the vendetta that had cost Ginevra’s brother and destroyed her family’s house. Piombo’s past massacre of the Portas made the union dangerous and impossible in his eyes, and his public repudiation left Ginevra only Luigi as shelter.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after Ginevra was expelled from her father's house she took refuge with Madame Servin until her marriage to Luigi. Madame Servin received her coldly—her husband blamed Ginevra for the trouble—and the woman’s selfishness, though upsetting, left Ginevra proud yet shocked. She rented a room near Luigi, who devoted himself to her and painted a bright future that lifted her spirits.One morning trunks from her mother arrived with a loving, sorrowful letter pleading that she reconsider the marriage. The letter moved Ginevra and for an instant she thought of returning home. But when Luigi appeared, her resolve to abandon him dissolved; she chose to bury her grief for the sake of their love.On the wedding day Ginevra was essentially alone; Luigi brought ordinary men as witnesses, a stark contrast to the usual gaiety. Her beauty silenced the small party as they passed joyful processions, highlighting their different circumstances. In the mayor’s office the clerk noted the absence of her parents and the mayor learned they had protested. The bureaucratic formality felt oppressive, reducing the ceremony to a legal act devoid of celebration.After signing the registers they left with mixed relief and sadness. Luigi’s gratitude and Ginevra’s quiet happiness carried them to the church, where an old priest married them in a modest chapel amid more festive weddings. The priest’s remarks about their parents’ absence stung, but a passing sergeant’s simple blessing comforted Ginevra.They moved into a modest apartment Luigi called their true wedding, filled with artistic supplies arranged for her; she delighted in the thoughtful touches and they felt they had found paradise despite limitations. At first they indulged one another, but soon reality pressed them to work: Ginevra copied paintings and won clients; Luigi used his handwriting skill to offer copying services, succeeded and hired assistants. Evenings were shared and, though Ginevra sometimes regretted the family life she had left, an anniversary portrait she painted for Luigi symbolized their wedded bliss as another year passed in contentment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of 1819 picture-dealers urged Ginevra to produce originals instead of copies as competition grew. Madame Porta regretted not turning earlier to genre painting, and though Ginevra tried portraiture she faced stiff rivalry. Despite modest savings, Luigi and Ginevra grew anxious about their future.That winter Luigi labored relentlessly in a crowded, less lucrative writing market. He could no longer afford help and had to work longer for the same pay. Ginevra painted several commendable works, but dealers shunned lesser-known artists and low prices brought few sales.Their household became dire; love and happiness were overshadowed by poverty. They concealed worries from one another: Ginevra lavished affection while Luigi hid his fears, each hoping love would sustain them. One night Ginevra woke to find Luigi gone and discovered him working late to avoid burdening her. Moved, she began coloring lithographs, working into the night while Luigi toiled in his garret. At dawn they pretended to sleep, each shielding the other from distress.Another night, exhausted and feverish, Luigi opened his window and saw Ginevra's lamp across the courtyard, realizing she too was working. He confronted her and learned she could not idle while he suffered. They shared a tender moment, watching dawn together and feeling tentative hope.Their struggle deepened when Ginevra gave birth to a son, Bartolomeo. Joy briefly masked their burdens, but soon they sold possessions and jewels. Poverty stripped them of comfort and pride. Months later Ginevra, frail and worn, struggled to care for a sickly child as Luigi, feeling helpless, sold her paintings and their remaining furniture for a pittance. Ginevra managed the household and tended their son while often starving herself to feed Luigi.One evening she offered Luigi her last piece of bread; realizing her sacrifice, he was engulfed by despair and left to seek aid. In desperation he sold himself as a military substitute to obtain an advance. He hurried home with the money, finding Ginevra asleep with their child. When he woke her she smiled, then noticed Bartolomeo cold and lifeless. Ginevra, delirious, professed her love and lamented their lost child; she died in his arms.As Luigi mourned, Ginevra's parents sat at home remembering their daughter. Before they could reconcile a messenger arrived with the news. The old couple were shattered—the cycle of suffering had claimed another victim, leaving only memories and grief.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Many incident-rich tales can be told loosely, but some human experiences demand a delicate, almost anatomical touch—portraits need a soul; certain truths surface only through subtle harmonies of time, circumstance, or inner moral inclination.Such revelations are required for this simple story, meant for grave, reflective readers who find sustenance in tender emotion. If the writer, like a surgeon at a dying friend’s side, reveres the subject, the reader should feel it too. The narrative speaks to those who have known private grief: the luxury of tears, the silent pangs of memory, regret for loss, and smiles at past joys. Others will set the book aside; those who have never buried a beloved will not grasp these pages. The writer assures the tale is true and worth the reader’s emotional investment.In contemporary France, speech varies by individual; Parisians describe the same person in amusingly different ways. A Practical man lists facts about Madame Firmiani: a fine house on the rue du Bac, well-furnished salons, an income of a hundred thousand francs, and a husband once receiver-general—solid, respectable people.A Lounger praises her social standing and excellent tea; an Egotist dismisses her salons as trivial, insisting a mind should transcend small talk. A Student calls her enchanting and eager to introduce you. A Fop laments faded beauty but admits lingering charm. A Connoisseur praises her painting collection. A Spiteful woman brands her a dangerous siren; an Attaché recalls past beauty. A Distinguishing observer notes elite connections despite modest means.A Duke professes not to know her, recognizing only those he has met. A Ninny mistakenly claims she sang at the Opera. Two old ladies trace ties to the noble Cadignan family. A Joke says her salon is safe because any scoundrels are of high quality. Others allege affairs—Murat’s mistress—or accuse her of coquetry that ruined a young man’s prospects.Madame Firmiani thus emerges through many lenses—each account reflecting the observer as much as the observed—and invites reflection on the deeper emotional truths that bind them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In January 1824 opinions about Madame Firmiani diverged so widely that no clear portrait emerged: widow or wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or otherwise, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. The variety of interpretations showed how society distorted a reputation, like lithographic blocks yielding different images from slight variations.That winter Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly provincial magnate from Touraine, grew intrigued. Shrewd and honorable and used to managing an estate, he cherished his nephew Octave de Camps. When Octave sold an estate without consulting him and a distant relative claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and been reduced to teaching mathematics, the old man refused to accept the worst and went to Paris to investigate.Using his faubourg Saint‑Germain connections, he adopted the name de Rouxellay and arranged to meet Madame Firmiani when Octave would be absent. He soon confirmed his nephew’s financial distress. De Rouxellay, no naive countryman—once a member of the King’s guard—knew society’s manners and politics. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomère, he expressed pity for Octave, wondering how a woman of such luxury could be linked to someone in such straits. Yet the salon’s atmosphere and her presence captivated him and forced him to reconsider his initial judgments.Madame Firmiani, said to be twenty‑five but nearer twenty‑eight, had married the elusive Firmiani at sixteen. At her height she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit, admired even by rivals. That popularity made her a target for malicious gossip; though unfounded, the rumors persisted, revealing the dangers of societal suspicion and hinting at her complex, intriguing character.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had gone; Madame Firmiani found him still seated when the clock struck two. “Madame,” he began as she rose, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.” She sat again, affected; Monsieur de Bourbonne could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure. He spoke bluntly: rumors said his nephew had ruined himself for her, living in a garret while she enjoyed wealth. She stopped him. “I know of them,” she said. “You are too polite to press and too gallant to question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe me: I scorn money. I married without fortune. Your nephew’s wealth or poverty matters not; I welcome him as a friend. My friends respect one another; I cannot accept those I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip and dishonesty.”“Madame,” the old man insisted, trembling, “I am almost a father to Octave. Pardon me one thing. I swear as a gentleman your answer shall remain here.” He put his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”“To any other man I would answer with a look,” she replied. “To you I ask what you would think of a woman who answered such a question. To confess love is the lover’s reward. To tell another—” She left the sentence unfinished, rose, bowed, and withdrew to her rooms.He left puzzled, thinking her either sly or angelic.The next morning Monsieur de Bourbonne found Octave in his garret and berated him for squandering the advantages of an uncle who meant to leave him an estate. “Have you been gambling? Lost at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie, I’ll return to my youthful ways.” He approved when Octave admitted he had “ruined” himself for Madame Firmiani.Octave produced a letter the woman had given him and asked his uncle to read. It revealed their relation and the moral test she had set. The letter began, in effect, “My beloved—” and went on to explain why she was sad. She said a wife should be gay and confiding, that a woman’s glory is in the man she loves, and yet she had been humiliated by what she learned of Octave’s family. Knowing that his father had unjustly deprived others of property made her feel that she could not respect him while he kept such gains. She urged him to consult his conscience: “If you come to me poor, my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. I am your wife, not your mistress. A man who loves a woman sincerely respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself.” She did not demand restitution as a command; she appealed to his honor and begged him to perceive his faults himself.Octave, moved to tears, said there was more, but the rest he smiled away as a lover’s secret. The uncle, surprised by the gravity of the letter and that they considered themselves married, asked whether Octave had gone into teaching and whether he still had the uncle’s capital. Octave explained that the letter had sparked a revolution in him; conscience made every pleasure feel like theft. He rushed to Madame Firmiani, and together they calculated what ought to be paid to the Bourgneufs—the family whom Octave’s father had wronged. Despite limited means, he condemned himself to pay three percent interest and to make restitution as far as he could. Madame Firmiani, prudent because of past insecurity, offered her savings and he accepted them.“You mean that adorable woman actually saves money?” the uncle cried, half mocking, half admiring.“Do not laugh at her,” Octave replied. He told his uncle that Madame Firmiani’s husband had gone to Greece in 1820 and died three years later; proof of his death and his will had been lost or stolen during the troubles there, so she had lived frugally to protect herself against possible loss of fortune. Octave had been trying to acquire property of his own to provide for her; he took pupils rather than rely on others. But the act of restitution had brought him joys beyond millions: finding the Bourgneufs living in misery—an old agent father, daughters keeping books and house, a sick mother—he felt like Providence in relieving them. Making amends gave him a purity of feeling he had thought he lacked; he called Madame Firmiani “my dear conscience” and hoped to earn an honest fortune by industry.Touched, the old man said, “Ah, my boy, you have your mother’s soul.” At that moment a carriage sounded outside. Madame Firmiani entered, a little annoyed to find Monsieur de Bourbonne there yet smiling. “I came to entreat my husband to accept my fortune,” she said. The Austrian Embassy had just sent documents proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet had kept safe. Octave could accept it all: he was richer than she by having “treasures here”—she laid her hand on his heart—“to which none but God can add.” She then laid her head on his breast in unrestrained happiness.Monsieur de Bourbonne concluded with an old-man’s reflection: “In my day we made love; in yours, you love. Women are all that is best in humanity; they are not even guilty of their faults, for they come through us.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Middle of the story — midpoint of the narrative. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once a dark, winding lane near the Hôtel de Ville that ended at the Rue Martroi, was closed by a turnstile until 1823. Less than six feet wide at its broadest, it held pooled gutter water and was perpetually muddy; sunlight touched it only briefly in summer, yet dampness lingered. Residents lit lamps at five in June and kept them burning through winter. Approaching the Marais felt like walking through cellars, as many old Paris streets did. The corner house at Rue du Tourniquet and Rue de la Tixeranderie still bore iron rings from chains once used for public safety.Built to combat humidity, the house had its cellars raised above ground and an entrance reached by three steps. The door formed a closed arch with a keystone carved as a female head. Three high windows, barred with iron, lit a small dwelling where two beds draped in green serge and an old woman tending a brazier—preparing a porter’s-wife stew—were visible. The kitchen-dining room was sparsely furnished but kept with evident cleanliness and thrift.The old woman’s pale, wrinkled face seemed part of the house itself. She sat by the fireplace with a look of mischief mixed with resignation while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. With slow fingers and antiquated spectacles the mother occasionally glanced out the window; the daughter bent over her work with focused modesty. Their modest home reflected the lives of working-class Parisian women. Despite poverty, the girl retained youthful charm—rosy cheeks and bright eyes—her spirit not wholly dimmed. The mother speculated about passersby and hoped for a better future for her daughter; the girl usually kept her gaze down, absorbed in embroidery.One August day in 1815 the mother noticed a new face among the regular passersby: a tall, thin man in black whose solemn bearing intrigued her. She fancied he was a clerk from the Préfet’s office and awaited his return. When he passed again, Caroline, roused from indifference, felt an unfamiliar sympathy, noting traces of suffering on his face; for the first time a passerby stirred such feeling, and she looked forward to another glimpse, intrigued by the possibility he might alter her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved erratically, no longer passing the Rue du Tourniquet at the same hour. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted manner—often lost in thought and seldom acknowledging her or her daughter Caroline. One mid-September morning Caroline’s lively presence caught his eye; their fleeting glances suggested a silent understanding.Weeks later he resumed passing twice daily, and their brief encounters became a guarded routine. Their wordless exchanges conveyed concern: Caroline sensed when he was troubled, and he knew when she worried about work. Both remained cautious, fearing intimacy might cause trouble.Annoyed by her daughter’s indecision, Madame Crochard turned her displeasure toward the Gentleman, who had once met her mild approval. As winter brought harder times, he overheard a revealing mother-and-daughter conversation about their finances and, moved, secretly left money for them. Caroline found it the next day; a silent moment of gratitude passed between them.In early May Caroline proposed a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—unexpectedly joined. Madame Crochard pretended to sleep, letting Caroline and the Gentleman speak freely. The countryside and their easy talk lifted his spirits; he began to see Caroline differently. Sharing family hardships and personal stories, their bond deepened. The day brimmed with laughter, and as evening fell Caroline voiced her newfound happiness. Roger—by that name now—playfully held her hand before leaving, sealing an unspoken promise of a brighter future.Roger and Caroline’s adventures continue in Paris. Caroline has spent a month in a charming second-floor flat on Rue Taitbout, furnished in an English-influenced style. Her bedroom’s gray hangings trimmed with green silk, comfortable chairs, and a writing table for love letters are matched by a bronze clock of Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-pattern carpet. As she readies for Roger’s visit, her hairdresser flatters her.On a chilly September day in 1816, dressed in a green grenadine gown trimmed with chinchilla, she steps onto the balcony to watch for Roger, heart racing. After fifteen minutes impatience gives way to joy when he arrives; they embrace passionately, rejoicing in their reunion after three days apart. He must attend a meeting with the Attorney‑General, which disappoints her; she playfully objects to his theatre visits without her and declares her deep affection. Perched on his lap, she says she wishes to take his name—Caroline de Bellefeuille—and use her earnings to help the poor. She plays a sonata as he leaves, watching him drive away, longing for his return.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years after settling in her Rue Taitbout home, Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille sits contentedly with her four-year-old son Charles, who plays with a rocking horse. Now twenty-four, her beauty reflects happiness and fulfillment. She has embraced motherhood, devoted to raising her children and cultivating their appreciation for goodness and beauty. Content with simple pleasures, she is indifferent to society’s expectations and never seeks luxury or status.Rocking her infant daughter Eugenie, Caroline recalls joyful visits to Bellefeuille with Roger. Their love has deepened in the simplicity of shared life, free from social complication, yet she wonders why Roger insists on secrecy and what their arrangement truly is.On May 6, 1822—the anniversary of a pivotal day—Caroline prepares for dinner, expecting Roger. He brings a document detailing Eugenie’s marriage portion and explains his view that Charles, as a boy, must learn self-sufficiency while Eugenie, as a girl, deserves a fortune. Their evening is warm and lively, ending with a magical lantern show for Charles. But as night deepens, Roger must leave; he promises three days off, yet duty and his habit of secrecy leave Caroline with the familiar ache of separation.Soon after, Caroline hastens to her mother, Madame Crochard, who is gravely ill. The widow’s acquaintances, unaware Caroline is her daughter, have summoned a priest for last rites. Arriving amid confusion and deceit, Caroline finds local women hoping to secure benefit from the widow’s wealth. Despite their intentions, Caroline cares for her mother tenderly. In her final moments, Madame Crochard laments their estrangement and reveals that the priest made her confess about Caroline’s benefactor—an admission that hints at a complex web of relationships and secrets to follow.To clarify those connections, the narrative backtracks to November 1805. A young barrister, Monsieur de Granville, precarious after leaving a gathering at the High Chancellor’s, receives a letter urging his swift return: a proposed marriage to Mademoiselle Angélique Bontems could secure his future despite the Bontems’ social stigma. Traveling to Bayeux, he finds the Bontems’ household stifling under the widow’s strict piety. During a church service he glimpses Angélique; her beauty captivates him and rekindles a childhood affection, setting the stage for a reunion that may alter both their lives. The intertwined stories of Caroline and Granville explore love, duty, and social expectation as each navigates personal desire against familial obligation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he frequented her dreary drawing-room and watched Angelique mark her trousseau. She never raised religion in argument; when he playfully handled her rosary or mocked practices she calmly returned it to its bag. Firm in the Church’s teachings, she insisted a mother must have a religion and asked how one could judge between disbelievers and God.Granville, taken by her conviction, sometimes mistook desire for love and was tempted to adopt her beliefs. Angelique reconciled feeling with duty, leaving him uncertain about his own emotions. On the day of the marriage contract Madame Bontems exacted a promise from Granville to respect Angelique’s practices and allow her freedom of conscience; Angelique’s gentle look convinced him and the Abbe Fontanon approved. After their wedding they moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor.Angelique persuaded him to take a ground-floor apartment in the Marais near a church and chapel, arguing it would be better for their future children. Granville, who preferred a more fashionable quarter, yielded. He installed his study and left furnishing to Angelique, who took delight in arranging the home. He was dismayed by the cold solemnity she created—a reflection of her austere upbringing. He tried to understand her choices and comforted her that happiness came from love and gentleness, not material things, yet grew frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm.For a time the couple seemed harmonious; Granville stayed absorbed in work and remained largely oblivious to Angelique’s strict observances—her Lenten austerities he passed off as singular zeal. He went to church with her on Sundays but did not grasp her fervor until a disruptive event.In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, Angelique’s spiritual director, arrived in Paris alarmed at what he saw as her moral decline and aimed to reclaim her from a lax lifestyle. Angelique adopted stricter practices that sparked misunderstandings about social obligations. Granville was irritated by her frequent excuses to avoid events, especially balls. After a tense evening he confronted her about her demeanor and dress, urging her to fulfill her social role. Arguments escalated: Angelique cited her director’s prohibitions; Granville argued the priest was overreaching. She even wrote to the Pope for guidance.The Pope’s reply sided with Granville, saying a wife should accompany her husband even at some personal cost. Rather than reconcile them, the decision deepened the divide: Angelique and her director dismissed it. Frustrated, Granville began asserting his preferences more forcibly—ordering meat daily despite her objections—and retreated from social life, weighed down by what he saw as her bigotry.Their home grew oppressive and joyless; the rigid piety affected everyone, including the servants. Angelique’s austerity dimmed her beauty; duty replaced warmth. Granville felt trapped in a marriage that left little room for affection. Their children, raised under strict rules, suffered: he sent his sons away to school early and planned to marry off his daughters as soon as possible. Increasingly isolated, he sought refuge in work and a legal book, while Angelique’s efforts to reconvert him only worsened their estrangement. The household became a cold, rigid place rather than a home of companionship.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between 1806 and 1825 the de Granville household showed little outward drama, but Madame de Granville was inwardly miserable. After losing his affection she turned to Mass and prayer for reconciliation, while Granville, a prominent judge, lived in the entresol to avoid her. Each morning a housekeeper brought messages; he routinely refused breakfast, citing urgent court business.At thirty-five, prematurely aged, she sank into bitterness. When Granville declined the presidency of a provincial court, friends and her confessor suspected an illicit affair—a mortifying idea to her. During Lent in 1822 her health declined from stress and fever, worsened by his indifference and by her pious efforts, which undermined attempts at warmth.Recovering in late May, she consulted the Abbe Fontanon, who revealed that Granville had lived seven years with a concubine, fathering two children and dissipating their fortune. Shocked and torn between faith and betrayal, she struggled to accept it. The Abbe urged patience and forgiveness; she resolved to confront her husband.Before she could, an argument elsewhere between Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille erupted: Mademoiselle declared hatred for Madame, blaming her for their unhappiness; Roger spoke of future hopes, until Madame de Granville fainted. On recovering she confronted Granville, who accused her of subordinating his happiness to piety and failing as a loving wife.Their exchange exposed irreconcilable differences: he insisted love requires sacrifice and, after years of neglect, had sought happiness elsewhere; she clung to moral righteousness. His admission and refusal to restore what neglect had extinguished left her devastated, the marriage plainly failed—love cannot be commanded—and she was left to face the consequences.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early one December night in 1833, a white‑haired man whose face showed grief more than years paced the Rue Gaillon and stopped before a modest two‑story house with a dim attic light. A young doctor, Horace Bianchon, emerged; recognizing the visitor, he offered his arm and bantered about the late hour and the slippery pavement. The visitor—Comte de Granville—answered with weary pride that at fifty‑five he was still hale and often walked home from the Courts or the club when not visiting in the evening.Bianchon teased that a man of his station, with fine horses and money, was courting robbery. Granville shrugged off the danger with melancholy, confessing with a hint of childish amusement that he had been spying on a shadow in the third‑floor window of Bianchon’s house. He took a morbid interest in that garret, likening his curiosity to a citizen’s pleasure in watching the Palais‑Royal being finished.When Bianchon offered to tell him who lived in the garret, Granville stopped him. He declared he would not pay to learn whether the shadow was man or woman, happy or miserable. Nine years earlier he had ceased to be surprised that old men found pastime in growing flowers; life’s events had taught him to distrust human affection. “I grew old within a few days,” he said. He had renounced attachment to living beings, clinging only to “unreasoning animals, or plants, or superficial things”—even the grace of Taglioni moved him more than human feeling. He abhorred life and insisted nothing could interest him.Bianchon probed: did he have children? Granville answered bitterly, listing them—an eldest daughter, the Comtesse de Vandenesse; another daughter married suitably through that sister; sons successful in law: a viscount who had been public prosecutor at Limoges and now President of the Court at Orléans, and a younger son a public prosecutor in Paris. Each had their cares; none had devoted themselves to him. If one had tried to fill the void in his breast, that child would have ruined his life by sacrificing itself to him—and why should it? Perhaps they were now waiting for his money. Bianchon protested; Granville, sarcastic and self‑aware, explained his philanthropy as self‑indulgence: he paid for sensations the way he gambled, seeking illusions that might make his heart glow while expecting no gratitude. He would see Bianchon die without blinking and asked that Bianchon feel the same toward him. Life’s events had flowed over his heart like lava over Herculaneum—the town remained, dead.Unable to leave him in that mood, Bianchon insisted he could treat Granville’s malady and then, reluctantly, described the garret’s occupant. The picture was stark: a woman of about thirty whose love had become fanaticism. She adored a young man of pleasing appearance but every vice—Solvet—a gambler given to wine and women, guilty of acts the law might punish. This woman had abandoned a comfortable life and an adoring husband, the father of her children, for this man. She worked day and night; he had stolen from her even funds saved for the children’s food and clothes. Only three days earlier she had sold her hair—the finest Bianchon had ever seen—and when the lover returned she could not hide the gold quickly enough. For a smile or a kiss she would give away the price of a fortnight’s subsistence. The toil had hollowed her cheeks; the children’s cries had broken her heart; she lay ill on her wretched bed that night. When Bianchon visited, the family had nothing to eat; the children were so weak they could not even cry.Despite himself Granville slid his hand toward his waistcoat pocket as if to help, but Bianchon begged him to pity the woman and to help cure her passion. Granville recoiled: how could he pity a misery whose joys he might have prized with his whole fortune? Did not thousands of dead men, thousands of sick and old men, long to barter everything for a return of youth and feeling? Bianchon murmured “Poor Caroline!” At the name Granville shuddered, gripped the doctor’s arm, and asked hoarsely whether the woman’s name was Caroline Crochard. When Bianchon confirmed it, Granville’s reaction was violent: he accused Bianchon of rousing the worst pain his heart could endure, an emotion he called a gift from hell. He vowed he knew how to pay such debts.They had reached the Rue de la Chaussée d’Antin when a shriveled night scavenger stood by the curb. Granville asked if the scavenger ever found thousand‑franc notes and, hearing that sometimes he did, handed him such a note with a grotesque injunction: spend it in the wine shop, get drunk, start fights, beat your wife, blacken your friends’ eyes—give work to the watch, the surgeon, the druggist, perhaps to the police, public prosecutor, judge, and prison wardens. “Do not try to do anything else, or the devil will be revenged on you sooner or later,” Granville said. He pointed out the scavenger’s corrupt face to Bianchon and declared he had thus “squared accounts with hell” and had found a perverse pleasure in the money. As for Caroline Crochard, he pronounced coldly that she might die of hunger and thirst listening to the cries of her starving children, convinced of her lover’s baseness; he would not give a sou to rescue her. Because Bianchon had helped her, he said, he would see him no more.Granville left Bianchon stunned and walked to the Rue Saint‑Lazare where a carriage waited. A servant informed him that his son, the attorney‑general, had been to see him an hour earlier and was waiting in his bedroom. Granville dismissed the servant, then confronted his son—in a tone at once imperious and unexpectedly tender—about coming after being ordered not to visit unless summoned.Eugène, the younger man, spoke with tremor: that afternoon a very young man arrested in the house of a friend he had robbed had appealed to Granville, claiming to be his son. “His name?” Granville asked. “Charles Crochard,” Eugène answered. Granville waved him down: that sufficed. He stood a moment, then spoke as a father. “Charles Crochard spoke the truth. I am glad you came to me tonight, my good Eugène.” He handed his son a bundle of banknotes—“a considerable sum”—and authorized him to use it as he saw fit in the affair. He trusted him implicitly and approved whatever arrangements Eugène might make now or later.The mood shifted. Granville’s last words of parental counsel and farewell were almost tender. He warned Eugène about the weight of marriage—“that act is the most important society requires of us”—and urged him not to undertake it lightly, to study the character of the woman he would partner with and to consult him. A lack of union between husband and wife, Granville warned—with involuntary shudder—led to terrible misfortunes and social punishment. He promised to write on the subject from Florence. Then, with a quietness that suggested this might be their last parting, he asked his son to kiss him and announced his plan: tomorrow he would beg dismissal from the King and go to Italy. The conversation closed on his wish to preserve his dignity as a father and as a former presiding officer of a supreme court of justice; he would not wish to blush before his son.Thus the scene presents two images of Granville: in the street a man hardened into cruelty and misanthropy, giving a thousand francs to be squandered so social machinery may be set to work on trivial moral wreckage; in his bedroom a father handing money and last counsels to his son, preparing to depart and to write advice about marriage from Florence. Between those moments stands Bianchon’s account of Caroline Crochard—a portrait of sacrifice and degradation—and the Comte’s icy rejection of any duty toward her, even as the name awakens a torment that proves he is not wholly dead to feeling.
