The other colonel ordered his battery forward; the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him over on the near side of his horse. Our Colonel, nearby, galloped up, calling ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam rose to his lips; he roared like a lion. Incapable even of a word or cry, he made a terrifying signal, drew his sword, and in two seconds his opponent lay with his skull split. The soldiers backed away.“The captain, nearly crushed, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This aggravated his rage. He felt pledged to protect the husband as he would the woman herself.“In the hovel beyond Zembin where I was so well received, this captain sat opposite me, and his wife was at the other end facing the Colonel. This Sicilian, Rosina, was very dark, with the sun’s fire in her almond-shaped eyes. She was terribly thin; her face dusted like fruit exposed on a highway. Scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair dishevelled under a shawl, she still had a woman’s graces; her small rose mouth, white teeth, and figure retained charms that misery had not erased. Rosina seemed fragile but wiry and springy. Her husband, a Piedmontese gentleman, had an ironical simplicity that betrayed cruelty beneath phlegm and indolence. He seemed unaware of any connection between his wife and the Colonel for three years; yet a thin, restless underlip turning down at the corners filled me with distrust.“The conversation was not lively when I arrived. My comrades ate in silence; they asked questions, mixed reflections on the campaign, and lamented the cold. A minute after I arrived, the colonel, having finished his meager meal, wiped his mustache, bade us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman, saying, ‘Rosina?’ and went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of the call was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing the annoyance she could not feel at seeing her thralldom flaunted without decency, and the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband. Yet there was in her rigid features a sort of presentiment; perhaps she foresaw her fate. Rosina remained quietly in her place.“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was settled in straw, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’ The tone was even more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel. All sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too.“‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband.“‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied seriously, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly.“Thereupon we lay down in the room, and before long all were sound asleep.“Next morning, each one, without waking his neighbor or seeking companionship, set out again on his way, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror ever enacted under heaven. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, most of us met again and walked on together, urged by the same necessity.“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse, we heard sounds like a roar, mingled with a woman’s feeble scream. We looked: the farmhouse had been barricaded and was in flames. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and an indescribable pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain was quietly approaching to join our caravan; we gazed at him in silence; he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’ We all walked on without a word to him.”“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.“It would be frightful to leave with this horrible picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to the doctor.“Yes,” the doctor replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption, in its last stage, left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking about four in the morning, signed to me with a friendly smile to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her face was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling sprang from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.“At this moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. I did not see the impatient gesture he made, expressing regret for having lost sight of his wife for a few of the last granted moments; but it is certain anyone but the dying woman might have misunderstood it. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, the Duke had a thousand odd surface ways which often lead a genius to be mistaken for a madman, explainable by the exacting needs of their intellect. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out, took her husband’s hand and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.PARIS, June 1839–42.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy (selected cross-references):- Bianchon, Horace: Father Goriot; Cesar Birotteau; The Secrets of a Princess.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“Ah! madame,” said the doctor, “I have some appalling stories, but each has its proper hour in conversation—you know Chamfort’s joke to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” the hostess replied.“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” they all cried.The doctor bowed and began.About a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the bank of the Loir, stands an old brown house with very high roofs, solitary and utterly neglected—no tannery or tavern near it. A garden slopes to the river where once-clipped box shrubs now run wild; willows rooted in the stream form a half-hidden fence. Weeds have taken the bank; fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, their suckers forming a thicket; espaliers look like a little copse. The once-graveled paths are overgrown—indeed there is no trace of a path. From the hilltop where the ruins of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, one can see that this spot must once have been the pride of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture. An arbor’s wreck and a partially standing table testify to that bygone care. The garden’s aspect suggests provincial peace—the epitaph of a worthy tradesman. To complete the mournful air, one wall bears a sundial with the motto “Ultimam cogita” (think on your last).The house itself is dreadfully dilapidated: the roof leaking and warped, shutters always closed, swallows nesting on balconies, doors shut. Grasses green the flagstones of the steps; ironwork is rusty; moon, sun, winter, and snow have eaten into the wood, peeling paint and warping boards. The silence is broken only by birds, cats, polecats, rats, and mice; to the observer an invisible hand has inscribed one single word over it all: Mystery.From the street you see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of children's holes; the gate, as the narrator later learned, had been blocked for ten years. Through breaches you view the courtyard: the same ruinous state as the garden side—paving-stones choked with weeds, cracked walls, blackened coping laced with pellitory, disjointed stone steps, a rotten bell-cord, broken gutter-spouts. What fire from heaven, what decree, has struck this dwelling? Has God been mocked, or was France betrayed? Reptiles crawl over it but give no answer. The empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.It was formerly a small fiefed estate called La Grande Bretèche. During the doctor’s stay in Vendôme—where he had been left to attend a rich patient—this odd dwelling became one of his keenest pleasures. Better than a ruin, it carried a secret, an unrevealed thought: the slow destruction of a standing house seemed like an avenging hand at work. Many evenings he climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braved scratches to enter the ownerless garden, and lingered for hours in its disorder. He would weave romances and indulge in melancholia there, relishing the unwritten poetry of ignorance; had he known the reason for the neglect, the charm would have been gone. To him the place represented various phases of human misfortune: sometimes a graveyard without the dead, sometimes a lepers’ home, sometimes the house of the Atridae—but above all provincial life, contemplative and like an hour-glass. He often wept there; he never laughed.Occasionally he felt involuntary terrors at the dull hum of a passing wood-pigeon overhead. The damp earth required watching for lizards, vipers, and frogs; a cold would seem to settle on your shoulders like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck. One evening a weathercock turned in the wind, its creaking like a cry from the house just as the doctor was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this enormous air of woe. He returned to his inn in a pensive mood. After supper, the hostess came into his room with a mysterious air to announce, “Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.”“Who is Monsieur Regnault?” the doctor asked.“What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,” she said and left.A tall, slim man in black appeared: hat in hand, receding forehead, small pointed head, colorless face like dirty water—a sort of usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he wore a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“Monsieur,” the doctor asked, “whom have I the honor of addressing?”The man sat by the fire, rubbed his hands and answered, “Dear me, it is very cold. I am Monsieur Regnault.”“My curiosity is piqued,” the doctor said.“I am notary at Vendôme,” Regnault replied. He quickly informed the doctor that the latter’s habit of walking in La Grande Bretèche’s garden constituted a misdemeanor since the estate was enclosed property, and as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret he begged the doctor to desist. He insisted he meant no offense and would be glad to allow free access if he were not bound by the testatrix’s instructions: he had never set foot in the house since the will was read. They had done nothing there but verify doors and windows to assess taxes; administering Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life. His account was long and voluble, but the doctor pressed him for the reason that La Grande Bretèche was left to ruin.Regnault delighted in relating his own history and the tale of Madame de Merret. He had been head-clerk in a Paris office that went bankrupt; unable to buy a practice in Paris in 1816, he came to Vendôme, bought his predecessor’s business, and married into local relations. Three months after being licensed, just before his marriage, he was sent for to the Chateau of Merret by Madame la Comtesse. The Count had gone to Paris and died two months previously after falling into dissipation. On the day he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted La Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it; some said she had burned the furniture and hangings, perhaps in the Merret meadow. Regnault had been summoned: the Countess was very ill and seemed unlikely to survive the night, having received the Sacrament. The maid who met him at the door was now a servant in the inn.When Regnault reached the chateau about eleven, he climbed the great staircase, crossed large, lofty, dark rooms—cold and damp—and reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. Rumors had painted her as a coquette; reality confounded that image. The enormous, old-fashioned room, papered with brown and filled with dust, had only an old Argand lamp for light. The bed was antique, the tester hung with flowered chintz; on a small table lay an Imitation of Christ and the lamp; a deep armchair for the maid and two small chairs formed the whole furnishing; there was no fire. If you had seen that vast room you would have felt transported into a romance; it was icy, funereal.Madame de Merret lay propped up, waxen and narrow-faced. Her hair, abundant but white as linen, was shown by a lace cap; her large black eyes, dimmed by fever, hardly moved beneath bony brows. Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands looked like bones covered with soft skin; veins and muscles showed—she must have once been striking, but disease had consumed her to a phantom. Her lips, pale-violet, scarcely moved when she spoke. Though Regnault’s work had exposed him to many deaths and last wishes, this solitary figure in her vast chateau was the most affecting: no sound, almost no breath, yet an immense presence. When her eyes moved, she tried to raise a hand, then, with difficulty, drew from under her pillow a sealed paper. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” she uttered, then clutched a crucifix, kissed it, and died. Regnault remembered a joy stamped on her last glance, an expression that made him shudder, and he took away the will: she had appointed him her executor.The will proved extraordinary. She left most of her property to the Vendôme hospital, with a few legacies. As for La Grande Bretèche, she ordered that, for fifty years from her death, it should remain precisely in the state it was at her death: nobody, whoever he might be, should enter the apartments, no repairs were to be made, and watchmen were even to be paid if necessary to secure this injunction. At the expiration of the term, if the will had been dutifully executed, the house would become the executor’s heirs’ property; otherwise it would revert to the heirs-at-law under terms set in a codicil not to be opened for fifty years. The will had not been contested. Regnault’s account thrilled him and he enjoyed the triumph of such a legal contrivance.The doctor, vivid imaginings spinning into a gothic romance, pressed Regnault for the specifics of the Countess’s eccentric injunctions, but the notary affected comical reticence. Eventually he regaled the doctor further with town gossip—diffuse, contradictory opinions that nearly put the doctor to sleep. Regnault’s monotone and self-satisfaction were too much for curiosity, and the notary took his leave, teasingly suggesting that many would wish to live fifty years longer, and hinting cryptically about age.After Regnault left, the doctor, still stirred, sat in his armchair when his landlady, Madame Lepas, entered. A buxom, florid Flemish woman with the keen scrutiny of an innkeeper, she had overheard the notary’s history. The doctor repeated the story of Madame de Merret, and Madame Lepas leaned close, an instinctive mix of constable, spy, and dealer in her look.When asked about Monsieur de Merret, she described him as extremely tall, a good gentleman from Picardy, taciturn and hot-tempered but generous in paying his debts, and well-liked by the common folk despite his haughty manner. He and Madame de Merret had once been a handsome couple; she was pretty, sweet, and wealthy—about twenty thousand francs a year. They were a splendid match and their wedding had filled the town. Were they happy? “So-so,” Madame Lepas said—the common folk were not intimate with their life. Madame de Merret was kind and pleasant but must sometimes have endured her husband’s temper; being of noble birth, his ways passed as his place required.“But there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?” the doctor pressed.“I did not say there was any catastrophe,” she replied. Yet she then confided what she knew. During the Emperor’s time, when Spaniards and prisoners of war were sent here, she was required to lodge a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Despite the parole, he had to present himself daily to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee with a fanciful name—Bagos de Férédia or something alike—which she had entered in her books. For a Spaniard he was notably handsome though short—no more than five feet two or three—but well made, with small, fastidious hands; he kept his hands, she said, as carefully as a woman keeps brushes. He had thick black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion the hostess admired, and the finest linen she had ever seen—finer than anything even princesses had worn at her inn, despite having housed personages like General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. The Spaniard ate little but had such polite, amiable ways that he was impossible to dislike; he spoke scarcely at all, answering rarely if spoken to—a manner the hostess guessed might be a national affectation.He was devout: he read his breviary like a priest and went to mass regularly. Most notably, he always took his station within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. At his first attendance there no one suspected intent; he never raised his eyes above his book, poor young man. In the evenings he walked the hill among the ruins of the old castle—a pastime that must have reminded him of Spain, which, they said, is all hills.Thus the scene closes on the picture of La Grande Bretèche, the Countess’s strange last will that sealed the house for half a century, and the shadow of a Spanish exile whose daily position near the Countess’s chapel and his solitary walks among the ruins suggest a link between foreign passion and domestic catastrophe—an implication the doctor, Regnault, and Madame Lepas allow the listener to supply, while the dilapidated house stands mute and enigmatic by the Loir.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening shortly after he arrived he stayed out late. I grew uneasy when he did not return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he had the key, and we did not wait. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he had seen the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he did come back I warned him about the weeds; he seemed annoyed at being seen.Eventually one morning we found he had not come back at all. Searching his things I discovered a note in his drawer, fifty doubloons—about five thousand francs—and a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he did not return he left the money and diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.My husband went searching and returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He had come early, unseen. After reading the letter my husband burned the clothes and, in keeping with Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced that he had escaped. The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas thought the Spaniard had drowned. I suspected otherwise, believing him implicated in Madame de Merret’s affair; Rosalie had told me the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was ebony and silver, and early in his stay Monsieur Férédia had owned a similar crucifix that I never saw again. “Should I feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs?” my hostess asked. “Aren’t they truly mine?”“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread—like entering a dark church at night with a faint light beneath a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, suddenly loomed in my imagination. I felt compelled to explore the house and uncover the heart of this solemn story that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme. Observing her, I sensed some inner thought beneath her blooming health—a sorrow or hope; she seemed to keep a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a woman haunted by a past crime. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of malice; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief tucked into her lilac-and-white-striped gown. “No,” I resolved, “I will not leave Vendôme without knowing la Grande Bretèche’s full story. To achieve this, I will make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.”“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, then regained composure.“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”Her response seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to the affair; she seemed bound to its core. It was not a matter for ordinary courtship; this girl contained the last chapter of a romance. From that moment my attention fixed on her. Observing her further, I noticed admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning I said to Rosalie:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a certain posture of confidence is necessary for storytelling. If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence a whole volume would scarcely suffice; as her account lies between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s version, I will summarize it concisely.Madame de Merret’s bedroom at la Grande Bretèche was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question Madame de Merret had been seriously ill; her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. One night he returned later than usual from the club, delayed by animated discussions and losses at billiards. He had previously relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; that evening he decided to check on her, perhaps to share his misfortunes or seek comfort. He entered thinking he heard a closet door shut; Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace, looking anxious.“You’re very late,” she said huskily.Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing, arms crossed.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He did not answer.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She wanted to be alone with her husband. Once Rosalie left—or lingered in the passage—Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, saying coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”That “No” pierced him; he did not believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand and said with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”Her extraordinary dignity filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve like a legend: “No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” She looked haggard.“Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, observing the ebony-and-silver crucifix, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before.”“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” he said, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited for her to arrive. “Go to bed, all of you,” he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss billiards and the club. When Rosalie returned she found them conversing amiably.Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the ground-floor reception rooms. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme; he had ordered a large quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he replied aloud.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason. “Gorenflot,” said her husband, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered: “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, to be paid on your wedding day—contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!”“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband quietly paced the room, watching the door, the mason, and his wife, without showing suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise; Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Both husband and wife remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door; the silence on Monsieur’s part was deliberate—he did not want to let his wife speak ambiguously. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason broke one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to him. They all then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.Before her husband turned around the poor woman nodded to the stranger the signal of “Hope.”At four in the morning in September the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning he casually mentioned, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy—she thought he would go to Duvivier’s.As soon as he left Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”In an instant Rosalie brought a cleaver and began demolishing the wall with an intensity beyond words. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. “Jean,” he added to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he would cut her off, saying without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After Bianchon finished this story, all the ladies rose from the table, shaken by the last words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration the Archbishop of Besançon regularly attended Baronne de Watteville’s drawing-room because of her devoutness.Monsieur de Watteville, descendant of the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After a quiet life in La Franche‑Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, an heiress; the match was arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the Second Restoration. After her grandparents’ deaths Madame de Watteville grew more pious and exerted influence in Besançon’s circles.Thin and plain, he worked as a turner and collected geological specimens. Many admired his noble soul: he accepted he would not dominate his wife and devoted himself to work and hobbies. The Rupt residence reflected both families’ standing—grand rooms, elegant furnishings, fine cuisine—and he often acted as his own butler. Madame’s fortune was considerable; his estate modest. Their intimacy with the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 Abbé de Grancey announced a favorable lawsuit verdict. The Vicar‑General, calling it unexpected in the political climate, credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, whose obscure origins intrigued the guests. Amedée de Soulas, present that evening, would later prove significant.France and England have long traded cultural influences—especially in language and fashion—so that what is "English" in Paris becomes "French" in London. Examples include the English national anthem "God Save the King," sometimes attributed to Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts that, after initial ridicule, spread across Europe. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, only for fashions to shift again.More recently England supplied French with terms like "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset, sparked a vogue for stylish young men in Paris that reached the provinces. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent ill at ease in the dull military town. He kept a spare apartment, lived modestly, yet cultivated an ostentatious appearance and maintained a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger," dressing him fashionably while subsisting on scant income.Seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was clever at economizing—often dining out to avoid household costs. His grooming and clothes were a façade meant to impress the local aristocracy and win Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His Parisian polish and travel stories masked financial strain.Besançon’s conservative "Colony" maintained strict hierarchies; Amedee’s quirks, like trouser-straps, both set him apart and allowed nobles to seem modern to visiting Parisians. Rosalie, raised strictly, was modest at eighteen but resolute; her mother fiercely guarded her honor, complicating Amedee’s courtship. His life thus illustrates social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraint as he strives to win Rosalie’s heart.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” Monsieur de Soulas began as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took rooms on the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie as ‘Albert Savaron’ and left his card with the local officials.”“The Savaron name is well known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville observed.“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms he must add a bar-sinister. The only surviving Brabant is Mademoiselle de Savarus.”“The bar-sinister denotes a bastard,” Rosalie chimed in, “but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble.”“Enough,” the Baroness cut in.“You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville said, and the Baroness urged Monsieur de Soulas to continue.“In a town like Besançon, where everything is catalogued, Albert Savaron was quickly accepted by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he intended—he sent his card to judges rather than calling. Three days later he was forgotten. He hired Jérôme, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. No one saw him much.”“Does he go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, breakfasts, works again, walks a little in his garden, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven,” Monsieur de Soulas said.“How do you know all this?” she asked.“I live on Rue Neuve and overlook his house; my servant speaks with Jérôme.” Babylas and riding provided the rest of the gossip.“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar-General.“The President assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man’s innocence, exposed the real culprits, and won their arrest. His defense astonished the Court. A merchant later gave him a difficult case, which he won. Monsieur Berryer advised we employ Savaron for our matter. Upon meeting him, I felt confident—and was right.”“Is he extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.“The first time I met him,” Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in an outer room lined with law books, simply furnished: an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, a green carpet. He was younger than I expected. He wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” exclaimed Madame de Watteville.“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. Black hair streaked with gray, thick and curly; a round white throat; a splendid forehead furrowed by deep lines of thought; an olive complexion; a square nose; fiery eyes. He has passion but calm, and a sweet voice that can thunder when needed. Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”“The second time I visited, he received me in his shabby bedroom. The third time, I found him having a frugal breakfast in his library. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”“Why leave Paris for Besançon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. The Abbe, having studied his head and heard him argue against a prominent Parisian lawyer, thought Savaron would make a significant impact.The Vicar-General’s portrait enthralled Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone exceptional, unlike the mundane Count who had courted her. She replayed the Abbe’s words and fixated on his description—“What a fine forehead!” she thought—comparing him in her mind to the men at the table. When her mother asked why she wasn’t eating, Rosalie answered she had no appetite, lost in thought about the Abbe’s prelate-like hands and the window she had glimpsed from her bed, now realizing it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.At dinner she asked whether the Chapter’s lawsuit was settled; the Abbe answered. Her mother laughed; Rosalie admitted the Abbe’s talk had engrossed her. That night she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse his windows. She felt a thrill watching candlelight through the trees and saw him writing, imagining the color of his furniture. “While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.The education of girls poses delicate questions: how much should they know? Too much enlightenment forces premature maturity; too little produces stunted minds. Rosalie’s crisis was triggered by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron—a sudden inward stirring that made the ordinary extraordinary.The next morning she saw Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. Having seen him, Rosalie resolved to enter his house, uncover his mystery, hear his voice, and glimpse his eyes. Throughout the day she embroidered with the concentration of someone who appears idle but is deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession—arranging to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service—hoping to encounter Albert.She visited her father in his workshop and suggested he turn larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. He delighted in the idea but she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding. Rosalie imagined a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.Sunday arrived. Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled; the cross-court walk quickened her heart. She arrived at church before Mass, prayed briefly, and checked if Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered. No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression: solemn, burdened, his deep gaze conveying intense thought—those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a bold moment she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a place to observe him during her confession. Time in the confessional seemed fleeting; by the time she emerged, Mass had ended and Albert had gone. “The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besançon? I must know everything! But how?”With this new desire, Rosalie concentrated on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother. From that Sunday, after their glance, she eagerly supported the plan for the belvedere.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. Rosalie mentioned a cool room in the grotto where they could take coffee; her mother approved and inspected the columns.She approved a site in the garden not visible from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but commanding a view into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior would be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor; Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier. Rumors spread in Besançon about the Wattevilles’ new garden feature.“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—” people said.“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” an Abbe added; the young man corrected him—“No, he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.” No one suspected that the girl thought a witless goose had devised the plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings and thereby increasing the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the belvedere, Savaron was launching another enterprise. Through quiet connections among local merchants he was starting a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, who believed in promoting Besançon as a trade hub. To compete with Strasbourg, Besançon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants eager to promote their own enterprise.Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, letting his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed across regions, and with a low subscription price it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers. To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while letting him believe he was gaining. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.After the third issue, the Review gained recognition among French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, stirring comment in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though higher circles of Besançon dismissed it for liberal views. Rosalie, hearing about the Review, persuaded her father to subscribe.“Papa,” she said, “there’s a Review published in Besançon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie who had shown him such affection, subscribed and lent her the four issues already published. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first story she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact on her was profound. She hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages she became convinced it reflected him; others noted that Albert drew from his own life in this confession.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men set out from Lucerne to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. Passing the hamlet of Gersau, Rodolphe was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay while his companion Leopold continued the tour.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together. Leopold was bound for a notary’s post in Paris; Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a harder past: his mother had sacrificed everything for his education, hoping to secure his future. Rodolphe was passionate and impulsive, contrasted with Leopold’s calm temperament.In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple and learned the woman was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. Of modest means, Fanny supported her father by translating and writing; Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.The next day Rodolphe obtained permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens—the family that rented to the Lovelaces. After hesitation he was allowed to explore and noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who suggested an unusual backstory. Eager to meet Fanny, he found the Bergmanns protective and resistant.That evening he heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later met her and Gina, but their conversation was cut short when an unknown assailant attacked and wounded Rodolphe. Fanny and Gina aided him and got him to safety.While he recovered Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments; she later revealed her husband was not as old as he had seemed and that she had married him under duress. She encouraged Rodolphe’s ambitions.Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past, arrived. Though they dreamed of freedom together, Rodolphe’s ambition, Fanny’s ties to her husband, and social constraints forced them to weigh love against duty and sacrifice, the landscape mirroring their intense emotions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed with the Bergmanns, observing Francesca with a mix of fascination and puzzlement. He saw in her both youthful imprudence and a resolute, sometimes resistant character. The old man treated her like a father; her gratitude revealed a native nobility that deepened the enigma she presented.Those days combined secret joys and melancholy, quarrels mingled with harmony. Rodolphe grew increasingly captivated by Francesca’s constant tenderness and jealous of trifles. One evening, when she said she wished to leave Gersau for its lack of luxuries, he asked if she valued luxury. She answered passionately, equating a love of luxury with an appreciation for art and beauty, while insisting she had borne hardship without complaint. Her playful claim that ordinary people aspired to greatness revealed her charm.Francesca then insisted Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and the need to protect her husband’s reputation. She professed love for Rodolphe but demanded secrecy, kissed him, and left him astonished.The next day, after the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then Geneva. To avoid local discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives and inquired after Italian refugees—hoping for a bookseller named Lamporani—but learned the only recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini. His first walk took him to Villa Diodati; there he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and felt dizzy. Learning she was at the Colonnas’ residence lifted a veil: the intimacy he’d felt now collided with the reality of her noble rank, and he feared he had been a mere whim.He left his card with Prince Gandolphini and encountered a false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from imposing. That night his passion intensified and he felt driven to raise his social standing. When he finally saw Francesca at the concert she was radiant; their eyes met in a charged recognition, and her beauty convinced him she had remained true despite her rank.Afterward he was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who teasingly called herself “Princess Rodolphini,” reviving memories of Gersau. She acknowledged his deep, faithful affection; he confessed ambition—he wanted to achieve greatness so she could be proud.Francesca’s life was marked by contradiction: born for the cloister, family tragedies forced a marriage to Prince Gandolphini to preserve status, yet she preferred independence, liberal ideas, and sympathized with revolutionary sentiments. Winter in Geneva flew by in her intelligent, beautiful company, but with spring Rodolphe grew restless; her calm contrasted with his need for more demonstrative feeling.One day on a terrace he bitterly lamented others’ happiness, especially that of a noblewoman. Francesca, wounded, withdrew; he found her in tears and, in a moment of passion, kissed her. She rebuffed him, asserted her dignity, and ordered him to leave—declaring they would not meet again until Naples. He, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Paris, Rodolphe found a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner. He thought himself unmoved, yet Francesca persuaded Schinner to paint originals for Rodolphe, Emilio, announcing their correspondence.Driven by love and ambition, he invested everything in a deceitful venture costing three years. When Villele's ministry fell he was ruined; after three months at Villa Gandolphini in Naples, where they recovered, he turned to politics.He tried to rebuild: his talents were acknowledged and a high post promised, but the July 1830 upheaval thwarted him. Only Francesca and God saw his brave efforts—a noble heart unfavored by fortune. Sustained by love, he faced trials. Lovers! Pray for him!When Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie flushed and wept. The novel—an early example of a man narrating his own love—was her first encounter with that voice and convinced her Francesca Colonna was Albert’s beloved.Curiosity hardened into resolve: despite insecurity about her looks and lack of musical talent, Rosalie was determined either to win Albert or to learn the truth. Her sheltered imagination spawned schemes—sneaking into his garden, writing to him, visiting her father’s land to force a meeting.From Albert’s window she saw Jerome, his servant, helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Mariette’s plainness and relative security explained Jerome’s interest, and the scene taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson—servants could circumvent her mother’s restrictions.The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jerome. Confronting Mariette about secret meetings, Rosalie unexpectedly offered help in exchange for access to letters Albert gave Jerome. Relieved by Rosalie’s discretion, Mariette agreed.Rosalie’s obsession deepened when she read a letter Albert had written to his friend Leopold. It revealed his noble lineage, past struggles, ambition, and steadfast devotion to Francesca. Absorbed by his character, Rosalie read more correspondence and grew painfully aware of her own inadequacies and the moral wrong of invading his privacy. Still, she took the letters as proof of love and punished herself with penances and self-imposed suffering.One evening Mariette brought a letter meant for Francesca. Torn, Rosalie read it; it confirmed Albert’s ongoing love and hopes for a shared future. Struck by their fidelity and by her unrequited longing, she resolved to step back—recognizing the nobility of their bond and yearning for the selfless love she had witnessed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. Alone, that struggle fueled her passion and dark plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; this is presented as a cautionary tale.That winter Albert de Savarus advanced his position in Besançon, eager for the Chamber’s dissolution. Backed by a wealthy contractor tied to the moderates, he pressed the old issue of the Arcier aqueduct. Though drawing from the Doubs made restoring Arcier seem absurd, Savarus argued the river water was unfit and promoted “Roman” water; the local Review echoed him and townspeople across parties accepted the idea. The controversy, driven by hidden interests, dismissed opponents as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the previously stagnant town, gaining influence without broad popularity.He also won lawsuits for local priests; ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in personal gain masked another form of moral usury: he expected a larger reward. In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with Léopold Hannequin’s funds, a move that also qualified him for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who discerned Savarus’s designs, called him remarkable and suggested he had taken a wrong path.Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, at first content merely to observe him. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, yielded ten thousand francs a year but was poorly managed by the inattentive Baron, left to an old steward, Modinier. The family often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot improved by the famous Watteville, who had built a dam and lake. Watteville’s heirs nonetheless pressed a dubious claim over the village of Riceys, whose inhabitants had long-held rights to the lands. The Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation his descendants continued to assert.Rosalie’s maneuvers were subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, enchanted by the lake and associating it with love. On a boat trip her father confided legal troubles with Riceys over land rights; Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate. She argued a lawsuit would add excitement and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey, clearly aiming to fix her future there and create gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing alignment would compromise his neutrality; the Abbé pressed political advantages, especially potential votes. Rosalie, determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election, wrote to the préfet revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but was ignored. She understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it.As the election approached, influential men unexpectedly nominated Savarus. Surprised, he had been unaware. The Abbé warned him of potential betrayals and advised transparency about his past. Savarus’s passionate speech captivated his audience, yet the préfet worked to undermine him, eroding support. The first vote disappointed Savarus; he returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and an uncertain future. Then the Abbé brought hope: he had secured behind-the-scenes support, promised the Rouxey case would be resolved in time, and suggested marriage to Sidonie de Chavoncourt might consolidate votes. Savarus, initially resistant, recognized the advantage and prepared to navigate the coming choices of love and politics.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine the next evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were filled with Besançon’s aristocracy to discuss the surprise nomination of a candidate—the daughter of the Rupts, a former Master of Appeals and secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch. Madame de Chavoncourt arrived with her younger daughter Sidonie; her elder sister, secure in her relationship, stayed away, a choice much remarked upon. Abbé de Grancey circulated, steering talk and probing an elderly statesman about whom the Elder Branch might field; they feared Berryer, noted the Duc de Fitz-James’s Toulouse nomination, and worried that backing Monsieur Savarus might drive Republicans toward them rather than the Moderates.By nine-thirty Albert had not come; Madame de Watteville took his lateness as an insult, while Madame de Chavoncourt urged patience and consultation. Rosalie, watching hostess and mother, noted the leniency shown Albert and hinted at a possible match between him and Sidonie. By ten impatience grew and some gentlemen began playing cards. The Abbé, uneasy, went to the window where Rosalie had withdrawn and suddenly cried, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went to Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent; the servant Jérôme said Albert had left in a post-chaise. The Abbé exclaimed ambiguously, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!”—and Rosalie’s face betrayed a joy the Vicar-General affected not to notice.Back in the drawing-room Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven about fifteen guests remained, including Madame de Chavoncourt and her daughters, the Abbes de Godenars and de Grancey, Monsieur de Vauchelles, Rosalie, Amédée de Soulas, and a retired magistrate who had supported Albert. Grancey stationed himself near the Baroness to watch Rosalie, whose pallor had turned to agitation.When Madame de Chavoncourt asked what had occurred, a servant brought a letter for the Abbé on a silver tray. He read it; Rosalie grew pale and he recognized the handwriting. After a meaning glance he folded the letter into his pocket and declared, “She is in love with Albert Savarus!” As he moved to leave, Rosalie stopped him: the letter, she confessed passionately, was from Albert, and she begged him to remain silent when he announced Albert’s withdrawal—knowing such a withdrawal would destroy any hope of her sister’s marriage to him.On her way to Mass Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette that Albert had suddenly vanished. An elderly Parisian had visited Monsieur Savaron; Savaron turned pale at the card, met him for over an hour, left with a large package, then hurried to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage with post horses, quit his post, and obtained a passport. His abrupt departure inflamed local prejudice against outsiders. Most soon forgot Albert—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness pressed Rosalie to marry; the Baron promised the family estate, les Rouxey, though his wife fretted over finances. A match with Monsieur de Soulas was arranged; Rosalie refused, insisting she was content. Tensions rose during a fraught summer at les Rouxey. After Mariette and Jérôme married, the Baron invested in the estate—fuel for the Baroness’s resentment. The Vicar-General tried to mediate.Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had sabotaged Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo, by intercepting their letters, causing their separation. Horrified by the moral weight, the Vicar-General urged her to seek forgiveness. Her guilt deepened when a Parisian clerk, acting for Albert, came to sell his belongings and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received Albert’s letter: he forgave Rosalie and wished to remain in solitude.The Vicar-General urged Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept de Soulas; she refused. After his death, Madame de Watteville married de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support. Now of age and financially independent, Rosalie went to Paris to confront the Duchess, publicly admitting at a ball her part in Albert’s downfall. She returned to Besançon having exposed the truth, but at heavy emotional cost—leaving a tale of love, betrayal, and the lasting consequences of ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville, noted in the Eastern provinces, lived alone at Les Rouxey—riding, hunting, and improving her estate. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger than her years; Monsieur de Soulas has aged and told Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!” Known for eccentric vagaries, she visited Grande Chartreuse each year, perhaps recalling a grand‑uncle who once broke monastery walls to gain his freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon ostensibly to marry; she returned after a Loire steamboat boiler explosion cost her a right arm and left leg and scarred her face. With failing health and constant pain she devoted herself to religion at the Chartreuse of Les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Vicomtesse de Beauseant: Father Goriot; The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Léopold Hannequin: Beatrix; Cousin Betty; Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Gaston de Nueil: The Deserted Woman- Duc Alphonse Rhetore: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus (Albert Savaron de Savarus): The Quest of the Absolute- Hippolyte Schinner: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Clarina Tinti: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Sweetheart, I too am free! I write first, unless you have already written to Blois.Do not be alarmed; my escape from the convent was simple. The conscience that tormented me gave way to tenderer reasons: my aunt feared I would waste away, and my mother relented. I am in Paris thanks to you. If you had seen me when I left you, you would have been proud of your influence on my young heart. We had been so intimate at the convent—our imaginations fused and that closed world became a playground—that when you left I sank into deep melancholy. I was like a turtle-dove widowed of her mate; death seemed sweet, and I nearly succumbed. To be at Blois, consumed by dread of taking vows and without you, made me ill.My aunt could not understand our private life or how the strict routine ill suited girls of our temperament. She had sacrificed for a brother; to expect the same of us would be too much. For a fortnight I suppressed everything, but letter-writing became my lifeline. I began a journal picturing your own, and dreamed of Gemenos as you will dream of Paris.One morning a lady companion and Philippe, my grandmother’s last valet, arrived to take me away. My aunt kissed me and spoke of the pride she saw in me, which might lead to heaven or hell. I kissed her hands and promised I would not return unless my heart were broken.At Beaugency I still felt stunned. No one was home to receive me—my mother at Bois de Boulogne, my father at council, my brother slow to dress. Philippe led me to the suite that had belonged to my beloved grandmother, the Princess de Vauremont, whose fortune had been left to me. Entering the rooms where she had died brought a wave of grief; though she died in 1817, the place seemed recently occupied. It pained me that so little had been done since her death: panels empty, marbles broken. Philippe told me the Princess’s furniture had been left to me in her will, and my father was awaiting a law that would restore lost property before renovating—an estimate of three hundred thousand livres had been set.At first despair gripped me: had my father meant to send me to the convent while sparing no expense on my household? But memories of the Princess softened me; the rooms preserved her spirit. I set Philippe to polishing and rearranging, giving the old furniture a youthful air.The bedroom and drawing-room, hung in yellow damask and scarlet and filled with family relics—Savonnerie tapestry, a timepiece from Marshal de Saxe, china from Marshal de Richelieu, my grandmother’s portrait—breathed of another century and her coquetries. I remembered how she tended her appearance to the end; traces of nocturnal visitors, disarranged furniture, card-tables, snuff remained. When I was sent to the convent in 1816 she bade me go without display, promising I should remain independent and marry whom I pleased. Six months later she died; her will, entrusted to the Prince de Talleyrand, hinted she disapproved of the vows. I hope to meet the Prince and learn more.Thus, though no one in flesh met me at first, the Princess’s presence comforted me, and I resolved to keep our pact: to tell each other the smallest domestic details. Send me every minute picture of your life.October 19.At half-past five, Rose announced my mother’s return. My mother’s suite corresponds to mine, and we share a secret staircase. I descended anxious about our reunion, wondering if I should feign affection. Her tact surprised me: she was neither distant nor effusive, but greeted me like a friend and kissed my forehead.She said she preferred my living with family to dying at the convent, and wished to be more sister than mother. I found her charming at thirty-eight—beautiful, with dark-blue eyes, a smooth forehead, a marvelous figure and hands—and I responded kindly. Her manner warmed; she took me in and said we had guests for dinner, advising I not make my first appearance in society before visiting the dressmaker.My father then arrived and greeted me affectionately, calling me his rebel daughter and praising my looks; he predicted pride in my future. My brother, Duc de Rhetore, arrived leisurely and kissed me; I was conscious of his indifference that I had not been at Blois when I was expected.I tidied my rooms and set to write you. Thus ended the return of an eighteen-year-old after nine years at a convent, to a noble household where the old ways linger. Tired, I kept convent hours and slept early.November 25.The next morning the rooms were ready; Philippe had put flowers in the vases. Breakfast was troublesome as convent habits left me hungry early. About one, my father knocked and entered to find me writing to you. He gave me a purse containing twelve thousand francs as the annual allowance he intended for me, and said my grandmother left me five hundred thousand francs in government stock, now yielding about forty thousand francs a year. He had planned to use this to set up my younger brother but now left the decision to me, trusting my good sense. He warned that a Chaulieu daughter must comport herself with pride; any slander might have dire consequences. His frankness pleased me and spared my pride.Philippe spent the morning arranging my transformation: dressmaker Victorine, corset-maker, shoemaker, glove-maker—all to fashion me out of convent attire. I was impatient to be made presentable; even a glovemaker was called. At lunch, my mother proposed we go to the milliner; I felt dazzled by this independence—like a blind man who had recovered sight. I began to sense how far I had been from society.My father seemed absent at lunch; he is deeply engaged in state affairs and had refused an appointment as Minister of State, preferring an embassy. At home, family life is fragmentary: my brother and father are often away, my mother devotes mornings to dressing and keeps a busy social calendar of drives, receptions, and theatre evenings. We meet mainly at lunch and dinner; otherwise each pursues his own life. I see now why family affection is so seldom; in Paris people find no time for private intimacy.Miss Griffith, my governess—recommended by the English ambassador—is a Scotch clergyman’s daughter, thirty-six, well-bred and meant to teach me English and chaperon me. She is cautious but accommodating; I quickly perceive she will be governed by me. She advised against being dazzled by society and against being seduced by what attracts me most—her moral lectures are brief and repetitive.I have not yet seen the streets; I must first be made presentable. The Italian opera opens soon, and my mother has a box. I read avidly; modern literature often dwells on love—the theme that once obsessed us at the convent. Corinne and Adolphe delighted me. When I asked if I might meet Mme. de Staël, my parents laughed and informed me she was dead.December.My Darling—tomorrow I make my debut. I have rehearsed all my follies, tried on stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments—performed a private inspection. Griffith alone witnessed my little play. In short, I have left the convent and been reborn as a frivolous society girl. I may be raw in some ways—shoulder lines not yet rounded—but there is strength, health, and a clear, vivid complexion. My features are correct, my hair pale gold with a darker center, my eyes bright and insolent, the mouth expressive. I have grace of gait and voice; my grandmother’s manner and voice are in me. If I wished, I can be innocent, witty, or melancholy enough to command admiration.My father has provided me with a trousseau—ribbons, shoes, gloves, dressing-case, scent-box, fan, sunshade, prayer-book, cashmere shawl—and promised riding lessons. I can dance; tomorrow evening I am to appear in white muslin, with a Greek garland of white roses, and I intend to play the simpleton to win the other women. My mother underestimates me and sees me as a simpleton; my brother treats me with polite contempt. I suspect Alphonse harbors secret discontent and jealousy toward our father, which none of the household understands.We are so unlike my parents: my father absorbed in public life and pleasures, my mother devoted to her toilette and society. They both know how to wear their parts; the show of unity in the family conceals indifference. It amazes me how Paris life can consume people from nine until the small hours, draining both energy and money. I am newly curious about this spell of society that keeps people enslaved and separate. For now I have sixteen hours a day to think, while those around me barely have minutes for reflection. There is much to learn and plumb in this world I sought; I am eager and anxious at once.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yesterday at two o’clock I drove along the Champs-Élysées and through the Bois de Boulogne on a beautiful autumn day that reminded me of the Loire. I finally felt I had experienced Paris; the Place Louis XV struck me as a testament to human artistry.I was impeccably dressed, pensive, and poised, yet no one smiled. The carriage moved slowly, matching my demeanor. Only one person, a duke—my father—acknowledged me with satisfaction; my mother blew a butterfly kiss. Griffith, ever observant, scanned indiscriminately. A young woman should know where to look.I was furious. A man inspected my carriage without seeing me—probably a carriage-maker. I had misjudged my appeal; beauty, it seems, is common in Paris. Hats were tipped to the less deserving while my mother drew admiration. I resolved to learn why.The men generally disappointed: unattractive, mere echoes of former elegance. Their fashion lacked the color and comfort of earlier days; hats perched like truncated columns. It seems easier to incite a revolution than to make a stylish hat, yet the French cling to ridiculous styles. Most faces looked worn, betraying thwarted ambition and bruised vanity; a fine forehead was rare. “And these are the products of Paris!” I said to Miss Griffith. “Most cultivated and pleasant men,” she replied. I was silent; a thirty-six-year-old spinster’s heart practices tolerance.That evening I went to a ball, staying close to my mother, who received all attention. I was merely a pretext for compliments aimed at her. She found me partners who all praised the heat and beauty of the ball as though I were freezing and blind; each man pretended it was the first time he saw me.My dazzling dress paled beside the extravagant attire of married women, each surrounded by admirers and watching one another closely. A few shone with genuine beauty—my mother among them. At a ball a girl is merely a dancing machine—insignificant.The men impressed me no more there than on the avenue: exhausted, bland, uniform. The proud, vigorous men of our ancestors seemed vanished. One man stood out for his beauty yet failed to stir me. He was not noble; no matter his genius, a commoner could not move me. He seemed absorbed in himself, as if intellectuals view women as objects. When they love, they should abandon their writing; otherwise their love is not genuine. I learned he is a professor and author, driven by ambition.I resolved not to quarrel with society for failing to recognize my merits and instead embraced the simple joy of dancing, which I thoroughly enjoyed. I overheard trivial gossip about people I did not know; perhaps my ignorance spared me. Society is a web of intrigue, and I am perceptive.Returning home pleasantly tired, I began sharing my experiences with my mother. She interrupted, advising me to confide only in her: “My dear child, it requires as much tact to know when to be silent as when to speak.” Her words revealed feelings that should remain concealed even from a mother. I realized the vast realm of feminine duplicity. In our innocence we might seem astute gossipers; a mere gesture or glance can teach profound lessons. Suddenly I felt overwhelming shyness. Must I never again express my joy in dancing? What of deeper emotions?I went to bed sorrowful, reeling from the clash between my candid nature and society’s harsh rules. Already the hedges of my life are flecked with white wool! Farewell, beloved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################How deeply your letter moved me, especially as our paths diverge: you to Parisian brightness, I to a quiet retreat.At the Castle of Maucombe my room is unchanged, now opening on the Gemenos valley I once saw without understanding. Soon after my arrival my parents took me to dine with M. de l'Estorade, a wealthy, frugal old gentleman. He lost his only son to Bonaparte’s conscription in 1813; the youth evaded the draft, joined the Emperor’s bodyguard, and vanished after the Battle of Leipzig. The Baron clung to hope and hoarded his income for him. Only later I learned my future husband had been slowly returning from Russia, Poland, and Germany; his fortunes turned in Berlin where the French Minister helped him home. The Baron, a small Provençal landowner with income near ten thousand livres, remains obscure enough in Provence despite these adventures.The combined sum from the late Mme. de l'Estorade’s estate and the father’s savings is roughly two hundred and fifty thousand livres—excluding property—a considerable fortune here. The Baron has just bought a poorly run estate he plans to plant with ten thousand mulberry trees. Now that his son is found, his one desire is to marry him to a girl of good family.My parents approved once they learned Renée de Maucombe would be accepted without a dowry and that her prospective inheritance would be acknowledged in the marriage contract. My younger brother Jean signed a document recognizing an advance on his inheritance. This is how the nobles of Provence work within Bonaparte’s Civil Code, which otherwise pushes many girls into convents rather than marriage.That dinner was the first meeting of your sweetheart and the exile. My father-in-law-to-be has no castle, only a simple country house at the entrance of a beautiful valley: unpretentious, four pebble walls and a red-tiled roof, with a typically Provençal garden enclosed by low pebble walls and a touch of neglect. Still, the house did its best for the dinner, serving us on old silver.The exile is emaciated and pale, marked by suffering. At thirty-seven he looks much older, with white streaks in once-beautiful hair; he is a little deaf, like the Knight of the Sorrowful Countenance. Despite his ravaged appearance, he seems pleased to be marrying the fair Renée de Maucombe.I have graciously agreed to become Mme. de l'Estorade, bringing the two hundred and fifty thousand livres, on condition that I may transform the grange into a park. I have asked my father for a water grant to help this. In a month I will be Mme. de l'Estorade, having made a good impression.While you prepare to enjoy Paris’s vibrant life, I descend from our shared dreams into the humble realities of country existence. I am determined to bring joy to this young man, who knew little youth, going straight from his mother’s arms to war and the harshness of Siberia. I will take pleasure in the quiet, improving the little oasis around our home with fine trees and greenery: a hillside park with perhaps a kiosk from which I can glimpse the Mediterranean, orange and lemon trees, and the poetry of the surrounding nature. My father-in-law and the Chevalier share my religious sentiments.My life will run like a smooth highway, free from the turmoil of another Bonaparte. If I have children they will be mine to nurture and guide; if you remain true to your destiny, your children will have in me a devoted protector. I bade farewell to the romances and adventures we once imagined: my milestones will be the care of the young Masters de l'Estorade and the mischief they bring. I will embroider their caps and be cherished by a frail man in the Gemenos valley. Perhaps one day I may visit Marseilles; otherwise provincial dangers are minimal. We will interest ourselves in the silkworms whose mulberry leaves we sell and share in Provence’s ordinary ups and downs. Quarrels seem unlikely: M. de l'Estorade has promised to leave household matters to me, and I shall see that he does.You, dear Louise, will provide the romance in my life. Tell me of your balls, your dresses, and the men you meet; your other self will always be with you, listening and feeling the excitement.
