If only I could enjoy some Parisian fun while you managed La Crampade, the name of our grange—poor M. de l'Estorade thinks he is marrying one woman; will he discover two?I must conclude—I am foolish only through you. One kiss on each cheek; my lips remain untouched, he has only taken my hand. P.S. I have just opened your third letter. I have about one thousand livres to spend; buy me pretty things we cannot find here or in Marseilles. Think of the recluse at La Crampade; neither family has anyone in Paris with taste to buy what we need. I will answer your letter later. Goodbye, dear.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################VI. DON FELIPE HENAREZ TO DON FERNAND PARIS, September.The heading shows the head of your house is safe. The massacre of our forebears in the Court of Lions forced us into double identity, but it taught an Arab cunning that probably saved my life.Ferdinand’s fear made his acting convincing enough for Valdez to believe him. Had I not intervened, the Admiral would have perished. The Liberals still do not grasp kingship. I know this Bourbon well; his repeated assurances of protection only confirm my doubts. A true Spaniard need not repeat promises; excess words often signal deceit.Valdez left on an English ship. Seeing the cause of my beloved Spain fail in Andalusia, I set my escape in motion. I ordered my steward, sent coral fishers to meet me on a coast, and while Ferdinand sought to seize me, I was already on my barony of Macumer among brigands who defy authority.The last Hispano-Moorish family of Granada has found refuge in an African desert, even a Saracen horse on an estate inherited from Saracens. The brigands who once feared my authority now shield me from the King’s vendetta, proud to defend the Duc de Soria, a Henarez—the first to visit them since the Moors ruled.I had hoped to live on the estate’s income, but Quevedo was right: I have many nominal possessions but no real livelihood—vast prairies without houses, virgin forests with no furniture. A million piastres and a resident master for fifty years would be needed to make these lands profitable. This must be addressed.Watching my noble country reduced to a corpse for monks to exploit moved me to tears; it foreshadows Spain’s grim future. In Marseilles I learned of Riego's fall. It struck me that my life would be prolonged, unnoticed martyrdom. Is existence worth living when one can neither die for country nor live for a woman? To love and to conquer—this was our law. Yet my sabre is broken, the palace in ruins, the spring dried to sand.Here is my last will and testament.Don Fernand, you now see why I restrained your enthusiasm and urged loyalty to the rey netto. As your brother and friend I implore you to obey; as your master I command. Approach the King and claim my dignities, property, office, and titles. He may hesitate, but tell him you are loved by Marie Heredia and she can marry none but a Duc de Soria. This will please the King: the Heredia fortune has long been a barrier to my ruin. Your proposal will seem to deprive me of a final resource, and he will gladly hand over my spoils.You will then marry Marie. Her secret love for you was no secret to me; I have prepared the old Count for your ascension. Marie and I discharged our family duties; everything favors you. You are handsome and possess Marie's heart. I am an ill-favored grandee for whom she harbors an unconfessed aversion; any reluctance due to my misfortunes you will soon overcome.As Duc de Soria, you will not regret my predecessor nor lose a maravedi. I will keep my mother's diamonds for my independence; the gap they leave can be filled by Marie's jewels. Send them with my nurse, old Urraca—the only servant I shall retain. No one makes my chocolate as she does.During our short revolution, my wants were reduced to bare necessities, covered by my salary. You will find the income from the last two years with your steward. This sum is mine, but a Duc de Soria cannot marry without expenses, so we will share it. You will not refuse this wedding gift from your brigand brother; I intend it so.The barony of Macumer, not Spanish territory, remains mine. Thus I still have a country and a name should I wish to re-enter the world.Thank Heaven, this concludes our business, and the house of Soria is saved!Just as I resigned myself to being merely Baron de Macumer, the French cannon announced the arrival of the Duc d'Angouleme. You will understand why I must stop here.October.When I arrived I had not ten doubloons. A poor leader finds means to live within calamities he cannot avert: for the vanquished Moor, a horse and the desert; for the frustrated Christian, the cloister and a few gold pieces.My resignation is weariness. I am not ready to abandon all thoughts of life. Ozalga gave letters of introduction, including one to a bookseller who serves our compatriots as Galignani serves the English. He found me eight pupils at three francs a lesson. I teach every other day, earning twelve francs—more than I need. When Urraca arrives I will pass my connection to another Spanish exile.I lodge in Rue Hillerin-Bertin with a poor widow who takes boarders. My room faces south, overlooking a small garden. It is quiet; I have green trees to gaze on, and I spend one piastre a day. I am amazed at the calm pleasure of this life, reminiscent of Dionysius at Corinth. From sunrise until ten I smoke and enjoy chocolate, contemplating two Spanish plants—a broom rising from a clump of jasmine—gold on white, colors that thrill any scion of the Moors. At ten I give lessons until four, return for dinner, then read and smoke until bed.I can endure this life of work, meditation, solitude, and modest society for a long time. Be happy, Fernand; my abdication brings no regrets. Five days have passed since I wrote my will; they feel like five centuries. Honor, titles, wealth—as if they never existed.Now that the conventional barriers of respect are down, I open my heart to you, dear boy. Though outwardly grave, my heart is full of tenderness and devotion—never granted to any object, not even she who was to be my bride since childhood. This is the source of my political enthusiasm. Spain took the place of a mistress and received my homage. Now Spain is lost. Beggared of all, I gaze upon the ruins of my former self and ponder the mystery of my existence.Why was life given this carcass, and when will it depart? Why did a race famed for chivalry infuse all its virtues into this last offshoot only for the seed not to sprout—no stem, no flower to scatter its Eastern perfumes? What crime did I commit before birth to inspire no love? Was I destined to be stranded, a useless hulk on a barren shore? In my soul I see deserts where my ancestors roamed under a scorching sun that shrivels life. Proud remnant of a fallen race, vain force, love wasted—an old man in the prime of youth—I shall await death's last grace. Under this murky sky no spark will rekindle these ashes. Thus my last words may echo Christ's: "My God, Thou hast forsaken me!" A cry of agony and terror whose depth few mortals have explored.You can now understand, Fernand, the joy I find in living through you and Marie. I will watch you with the pride of a creator satisfied with his work. Love each other well and continue to do so; any discord between you would pain me more than it would you.Our mother had a premonition that events would one day fulfill her wishes. Perhaps a mother's longing is a pact with God. She often revealed a heart that rightly belonged to both her sons, yet was given to you alone by an irresistible passion. Her spirit will hover above you as you bow at the altar. What I have done pleases our womenfolk, the dead, and the King; it is God's will. Do not resist, Fernand; obey and remain silent.P.S. Tell Urraca to call me nothing but M. Henarez. Do not mention me to Marie. You must be the sole living soul to know the secrets of the last Christianized Moor, whose veins carry the blood of a great family that began in the desert and is now about to die out in a solitary exile.Farewell.VII. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBEWhat! To be married so soon? This is unheard of. After a month you become engaged to a stranger about whom you know nothing. He may be deaf—there are many kinds of deafness! He could be sickly, tiresome, insufferable!Don’t you see, Renée, what they want from you? You’re needed to carry on the glorious stock of the l’Estorades, that is all. You will be buried in the provinces. Are these the promises we made each other? Were I you, I’d rather sail to the Hyères islands in a caique, risk capture by an Algerian corsair, and be sold to the Grand Turk. Then I could be a Sultana and make a stir in the harem while I’m young—yes, and afterwards too!You are leaving one convent for another. I know you; you are a coward, and you will submit to family life with docility. But I will guide you; you must come to Paris. There we shall drive men wild and hold court like queens. Your husband may join the Chamber in three years. I know how those members work; you will work that machine well; you can live in Paris and become, as my mother calls it, a woman of fashion. Oh! do not think I will leave you in your grange!Monday.For a fortnight I have lived the life of society: one evening at the Italiens, another at the Grand Opera, and always a ball afterward. Society is bewitching. The Opera’s music enchants me; while my soul is immersed, I am the focus of admiration and opera glasses. Yet none of the young men have stirred me; not one has evoked the feeling I feel listening to García in Otello. How jealous Rossini must have been to express jealousy so well! What a cry in "Il mio cor si divide!" I speak Greek to you, for you’ve not heard García, but you know how jealous I am!What a wretched dramatist Shakespeare is! Othello wins battles and leaves Desdemona meekly neglected. Such a sheep deserves slaughter. Let the man I love beware of thinking of anything but loving me!I prefer the long trials of old chivalry. That lout of a young lord who took offense because his sovereign lady sent him among the lions to fetch her glove was impertinent. Doubtless the lady had some exquisite prize for him, which he lost—the puppy!But I ramble. My father is likely to represent our master the King in Madrid. I say "our" master, for I shall be part of the embassy. My mother wishes to remain; my father will take me to have a woman with him.This seems simple, but there are complications. In a fortnight I have probed the household’s secrets. My mother would accompany my father to Madrid if he took M. de Canalis as secretary. But the King appoints secretaries; the Duke cannot offend the King, who hates opposition. The cunning diplomat thinks he cut the knot by leaving the Duchess here. M. de Canalis, the great poet, cultivates my mother’s society and studies diplomacy with her from three to five. Diplomacy must be a fine subject, for he keeps at it as regularly as a gambler at the Stock Exchange.The Duc de Rhetore, our elder brother, solemn and capricious, will remain in Paris. Miss Griffith has discovered Alphonse is in love with a ballet girl at the Opera. How can one fall in love with legs and pirouettes? Our brother only attends when Tullia dances; he applauds and leaves. Two ballet girls in a family are more destructive than the plague. My second brother is with his regiment; I have not yet seen him. Thus I must act as the Antigone of His Majesty's ambassador. Perhaps I may marry in Spain; perhaps my father imagines a marriage without a dowry. He asked if I’d accompany him and offered me the use of his Spanish master."Spain, the country for castles in the air!" I exclaimed. "Perhaps you hope it will mean marriages for me!"He only gave a knowing look. For days he has teased me at lunch; he watches me, and I dissemble, playing cruelly with him as father and ambassador in petto. He asked my opinion of some young men and girls. I answered with inane remarks about hair, faces, and figures. He seemed disappointed and inwardly blamed himself for asking."Still, father," I added, "don’t suppose I’m saying what I really think: mother made me afraid the other day that I had spoken too frankly about my impressions.""With your family, you can speak quite freely," said my mother."Very well, then," I continued. "The young men I’ve met are too self-centered to excite interest; they’re taken up with themselves. They put on an expression to talk to us, then drop it, apparently satisfied that we don’t use our eyes. The man as he converses is the lover; silent, he is the husband. The girls are so artificial that it’s impossible to know them, except from their dancing; their figures are not a sham. But the alarming thing is society’s brutality. Small incidents when supper is announced give one an idea—comparing small things with great—of what a popular uprising might be. Courtesy is a thin veneer over general selfishness. I imagined society to be very different. Women count for little in it; perhaps this is a survival of Bonapartist ideas.""Armande is coming on extraordinarily," said my mother."Mother, did you think I would never get beyond asking to see Mme. de Staël?"My father smiled and rose.Saturday.I left out one thing. The love we pictured is extremely well hidden; I have seen no trace. True, quick exchanges of glances occur in drawing-rooms, but how colorless it all is! Love as we imagined it—a world of wonders, glorious dreams, charming realities, sorrows that awaken sympathy, smiles bringing sunshine—does not exist. The bewitching words, constant interchange of happiness, misery of absence, joy of presence—where are they? What soil produces these radiant flowers of the soul? Which is wrong? We or the world?I have seen hundreds of men; not one has moved me. No proof of admiration or devotion on their part, not even a sword drawn in my defense, would have touched me. Love is so rare that one might live a lifetime without meeting the being who can make one happy. The thought chills; if found too late, what then?For days I have trembled at the thought of women’s destinies and begun to understand why so many wear sad faces beneath social excitement. Marriage is mere chance. Look at yours. A storm of wild thoughts swept me. To be loved every day the same, yet with a difference, to be loved as much after ten years as on the first day—such love demands years. The lover must languish; curiosity must be piqued and satisfied, feelings stirred and reciprocated.Is there a law for the inner fruits of the heart, as for nature's visible fruits? Can joy be made lasting? In what measure should love mix tears with pleasure? The convent’s monotony seems the only real life; while wealth, splendor, tears, delights, triumph, joy, and the satisfaction of an equal, shared, sanctioned love appear idle visions.I see no room in this city for the gentle ways of love—precious walks, the full moon on water, the suitor pleading in vain. Rich, young, and beautiful, I have only to love, and love would be my life; yet in three months nothing has appealed to me among the bright, covetous eyes here. No voice has thrilled me, no glance brightened my world.Music alone has filled my soul; it has even taken the place of our friendship. At night I linger by my window, summoning the future from its mystery. Sometimes, setting out to drive, I dismount and walk the Champs-Élysées imagining the man who will awaken my slumbering soul is near. Instead I meet mountebanks, vendors, jugglers, and secret lovers. I am tempted to stop them, ask, "You who are happy, tell me what is love." But I repress the impulse and return to my carriage, swearing to die an old maid. Love is an incarnation, and how many conditions must be met before it can occur! We are not certain we shall never quarrel with ourselves; how much less certain with another? Only God can solve this.I begin to think I’ll return to the convent. If I stay in society I may do foolish things, for I cannot reconcile what I see with my sense of delicacy and principle.Ah! my mother is the happiest, adored by Canalis, her great little man. I sometimes crave to know what goes on between them. Griffith tells me she has passed through such moods; she has longed to lash out at women whose happiness is evident and blackened their characters. According to her, virtue is burying savage instincts in one’s heart. But then what becomes of the heart? It becomes a sink for the worst in us.It is humiliating that no admirer has yet appeared for me. I am marriageable, but having brothers and a family sensitive about honor may keep men away. If that is why, they are cowards.The parts of Chimène in the Cid and the Cid himself delight me. What a marvelous play! Farewell.VIII. THE SAME TO THE SAME January.Our master is a poor refugee, hiding because of his part in the revolution quelled by the Duc d'Angouleme—a triumph that has led to splendid fêtes. Though a Liberal and likely a man of the people, he has piqued my interest; I suspect he was condemned to death. I make him talk to uncover his secret, but he has a truly Castilian taciturnity, proud as if he were Gonsalvo di Cordova, yet angelic in patience and gentleness. His pride is not irritable like Miss Griffith’s; it belongs to his nature, compelling civility through perfect manners and keeping us at a distance with the respect he shows.My father says much nobility dwells in Señor Henarez, whom he humorously calls Don Henarez. A few days ago I took the liberty of so addressing him. He raised his eyes, usually cast down, and flashed a look that abashed me; he certainly has the most beautiful eyes imaginable. I asked if I had offended, and he replied in grand, rolling Spanish:"I am here only to teach you Spanish."I blushed and felt snubbed. I nearly retorted sharply, but remembering what our dear mother used to say, I instead replied:"It would be kind to tell me if you have any complaints."A tremor passed through him; the blood rose in his olive cheeks. He answered with some emotion:"Religion must have taught you, better than I can, to respect the unhappy. Had I been a don in Spain and lost everything in Ferdinand VII.'s triumph, your witticism would be unkind; but if I am only a poor language teacher, is it not heartless satire? Neither is worthy of a young lady of rank."I took his hand, saying:"In the name of religion, I beg you to pardon me."He bowed, opened my Don Quixote, and sat.This little incident disturbed me more than a harvest of compliments and glances. During the lesson I watched him closely, which I could do safely, as he never looks at me.From my observations, the tutor, whom we assumed to be forty, is under thirty. My governess admired the beauty of his black hair and pearly teeth. His eyes are velvet and fire, but he is plain and singular. Though Spaniards are often called unclean, this man is meticulously groomed, with hands whiter than his face. He stoops slightly and has an odd, large head. Smallpox scars give his ugliness a piquancy; his prominent forehead and shaggy brows give a harsh, frowning, plaintive look, like a sickly child whose life depends on superhuman care. As my father noted, his features recall a shrunken Cardinal Ximenes. The natural dignity of his manners disconcerts the dear Duke, who dislikes him and never feels at ease before superiority.Once my father knows enough Spanish we will go to Madrid. When Henarez returned two days after his reprimand, I said to show gratitude:"I have no doubt you left Spain because of political events. If my father is sent there, as seems likely, we might help you obtain a pardon, if you are under sentence.""It is impossible for anyone to help me," he replied."But," I pressed, "is that because you refuse help or because the situation is impossible?""Both," he said, bowing, in a tone that closed the matter.My father's blood boiled. I was offended by this haughty posture and promptly dropped Señor Henarez.Yet something admirable lay in his refusal of aid. "He would not accept even our friendship," I thought while conjugating a verb. Suddenly I spoke to him in Spanish. Henarez replied that equality of sentiment is necessary between friends, which did not exist here, making the matter pointless."Do you mean equality in feeling, or equality in rank?" I demanded, eager to provoke him.He raised those awe-inspiring eyes, and mine fell. Dear, this man is a hopeless enigma. He seemed to ask whether my words implied love; the mixture of joy, pride, and agonized doubt in his glance moved me. It was clear that advances, taken lightly in France, might complicate with a Spaniard, and I retreated, feeling foolish.The lesson ended; he bowed, and his eyes expressed a humble prayer: "Don't trifle with a poor wretch."This sudden variance from his usual grave dignity made a deep impression. It seems horrible to think and say, but I can’t help believing there are treasures of affection in that man.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################All is over, my dear child; it is Mme. de l'Estorade who writes. Between us nothing has changed; it is only one girl the less.Do not be troubled; I did not consent rashly. My life is planned, and certainty suits me. A moral resolve has removed chance. We have property to develop and a home to beautify; I must manage a household and reconcile my husband to life. I shall likely have children to educate.Everyday life cannot be cast in a heroic mould, yet I can still explore the infinite within our familiar tasks. My humble duties allow passion; restoring faith in happiness to the suffering is a noble way to relieve life's monotony. I see little room for suffering and much good to be done. My love for Louis de l'Estorade does not make my heart race, yet nothing in him offends me.You may wonder about my instincts for greatness and the mental energies that bind us. That has troubled me, yet those faculties remain mine, concealed and employed for our family's welfare. The time for a woman to seek admiration is short; if my life is not grand, it will at least be calm and free from shocks.Nature has favored our sex with the choice of love or motherhood. I have chosen motherhood: my children will be my gods, this spot of earth my Eldorado.I can say no more today. Thank you for all you have sent; please see the enclosed list of my needs. I am determined to live with refinement and some luxury, taking from provincial life only what adds charm. Solitude never vulgarizes a woman—she remains herself. My father-in-law is delighted, refuses me nothing, and is sending workmen from Paris to renovate the house.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADEJanuary.Oh Renée, you have made me miserable. Your beauty, proud features, rich soul and exquisite heart will be wasted in a dull marriage you will come to despise; your children will be monsters. You seem resigned, neither hopeful nor afraid; yet what if one morning a man awakens you? The thought chills me. At least you will have a friend and become the guardian angel of your valley, learning to cherish its beauty until it becomes part of you. I foresee letters echoing the valley’s monotony. I will remain a heart untouched by petty society, all yours.Monday.My Spaniard bewilders me with his calm, severe dignity. His solemn silence and reserve provoke curiosity; how strange that a language teacher should fascinate me more than any nobleman. His coldness piques me—he keeps his distance, wrapped in pride and mystery, while I am bold. This amuses and irritates me.Yesterday I said something that struck M. Henarez; he did not answer, and the lesson ended with a bow implying he might not return—a prospect I prefer, for I dread sentimental catastrophe. I’ve been reading Rousseau and find his moralizing passion detestable; Richardson’s Clarissa captures English women better. Love is, for me, a subjective poem; everything written about it is both false and true. Since you will be an authority on married love, I feel called to remain unmarried and seek a grand passion to enrich our shared experience. Tell me every detail of your first days with that strange creature called a husband; I promise to do the same if I ever find love.Farewell, poor martyred darling.XI. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEULa Crampade.Your Spaniard and you make me shudder. Dismiss him. Everything you describe fits a dangerous adventurer who risks all. He cannot be your husband or lover. I will write more about my married life when my heart is less anxious from your letter.XII. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADEFebruary.This morning my father visited, more thoughtful than usual. After flattering me and admitting he’d misjudged my intelligence, he launched into politics: France is precarious—its Revolution persists in law and thought; the King is a head without arms, nobles lack authority over elected men, and the family has ceased to exist—individuals and State coffers replacing family spirit. He insisted a state cannot stand without paternal authority and warned of calamity if our party fails. Half-joking he asked me to persuade him to sacrifice my inheritance for my younger brother; I asked how I could enjoy life without a fortune. He laughed, then seriously noted my future husband will not take me without a dowry. We parted in good humor.Your letter made me reflect on the abyss you warned about. I took precautions with Henarez, whose gaze unsettles me. I hesitated to tell my father I wanted no more lessons, yet I’m drawn to Henarez. His voice thrills me; his manners are simple and genuine. After a lesson he almost kissed my hand, then stopped, terrified. I smiled, understanding the courage it takes for a plebeian to love a noblewoman; my smile encouraged him, his eyes were wet, and I felt a strange happiness. He fascinates me.Friday.He is handsome and speaks admirably, revealing intellectual power and a new world. He told of a valet who died for a queen’s glance and I was captivated. At a ball M. de Vandenesse hinted Henarez was of noble descent—the Duke de Soria, whose titles were confiscated by King Ferdinand. Suspense followed.In our next lesson I suspected him; he denied it, saying he was merely a servant of the King of Spain. His reticence provoked me; I devised a test: I had him translate a letter I wrote describing my ideal lover. He was visibly moved and asked to keep the letter as a remembrance. He left asking to speak with my father; from my father I learned Henarez is now the Baron de Macumer, with property in Sardinia.Two weeks have passed since I saw him; I wonder what he is doing. He has become necessary to me, and I am torn between love and the reality of my situation. Sunday at the Italian Opera I felt his fiery gaze. He has a genius for love.XIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEULA CRAMPADE, February.My dear Louise, I waited to write until I had learned enough for your happiness. The difference between a girl and a married woman is vast; one cannot fully understand the other. I chose to marry Louis de l'Estorade rather than return to the convent. The seriousness of marriage terrified me, yet I realized friendship might be the key to a happy marriage. I accepted his love believing it would not be slavery but a reign.We agreed to separate marriage from certain marital rights to preserve independence. I watched Louis to see if suffering had softened or hardened him. His love proved a passion, and I judged I could risk much. I proposed a friendship in which we would share life’s burdens; I asked to be left free, promising to attend to his feelings. He was overjoyed, and we married soon after.I am nearly happy now, though I must conceal my full contentment from him, for he is sensitive. For three months we lived much as before; I studied our relationship and saw how my presence changed him. I believed I could bring new life to my husband before bearing him children. His happiness brightened our home; even my father became generous. I manage the household with economy—seeking pleasure without excess.I harbor ambitious plans for Louis, hoping to see him in politics; he has embraced my designs, showing the depth of his affection. To conclude, here is a letter he wrote expressing his love: he sees me as a treasure, and I feel proud of our relationship.XIV. THE DUC DE SORIA TO THE BARON DE MACUMERMADRID.My dear brother, I did not make you Duc de Soria to betray the name. I cannot tolerate your exile while we enjoy happiness. Marie and I will delay marrying until you accept the money Urraca offers. We have prayed for your contentment; I believe Heaven will send you the love you seek. Marie and I will always be grateful to you. Write to us through the Nuncio. Farewell; take pride in the happiness you have brought us.XV. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADEMarch.Ah, my friend, marriage has made a philosopher of you! Your prudent calculations trouble me. Do you think you can convert me to matrimony by cautious arithmetic? We left Blois innocent, armed with meditation, and now your weapons have turned against you. If I did not know you were pure, I would suspect vice.You have made a holocaust of your youth—becoming a miser before your time. If Louis loves you, he will never realize your actions spring from necessity. Love is the first principle of all virtues; I cannot accept justifying marriage by calculation. You act a man's part; Louis must play the woman!Your letter plunged me into thought; I see now the convent cannot replace a mother. Please do not turn away from my cries!Monday.Last night I looked at the stars and pictured my Spaniard in a tree by my window. I felt both joy and fear. He blew a letter to me through the window, but I burned it unread—it felt improper to read such an offering. At the opera he seemed sad; I learned he had renounced a rich heiress for another man’s sake. I was wretched for burning his letter—what might he have confessed? To be loved in secret, to know a man is devoted—how can I resist?When I returned home I reopened my window, hoping he might be there. He was, and I received another letter, which I read and cherished.XVI. THE SAME TO THE SAMEMarch.I am dressed in white, with camellias in my hair. I feel an odd desire to delay my decision and make him wait. What a vision—driving my barouche, seeing him, the thrill. I complimented him on his horse; he said it was a gift from his steward. Henarez is no longer just a simple man; he grows more distinguished. Do my love affairs amuse you? I am a little off my head and must stop.XVII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE CHAULIEUApril.My angel, you have grieved me. You do not realize the force of the word indissoluble in marriage. Each marriage has laws shaped by place and character: a wife of a leader must resign to his guidance; a wife of a fool must take control. You speak of vice, but passion can be admirable when it springs from the heart. We are answerable to God and ourselves for our happiness.You will find a wife’s calculation necessary to maintain happiness. I have reflected on the duties of wife and mother, and I believe a sublime hypocrisy may be required to reach the ideal of a perfect woman. I persist in my principles, convinced virtue varies with surroundings. If I wished to make Louis unhappy, I would leave him the helm entirely.I may not have married a man of rank, but I hope to further him toward it. Five years from now we will see if I was mistaken. You taught me what a woman sacrifices in marriage; regret is not remorse, but I feel the loss of freedom. You say marriage has made you a philosopher; I feel far from it. I have read Bonald, who revealed the strength of the family.Farewell, my dear friend.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Well, my Renee, you are a wonderful woman, and I now agree that we can only be virtuous by cheating. Will that satisfy you? The man who loves us is our property; we may make a fool or a genius of him as we please—though the former is common. You will make yours a genius and keep the secret—two heroic acts, if you will!Ah, if there were no afterlife, how nicely you would be sold, for this is the martyrdom you are choosing. You want to make him ambitious and keep him in love! Child, surely keeping him in love alone is punishment enough.Tell me, to what extent is calculation a virtue, or is virtue calculation? You won’t say? We won’t quarrel; we have Bonald to refer to. We are, and intend to remain, virtuous; still, I believe you, with all your little knavery, are a better woman than I.Yes, I am shockingly deceitful. I love Felipe and conceal it with hypocrisy. I long to see him leap from his tree to my balcony—and if he did, how I would wither him with my scorn! I am frank with you.What restrains me? Where is the power that prevents me from telling Felipe how supremely happy he has made me with his pure, absolute, overflowing love?Mme. de Mirbel is painting my portrait, which I intend to give him. Love animates life; every hour, every trifle matters, blurring past, present, and future. Is it still so at the heights of happiness? What is happiness—soothing or exciting? I am restless, dragged to him despite reason and propriety. At least I can better understand your feelings.Felipe’s happiness rests in feeling himself mine; his aloofness and strict obedience irritate me, just as his profound respect did when he was my Spanish master. I want to cry out, “Fool, if you love me as a picture, what will it be when you know the real me?”Oh, Renee, you burn my letters, don’t you? I will burn yours. If anyone else read these thoughts, I would send Felipe to silence them; perhaps even kill the owners for security.Monday.How can one fathom the human heart? My father should introduce me to M. Bonald; I would ask him. I envy God, who can read the heart’s undercurrents. Does Felipe still worship me? That is the question.If I ever detect the slightest fall in the respect he used to show me as my instructor, I believe I would have the strength to end it. “Why these fine words, these grand resolutions?” you will say. Dear, I will tell you.My charming father, devoted as an Italian cavaliere servente, had my portrait painted by Mme. de Mirbel. I managed to get a copy for the Duke and sent the original to Felipe with these lines:“Don Felipe, your single-hearted devotion is met by a blind confidence. Time will show whether this is to treat a man as more than human.”It was a rich reward—both promise and challenge—given without fully committing. If his reply included “Dear Louise!” or even “Louise,” he would be lost!Tuesday.No, he is not lost. The constitutional minister is perfect as a lover. Here is his letter, paraphrased for its essence:He says every moment away from me was filled with ideal pictures; his eyes closed to the world, fixed on my image. My portrait is a talisman; my blue eyes, brought from paint into flesh, haunt him. If he delayed writing, it was because he could not tear himself from my presence, which wrung from him secrets he struggled to keep.Closeted with my likeness, he surrendered to complete happiness for the first time. He speaks of placing me between the Virgin and God in his thoughts, of an agony that he will not voice because a single altered glance from me would be torture. “Queen of my life and soul,” he calls me, longing for but a fraction of the love he bears.He describes doubt that wrecks him, like a criminal awaiting a verdict. A smile from me alone could calm his dread of displeasing me. From birth no one, not even his mother, smiled on him; the girl meant for him rejected him for his brother. In politics too he has suffered—thwarted, distrusted, regarded almost as an enemy. To be loved had become a dream lost to him until I appeared.Knowing his graceless exterior and the odds against him, he joined my service because devotion alone could justify his passion. Looking at my portrait alternates hope with fear: hope that, seeing his constancy, I might yield; fear that I will never love him. He promises that if his daring offends, he will return to being my humble servant; but if I consent to try to love him, he begs mercy in breaking it gently to one who has placed his happiness in serving me.My dear, reading this, he rose before me—pale, humble, but full of a tenderness that was not the ordinary flourish of lovers. It was the breath of happiness.The weather has been atrocious; impossible to go to the Bois without arousing suspicion. Even my mother, who often goes out regardless of rain, remains at home.Wednesday evening.I have just seen him at the Opera; he was another man. He came to our box, introduced by the Sardinian ambassador.Having read in my eyes that his audacity was well taken, he seemed awkward and addressed the Marquise d’Espard as “mademoiselle.” A light brighter than the chandeliers flashed from his eyes. He left with the uncertain air of a man unsure of his next move.“The Baron de Macumer is in love!” exclaimed Mme. de Maufrigneuse.“Strange, isn’t it, for a fallen minister?” replied my mother.Outwardly I kept my composure, regarding Mmes. de Maufrigneuse and d’Espard and my mother as if they spoke a foreign language; inwardly my soul rose in waves of intoxicating joy. There is only one word for what I felt: rapture. Such love as Felipe’s makes him worthy of mine. I am the very breath of his life; my hands guide his thoughts. I have a mad longing to see him clear every obstacle and come boldly for my hand. Then I would know whether this storm would calm at a glance from me.Ah, my dear, I stopped here, and I am still trembling. As I wrote, I heard a noise outside and rose to look. From my window I saw him coming along the wall at the risk of his life. I went to the bedroom window and made him a sign; it was enough. He leaped from the wall—ten feet—and then ran along the road, showing me he was not hurt. That he thought of my fear moved me so much that I am still crying; I don’t know why. Poor ungainly man! What was he coming for? What had he to say to me?I dare not write my thoughts and will go to bed joyful, imagining all we would say if together. Farewell, fair silent one. I have no time to scold you for not writing; it has been over a month since I heard from you! Does this mean you are finally happy? Have you lost the “complete independence” you were so proud of, which nearly played me false tonight?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If love is life’s essence, why is it excluded from marriage? Society demands women sacrifice for family, breeding marital discord. Anticipating this, men built defenses against women; without them, theories that subordinate women could be overturned by love or aversion. Marriage now embodies opposing forces the law should reconcile. Your letter challenges the foundations laid by the sage of Aveyron, under which I once found comfort. Laws made by men accept conjugal love without passion, allowing women to yield without desire, reflecting Nature’s emphasis on procreation.Once a person, I now feel like property. I have swallowed many tears alone, aching for a comforting smile. Why are our fates so different? You thrive in lawful love where virtue aligns with pleasure, while I face an uncertain future.You are loved and adored. Embrace this noble romance we long dreamed of. Your beauty, refined and spiritual, was made to enchant. Guard your heart’s secret and test Felipe’s love—and, more importantly, test your own. Novelty and the illusion of happiness easily mislead.You remain independent, dear friend; I urge you not to rush into marriage without assurance. In intimate conversation, a gesture or word can reveal hidden depths. You have the courage to explore where others falter.I watch you anxiously from afar; my heart is in sync with yours. Write to me about everything; your letters ignite passion in my otherwise mundane life. I oscillate between despair and hope—perhaps expecting too much. In youth we often believe ideals and reality will align.Seated beneath a rock in my park, I concluded that love in marriage is a fortunate accident, not a universal rule. The philosopher rightly views the family as society’s unit, placing women in subservience as history shows. The solution to our dilemma lies in motherhood, which I yearn for to channel my energy and heart.Louis remains cheerful; his love is active while mine is passive. He enjoys the flowers of my affection without acknowledging the toil behind them. I play the devoted mother, sacrificing for his happiness, and that sacrifice gives me joy. I await the rewards of my efforts, hoping motherhood will fulfill my aspirations and compensate for what I surrender.---Dear wedded friend, your letter arrived just as I contemplated a bold step. A strange yearning for the unknown revealed the conflict between societal and natural laws within me. I want to speak with Felipe alone, under the lime trees in our garden. This suits the modern girl I’ve become, my father observes.There is method in this seeming madness: I wish to reward Felipe for his nights beneath my window while testing his reaction to my boldness. If he respects my folly, he may imagine me as his wife; if not, farewell, Don Felipe.Meeting him this way seems less risky than encounters in society with prying eyes. I longed for your company to share the anticipation of our first clandestine meeting. I sent Felipe a tracing of the garden key with a note urging him to stop his reckless behavior, warning that if he fell it would tarnish my reputation. Yesterday I took Griffith to the garden, and after a delicious moment of tension, I saw Felipe approach like a shadow.When he reached the garden, I introduced him to Griffith, who withdrew a few steps. The night was mild and heavy with scent; I felt intoxicated by the thrill of being almost alone with him. We savored the moment before I urged him to abandon his reckless climbing, fearing scandal.His pallor revealed fear; I reminded him that if he had been caught, suspicion would fall on my family. He apologized, and I reassured him my actions were not as mad as they seemed.After a few moments of silence he expressed joy and reverence for me, acknowledging that my actions were sacred. I told him I was on the path to loving the man who understands me, but that my heart would belong only to someone who truly knows it. He expressed gratitude for my acceptance; I noted he should prove himself worthy of my affection. We walked and talked; his tenderness reminded me of divine love. His low, melodic voice conveyed his feelings beautifully.I dismissed him, aware of our meeting’s implications. Griffith remarked on my commitment, but I assured her that in France my intentions were merely exploratory.Once again I met Felipe; he appeared timid and reverent, which I found appealing. He seemed to sense my power, yet remained unaware of the depth of my feelings. I am still fond of him, but I keep my composure.Love appears in two forms: one that commands and one that obeys. Can both coexist? Will Felipe ever become my master? These questions unsettle me.He remains oblivious to my true nature. If I were in his position, I would see Mlle. de Chaulieu as a calculating coquette. I still care for Felipe, but I feel calm and in control now. The thrill of uncertainty has faded, and I fear I may have lost the excitement of our initial encounters.Is suspense always more pleasurable than fulfillment? Do we exaggerate our feelings by letting imagination run wild? These thoughts haunt me as I consider another secret meeting in the garden—this time without Griffith. How far can I go? Imagination knows no bounds, but pleasure has its limits. How can a woman reconcile these two aspects of her existence?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I am displeased with you. If you did not cry over Racine's Berenice and feel it as a profound tragedy, we have no connection and should part. Answer me satisfactorily, or I will forget you; you will be M. le Baron de Macumer to me, or cease to exist.At Mme. d'Espard's yesterday your self-satisfied air disgusted me and your confidence alarms me. You have lost the humility of your first letter; instead of the absent-mindedness of a lover you polished epigrams. That is not the behavior of a true believer, who should stand in awe of his divinity.If you do not see me as the very breath of your life, a being above others, then I must be less than a woman in your eyes. You have bred a spirit of mistrust that drowns my tenderness. Reflecting on our past, I feel justified in my suspicions. Understand, Prime Minister of all the Spains, that I have contemplated the vulnerable state of our sex. My innocence has lit my path, and I have emerged unscathed.Let me repeat what youth taught me: deceit and broken promises are often punished, but love answers only to its own law—the victim is accuser and judge. The most heartless betrayals remain secret, known only to the two hearts involved. How can the injured reveal them without wounding herself? Love gives its own justice, which the world ignores.I have resolved never to forgive a serious offense; in love, what is not serious? Yesterday you looked like a man who had already succeeded. If that certainty has stripped you of the charming uncertainty that once characterized you, I blame you. I want you neither timid nor presumptuous; I do not want you to fear losing my affection—that would be an insult—yet I also do not want you to treat your love lightly. Your heart should never be freer than mine. If you cannot grasp the torment a single stab of doubt can inflict, beware the lesson I might teach you.In a single glance I entrusted my heart to you, and you understood it. The purest feelings of a young girl are yours. My reflections have enriched my mind; yet if a wounded heart seeks counsel from reason, it may ally itself with darker knowledge and daring.I swear to you, Felipe: if you love me as I believe, and if I sense even the least decline in the fear, obedience, and respect you have shown, or if the pure flame that ignited my heart grows dim, expect no reproaches. I would not burden you with letters of weakness, pride, or rage. But if I became silent, my face would announce that death was near. I would not perish, however, until I had branded you with infamy and sown eternal sorrow in your heart; you would behold the girl you loved dishonored and lost, condemned to suffering.Do not make me envy the old, happy Louise, the object of your pure worship, whose heart thrived in happiness—Senza brama, sicura ricchezza! I have searched the Inferno for the most terrible punishment, a mental torture to link with divine vengeance.Yesterday, as I observed you, doubt pierced me like a cold dagger. I mistrusted you, and the pain was unbearable. If my service is too exacting, leave it; I would not retain you. I do not need proof of your cleverness. Keep your wit for me. Do not present a polished façade to others for flattery or praise. Come to me burdened with scorn or hatred, the target of calumny, told that no woman loves you; then you will discover the treasures of Louise's heart.We are truly rich only when our wealth lies so deep the world could trample it without knowing. If you were merely handsome, I doubt I would have noticed you or found the myriad reasons for my love. We understand little of those reasons, as little as we know why the sun makes flowers bloom. Yet one cause I will reveal: the character and individuality that give meaning to your face are a mystery to all but me. My love transforms you into the most lovable of men, and that is why I wish to keep your mental gifts for myself. To others they should be as meaningless as your eyes and features. Let it be mine alone to ignite your intelligence, as I bring light to your eyes.I envision you as a proud Spanish grandee, cold and haughty, a monument to be admired from a distance—ruins of barbaric power—rather than a figure of Parisian affability. Have you forgotten my ideal portrait? Your excessive cheerfulness betrayed your love. Had I not restrained you, you would have revealed to the sharpest salons of Paris that your inspiration came from Armande‑Louise‑Marie de Chaulieu.I believe too much in your greatness to think your love is merely strategic; yet if you did not show childlike simplicity with me, I could only pity you. Despite this fault, you are still deeply admired by LOUISE DE CHAULIEU.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When God sees our faults, He also sees our repentance. My beloved mistress, you are right; I thought I had displeased you but did not know why. Now that you have explained, I find new reasons to adore you. Like the God of Israel, your jealousy is a holy and precious sentiment. Be jealous of your servant, Louise; the more you strike, the more contrite I will be, proving my devotion to you.If the effort to overcome my timidity was invisible to you, will Heaven reward it? It cost me much to show you my true self, as I was before love. In Madrid I was thought a good talker; I wanted you to see whatever gifts I had. If that was vanity, I have been punished.Your last glance unnerved me. I quailed more than when I faced danger at Cadiz. I sought the cause of your displeasure; the lack of sympathy between us was intolerable. I wish only to act by your will, think your thoughts, and share your joys and sorrows. The torment lay in a breach of the feelings I believed we shared."I have vexed her!" I repeated, distraught. Noble, beautiful Louise, your words have illuminated my feelings. If this is chastisement, what sweetness lies in your rewards?To be your servant is happiness enough. You have given me life I thought lost. I no longer draw a useless breath; I have something to devote myself to, even suffering for you. I will always remain as when I first offered myself as your bondman. If you were dishonored, my heart would cling to you even more, seeking to heal your wounds and pray for your innocence.Did I not tell you my feelings are more than a lover’s? I will be your father, mother, sister, brother—anything or nothing, as you wish. If the father and brother sometimes fade behind the lover, they are still there, though hidden. If you could read my heart when you appear radiant in beauty, you would know how free from selfishness my pride is when I hear praises of your loveliness. When you greet me, I feel God’s blessing on me, and the joy lingers long after.Can you be unaware of how you are loved? After seeing you I return to my study; the glitter of its ornaments pales before your portrait. I lose myself in thought, linking my happiness to verse. Have you never heard the whispers of my prayers reaching you?Through silent contemplation of your face I have deciphered every feature and its connection to your spirit's grace, and I have penned sonnets in Spanish about your dual beauty—unfit perhaps for your eyes, yet constant offerings. Not a moment passes without thoughts of you; my whole being is bound to you, and without you I would ache.Now, Louise, can you realize the torture of knowing I displeased you without knowing why? My ideal life was cut short, and I feared you had ceased to love me. Your letter turned my despair into joy. Oh, might I listen forever to such chiding!Once, a child, after falling, turned to his mother and said, "Forgive me." Hiding his hurt, he sought pardon for the pain he caused her. Louise, I am that child, and I remain so. Here is the key to my character, which I humbly place in your hands.But fear not, there will be no more stumbling. Keep tight the chain that binds me to you, so that a touch may convey your lightest wish to your devoted slave, FELIPE.---My dear friend, how can you, who married a broken-down invalid to mother him, understand the shifting drama of love, where death lies in a glance or a light reply?I reserved one last test for Felipe, to see whether his love was as blind and unwavering as a Royalist’s for his King. He walked with me all night without suspicion; the next day he loved me more, with reverence and humility. He is a true Spaniard, scaling my wall to kiss my hand from my balcony, risking his life.But this is nothing; Christians suffer for heaven. I spoke to my father, the royal ambassador-to-be, with a smile and a jest: friends say he is marrying Armande to an ambassador's nephew for wealth and title. Your daughter weeps but bows to her father's authority. Some say she hides a worldly soul behind her tears.We are going to the gentleman's box at the Opera tonight; M. le Baron de Macumer will visit us there."Macumer needs a spur," my father said, smiling."You mistake Clarissa Harlowe for Figaro!" I replied scornfully. "When you see me with my right hand ungloved, you will mark your displeasure at this gossip.""I can rest easy about your future. You have no more a girl's head than Jeanne d'Arc had a woman's heart. You will be happy, love no one, and allow yourself to be loved."This was too much; I laughed."What is it, little flirt?" he asked. "I tremble for my country's interests..." I added, with a smile, "At Madrid!""You have no idea how this little nun has learned to make fun of her father," he told the Duchess."Armande makes light of everything," my mother replied."What do you mean?" I asked."You are not even afraid of rheumatism on these damp nights," she said, glancing at me meaningfully."Oh!" I answered, "the mornings are so hot!""It's high time she were married," my father said."If you wish it," I replied demurely.Two hours later my mother, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and Mme. d'Espard bloomed in the box. I sat aside, unseen, to observe.Macumer arrived and put up his opera-glasses to look at me. In the first interval the Comte Henri de Marsay, always ready with an epigram, entered smiling. He greeted my mother and the others, then turned to me: "I do not know whether I shall be the first to congratulate you on an event that will make you the envy of many.""Ah! a marriage!" I cried. "Is it left for me, a girl fresh from the convent, to say that predicted marriages never come off?"M. de Marsay whispered to Macumer; I sensed he warned him about me. Macumer met him with noble scorn, saying he was "not in love with any little coquette." His demeanor delighted me; I caught sight of my father ready to act.Felipe showed no fear or doubt. His faith is in me; society’s lies cannot hurt him. Not a muscle of his face stirred.The two young counts left, and I laughed at Macumer: "M. de Marsay has been treating you to an epigram on me.""He did more," he replied. "It was an epithalamium.""You speak Greek to me," I said, smiling.My father spoke with Mme. de Maufrigneuse about the scandalous gossip surrounding me; he intended to take a turn to avoid suggesting the marriage. The Duchess and Mme. d'Espard exchanged glances—women scent love like perfume.Having enjoyed Felipe’s composed response, I signaled him to come to my window by the dangerous route. Later I found him, upright as a statue, studying the light in my room."My dear Felipe," I said, "You acquitted yourself well tonight; you behaved as I would have if told you were marrying.""I thought you would hardly tell others before me.""And what right have you to this privilege?""The right of one who is your devoted slave.""In truth?""I am, and shall ever remain so.""But suppose this marriage was inevitable; suppose I had agreed..."His eyes flashed, measuring the danger. A power stronger than passion held him back."An Arab cannot take back his word," he said. "I am your slave; my life is not mine to destroy."I placed my hand on his as I said, "Felipe, my beloved, from this moment I am your wife in thought and will. Go in the morning to ask my father for my hand. He wishes to retain my fortune, but if you acknowledge receipt of it in the contract, his consent will be given. I am no longer Armande de Chaulieu. Leave me; no breath of scandal must touch Louise de Macumer."He listened, pale and trembling, then cleared the ten feet to the ground in safety. It was agonizing, but he waved goodbye and disappeared."I am loved then," I thought, "as never woman was before." I fell asleep, content, my destiny fixed.The next day my father summoned me to his private room; I found the Duchess and Macumer. After civilities I said that if my father and M. Henarez agreed, I had no reason to oppose their wishes. My mother invited the Baron to dinner; we drove in the Bois de Boulogne, and I smiled ironically at M. de Marsay as he passed.Felipe has had his cards reprinted as follows:HENAREZ(Baron de Macumer, formerly Duc de Soria.)Every morning he brings me a splendid bouquet, hidden within which is a letter containing a Spanish sonnet in my honor.To keep this letter concise, I send you only the first and last of these sonnets, translated for your benefit.FIRST SONNET (essence)I have braved bulls and battles, scaled redoubts, staked life on dice. Yet before your sweet eye my courage deserts me; I tremble, shrink, and lose my nerve as a child with ghosts.SECOND SONNET (essence)I sought sleep to dream of thee, but jealous wakefulness kept me on the balcony. Heaven itself seemed robbed of color and light by thy beauty: the moon’s silver and the stars’ sparkle appear pale beside you; your brow wears its snowy shine, your eyes hold heaven’s blue.What a gracious way to tell a young girl she fills a man’s life! Tell me what you think of this love, which lavishes earth and soul. Only in these ten days have I understood Spanish gallantry.Ah, dear, what is happening at La Crampade? How often I stroll there, inspecting our crops! Do you have news of our mulberry trees and last winter's plantations? Does everything prosper as you wish? While buds open on our shrubs, have they also blossomed in the bosom of the wife? Does Louis continue his madrigals? Do you enter each other's thoughts? I wonder if your wedding peace is better than the raging torrent of my love! Has my sweet lady professor taken offense? If so, I would send Felipe post-haste to plead for my pardon—or her head. Sweet love, my life here is a splendid success, and I want to know how it fares with life in Provence. We have just added a Spaniard with the complexion of a Havana cigar to our family, and your congratulations still await.Seriously, my sweet Renée, I am anxious. I fear you may be eating your heart out in silence, afraid to cast a gloom over my sunshine. Write to me at once, naughty child! Tell me your life in every detail; tell me whether you still hold back, whether your "independence" stands erect, or has fallen on its knees, or is sitting comfortably, which would indeed be serious. Can you suppose the incidents of your married life are without interest for me? I muse over all you have said. Often, at the Opera, while absorbed in watching the dancers, I think, "It is half-past nine; perhaps she is in bed. What is she doing? Is she happy? Is she alone with her independence, or has it gone the way of other dead and cast-off independences?"A thousand loves.---Saucy girl! Why should I write? What could I say? While your life is varied by social festivities and the passions of love, mine is as monotonous as a convent.We go to bed at nine and rise with daybreak. Meals are served with maddening punctuality. Nothing ever happens. I have adjusted to this routine; perhaps such fixed laws give the world stability.I have set rules of dress, which occupy my mornings. I feel it is my duty as a wife to look charming, and it pleases both Louis and the good old man. After lunch we walk; when newspapers arrive I disappear to manage household affairs or read—often writing to you. I return before dinner; after, we play cards or receive visits. Thus my days pass between a contented old man, who has done with passions, and the man who owes his happiness to me. Louis’ joy warms my heart.For women, happiness cannot consist solely in satisfying desire. In the evenings, when I can relax, I imagine your life, its riches and passions, and wonder what such a stormy preface can lead to. May I not swallow the book itself? For you, my darling, love's illusions are possible; for me, only the facts of homely life remain. Your love seems a dream!I find it hard to understand why you insist on romanticizing it. Your ideal man must have more soul than fire, more nobility and self-command than passion. You demand sacrifices to be rewarded by them, testing Felipe to see if desire, hope, and curiosity endure. But behind your stage scenery lies the altar where everlasting bonds are forged. The day after your marriage, the graceful structure raised by your strategy may fall before the reality that transforms a girl into a woman, a gallant into a husband. There is no exemption for lovers; for them as for ordinary folk like Louis and me, the great "Perhaps" of Rabelais lurks beneath wedding rejoicings.I do not blame you for your rashness in speaking with Felipe in the garden or spending a night with him; but you play with life, and I fear it may turn the tables. I dare not give you the counsel my experience suggests, but let me repeat from my valley that the viaticum of married life lies in resignation and self-sacrifice. Despite your tests, I see that marriage will mean to you what it has meant to me. The greater the passion, the steeper the precipice we have hewn for our fall—that is the only difference.Oh, how I wish to see the Baron de Macumer and talk with him! Your happiness lies so near my heart.---As Felipe has acknowledged my fortune, my mother has become friendlier, calling me sly-boots and a woman of the world."But, dear mama," I said before the contract was signed, "you attribute cunning to what is truly the outcome of the most unselfish love! I assure you I am not the 'woman of the world' you think.""Come, Armande," she said, drawing me close, "you did not want to return to the convent or die an old maid. Like a noble Chaulieu, you recognized the need to build up your father's family."I glanced at my father and said, "I love Felipe de Soria with all my soul. Though this love sprang up without my knowledge, I swear I never yielded until I recognized in him a character worthy of mine.""But, my dear," she interrupted, "he is as ugly as...""As anything you like," I retorted, "but I love his ugliness.""If you love him, Armande," my father said, "and can master your love, do not risk your happiness. Happiness in marriage depends largely on the first days—""Days only?" my mother interrupted. "You had better leave us to talk.""You are to be married in three days," the Duchess began, "so I must give you the serious advice every mother owes her daughter. You are marrying a man you love, and there is no reason for pity. I have only known you for a year, but it is long enough to love you. You have gratified maternal pride and shown yourself a sweet daughter. I promise you will always find a staunch friend in me.""You smile? Alas! it often happens that a mother who has lived well with her daughter as a girl comes to cross purposes with her as women.""I want your happiness, so listen. The love you feel is that of a young girl, natural to us all, for it is a woman's destiny to cling to a man. Unfortunately, there is but one man for a woman! Sometimes fate’s choice is not ours. If we cannot love the man family chooses, the fault may lie in both or circumstances. Yet there is no reason the man chosen by family should not be the one we can love. The barriers that arise between husband and wife often stem from a lack of perseverance on both sides. Transforming a husband into a lover is as delicate as making a lover into a husband, which you have just proved marvelously successful."Your happiness is my object. Never forget that the first three months of marriage may bring misery if you do not submit to the yoke with the same forbearance, tenderness, and intelligence you showed during courtship. You have indulged in innocent pleasures of clandestine love. If the culmination of your love begins with disappointment, come and tell me. Do not hope for too much from marriage; it may bring more discomfort than joy. The happiness of your life requires as much patient cherishing as the early shoots of love."If you lose the lover, you will find in his place the father of your children. This is the secret of social life. Sacrifice everything to the man whose name you bear, whose honor cannot suffer without involving you in frightful consequences. Such sacrifice is not only a duty for women of our rank; it is also wise policy. This is the mark of great moral principles—they hold good from every aspect. But I need say no more."I fancy you are jealous, and if you knew how jealous I am! But do not be foolish about it. To publish your jealousy is like playing politics with your cards on the table; those who let their game be seen learn nothing of their opponents. Whatever happens, we must know how to suffer in silence."She added that she intended to have a serious talk with Macumer the evening before the wedding.I kissed her hand, dropping a tear, moved by her words. In her advice I saw high principles and tenderness, and she understood my character. Her words summed up the lessons life had taught her."Dear little girl, you've got a nasty crossing before you. Most women, in their ignorance or disenchantment, are as wise as the Earl of Westmoreland!" We laughed at the joke about a gentleman who feared crossing the Alps.You will understand, Renée, that your gloomy philosophy and my mother's lecture revived fears that had disturbed us at Blois. As marriage approached, I needed all my strength and affection to face the passage from maidenhood to womanhood. All our conversations returned, and I re-read your letters, feeling a vague sadness.This anxiety had one advantage: it helped me adopt the proper expression for a bride. Consequently, on the day of signing the contract everyone said I looked charming and quite right. This morning at the Mairie it was informal, with only witnesses present.I write this tail to my letter while they prepare my dress for dinner. We shall be married at midnight at the Church of Sainte-Valere, after a gay evening. I confess my fears give me a martyr-like air to which I have no right, but which will be admired—I cannot conceive why. I am delighted to see that poor Felipe is as timid as I; society grates on him, and he feels like a bat in a glass shop."Thank Heaven, the day won't last forever!" he whispered innocently.In his bashfulness he wished for no one there.The Sardinian ambassador presented me with a pearl necklace linked with six splendid diamonds—a gift from my sister-in-law, the Duchess de Soria. Along with it was a sapphire bracelet engraved with the words, "Though unknown, beloved." I could not accept these without consulting Felipe."For," I said, "I would not want you to wear ornaments from anyone but me."He kissed my hand, moved, and replied, "Wear them for the inscription and the sincere feeling."Here, then, my poor Renée, are the last words of your girl friend. After the midnight Mass we set off for an estate Felipe bought in Nivernais, on the way to Provence. My name is now Louise de Macumer, but I leave Paris in a few hours as Louise de Chaulieu. However I am called, there will always be but one Louise for you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I have not written to you, dear, since our marriage nearly eight months ago, and not a line from you! You are inexcusable.We set off in a post-chaise for the Castle of Chantepleurs, which Macumer has bought in Nivernais on the Loire, sixty leagues from Paris. Our servants, except my maid, were there before us; we arrived the next evening after a rapid journey. I slept from Paris to beyond Montargis, my head on Felipe's shoulder. His tenderness moved me; he kissed my forehead twice and looked at me with unchanging passion.At Briare we breakfasted in the carriage, talking like in our old Blois days and admiring the Loire. By half-past seven we entered the beautiful avenue to Chantepleurs, dined at eight, and retired to a charming Gothic bedroom, made comfortable with modern luxuries. Felipe, often thought ugly, seemed beautiful to me in his kindness. He felt like an old friend, though he later confessed to storms of passion hidden beneath a calm exterior.I told him there was nothing terrible in marriage; he asked why fear it, reminding me he had never been false to his promise. His words and glances mastered me. I thought of duty and the unfair constraint placed on a woman's delicate feelings: what force seeks to crush a woman's sensitivity under obligation? Love should guard its rights and bloom freely under its warmth.Oh, my noble Renée! I understand you now. The woman who has not used marriage to legalize her heart's choice has only motherhood left to turn to. When earthly ties fail, the soul seeks the divine.A hard truth: only truly great men know how to love. They balance sensual and spiritual forces; weaker men confuse them. Great souls express instinct with grace, tempering passion with reverence. The depth of feeling follows the caliber of intellect; hence only men of genius possess a woman's delicacy. When mind, heart, and senses unite in rapture, we briefly soar to heaven.This was the philosophy of my first three months of marriage. Felipe is angelic, another self; I can think aloud with him. His greatness surpasses my understanding, and our bond deepens with possession. I foresee years of marriage will only strengthen his affection and our connection. What joy!Pleasure invigorates me; its sunshine becomes part of my being. The intervals between ecstasies are like short nights between long summer days. What happy chance has given me such a destiny? My mother's fears proved unfounded; your fears and mine dissolved.We spent seven and a half months at Chantepleurs like runaway lovers in blissful solitude. One morning, in particular happiness, I reflected on my life and thought of your prosaic marriage. I grasped the meaning of your life. Oh, my sweet, why do we speak different languages? Your marriage of convenience and my love match are worlds apart. You walk the earth, while I soar in the heavens. Love crowned me queen; you reign by duty. My heights are so lofty that a fall would shatter me.But I will not dwell on that. I hesitate to describe the vibrant joy of love's springtime as we know it.For ten days we have been in Paris, staying in a lovely house on the Rue du Bac, prepared by the architect who decorated Chantepleurs. I have been listening to Rossini's divine music, which once soothed my restless heart. They say I am more beautiful now, and I take childish pleasure in being called "Madame."Friday morning.Renée, my dear, my happiness draws me back to you. I feel I can be more to you than ever. I have studied your married life alongside my own, and you emerge as great, noble, and splendid in your goodness. I declare myself your humble admirer and friend. My marriage has been a joy; I would have perished had it been otherwise. And you live! What feeds your heart? I will never mock you again. Mockery comes from ignorance; we jest at what we do not understand. "Recruits laugh where veterans are grave," as the Comte de Chaulieu said.I suspect you have not told me everything. You hide wounds; I believe you suffer. From afar I wove romantic theories: perhaps you merely experimented with marriage, and what brought me happiness brought you pain. Your sacrifice seems unrewarded, wrapped in social axioms. If self-denial is noble, what of my joy, sanctified by church and mayor? Is it unnatural?For the honor of the law and to complete my happiness, I long to see you content. Oh, tell me you see a dawn of love for Louis, who adores you! I return to this midday blaze, fearing it may consume me.Dear Renée, do you remember how you once said, "I love you so, Louise, that if God appeared to me, I would pray for all your sorrows to be mine"? I take up your prayer, imploring Heaven to grant you a share in my happiness.I have an idea. I suspect you are nurturing plans for Louis de l'Estorade. Very well; help him get elected deputy in the upcoming election. He will be nearly forty then, and the Chamber won't meet for six months, so he will qualify for a seat. You will come to Paris—wouldn't that be enough? My father and my friends will know and admire you. If your father-in-law consents to a family, we could secure the title of Comte for Louis. That would be something! And we shall be together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, your letter left me dizzy with joy and a little envy. I held it at sunset, tears glistening, beneath a barren rock overlooking the Mediterranean where I’ve set climbers to one day cover it—a spot I call Louise's seat. It symbolizes my solitude yet reminds me I am not alone.Reading you stirred the child within me: a mix of warning, delight, pain, promise, and fulfillment that convinced me I will one day know laughter and love. I now understand my purpose: to bring forth life. The certainty of motherhood has turned my sacrifices into deep joy, surpassing any personal passion.For you, love reflects Felipe’s ardor; my love will return in my children. Your golden harvest may fade, but my delayed motherhood will be enduring and renewing. Love can be a beautiful gem taken from Nature, but motherhood is Nature’s purest joy. A smile has dried my tears; while love makes Louis happy, marriage has made me a mother, and in that I find happiness.I returned to my white grange to sit with these thoughts. For five months the miracle of nature has been unfolding within me, yet I remain emotionally detached. My family is excited; I feel cold and hesitant to confess my true feelings—motherhood is still abstract.Louis was surprised, which shows how little the father's role seems to matter here. My doctor says childbirth is governed by chance, though children born of passionate love may be luckier. Perhaps there are joys for you I will never feel, since your love for Felipe differs from my sense of duty.These reflections accompany my physical discomforts: nausea and odd cravings. While Louis brings home the finest oranges, I crave the half-rotten ones, finding them delicious despite their smell. This taste has eased my sickness and strengthened me.I plan to stay on the grounds to avoid being seen. I wonder when the true sense of motherhood will begin and hope it won’t arrive with suffering. Farewell, my fortunate friend—your love story inspires me and I long for every detail. Remember to pray and do good: religion has grown more important to me since my marriage. God bless you!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame,—My wife wished you to hear first: Renée has given birth to a healthy boy. We are postponing his baptism until your return to Chantepleurs and hope you will come to La Crampade as godmother to our firstborn, Armand‑Louis de l’Estorade.Renée endured pain with remarkable patience; the thought of bringing us happiness sustained her. Little Armand is a beautiful infant, resembling Renée in features and eyes—a promise of intelligence. The physician assures us Renée is out of danger and proves an excellent nurse; we freely express our joy. This joy has revitalized our home and transformed my wife's life; I hope it will soon be yours.Renée has prepared a suite for you; our warm welcome will make up for any lack of luxury.I thank you for your timely kind thoughts. The birth of a grandson reconciled my father to heavy sacrifices: he bought two estates, and La Crampade now yields thirty thousand francs annually. He will seek the King’s permission to make it an entailed estate; your assistance in obtaining the title you mentioned would greatly benefit your godson.I will follow your suggestions to facilitate your reunion with Renée during the Chamber sessions. I am working to establish myself professionally, and nothing would encourage me more than your interest in little Armand. We earnestly invite you to come and, with your beauty, grace, playful spirit, and noble heart, be a good fairy to our son; you will win our everlasting gratitude.Your very humble, obedient servant,LOUIS DE L’ESTORADE.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXX. LOUISE DE MACUMER TO RENEE DE L'ESTORADEJanuary 1826.Macumer woke me with your husband's letter. Yes, we'll go to Chantepleurs at the end of April. I am delighted to travel, to see you, and to be godmother to your first child. I insist on having Macumer as godfather; sharing the role would be unbearable — if you could have seen the look he gave me, you would know how dearly he cherishes me.I long to visit La Crampade; I may have a child there too. Can you picture me torn between you and an infant? If anything ever supplanted my place in your heart, I hardly know how I'd bear it — Medea's anger seems less extreme now!So: you have the fruit without the flowers, I the flowers without the fruit. Our lives contrast, yet we have the philosophy to endure. Ten months married is too soon to give up hope.We live happily, as happy people do. Society now calls the Baronne de Macumer prettier than Louise de Chaulieu—love is a wonderful cosmetic. Driving along the Champs-Élysées this January I reflected on how much has changed since last year.If I lack a mother's joys, you can share them with me and I will learn by sympathy. Still, I cannot imagine anything surpassing the rapture of love; often I think it would be better to die at thirty, preserved from later disappointment, than to carry a young heart in an aged body among cold faces. Felipe and I quarreled about this: I wanted him to have the strength to let me slip away at thirty so I might pass from one dream to another; he refused and turned pale. This distinguished statesman can be surprisingly childlike. Now that we share such confidences, we often surprise each other.Felipe and I would like to send you a gift, dear mother — something to bring you joy and to remind you of us. Tell me what you would like. I thought of a luncheon service with baby figures, since lunch is our happiest meal. Let me know soon; we must order it.Farewell, dear nursing mother. May you enjoy all the delights of motherhood. I eagerly await your first letter describing your experiences. Some parts of your husband's letter moved me; I will tell my godson how dearly he must love you. Much love, my sweet one.XXXI. RENEE DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE MACUMERIt has been nearly five months since the baby was born, and I have found no moment to write. When you become a mother, you will understand.
