Please write more often! Share your joys; I am happier than you can imagine.I went to the parish church for the Mass of recovery from childbirth, supported by both grandfathers. Kneeling before God, overwhelmed with gratitude, a single image remains: the Virgin on the altar seeming to bend and point to the infant Christ, who smiled at me. With a heart full of love, I held little Armand for the priest to bless, looking forward to his baptism.How sweet the word "child" is! In the last two months of pregnancy I wandered through the gardens, scarcely conscious of the burden I carried. Gloomy forebodings haunted me; my heart did not respond to the thought of the little one, though he reminded me with lively kicks. When labor came, I summoned all my strength and bore the agony; I sank into a stupor, feeling like two beings: my body in pain and my soul at peace. Pain formed a halo; at its climax I screamed, and then the newborn's cries silenced my suffering.They laid me back, and it felt like paradise despite exhaustion. Happy faces pointed to the child, and I cried out in terror, "It looks like a little monkey! Are you sure it's a child?" My mother, now my nurse, reassured me: "You've got the finest baby in the world. Don't worry; be a mother." I slept and left things to nature.Waking from those first days' pain was heavenly. A ray of light pierced the darkness, and my heart opened like a flower. The moment the little one took my breast was pure joy—this felt like the dawn of motherhood.Nursing is a miracle of transformation. The cries go straight to your heart; the child's eyes and lips express everything. What else matters? The father could be forgiven nothing that disturbed the baby. The bond between mother and child is profound; the joy of nurturing is unparalleled.At five months my little one has become the prettiest darling: he cries little and is content in our love. I strive to envelop him in affection.My feelings for Louis have changed into a tender fondness, perhaps surpassing romantic love: unselfish gratitude that lights the future and recalls fond memories. The old L'Estorade and his son have redoubled their devotion; each look from them brings me joy. Louis is ecstatic at having a son—he watches him sleep for hours—and the fear of childlessness has given way to renewed purpose; he works harder, driven by fatherhood.I grow happier by the hour. The bond between mother and child strengthens continually. This love is natural and enduring, unlike romantic passion, which can wane; a mother's love grows with the child's needs and combines passion, duty, and joy. Here a woman's true sphere unfolds, where self-sacrifice flourishes without jealousy.Society reinforces nature's instincts: a mother cherishes the child who opens these delights and teaches her the art of motherhood. A mother learns from her child, and her character deepens through these duties and pleasures; a woman without children is incomplete.Hasten, my sweetest, to fulfill your mission. Your happiness will multiply with mine.23rd.I had to leave you because my godson's cry reached me from the garden. I wanted to say farewell. Rereading this letter, I am surprised at how ordinary my feelings sound; yet every mother must have felt this way. I am as happy now as I once was miserable. This place is my land of promise—the desert is past. A thousand loves, darling. Write to me, for I can now read about your happy love without tears. Farewell.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Do you know, dear, it has been over three months since I last wrote? I must own I failed to answer your last letter, but you won’t mind?Macumer and I took your silence as consent about the baby-wreathed luncheon service; it’s now bound for Marseilles after six months’ work. When Felipe asked me to inspect it before packing, I realized we hadn’t exchanged a word since your letter that showed the heart of a mother.My sweet, I blame dreadful Paris—what is your excuse? Society here is a whirlpool. Didn’t I tell you one must conform? It drives out sentiment, monopolizes time, and can consume the heart. Moliere’s Celimene is the epitome of the society woman—timeless and apt.Without my breastplate—my love for Felipe—I should be lost. This morning I told him he is my salvation. Evenings are full of parties; nights my heart heals in passionate love.I dine at home only with friends; I spend one afternoon a week in the house—Wednesday. I have entered the social fray, competing with Mmes. d’Espard and de Maufrigneuse; my house is lively. I became fashionable for Felipe’s pleasure. My mornings are his; from four in the afternoon until two in the morning, I belong to Paris. Macumer is an admirable host—witty, dignified, courteous. No woman could help loving such a husband.My parents have gone to Madrid. With Louis XVIII gone, the Duchess obtained my father’s appointment as attaché. My brother, the Duc de Rhetore, now acknowledges me. My younger brother, the Comte de Chaulieu, owes me for spending my fortune to secure him an estate and a marriage with Mlle. de Mortsauf, an heiress. The King will confer the de Lenoncourt and de Givry titles on him to preserve their legacy. Mlle. de Mortsauf, sole heiress of the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, is expected to inherit over a hundred thousand livres a year. My father insisted the de Chaulieu arms be placed on the de Lenoncourt escutcheon, making my brother Duc de Lenoncourt. The young de Mortsauf is gravely ill, so the marriage waits until next winter, after mourning.My father’s plan has justified itself, earning me compliments and softening explanations of my marriage to society. The Prince de Talleyrand, kindly toward my grandmother, is now warm to Macumer. Society that once criticized me now admires me.I now reign where I was once insignificant. Macumer is envied as the husband of “the most charming woman in Paris.” Many women vie for that title; men murmur and gaze. This adulation flatters vanity—I see why women pay dearly for such fleeting privileges. This triumph is intoxicating; I understand how women become selfish and frivolous under admiration. Society’s demands consume your spirit; you give yourself for smiles and jealousy and receive only flattery. You know the cost, yet can’t escape the vortex.Ah, how I crave a true friend! Felipe’s love and devotion are my treasure, and my heart longs for you. We are joyfully preparing to move to Chantepleurs, where we can escape the farce of Paris. After rereading your letter, I realize no fashionable woman in Paris can be a good mother.Goodbye for now, dear. We will be at Chantepleurs for only a week and hope to see you around May 10th. We will meet again after more than two years! How we have changed—we are both matrons now, you in motherhood, I in love.If I haven’t written, it isn’t because I’ve forgotten you. How is my little godson? He must be over nine months now; I long to see his first steps, though Macumer says even precocious infants don’t walk at ten months.We will have delightful gossip and see if a child truly spoils a pattern.P.S. If you reply, address it to Chantepleurs. I’m just leaving.---My child, if you become a mother you’ll find it impossible to write during the first two months of nursing. Mary, my English nurse, and I are exhausted. I resolved to do everything myself—sewing baby clothes, embroidering caps. I am a slave, day and night.Master Armand-Louis eats whenever he pleases, and I must constantly change, wash, and dress him. I love watching him sleep, singing to him, and carrying him about, leaving little time for myself. Society has been to you what motherhood is to me!I cannot express how rich my life has become, and I long for your visit to see for yourself. I fear he will soon be teething, and you may find a fussy baby. So far he hasn’t cried much because I’m always attentive. Babies cry when their needs aren’t met, and I’m vigilant. My heart has expanded while yours shrinks under society’s demands. I eagerly await your arrival, wanting your thoughts on l’Estorade just as you’re curious about Macumer.Write to me from your last stop. The gentlemen want to meet our distinguished guests. Come, Queen of Paris, to our humble home, where love will greet you!---The name on this letter shows my petition has been granted: your father-in-law is now Comte de l’Estorade. I wouldn’t leave Paris until I secured this for you; I’m writing in the Keeper of the Seals’ presence—he has just told me the patent is signed.Goodbye for now.---I am ashamed that my sudden departure will surprise you. But I must be honest: I am horribly jealous.Felipe’s eyes rested too often on you. Your little talks at the foot of your rock tortured me, and I became irritable. Your beauty reminded him of his homeland and Marie Heredia; I can be jealous of the past too. Your magnificent features stabbed me in the heart. I tried to enhance my appearance, but everything paled beside you.Felipe doted on the child, whom I began to resent. Yes, I confess—I wanted that exuberance of life for myself. I saw regret in Macumer’s eyes, and I wept for two nights. I was miserable in your home. You are too beautiful and triumphant for me to endure.You complained of your lot, but L’Estorade is presentable, charming, and likely to become a deputy. His exile has given him a calm, dignified air, useful for a politician. Macumer, too, should achieve high place.Having satisfied myself of your happiness, I went to Chantepleurs, where Felipe must pursue his aspirations. I won’t receive you there without a fine baby to match yours.I know I deserve your scorn. I am a fool, a wretch—jealousy is a poor virtue. I am not angry with you, but I was miserable; you will forgive my escape. Two more days and I might have made a scene.Despite my jealousy, I am glad to have seen you in the pride of motherhood. Even in Marseilles I feel proud of you as a splendid mother.You are born for motherhood; I am not. A good mother and a passionate wife both require intelligence and grace. I watched you and admired you. Your children will be happy, not spoiled, by your tenderness and reason.Tell Louis the truth about my departure, but find a decent excuse for your father-in-law and family. Felipe doesn’t know why I left, and he never will. If he asks, I’ll fabricate a pretext—perhaps that you were jealous of me. Forgive my little fib.Goodbye. I write in haste; the postilion waits.Kisses to my dear godson. Come to Chantepleurs in October: I’ll be alone while Macumer is in Sardinia planning estate improvements. That seems to calm him; sharing plans with me unsettles him. Goodbye!---Dear sister, we were astonished at lunch to learn you had left, especially after your letter. You were happy; there was no reason for such objections. My sentence: you return at my first summons. Your letter didn’t say your next stop, so I address this to Chantepleurs.Listen, dear sister of my heart. My focus is your happiness. Your husband commands respect—not only a grave exterior but a revealed power and a fire in his eyes. He has been Prime Minister and adores you, which makes him a profound dissembler. Extracting secrets from such a diplomatic soul requires skill.You know my role is reason while yours is imagination. I am a simple country vicountess, ambitious for family prosperity. Macumer, the Duc de Soria, has a name, and you, a duchess by right, reign in Paris, where it’s hard to maintain power. You have a considerable fortune, which may double if Macumer develops his Sardinian estates. If anyone has the right to be jealous, it’s not you. Thankfully, we both have generous hearts that place our friendship above pettiness.I know you must feel ashamed of fleeing, but your flight won’t spare you from my words. Read carefully; this concerns you more than Macumer.Firstly, you do not love him as a wife should. In two years you may tire of adoration. You will never see Felipe as a husband; he will always be a lover to you. You do not revere him as a woman should her god. I have studied love, and this is not love.You cannot escape the fate of all queens. One day you will long to be treated as a light-o’-love—to be swept off your feet by a strong man who won’t worship you. Macumer loves you too much to resist or criticize. Sooner or later you will scorn this excessive devotion. He spoils you, as I spoiled you at the convent; you are enchanting, and there’s no escaping your charms.Worse, you are candid; our happiness often relies on social hypocrisies you won’t embrace. Society won’t tolerate a wife’s power over her husband. Convention demands that a man not show himself as lover to his wife, nor may a married woman act as a mistress. You both disregard this.The unpardonable sin in society is to be happy; if happiness exists, it must remain hidden. More importantly, equality between lovers shouldn't appear in marriage, or it undermines social order. It is painful to see a man become a nonentity, and worse to see a capable man reduced to that. You will reduce Macumer to a shadow; your household will lack balance. You will regret it, but by then it may be too late. Women often lack the qualities essential to family life. Macumer senses this; his Sardinia visit suggests he hopes to regain himself.You use the power his love gives you. Your position shows in your gestures and tone. You are the wanton your mother called you! Have you ever heard me contradict Louis? I respect his public authority. If I want to advise him, I wait for private moments; I never assume the role of mentor. If I didn’t present myself as submissive publicly, he would lose confidence. The good we do for others is spoiled unless we erase ourselves so they feel no inferiority. There is sweetness in these hidden sacrifices; it is a triumph when you praise Louis.This is my counsel. Right now, you love Felipe not as a husband but as a reflection of yourself. Your father’s words ring true: beneath your passion lies a selfishness typical of a great lady. How I must love you to speak such bitter truths!If you promise not to tell the Baron, I’ll share the end of our talk. We praised you, and he soon sensed my sisterly love. I ventured, “Louise has never struggled with life; fortune has spoiled her, and she might pay for this if you weren’t there to be father and lover.”“Ah! but is it possible?” he broke off—enough for me. If you had stayed, he would have spoken more freely.Think of the day when your husband’s strength wanes, when pleasure turns to satiety, and he feels diminished before you. The stings of conscience will awaken remorse, painful for you, as you’ll feel responsible and end up despising the man you failed to respect. Remember, scorn can become hatred. You are too noble to forget Felipe’s sacrifices, but what more can he give when he has already given himself fully? Woe to those who leave no desire unsatisfied—women can be insatiable in love.Oh, Louise, change while there’s still time. If you treated Macumer as I treat l’Estorade, you could awaken the hero within him. Wouldn’t you feel pride in using your power to elevate a gifted man, as I do with one of lesser stature?Had you stayed I would have told you this in person, but I know you will read and weigh my warnings. You have everything to be happy; don’t spoil your chances. Return to Paris as soon as Macumer does. The demands of society, which I complain about, are necessary; otherwise you’ll be lost in self-absorption. A married woman shouldn't be too generous with herself; a mother should reserve sacred hours for herself—children should not take her presence for granted.Farewell, my dear jealous soul! Many women would be flattered by your pangs, but I can only mourn, for what is not mother in me is your dear friend. A thousand loves. Make whatever excuse you must for leaving; if you doubt Macumer, I am sure of Louis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My beloved,—Eager for Italy; glad Macumer is here; Sardinia postponed. Italy enchants; churches may sway Protestants. Macumer was warmly received, and Felipe hopes for Sardinia's embassy in Paris.Send letters to Florence; I'll write from Paris. A week here, then Florence, month in Tuscany, Naples; Rome in November; back via Venice in December and Paris in January.It feels like a honeymoon. Goodbye, darling; don't mind infrequent letters—I'm absorbed in sightseeing.Reply to your Marseilles letter awaits Chantepleurs; honeymoon heightens concern—send from Nivernais. Rumour: government will dissolve Parliament, unlucky for Crown—last session would have helped. Louis turns forty in 1827. Father will stand, resign. Your godson walks, thoughtful. I nursed Armand; will wean him December; year suffices. You're making an impression in Italy. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Your dreadful letter reached me, forwarded by my steward as I asked. Oh, Renée — I will spare you unnecessary complaint and tell you only what it produced.We had just returned from a reception given by the ambassador, where I shone and Macumer was utterly captivated. Then I read your harsh reply to my letter and sobbed. My dear Arab flung himself at my feet, calling you mad. He led me to our balcony; speaking Italian, his declarations were like poetry. He swore that even if your predictions were true, he would not trade one moment of our love for a lifetime of convention—he would choose our love and death over a dull existence with children.While he whispered, a bat's cry startled us and I fainted. I am better this morning, though that sound haunts me. Upon waking I knelt before Felipe, looked into his eyes, and confessed: "My love, I may be naive, and Renée might be right. Perhaps I love only your love, but this is the only feeling in my heart. If anything about me disturbs you, tell me. I want to follow your wishes." Macumer was too choked with emotion to answer.I thank you, Renée; your letter revealed the depths of Macumer's love. Rome is the city of love, where passion should flourish alongside art and faith.In Venice we shall meet the Duc and Duchesse de Soria. If you write, address Paris; we leave Rome in three days after the ambassador's farewell.P.S. Dear, naive child, your letter shows you know love only in theory. Love is a force that yields diverse fruits beyond any theory's grasp.My father has been elected to the Chamber, my father-in-law has died, and I am near my second confinement; these mark the year’s end. I mention them first to spare you the fright of the black seal.Your letter from Rome chilled me. You and Felipe behave like children. Either he is a deceitful diplomat, or his love is like a man’s for a courtesan—lavishing affection while knowing she is unfaithful. Enough. You say I rave, so I will hold my tongue: from our differing fates I draw a harsh lesson—do not love if you wish to be loved.When Louis joined the provincial Council he received the Legion of Honor cross. Nearly three years on, my father asks the rank of Officer for his son-in-law. I would value your influence with the right person, but do not entangle yourself in my father's affairs. The Comte de Maucombe seeks a marquisate for himself; reserve efforts for me. When Louis becomes a deputy next winter, we will be in Paris to secure him a government post so we may live on his salary. My father sits between center and right; a title will satisfy him. Our family was noble even under King René, and Charles X will likely grant a Maucombe title. My fear is my father using this to favor my younger brother—if a marquisate is dangled, he will focus on himself.January 15th.Ah, Louise, I have been through hell. I tell you because you are like a sister; still, I doubt I shall ever relive those five ghastly days. The word "convulsions" sickens me. Five days felt like five centuries. A mother who has not suffered this cannot comprehend it.The night before, the weather was close; I thought Armand affected by teething. Usually sweet, he grew peevish, crying and breaking his toys. I put him to bed beside me. Toward morning he cried "Mamma!" in a strange voice and twitched. I rose for a drink; as I handed him the cup he was motionless, repeating "Mamma!" in a voice hardly a voice. His hand was stiff. He gulped water convulsively, clung to me, eyes rolling back, limbs rigid. I screamed for Louis."A doctor! Quick!... he is dying," I cried.Louis vanished. Armand gasped "Mamma!" then lost consciousness. Veins on his forehead swelled, and convulsions began. For an hour before doctors arrived I held my lifeless baby—my pride and joy. His eyes! I cannot think of them without horror. My pretty Armand was a mere shell—black, shriveled.Two doctors from Marseilles hovered like birds of ill omen. One blamed brain fever, the other called them ordinary convulsions. Our country doctor offered no opinion. "Teething," said one; "fever," said the other. They agreed on leeches to the neck and ice to the head. To see a child like that—purple, silent—was horrible. They even cut his curls for the ice. The convulsions returned every ten minutes; the child writhed, white then violet, limbs clattering like wood. This unconscious flesh had smiled and said "Mamma!"—the thought pierced my soul.After four days and nights, the doctors recommended a dreadful ointment to create open sores. Sores on my Armand! Five days earlier he had been playing and laughing. I refused, trusting nature. Louis, who believes doctors, scolded me. But in those moments when the disease mimicked death, I felt that remedy might save him. His skin was dry and rough; the ointment would not work. I wept; the doctors were at dinner!Alone with him, I stripped off the medical appliances, pressed him to my bosom, and laid my forehead against his, begging God to spare him. For minutes I held him, longing to die with him. Then his limbs relaxed; the writhings ceased; the ghastly colors faded. I screamed; the doctors rushed in."He is saved!" cried the oldest doctor.What music those words were! Two hours later Armand returned to life. I was crushed, saved only by the joy of his recovery. What tortures bind a mother to her child! Must the nails be driven to fasten him to my heart? Was I not mother enough? I who wept at his first words and steps—why these horrors?Now Armand plays and laughs. What causes this dreadful disease in children? I ponder it, especially as I am again with child. Is it teething? A brain disorder? A nervous susceptibility? Our country doctor blames nervous trouble from teething. I would give every tooth in my head to see Armand's through. One white pearl peeking from swollen gums brings a cold sweat. The heroism with which my angel bore suffering proves he is his mother’s son. A look from him pierces me.Medical science offers no clear explanation for this tetanus that appears and vanishes swiftly, unpreventable and uncured. One thing is certain: it is hell for a mother to see her child in convulsions. How passionately I hold him now! I could walk forever with him in my arms!To have endured this six weeks before my confinement made it worse; I feared for the coming child. Farewell, my beloved. Do not wish for a child—this is the essence of my letter!Poor dear, Macumer and I forgave you upon hearing of your terrible trouble. I felt pain reading of your double agony; there seem compensations in being childless.I write to tell you Louis has been promoted; he may now wear the ribbon of an Officer of the Legion. You are fortunate, Renée—likely to have a little girl, as you wished!My brother's marriage to Mlle. de Mortsauf was celebrated on our return. Our gracious King, who is extraordinarily kind, has granted my brother the reversion of the post of first gentleman of the chamber, presently held by his father-in-law, on condition the Mortsauf scutcheon be placed alongside the Lenoncourt’s."The office ought to go with the title," he told the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry.My father is justified a hundredfold. Without my fortune none of this would have happened. My parents came from Madrid for the wedding and will return after the reception I am hosting for the newlyweds.The carnival will be lively. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria are in Paris, which unnerves me. Marie Heredia is one of Europe’s most beautiful women, and I dislike the way Felipe looks at her. Therefore, I am extra sweet and affectionate. Every look and gesture says what my lips do not: "She could not love like this!" Heaven knows how lovely I am! Yesterday Mme. de Maufrigneuse said, "Dear child, who can compete with you?"I keep Felipe entertained, making his sister-in-law seem dull by comparison. I am less sorry that a little Abencerrage is not on the way, since the Duchess will likely remain in Paris for her confinement and lose her beauty. If the baby is a boy, he will be named Felipe in honor of the exile. Fate decrees I shall be godmother again.Goodbye, dear. I plan to go to Chantepleurs early this year: our Italian tour was shockingly expensive. I will leave around the end of March to economize in Nivenais. Besides, I am tired of Paris. Felipe and I long for the tranquility of the park, cool meadows, and the sparkling Loire. Chantepleurs will be delightful after Italy; splendor becomes tiresome, and a lover's gaze is more beautiful than any opera or painting!We expect you there. Do not fear my jealousy. You are free to test Macumer's heart and expose his doubts. I am utterly indifferent. Since that day in Rome Felipe's love for me has deepened. He just told me (looking over my shoulder) that his sister-in-law, Princess Heredia, his childhood dream, has no brains. Oh, my dear, I am worse than a ballet dancer! If you knew the joy that remark brought me! I pointed out her faulty French—she says "esemple" for "exemple," "sain" for "cinq," "cheu" for "je." She is beautiful but lacks charm and wit. When praised she seems puzzled. Felipe could not have lived two months with Marie after marriage. Don Fernand, the Duc de Soria, suits her perfectly: generous but a spoiled child. I am tempted to be mischievous and make you laugh, but I will refrain. Much love, darling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My daughter Jeanne-Athenais is two months; godparents: mother and Louis' grand-uncle. Your godson says "Matoumer"—he's teething. I watch him anxiously but can't keep him near; doctors demand precautions and his discomforts persist. Don't tell Felipe—she resembles me and might outshine Armand.Father saw Felipe thin; you seem unwell. Your letter was shorter, less affectionate—whim? The nurse is annoyed; Athenais hungry. Farewell; write letters.My dear Renée,For the first time I am truly alone and in tears. I sat beneath a willow by the Chantepleurs marsh—pretty but desolate without the sound of children. Nearly three years of marriage and no child; your household full of little ones makes me examine my heart. I would brave any suffering, even what you endured at my godson’s birth, to hold a cherub of my own—an Athenais of my own! I imagine her looks and sweetness, yet you tell me nothing—just like you, Renée. I am sure you share my distress.Each disappointment plunges me into melancholy, from which I compose mournful elegies. When will I embroider caps and sew lace for a tiny head? Will I never hear “Mamma” or have a little despot tug at my dress? Will my gravel lie untouched by carriage wheelmarks, my courtyard free of broken toys? I long to watch a life unfold—another Felipe, only dearer; a son to become my second self. My park and château seem cold and empty. A woman without children feels unnatural; we exist to be mothers. Barrenness is a bleak fate. If I cannot be a mother, I will at least indulge in romantic sorrow.I told my Moor of this, and he wept; he cannot bear jokes on his love. Sometimes I dream of pilgrimage to carry my longings to some shrine. Next winter I shall seek a physician. I am too angry with myself to write more. Farewell.---A whole year has passed with no letter—what does it mean? I am hurt. Is Louis, who visits so often, a sufficient substitute? It is not enough to know you are well; I want your feelings. Your silence and seclusion in the country while your husband gains Parliamentary honors alarm me. His eloquence has secured favor, and he will likely receive a fine post when the session ends. Are you spending your time writing him counsel?Why did you not come to Paris? I could have enjoyed your company for four months. Louis told me you would fetch him and have your third confinement in Paris—what a monstrous Gigogne of a mother! After pestering Louis, I learned his grand-uncle, Athenais’ godfather, is very ill; perhaps you, as a careful mother, wish to secure a legacy from your husband’s last relative. Rest easy; we are all working for Louis. Martignac will likely place him in the audit department. But if you will not tell me why you hide in the country, I shall be cross.Are you afraid that your family’s political wisdom should seem to center in you? Is it the uncle’s legacy? Do you fear you would be less to your children in Paris? Or are you simply too vain to show yourself in the metropolis while expecting? Vain thing! Farewell.---My dear friend, have you forgotten my two little brown heads, my subjects and tyrants? I could not drag a four-year-old and an almost three-year-old to Paris while expecting again. I would not trouble you or disturb your husband with such a tumult. I detest hotel life. When I come to Paris for the session it will be to my own house: Louis’s uncle, pleased with his grand‑nephew’s prospects, gave me two hundred thousand francs to buy a house near you, and my mother sent thirty thousand for furnishings. Louis is charged with finding a place; I will do my best not to disgrace you.I appreciate all you have done for Louis at court; yet I do not want him placed too conspicuously. I prefer the Audit Office for steadiness rather than a post that would commit him too much. Our affairs are in good hands; once the steward understands the details, I will support Louis.As for writing long letters—I cannot. One day is like another here: the children are either well or ill, and that sets the tone. In this solitary grange, minutes become hours depending on their health. My delightful hours come only when they sleep and I am no longer needed to rock or soothe them. When they lie beside me, I think, “Nothing can go wrong now.” Yet every mother is haunted by imagined dangers: razors, fires, ponds, the demon of convulsions. We spend our days on a stage of small dramas, tender and terrible.At night I dream of their future, comforted by the watch of their guardian angel. Sometimes Armand calls in his sleep; I kiss his forehead and his sister’s feet and watch their beauty. Once an angel roused me to Athenais’ cradle—her head was too low and Armand’s feet purple with cold. How vital it is to have one’s children at night! A nurse cannot soothe them as a mother can after a nightmare. I have learned to sleep lightly, my ear tuned to every sigh or rustle by their bed.Morning brings their chatter. Nais strains to reach me while Armand climbs up like a little monkey; they transform my bed into a playground and I am at their mercy. The baby tugs my hair and Armand guards my breast like a jealous sentinel. Their antics replace sleep; their laughter repays all care.Then the dressing begins: dressing gown, apron, sleeves rolled up, and Mary’s help in a series of baths and scrubbings. Bath time is a peril—so much crying results from a misjudged temperature or a dropped brush. Amusement is necessary—paper boats and glass ducks—to get them to submit to the bathtub. I contrive diversions: prayers, scoldings, promises; motherhood becomes a game of cleverness matched to a child’s cunning. Our triumphs are private: Mary and I rejoice when, after a struggle, the children are at last clean and content. Only God, His angels, and perhaps you can grasp the satisfaction of such small victories.English women excel at arranging material comforts; their nurses and systems are admirable. My children will always be bare-legged in summer and in woolen socks in winter; no swaddling or tight bandages for them. A true mother, though, is never free; the helplessness of many French infants gives nurses a freedom their charge’s mothers would not allow. That is why I do not write: besides managing the estate, I am raising two children, with a third on the way.Motherhood demands silent self‑denial and attention to minute details—a mother blows on the spoon for her child’s soup, minces the chop, endures the annoyances. No servant or foreign nurse can replace a mother in those daily contests where gentleness must soothe every little grief. Louise, caring for these darlings, engages my whole soul. A crying child condemns the nurse or mother unless pain is natural. With two to tend (and soon three), they occupy all my thoughts—sometimes even you, whom I adore, recede to a memory.Often my dressing is not finished until mid-afternoon. I distrust mothers whose rooms are immaculate and who always look perfect. Yesterday, in a lovely April, I planned an outing with the children—an epic for a mother. I had laid out Armand’s little black velvet jacket, a new collar, a Scotch cap; Nais in white and pink with a baby cap. The little heads were to be brushed; Armand’s curls and Nais’ soft hair arranged; my heart was in my mouth. Once outside, proud of my work, I admired Armand’s princely air as he helped Nais along the path. Then a carriage approached and in our scramble the children tumbled into a puddle, wrecking my preparations. We had to return and change: a crying baby and a soaked child left the mother with no thought for herself.At dinner I have oft achieved nothing. Feeding two, securing bibs, turning up sleeves, cajoling food—these recurring cares, joys, and catastrophes make me neglect my own tidiness. To write you I have let them cut pictures from novels and arrange my silks; they are absorbed and content.Yet I have nothing to complain of. My children are robust and inventive; they amuse themselves more readily than you might think. A little freedom and a few pebbles, shells, and sand do more for them than many toys. Armand talks to flowers, flies, and chickens; he imitates and admires insects and asks “why” about everything. It is touching to see his curiosity—he even thinks of you, his godmother, as a fairy.Let me not sadden you with all my joys. A poor man once followed us begging—beggars know a mother with a child cannot resist. Armand, innocent of hunger or money, offered the beggar the trumpet I had bought him: “Here, take this!” That moment of spontaneous charity was incomparable. The poor man, moved, kept it, saying, “I too, madame, have had children.” I wept at the sweetness.I shudder at the thought of Armand going to school: I have only three and a half years before public institutions tear him away. The bright flowers of his sunny childhood will be cut by the scythe of education; his gracious candor may be lost. What will they do to the thinking being he is, to the curls I brushed and kissed so often? And you—are you still in love with Felipe? I have no fear of the Saracen. Goodbye; Nais has tumbled, and this letter threatens to become a volume.---My sweet, tender Renée, you will have learned from the papers of the terrible calamity that has crushed me. I could not write for twenty days; I did not leave his bedside. I received his last breath, closed his eyes, kept vigil with the priests, and said the prayers for the dead. The pangs I suffered felt like a just punishment; yet when I saw the calm smile on his lips, his final farewell to me, how could I think I had caused his death?He is gone and I am bereft. To you, who knew us both, what more need be said? I would give my share of Heaven to bring him back even for a second. Will you not come soon and soothe me with such impossible promises? You told me beforehand that he would suffer through me. Was it so? Yes; I had no right to all his love. Like a thief I took what belonged to him and in my frenzied grasp crushed the life out of my bliss. The madness is over, but I feel alone and crushed. Merciful God! what torture can surpass the misery in that single word: alone?When they took him away I lay on the same bed hoping to die. There was but a door between us and I thought I could force it. I was too young for death; after forty days of cruel care and attention, I was slowly brought back to life. Now I sit in the country by a window, surrounded by the flowers he had loved and made flourish, looking at the view he admired. Ah, Renée, how new surroundings wound when the heart is dead. His flowers torment me; to walk in my garden is to walk upon him. When I first ventured out I trembled and could not move.My parents are in Spain, my brothers far off, and you detained in the country. But two angels of mercy comfort me: the Duc and Duchesse de Soria hurried to their brother in his illness and have been everything I could wish. The last nights found us gathered in mute grief around this great man—rare in any age, distinguished by mind and heart. Felipe’s calm resignation was angelic. His last words to me were simple and frank about his will and his love; he blessed me on his lips and spoke of my memory more fondly than any other. His brother forgave him, knowing what love is.I owe my life to my brother-in-law and his wife; they wish to take me to Spain. To no one but you can I freely pour out my grief. A sense of my own faults sits heavy: my demands, jealousy, and anxious unrest may have worn him down. My love was dangerous because we were so alike in sensitivity; my careless words pierced him to the heart. You cannot imagine how submissive he was: I only had to tell him to leave me alone, and he obeyed without a murmur. His last breath blessed me, saying that a single morning with me was worth more than a lifetime with another. My tears fall as I write.This is my life now: rising at midday, going to bed early, lingering over meals, standing motionless before a plant, gazing into the trees, finding significance in trifles. I seek shade, silence, and night. I drag through each hour, taking a gloomy pride in surviving them. My park gives me company enough; everything there echoes my vanished joy, visible only to me.If I still live it is because Heaven tempers grief to the strength of those who bear it. Only a woman can know what it is to lose a love that was genuine—one that satisfied spirit and flesh. How rare a man so gifted that worship does not feel debasing! If such fortune befalls us once, we cannot hope to find it again. Men of true greatness, veiled by poetic grace, charm with a high spiritual power—beware of love: it may ruin you and the woman you love. That is my cry as I walk the paths of my wood.And he has left me no child! That love, rich in smiles and tenderness, left no fruit. I feel cursed. Is it that intense, single‑hearted passion bears no children? Is only a marriage of reason blessed with progeny? Does Heaven envy our passions? Such wild questions haunt me.You are the one person whose company I could bear. Come to me; none but you should help Louise in her sorrow. The day I first donned my widow’s bonnet—when I first saw myself in black—I fell back and wept until night. I weep again at the memory. Bring your children; you can nurse your baby here without making me jealous; what is gone is gone, and I will be glad to see my godson. Felipe wished for a child like little Armand. Come, then—come and help me bear this woe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My darling—I'll be near when you read this; Louis remains in Provence seeking re-election. Weep—tears will bring joy when you reunite. He journeys to Heaven; duties loosen chains. In my arms you'll lift your head to him—pure, noble, bearing the good. While preparing, Armand cried, "Godmother, I want to see her," making me jealous—perhaps he's yours.Yes, Renée, it is true: I have sold my house, Chantepleurs, and the farms in Seine-et-Marne — but I am neither mad nor ruined.After settling everything I have 1,200,000 francs from my late husband’s fortune. I invested 1,000,000 in the Three per Cents at fifty; it now yields 60,000 francs instead of the 30,000 the property produced. My former life was tedious — six months in the country managing leases and impatient farmers, the rest in Paris plagued by delays and legal troubles. For a twenty-seven-year-old widow it was intolerable. Now my fortune is secured with the State. Instead of paying taxes, I receive 30,000 francs every six months from the Treasury, handed to me by a pleasant clerk. If the nation failed I should still be as well off as before, and I have time to save a little capital. Besides, the Comte de l’Estorade is a peer of this semi-republic and a friend at Court, so I have no cause for anxiety.What drove me to these calculations? Love.I must tell you plainly why I acted as I did. I am to be married in a village near Paris. I love and am loved. Forgive me for keeping this from you and de l’Estorade; I wished to spare you both anxiety. You know my jealousy; I thought secrecy kinder.The man I love had 30,000 francs of debts, which I have paid. You may suspect an adventurer, but I investigated him. He has courted me nearly two years. I am twenty-seven and he twenty-three — yes, a wide gap. He is a poet who has lived on very little and spent more time dreaming than making a living. Practical people think artists unreliable; I understand that prejudice.But my love is deliberate, not a caprice. He waited patiently; his love is noble and tender. My beloved is Gaston, the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, who died tragically. He was raised by his brother Louis, a sailor, whose letters once encouraged him; now Louis has vanished and Gaston is often despairing.Gaston believed genius would bring fortune, yet his life has been hardship: failure to establish himself in literature, mounting debts, humiliation. I met him at Mme. d’Espard’s and felt an immediate sympathy that has grown into passion. He has talent and pride — qualities that can intimidate a woman — but his love for me seems genuine. He knows nothing of the depth of my feeling.I am confident of our happiness, though I fear losing him or my charms as he remains young. I mean to nourish our love, to be loved for myself. If his affection should wane, I would blame myself rather than him. My nature inclines to be a lover rather than a mother; should disaster come, Renée, I beg you to care for my children as my legacy. Your tenderness would make my death less bitter.I have resolved to marry quietly. I will retain control of my property; Gaston must not know everything. I propose to assign him stock yielding 12,000 francs a year, which I shall place where he will find it on the eve of our wedding. If he refuses it I will break the engagement.This confession has fatigued me; I will continue later. I must spend tomorrow in the country.October 20th.I have taken steps for secrecy. I bought a small property near Ville-d’Avray: twenty acres, a little lake, and a charming garden. It is secluded, surrounded by woods, and beautifully laid out. My chalet, inspired by Swiss designs, is simple and solid, well-equipped and hidden from view. There is a separate house for servants and a concealed entrance; I have chosen discreet, loyal staff. All my cherished belongings from Paris are here, creating a simple but elegant home. The valley’s tranquility moved me to tears on my first visit; I paid the architect without hesitation, insisting he tell no one I was the owner.Now you understand my actions and the purpose of my investments. I have cleared financial burdens to focus wholly on him. Gaston knows nothing of my plans; our life at the chalet will be modest, though I shall spare no care in preserving my appearance for him. I want him to love me at the end as at the beginning.In two days I shall be Mme. Gaston. Is it fitting for a Christian to love so deeply?My man of business says he has lost a client. And you, my dear, may feel you are losing a sister.Address future letters to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We shall keep our names and our retreat secret from the locals and obtain provisions from Paris. Farewell. Writing this I feel grief and joy together — proof that my love for Gaston is as deep as the sorrow of leaving you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My Dear Daniel,I need two witnesses for my marriage; please come tomorrow evening with our friend Joseph Bridau. My future wife wishes to live in complete retirement, which explains our infrequent meetings this past year. After the wedding we will be apart for a long time, but I know you will understand. Friendship can endure absence; I shall miss you, yet I shall not see you in my own home. She has sacrificed the closeness of a childhood friend for me, and I must do the same.This shows our love is deep and genuine, founded on mutual understanding. It gives me immense joy, yet I carry a painful secret. You know how desperate my finances have been, and only through your support have I managed. My wife is wealthy; I am not. I once joked about wanting a rich heiress, but now that it is real I feel a bitter humiliation I cannot shake. This shame confirms my love for her, for I do not recoil from it, though it overwhelms me.Otherwise, my dreams have come true. She is the most beautiful and noble woman, with a charming personality and a keen mind. At twenty-seven she is a widow without children, lively yet sometimes melancholy. Despite her aristocratic background, she loves me despite my humble origins. Our love has grown over time; we share a deep emotional connection and avoid the physical aspect of love out of mutual respect. Once she confessed that her marriage was her regret, revealing the depth of her feelings. You, Daniel, are a giant among men, and you love a remarkable woman, just as I love my Armande.Louise, can it be that you intend a life of wedded solitude after sacrificing your first husband? You seem to court future misery. I see you are resolved, and the man who won your heart must possess a rare charm. But have you forgotten your earlier criticisms of young men, when you claimed they had tarnished purity?You are lucky to believe in happiness, and while I cannot blame you, I must warn you. Nature and society conspire against absolute happiness, and I foresee some disaster arising from your boundless joy. I have no objections to your fiancé, but I hope you will describe him to me one day.I have not told Louis about your marriage; it would only invite his ridicule. I will simply say you wish to keep it secret. You are brave to face winter first, and I will be your discreet friend. Your journey into this emotional wilderness is like an explorer’s perilous expedition, though fortunately only a short distance from Paris. I wish you a safe journey and a swift return.What has happened to you, my dear? After two years of silence, Renee has a right to worry about Louise. Is this what love does, scattering friendships like ours? You must admit that, while I am devoted to my children, a mother’s love is expansive and does not diminish other affections. I miss your letters and long to see your face.Regarding our situation, I will be brief. Your last letter contained sharp comments about our political position. You mocked us for keeping our posts in the Audit Department, which Louis owes to Charles X’s favor. But how could we provide for Athenais and Rene on an income of forty thousand livres, of which thirty thousand is entailed? We must live on our salary and save the estate’s income for their future. In twenty years we will have saved enough to provide for both children.Louis has been raised to the Peerage and is now a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. He has made himself useful in the Chamber and was offered an embassy, which I declined for the sake of our children’s education. I will not leave Paris until Rene has finished his schooling.We could not remain strictly loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without jeopardizing our children’s prospects. A mother is not called to be a Decius, especially now. In fifteen years, Louis will retire on a good pension, and I hope to secure a post for Armand in the Audit Department.I am a happy mother; my children give me endless joy. Armand is now a day scholar at Henry IV’s school. I wanted him in public school but could not bear to part with him, so I arranged for him to attend by day and return home each evening. He is the same charming boy you loved, and his tutor praises his progress.Home education has drawbacks: children raised at home may be exposed too early to the world’s passions and may lack the social skills gained with peers. A mother must keep her children from society’s distractions, as I have done. Now that I am thirty, I feel a duty fulfilled. My children seem to sense my thoughts and respond. Armand, once dull, has lately excelled, winning prizes for translation and essays. I felt immense pride when his name was called and longed to shout, “I am his mother!”Rene, inspired by Armand, wants to attend school too. The three children can be noisy, but I cherish every moment. I am the only one allowed to care for them; the joys of motherhood outweigh the challenges.I must study their characters closely, for a mother alone discerns her children’s natural aptitudes. Home-bred children often develop ease and tact, compensating for lack of innate ability. I believe Armand will become an admirable public servant, while Rene will be a bold sailor.Nais is so dear to me I can hardly see her as separate from myself. I want her to be happy and will only give her to a man who truly loves her. Yet I worry about her future and the risk of a poor marriage. Your silence has reminded me of the anguish marriage can bring a mother. Farewell for now, dear Louise, and please write back.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Renée, after two years of silence you wonder why I have written so little. Words fail to express my happiness. We bear everything with ease; we are in perfect harmony. There has been no discord in our feelings, and each day brings its own joy. We do not fear monotony; rather we regret that our time together may not suffice for all the expressions of our love. We take more pleasure in each other's company than on the first day, and every hour reveals new reasons to love one another.Every evening we talk of visiting Paris, of the new boulevards and the finished Madeleine, but in the morning we linger over breakfast and take a siesta. Gaston gazes at me as if I were a painting, and we both shed tears of joy reflecting on our love. I still play the mistress, pretending to give less than I receive, and delight in the small coquetries of our life. There is something sweet about passion tempered by tenderness; Gaston, my master, remains a timid suitor at my command.You asked me to describe him, yet it is impossible to portray the man we love without emotion. Society alters men so that the public man can differ from the man of deep feeling. The charming suitor may lack qualities a woman values, while an unrefined man may possess a profound capacity for love. Our ideal is a man whose outward appearance aligns with his true nature, a grace that cannot be acquired. This ideal haunts every woman's imagination, and in Gaston I have found it.Ah, I never knew love could be so intertwined with youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston is unpretentious, moving with effortless grace. When we walk together our bodies seem to fit perfectly, a single figure gliding through the woods. Our thoughts, desires, and words harmonize. Sometimes we walk in silence, listening to the rain and admiring the sunset, feeling as if in silent prayer for our happiness. A timid kiss exchanged in nature feels like an act of worship.We return home each time more in love than before. Such passion between older married people would scandalize Paris; only in the woods can we express it freely.Gaston is of average height, well-proportioned, and agile. He has a fair complexion, dark hair, and a striking resemblance to Louis XIII. His honorable poverty has spared him the vices that ruin many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he has a robust constitution. His blue eyes, full of tenderness for me, can flash with intensity at inspiring thoughts.He has a remarkably even temperament. I have heard many tales of women’s troubles with men dissatisfied with themselves, but Gaston is free from such burdens. I have learned that marrying an older man can lead to unequal unions, and I urge you not to let Athénaïs marry one.His smile is a treasure—always fresh, full of unspoken love and gratitude. Our shared experiences have become part of our joy, and even the smallest details of nature remind us of our time together. We share a deep connection, as if we are two editions of the same book. Our ability to view subjects broadly brings constant pleasure, and this unity of thought feels like a pledge of love.Despite these pleasures, life here is laborious. I do my own room, refusing a hired hand to intrude on my personal space. My room is meticulously arranged so that Gaston finds it pleasing. I rise early, bathe in cold water to refresh myself before he wakes, and emerge like a newly opened flower. He appreciates this freshness without knowing the effort behind it.My daily grooming is handled by my maid, and I dress several times a day for Gaston’s delight. We take great interest in our flowers and horticulture, surrounding our chalet with beauty. Our garden is a source of pride; we eagerly tend our fruits and vegetables and sell the surplus. This abundance is a comfort hard to find in Paris.To keep Gaston's mind engaged I encourage him to finish plays begun during harder times. Literary work requires reflection and inspiration, and I assist him so our winter evenings are never dull. Our servants have an easy time; they need no reprimand. They present us as a traveling couple content with their roles.I strive to fill the role of society for Gaston, ensuring his solitude does not become tedious. I have learned true jealousy—the kind born of love. The mere thought of him ceasing to love me sends shivers through me; I tremble before him.Alas, I am still without a child. I long for the day when our home will be filled with the laughter of children running through our garden. It pains me to see your lovely children, for my life feels narrower without them. Motherhood expands a woman's heart, while love can be selfish. I envy your happiness and the joy you find in your children.As I approach thirty, I feel the limits of my beauty closing in. Though still attractive, I worry about aging while Gaston remains young. I often ask him to promise he will tell me if his love ever fades, and he swears it will not, finding the idea absurd.Goodbye, dear friend. Shall we let years pass without writing again? Happiness can be monotonous, perhaps why Dante seems greater in the Paradiso than in the Inferno. I am not Dante, only your friend, and I do not wish to bore you. Write to me—your children supply endless stories—while I... No more of this. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, I have read your letter repeatedly; its tone is childish. You forget that pure love belongs to nature and is distorted by civilization. Society built the family as the foundation of organized life at the cost of individual desires. A married man dedicates himself to his wife, who benefits from his efforts.You have taken a mistaken path: the dream of being adored suits the young, not a mature woman. Vanity may enjoy adoration, but true fulfillment lies in the life of wife and mother. Return to Paris; fear the loss of ordinary happiness more than hardship.Life’s simple pleasures—bread, air, silence—sustain us. Passionate love exhausts; motherhood’s affections endure and reward. I chose self‑sacrifice and would choose it again. Your life, masked by romance, is rooted in selfishness; I plead with you as I watch your happiness tested.Life seeks balance; excess brings pain or fleeting pleasure. You are ill because you cling to a passion a marriage should steady. The glory of home is calmness and shared life, which you overlook. You, more passionate than tender, would be both wife and mistress, undermining marriage’s sanctity.If you could understand a mother’s sweet self‑discipline. My proud nature softened into gentle melancholy, rewarded by motherhood’s joys. I fear your life may darken. I had hoped time would restore reason, but your appetite for love seems insatiable. Farewell; I despair that my letter, meant to reconcile you with society, only revealed what I see as selfishness.---Renee, calamity has struck—no thunderbolt like this for poor Louise. Doubt is my calamity; certainty would be death. The day before yesterday, after dressing, I looked for Gaston to walk but found him gone. In the stable his mare Fedelta was in a lather; it implied he’d been to Paris without telling me. Suspicion seized me.When Gaston returned he carried me to the parlor; I would not speak and fled to my room to weep. He waited patiently. I asked about mud on Fedelta’s legs; he teased, but I saw no disloyalty in his eyes, though I remained unconvinced.We spent the day together; my heart clung to him. I hated that mare for my distress. I asked if I bored him; he reassured me. I suggested selling Fedelta; his response made me feel inadequate. Alone, I was consumed by despair.Next morning I told him to take Fedelta to Paris; my tone betrayed turmoil. He urged trust; I tried to suppress suspicion. He left, and I watched him go with a sense of doom.When he returned late with a whip as a gift I felt it only distracted from something deeper. I later learned he bought it from Verdier, which confirmed my fears. I resolved to keep watch while staying silent.Days passed; Gaston changed, working harder—seeming to need money. His drawer was empty; he received letters he quickly tore up. Jealousy consumed me; I followed him to the post office and confronted him about a letter from Nathan announcing a play’s acceptance.Despite his success, I couldn’t shake that he hid something. The conviction that he had a rival drove me to find the truth.After investigation I learned Gaston visited a woman in Paris; I followed and found she was his brother’s widow, legally married. The revelation shattered me: he had been providing for her and her children. I returned tormented by thoughts of betrayal and jealousy.Despite the pain, my love for Gaston remained. I struggled with his past and wanted to confront him about his feelings for the widow, but feared the truth would destroy me. I even pondered my own end, unable to bear the suffering.---My dear Louise, I write urgently; your last letter alarmed me. I confided in Louis to save you. Though our methods may seem extreme, the outcome is satisfactory. I even involved the police, kept secret among us.Gaston is not as you feared. His brother Louis died in Calcutta, leaving a widow and two children. Gaston has secretly supported them, using his earnings for his sister‑in‑law and her children. The woman you mistook for a rival is not a threat but a mother in need; Gaston acted from kindness and duty.You should love him more; he has shown sensitivity and care. Welcome the widow and her children into your life—perhaps adopt the boys. This revelation should give you peace, not despair.Ah, my dear friend, what can I say? It is too late for me. My actions have sealed my fate. Tormented by love for Gaston, I cannot bear losing him. I am filled with despair and long for relief. Come, dear friend, and witness my decline.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My love,—Take the children to Provence without me; I must stay with Louise, who has only a few days left. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whom I worry.You know the letter that sent me rushing to Ville d'Avray, gathering doctors along the way. Since then I have not left my dear friend, sitting up every night for two weeks.When I arrived, Louise and Gaston seemed charming and happy, but it was a façade. She whispered, "He must not know; I am dying." Her hand was burning and her cheeks flushed. I was glad I had left the doctors in the woods until needed."Leave us for a little," she told Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have secrets to share."Alone, she collapsed into my arms and wept. I urged her to tell me. "I have brought the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, along with Bianchon," I said."Ah! Have them in at once if they can save me! The passion that drove me to death now cries for life!""But what have you done to yourself?""I have brought myself to the last stage of consumption in just a few days.""But how?""I got myself into a profuse perspiration at night, then lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, but I am dying!""Send him to Paris; I will fetch the doctors myself," I said, rushing out to find them.After the consultation none of the doctors gave hope; they believed Louise would die with the fall of the leaves. Her strong constitution had served her scheme, but a few days of folly made her case desperate.I was stunned. I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. Crushed, I could not escort the doctors out. In my bitter thoughts, Louise's clear voice startled me: "So there is no hope for me!"She made me get up and took me to her drawing-room. "Stay with me to the end; I won't have sad faces around me. I must keep the truth from him. I know I have the strength to do it. I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood.""For him, I can see I would have made his life miserable. My jealousy has already wounded him. When my suspicions met indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. There are people who have lived sixty years without two years of real life, while my thirty years are doubled by the intensity of my love.""For him and me, the end is happy. But for you, dear Renée, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss that cannot be repaired. You alone have the right to mourn my death."Then, more gravely, she drew a lesson: her death proved, sadly, that marriage cannot rest on passion alone. "Your life is noble," she said to me, "giving your husband growing affection, while my passionate eagerness diminished. Twice I have gone astray, and twice Death has reached for my happiness. The first time he took the noblest of men; now he takes me from my poet husband, with all his beauty and grace.""I would not complain. I have known two men of nobility—one in mind, the other in form. I die adored—what more could I wish? Perhaps time to draw near to God, whom I may have thought too little of. My spirit will fly towards Him, full of love, praying to be reunited with the two who made my life a heaven."She warned that her example might tempt others to expect impossible felicities. Then, with characteristic disdain for the social fuss about women's emancipation, she said women were weak and should yield their wills to their husbands; the clamour they had made was ridiculous, betraying their childishness.She spoke in that gentle, grave fashion that made truths sound pretty, until Gaston entered with his sister-in-law, the children, and the English nurse he had fetched from Paris."Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!"She played hostess to Mme. Gaston and insisted the chalet be hers. I wrote to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, and to Madeleine. It was time. The next day Louise, exhausted, could not go out; she only rose for dinner. Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her brothers, and her mother arrived. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage faded; her father and brothers visited daily, and duchesses spent evenings at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences petty feelings.Louise remained charming, graceful, witty, and tender. She kept her perfect tact and lavished her brilliant mind on us—one of Paris's queens to the last."I want to look well even in my coffin," she said with that matchless smile, lying on the bed where she would linger for a fortnight.Her room lacked the usual sick-chamber air; medicines and nursing supplies were hidden."Is not my deathbed pretty?" she asked the priest who came to confess her.We watched her like misers. The anxiety and harsh truths had prepared Gaston for the worst. He is brave, but the blow has struck deep. I would not be surprised if he followed her soon. Yesterday, walking by the lake, he said simply, "I must be a father to those two children," pointing to his sister-in-law with the boys. "But I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them and persuade your husband to accept the role of guardian."He wore smiles for Louise, but I saw through the façade; his courage matched hers.Louise wished to see her godson, but I preferred he remain in Provence; she might wish to remember him generously, putting me in a difficult position.Goodbye, my love.August 25th (her birthday).Yesterday evening Louise was delirious briefly, but even her babble was beautiful—proof that the madness of gifted people is not that of fools. In a faint voice she sang Italian airs from I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moïse. We stood around her bed in silence; not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes, as we felt her soul departing. She could no longer see us, yet the charm lingered in the melody, sweet and otherworldly.During the night the death agony began. It is now seven in the morning, and I have just raised her from bed. A flicker of strength returned; she wished to sit by the window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving us with an empty shell.The last sacrament had been administered the evening before, without Gaston's knowledge—he had been sleeping during the ceremony. After she was set at the window, she asked me to read the De Profundis in French, facing the lovely scene she had created. She repeated the words after me and pressed her husband's hands.August 26th.My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. Oh! I want my children! Bring me my children!######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Comtesse Bolognini.Madame, recalling the pleasure your conversation gave a traveler in Milan, I place this work at your feet, hoping you will shelter it with your name as you once protected the tales of a beloved writer. You have a daughter, Eugénie, whose beauty and intelligence promise a happy childhood, unlike the rigid upbringing suffered by the Eugénie of these pages. Though Frenchmen are often called fickle, I am Italian in my constancy and memory. Writing “Eugénie” brings back the laughter and warmth of our time in Milan; I now regard you as a distant, treasured masterpiece.Your devoted servant,De Balzac.A DAUGHTER OF EVECHAPTER I. THE TWO MARIESIn a sumptuous blue-velvet boudoir on rue Neuve-des-Mathurins two young women wept by the fire. The room belonged to Ferdinand du Tillet, a rich banker who bought social standing with marriage but lacked true aristocratic ease. Madame du Tillet clasped her sister Madame Félix de Vandenesse’s hand as they exchanged confidences. Raised by a strict, pious mother and married without youthful freedoms, they entered matrimony naive about life and love. Schooled only in religion and needlework, they retained innocence but lacked the experience to handle adult passions; marriage, once imagined as escape, revealed unforeseen realities.CHAPTER II. A CONFIDENCE BETWEEN SISTERSMadame de Vandenesse, distraught by marital troubles, poured out her despair over a husband who seemed indifferent to her inner life. Madame du Tillet, equally helpless, described her own cold, listless domesticity. Each confessed a craving for passion and excitement suppressed by society and duty; each sought consolation and encouragement in the other.CHAPTER III. THE HISTORY OF A FORTUNATE WOMANFélix de Vandenesse, of noble lineage, had married hoping to recreate an idealized love. The duties and constraints of his class, however, cooled the relationship. Though kind and attentive, he could not supply the romantic intensity his wife desired. Monotony awakened in her a longing for deeper emotional fulfilment and tempted thoughts of love beyond marriage.CHAPTER IV. A CELEBRATED MANRaoul Nathan, an advancing literary figure, entered society with talent, audacity, and caustic wit. His unconventional manner—attractive and unsettling—caught Madame de Vandenesse’s attention at Madame de Montcornet’s salon; their early exchanges hinted at an unspoken attraction. Nathan, juggling a fraught career and private troubles, saw her as a potential muse, though social position and decorum threatened any open liaison.CHAPTER V. FLORINEFlorine, a gifted actress and Nathan’s long-term companion, balanced affection with practicality: she sustained his spirits and career while depending on him for protection and status. As Nathan’s admiration for Madame de Vandenesse grew, Florine felt the shift and became insecure and jealous.CHAPTER VI. ROMANTIC LOVENathan and Madame de Vandenesse bonded over art and literature. Their encounters grew intimate, bringing exhilaration and peril. She struggled with guilt and desire, torn between loyalty to her husband and the lure of a passionate life, fully aware of the social consequences of yielding.CHAPTER VII. SUICIDECrushed by career setbacks and inner despair, Nathan contemplated suicide. A chance meeting with Madame de Vandenesse revived his will to live; seeing his desperation, she resolved to protect and encourage him, countering both his darkest impulses and the self-destructive streak in his ambitions.CHAPTER VIII. A LOVER SAVED AND LOSTAfter his crisis, Madame de Vandenesse grew more involved, balancing protection with her own feelings. Their relationship became both strength and turmoil as secrecy and social risk complicated them. As Nathan’s fortunes improved, he found himself torn between Florine’s practical devotion and the countess’s inspiring influence; Florine’s jealousy intensified the strain.CHAPTER IX. THE HUSBAND’S TRIUMPHFélix, sensing his wife’s attachment to Nathan, moved to preserve their marriage. Determined to discover the truth and shield her from scandal, he acted to defend her honor and their union. The characters remain intertwined by love, ambition, loyalty, and expectation, leading to a dramatic clash between desire and duty.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################On a Sunday morning in April 1813 a luxurious cabriolet drew up before the Tuileries carrying an anxious, aged father and his beautiful daughter, Julie. Proud of the attention she attracted, he accompanied her to the gardens to see the military review commanded by Napoleon—the last before a disastrous campaign. Though the review had not yet begun and he urged them to leave, Julie, afraid of missing the Emperor, insisted on staying. An officer recognized her and admitted them to a prime vantage point. The spectacle—the Old Guard preparing, the crowd’s buzz, the mounting anticipation—was magnificent. When Napoleon appeared, cheers rose. Julie was captivated, but her eye soon fixed on a young officer on the Emperor’s staff, Colonel Victor d’Aiglemont. A pang of jealousy and an unexpected attraction seized her, while her father’s anxious manner reminded her of their precarious situation. As the review ended, he warned her that youthful dreams could lead to sorrow and advised against falling for a man like Victor, whom he deemed unworthy. Despite his cautions, Julie felt drawn to Victor and their attachment deepened.Nearly a year later, in March 1814, Julie and Victor were traveling together. The once-vibrant girl had become a shadow; her health faltered as Victor, now a colonel, was absorbed in military duties and later in adapting to the shifting political scene. Isolated and yearning for affection, her need grew while Victor’s attention waned. During their travels they encountered an Englishman, Arthur, detained in France. Julie felt an unexpected kinship with him; Arthur’s quiet admiration and gentleness contrasted sharply with Victor’s indifference, and she was drawn to Arthur as a steady presence. Torn between marital duty and desire, she wrestled with feelings that deepened her inner conflict.As Napoleon fell and the political landscape shifted, Victor’s ambitions altered—he grew more involved with the new regime and increasingly self-interested. When his infidelities surfaced, Julie’s heartache deepened; she recognized his selfishness and felt the weight of betrayal. In desperation she sought solace in Arthur, though the moral and social consequences pressed upon her. Trapped in a loveless marriage and isolated, she resolved to refuse passivity and defy societal expectations to reclaim her happiness. The narrative is a poignant exploration of love, sacrifice, duty, and the quest for fulfillment.Between the Seine and the Loing lies a flat, dreary country edged by the Forest of Fontainebleau, its southern limits marked by Moret, Montereau, and Nemours. A monotonous horizon breeds melancholy. At its center stands the old château of Saint-Lange, once a sign of wealth now diminished.In late 1820 a young woman known in Paris for her beauty and wit arrived at Saint-Lange, startling villagers unaccustomed to gentry. The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s frailty and withdrawal fueled gossip: some blamed her husband’s gambling in Spain and expected the estate to be sold, prompting locals to eye their own interests.The Marquise lived in isolation, rarely leaving her room and only dining briefly with her daughter. At twenty-six she bore a profound, unacknowledged grief that had extinguished maternal instinct and made her feel like a ghost. Her anguish sprang from a lost love — a man who had died to preserve her honor — and from the social demands that forbade open mourning. Youth and beauty meant little without the true love and joy she had imagined; her respectable marriage offered no emotional fulfillment.The flat horizon mirrored her desolation. Seasons passed while her heart stayed heavy with unfulfilled longing; she felt trapped in a life of appearances, consumed by what might have been and by guilt for maintaining a façade of happiness.One day the village priest, M. le Curé, visited and, sensing her suffering, spoke of the universality of grief and the sustaining power of faith. At first she dismissed him, convinced her pain unique. His tale of a father who had lost all his sons, however, placed her sorrow in a broader context and offered unexpected solace.Gradually she confided in the priest, revealing her isolation and the emptiness of her motherhood: she saw her daughter Hélène not as the object of maternal love but as a living reminder of the lost man; duty, not affection, defined their relationship. The priest urged her to find strength in duty, but she wrestled with inadequacy and bitterness toward social constraints that stifled her true self.Spring’s work on the estate brought temporary reprieve. By October she left Saint-Lange with some regained vitality, though scars remained; she encountered the curé again but avoided him, signaling a desire to move forward without the château’s painful memories. Her quiet struggle — between public expectation and private desire, duty and identity — reveals the costs of rigid social structures and the burdens placed on women.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At Madame Firmiani’s ball, M. Charles de Vandenesse, a promising young diplomat about to leave Paris for the Congress of Laybach, came to take his leave. Near thirty and accustomed to a transient life, he treated existence philosophically, favoring ambition over romance and finding Parisian society largely superficial.Madame Firmiani introduced him to the enigmatic Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman wrapped in mystery and known for past affairs. Also thirty, the Marquise combined delicate beauty with an air of melancholy that contrasted sharply with the surrounding frivolity. Vandenesse was struck by her depth and by the disparity between her sensibility and her dull husband, who had profited from her influence. Beneath her calm he sensed profound sadness and unfulfilled dreams.Their conversation moved from light banter to discussions of love and feeling, revealing a connection that both excited and troubled him. He asked to visit again; in the days that followed she occupied his thoughts. Their encounters blended friendship and unspoken desire, leaving Vandenesse to wrestle with the implications of feeling for a married woman.The Marquise was drawn to him but divided by loyalty to the memory of a deceased lover and by social constraints. As their relationship deepened, obstacles multiplied—her past, society’s expectations, and his ambitions—producing moments of joy and anguish. A tender kiss marked a turning point, only to be shattered by her husband’s intrusion. Their bond testified to love’s power to cross boundaries, yet remained weighed down by duty and unfulfilled longing.Between the Barrière d’Italie and the Barrière de la Santé, the boulevard toward the Jardin des Plantes presents a stirring view of Paris: a tree-lined avenue dropping into a valley where factories and the brown Bièvre mingle with greenery; the squalor of Faubourg Saint-Marceau set against the domes of the Panthéon and Val-de-Grâce; the observatory’s windows and the Invalides’ lantern showing between familiar landmarks. To the right, the canal Saint-Martin runs alongside lime trees and Roman-style granaries; beyond, the smoke-streaked slopes of Belleville rise to the clouds. When sunlight floods this quarter, tiles gleam, foliage brightens, and the city seems at peace.One morning, while admiring this view and brooding over wealthy travelers who mock France, I was startled by a sudden kiss. Nearby stood a neatly dressed young woman with a handsome man and a charming little boy; their joy was immediate. A second child, a dark-haired, grave girl of seven or eight, stood apart.
