Gobseck shifted tactics—possession being as good as title—and offered eighty thousand francs if the couple left the diamonds with him.The Count’s pride flared; the Countess seemed stupefied. Maxime, her lover, blustered; Gobseck, unmoved, produced a cheque for fifty thousand francs on the Bank of France and thirty thousand in protested drafts, even two pistols to underline his implacable temper. Cornered, they accepted. Gobseck exulted at having obtained such stones “tolerably cheaply”; his ferocious delight in possession chilled me.Soon after, the Count burst into Gobseck’s room, furious that his family diamonds had been bought. Gobseck replied coldly: if Mme. la Comtesse could sign bills, she could engage in business; whether they were the Count’s could not be proven without bringing the wife into court and exposing her. He mocked the Count’s authority; our dispute turned to law: in equity perhaps, legally no. I advised the Count to come to terms—pay for the diamonds or secure a redemption clause—since litigation would be ruinous and humiliating, and the buyer could claim good faith. With my help, the Count signed an acknowledgment of receipt of eighty-five thousand francs, including interest, in consideration of Gobseck’s promise to return the stones.Days later the Count returned in agitation and, in fear, asked me to draw papers to transfer his estate to Gobseck as a simulated sale, Gobseck to hold it in trust and later restore it to the eldest son. He feared his son’s favoritism for his mother and distrusted the boy’s future loyalty. I warned that such a counter-deed risked disinheriting other children; he wept and agreed to provide for them in the instrument. I believed it a desperate but just precaution.The deed was signed but not delivered to me. Months later, Gobseck, dining at my house, hinted the Count was dying and that he and I were the only ones trusted by him. I resolved to visit the Count, fearing for the counter-deed. At the Count’s lodging I found the Countess playing with her children, pale and anxious. She refused to let me see her husband and feigned devotion so perfectly some thought her penitent. She watched the death-chamber from an adjoining salon, a vigilant, embittered guardian of her children’s future, questioning little Ernest about what he had heard. Her motives were mixed: maternal anxiety, fear of destitution, repentance for past wrongs, and a calculating will to recover the family fortune.Suspecting the Countess of seeking the counter-deed, I took precautions. I sued the Count for a sum ostensibly due to Gobseck so I could, on his death, attach the estate and secure the deed; I bribed a servant to tell me when the Count was gravely ill. My plan was to intervene quickly, threaten to affix seals, and secure the document before the Countess could act. Money governed these manoeuvres at the bedside.For two months the Count lay prostrate, living in squalor he seemed to prefer. He hated daylight, rejected nurses, and reveled in discomfort. One morning he summoned Ernest and, in a moment of lucidity, entrusted the boy with a sealed packet addressed to me, begging him to carry it to the post and to keep the secret even from his mother. Ernest promised.When Ernest returned to his mother, she drew him to her knee and pressed him until he refused to repeat what his father had said. Furious, the Count confronted her. The scene was brutal: he accused her of filling his life with trouble and trying to corrupt their son; she begged for mercy and vowed to expiate her sins if the children could be spared. The Count, exhausted of affection, called her a bad wife and mother; she fainted. Priests administered the sacraments. At midnight he died.Gobseck and I arrived soon after. The house was in chaos. In the death-chamber and adjacent salon the Countess had been ransacking drawers and trunks; her search had been violent—mattress overturned, drawers emptied, papers and furniture strewn. A paper bearing the Count’s arms lay at her feet; she had, in panic, thrust some documents into the grate and was partially burning them when we entered. I snatched an addressed paper from the hearth: it was to me. Confronted, the Countess was near collapse, torn between shame and fear. Gobseck, cold and triumphant, reminded her—without mercy—that he was the legal owner of what her husband had sold. He asked if I would judge him; I could not deny that under the law he had rights. I left her beside her husband’s corpse while Gobseck declared himself trustee of the estate.From then on Gobseck managed the property with the hard efficiency of a usurer turned landowner—leasing the mansion, spending summers on the estates, repairing mills, planting timber. He became, in all but name, lord of the manor, showing no softness. When I suggested he help young Ernest, he scoffed: adversity is the best teacher; let the youth fend for himself. My attempts to move him to honor the Count’s intentions and provide for the children met the old man’s icy thrift.Time passed. Gobseck grew feebler but retained his iron will. He kept his rooms exactly as always and received clients even from his bed. During financial reorganizations after France recognized the Haitian Republic, his knowledge of claims and fortunes made him influential; he devised agencies, took slices of large transactions, and accepted gifts—plates, snuff-boxes, game—without ever parting cash. He was as thin as ever, his avarice concentrated to a point.Then Gobseck’s death came. Summoned to his bedside by his old porter, I found him kneeling by the grate, muttering bequests and naming people—half delirious, half lucid. He died with a final enumeration of riches. At the hearth, amid cinders, I dug and found metal: coins, receipts, tucked papers—proof of the vast hoard he had amassed. I ordered the justice of the peace to seal everything at once.The rooms revealed the scale and eccentricity of his thrift: one chamber reeked of putrid pies and moldy fish among chests of tea and coffee; others were packed with furniture, silver-plate, vases, pictures, books, and curios—many likely presents or pledged goods. Jewel-cases bore ciphers and coats of arms; fine linen and weapons lay in confusion. Every item had been argued over, bartered, haggled for. Some bargains remained unsettled; dealers who had expected presents or returns balked at Gobseck’s stingy terms. The stench and clutter bespoke a miser’s final degradation.There was talk of Gobseck’s intended bequests: he had given an address for a supposed heiress, a woman called “La Torpille” in the Rue Vivienne; I foresaw the absurdity of pouring an immense fortune into such a character’s hands. But more immediate was the question of the Restaud estate: the Count’s counter-deed, the burned fragments, the tormented Countess, and young Ernest’s uncertain inheritance. Despite Gobseck’s harshness, Ernest would, in time, inherit a fortune sufficient to marry Mlle. Camille and to provide for his mother and siblings—assuming the legal knots could be untangled and the provisions enforced.Thus closed the arc of transactions, betrayal, and legal scrimmage: Gobseck’s cold mastery of money had altered an aristocratic family’s fate; the Count’s plans were thwarted by rage and sickness; the Countess’s frantic attempts to save her children had degenerated into crime; and a miser’s death revealed treasures amid squalor. The outcome promised both restitution and new trials, as the rightful heirs would have to navigate tangled transfers and the old usurer’s secreted wealth.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Monsieur de Manerville, a respected Norman who married a wealthy Bordeaux heiress, became attached to her Gascon estate, the château de Lanstrac. He avoided the Revolution by going to Martinique in 1790 and returned to find Lanstrac well managed by Mathias, a progressive notary’s clerk. After Madame de Manerville’s death in 1810, the old man grew miserly and deprived his only son, Paul, of resources.Paul returned from college in 1810 and endured three years of strict paternal control that stifled his character. Though physically brave, he lacked the will to oppose his father and so lost moral courage; his repressed feelings made him timid in society. Confined by the old mansion, he failed to connect with peers and felt out of place at his father’s royalist gatherings among the remnants of nobility, where he was bored and isolated.To escape monotony, Paul practiced fencing and riding—accomplishments his father insisted a gentleman possess—and read novels. His father’s death finally freed him: he inherited substantial estates and well-kept property but retained an aversion to Bordeaux and Lanstrac, scenes of his constrained youth. Seeking a new life, Paul invested in consols and traveled across Europe, serving at several embassies. Years of indulgence and poor management depleted much of his fortune, and he resolved to return to Bordeaux to settle as a country gentleman. Conscious of rank, he felt compelled to marry well, believing his foreign experience had refined him for a successful match.Despite extravagance, Paul had harmed no woman and kept a reputation for integrity; his household remained tasteful. He told his friend de Marsay of his wish to marry. De Marsay warned that marriage brings ridicule, responsibility, and loss of individuality. Paul replied that he sought emotional companionship, not social advantage, and was ready for family duties; de Marsay remained skeptical.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite earlier doubts, the Comte de Manerville returned to Bordeaux in the winter of 1821 resolved to carry out his plans. Restoring and furnishing the family mansion enhanced his reputation for elegance, and his Parisian manners quickly secured him a leading place in Bordeaux’s royalist society. An old marquise called him “the pink of fashion,” a phrase liberals satirized and royalists admired.Paul de Manerville enjoyed the attention. Charming, witty, and attentive to women, he combined a touch of foppery with genuine concern for his appearance. Of medium height, healthy complexion, and graceful movements, he preferred receiving happiness to bestowing it and sought the understanding and encouragement he believed marriage would provide. Young and averse to mundane pursuits, he nonetheless accepted that provincial life required matrimony and domestic variety. His inclination was sharpened by a secret love for Mademoiselle Evangelista.Years earlier an affluent Spaniard, Evangelista, had settled in Bordeaux and, by fortune and connections, entered noble salons. His Creole wife, from an illustrious Spanish family, maintained their social position with extravagant tastes, indulging whims regardless of cost and backed by her devoted husband. After Evangelista’s death in 1813, Madame Evangelista—now a wealthy widow with a beautiful daughter—continued living lavishly even as her fortune waned.By Paul’s return Natalie was a striking young woman, yet proposals had not come, largely because her mother’s extravagance discouraged suitors. Gossip exaggerated the situation, claiming only a prince could afford her. Accustomed to luxury and adulation, she remained largely ignorant of life beyond privilege.Six months after Paul’s arrival they met at a high-society event and found mutual charm. Madame Evangelista perceived his interest and hoped he would become her son-in-law; Paul began to picture Natalie as his future wife. He had known of the Evangelista fortune since childhood, which eased his approach. Dismissing warnings about their extravagance with Parisian disdain, he navigated provincial gossip without deterrence. Madame invited him to formal dinners, and his appetite for marriage deepened.Spending time with the Evangelistas, he felt proud of their company. Society soon treated him as Natalie’s suitor, and he relished the attention. Royalist circles called the match prudent given Natalie’s beauty and her mother’s intelligence and connections. Madame, forty and still striking, retained a reputation for virtue that enhanced her allure. Their relationship contained mutual understanding: Madame saw Paul as a means to advance socially in Paris; Paul felt flattered.A year after his return people concluded he was courting Natalie, though neither mother nor daughter actively pressed for marriage. Natalie’s charming reserve intrigued him; he rationalized that marriage would bring happiness and secure his position, brushing aside potential difficulties. Sheltered and naive, Natalie was attracted to Paul’s charm but inexperienced in married life; Paul, enamored yet cautious, wondered if he could manage someone who might not fully appreciate him.The narrative widens to note that in French society marriage is often governed by negotiations over marriage contracts—frequently overlooked in literature but crucial in shaping couples’ lives. Those impending discussions would influence Paul and Natalie’s future, foreshadowing social and personal complexities ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In winter 1822 Paul de Manerville asked for Mademoiselle Natalie Evangelista’s hand through his great-aunt, the Baronne de Maulincour, who lived in Médoc. After discussing the dowry with Madame Evangelista, the baroness reported the marriage probable: Natalie’s dowry would be her patrimony; her mother would contribute nothing. She urged Paul to consult Maître Mathias to protect his interests in the contract.Paul, anxious, visited Madame Evangelista, who proposed both families’ notaries meet but privately admitted she owed her daughter a large sum from her late husband’s estate and could not pay. Fearing Paul might withdraw and harm Natalie’s prospects, she summoned Maître Solonet, who promised they could conceal the difficulty and arrange a favorable contract.That evening Mathias and Solonet met. Mathias, cautious and traditional, sought to safeguard Paul; Solonet favored practical, flexible solutions. While Paul and Natalie exchanged tender moments at dinner, the notaries debated the financial risk: Mathias warned that Natalie's family had squandered wealth; Madame Evangelista and Solonet suggested selling her house to pay debts. Reluctantly they compromised: Madame Evangelista would place an entail on her property, protecting Natalie while limiting Paul’s exposure. The marriage could proceed, but tensions remained, foreshadowing further tests of love, ambition, and money.The next day Elie Magus, then in Bordeaux, answered Madame Evangelista’s summons expecting to discuss jewels for Comte Paul’s fiancée, Mademoiselle Natalie. Instead he appraised the mother-in-law’s possessions, valuing her necklace and earrings at 250,000 francs and the “Discreto” at 100,000 francs, warning that selling them would produce a heavy loss.That conversation ignited staff rumors and society gossip about the marriage’s stability: whispers that Madame Evangelista was selling her house and that the union might be in jeopardy. Notaries denied the claims, but public skepticism—fueled by envy of the Evangelistas’ wealth—persisted. Paul organizing a hunting party only heightened speculation. Madame de Gyas, under the pretense of concern, visited Natalie and her mother to relay the tales; they dismissed the gossip, recognizing its self-interested origins, especially from mothers eager to marry off their daughters advantageously.To counter the rumors and display her wealth, Madame Evangelista planned a grand fête—the fête of the camellias—combined with finalizing the marriage contract. As the day neared she grew uneasy about the contract’s terms, fearing her daughter’s fortune might be endangered.On the day of signing the mood was festive: guests, Natalie radiant in bridal attire, and Paul evidently enamored paid little attention to legal details. Madame Evangelista, however, concentrated on the contract. When notaries explained that if Paul died first Natalie would inherit only a fraction of her expected fortune, she was struck. Despite the personal cost, she resolved to protect Natalie’s future, transforming from careless spender into a shrewd strategist bent on reclaiming the family fortune and influencing Paul, setting the stage for future conflict.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The fête had briefly distracted Paul from his anxieties, which returned that night. He feared Madame Evangelista might have deceived him without Mathias’s intervention, but comforted himself that in two days Natalie would be his wife and their finances settled, resolving to remain watchful. Unknown to him, his future had shifted: the astute woman in his life had become an adversary, contemplating withdrawing her interests from their shared future. He failed to perceive the cunning of his Creole mother-in-law—part European intellect, part tropical passion, part Eastern indifference—awakened by the prospect of losing her daughter. With a day until the marriage, Madame Evangelista realized she could shape the couple’s life: Natalie trusted her implicitly, and a single conversation might alter their fate.The next morning, seated on Natalie’s bed, Madame Evangelista recalled their harmonious past and mourned the thought of her daughter bound to a man she might have to obey. Natalie dismissed obedience, expecting love to govern. Madame warned that conjugal love can fade if not carefully cultivated and urged Natalie to establish authority from the start. She proposed leaving to avoid complicating the new household and counseled Natalie to feign obedience while subtly asserting dominance.Their talk outlined practical marital strategies: keep an active social life to hold Paul’s interest; avoid constant proximity that breeds indifference; preserve allure and independence; and keep him entertained so the relationship stays vibrant. Overwhelmed, Natalie sought guidance. Madame promised continued contact and visits but insisted that some separation was necessary for Natalie’s happiness and prospects.When Paul arrived he tried to hide his unease; Madame noticed and offered to sell her jewelry to support the couple, framing it as a sacrifice for Natalie’s vanity and future. Paul, moved, accepted. Solonet, the notary, brought news of potential buyers for Madame Evangelista’s property, which could provide a substantial financial boost; Natalie agreed to the sale.Determined to secure her daughter’s prospects, Madame resolved to step back from their Parisian life and support them from afar so Natalie and Paul could form their own dynamic. As the midnight wedding neared, preparations carried a foreboding air and guests wondered at her absence. After the ceremony she played the grieving mother, extracted promises from Paul to secure her influence, and sent the newlyweds to Paris. Alone with Paul, Natalie began to assert her authority, setting the stage for the complex dynamics of their marriage.Five years later, in November, Comte Paul de Manerville, cloaked and somber, arrived at the home of his old friend Monsieur Mathias in Bordeaux. The retired notary was absent; his housekeeper led Paul to the late Madame Mathias’s room, where he stayed until evening. When Mathias returned and found Paul there on the anniversary of his wife's death, he showed concern and embarrassment over public notices of Paul’s financial ruin. Mathias blamed Paul’s marriage to Natalie Evangelista for his downfall, though he was relieved to have preserved the estate titles even as the usufruct was lost.Paul appeared altered but calm. Over a meal, Mathias lamented the seizure of Paul’s properties. Paul then revealed he had left Natalie without her knowledge and intended to sail to Calcutta to rebuild his fortune. Mathias, shocked that a count would enter commerce, was told Paul no longer considered himself a count and planned to succeed under another name. Paul outlined enormous debts from lavish spending and gifts to Natalie; he admitted selling assets and borrowing heavily from usurers to sustain their lifestyle, which produced the present catastrophe.Despite Mathias’s doubts and skepticism about de Marsay—who was supposed to send funds—Paul insisted India was his only chance and vowed to return wealthy by forty. The next day Mathias received de Marsay’s bill of exchange confirming some funds would arrive, but Paul remained determined to leave. Mathias lamented losing his friend as Paul boarded the ship and waved goodbye.At sea, Paul was consumed by memories and his love for Natalie, torn between regret and resolve. Struck by seasickness, he spent days in his cabin reflecting on his choices. When he recovered, he opened letters from Natalie and de Marsay. Natalie’s letter revealed anguish and a vow to support him: she pledged her jewels to pay his debts and promised to live at Lanstrac with her mother and economize. De Marsay’s letter detailed Paul’s ruin and exposed the mother-in-law’s profiteering. Confronted with love and treachery, Paul felt anger, helplessness, and remorse as he sailed away from France uncertain of the future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Railroads promise to render many contemporary industries obsolete and will change travel around Paris; elements of the Scene will one day seem relics. The once-common "coucous" of 1830 were already fading by 1842, surviving mainly at occasional suburban spectacles. In 1820 many celebrated districts still lacked regular stage-coach service, so local short-route vehicles remained vital.The Touchards, father and son, held a near-monopoly on travel within roughly forty-five miles from their base in the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis, yet they faced lively competition from local coucous on shorter routes. Parisians’ appetite for the countryside let small, familiar enterprises thrive: proprietors—often innkeepers—knew the roads, undercut prices, bent rules, and earned local loyalty. These coucous offered speed and convenience that could challenge the Touchards’ more formal coaches until omnibuses later eclipsed them by carrying more passengers more efficiently.One lucrative contested line ran from Paris to Beaumont-sur-Oise, with multiple rivals in 1822 despite Touchard efforts to cut fares and improve equipment, because profitable stops like Saint-Denis and Saint-Brice ensured demand. The Touchards even extended to Chambly while rivals reached Beauvais. By contrast, the road to Isle-Adam, prized for its quarries and picturesque terminus, was served by only two coaches; its terminus discouraged speculation, giving Pierrotin and his colleague a virtual monopoly on a route locals often preferred to the crowded Beaumont coaches.Pierrotin, in his forties, had taken over his father’s coucou after leaving the cavalry in 1815. He married an innkeeper’s daughter, enlarged the business, and combined intelligence with military precision. His dress—sturdy boots, bottle-green trousers, a blue embroidered blouse—matched his trade and hinted at the soldier’s respect for order. Yet his daily work inclined him to regard passengers as cargo.Sensing the industry’s future, Pierrotin tried to modernize: he ordered a larger coach from a respected maker; his fleet still included an inherited coucou painted yellow with blue trim—built for six but often overloaded—and a noisy four-wheeled coach that could hold seventeen though officially limited to fourteen. The four-wheeler was so recognizable villagers announced its approach. Pierrotin also paid taxes only on the coucou, using a special permit for the larger coach to skirt regulations.On a Saturday morning in autumn 1822 Pierrotin stood outside the Lion d’Argent, watching coaches depart and fretting over scarce passengers and parcels. Optimistic about the new coach but burdened by a thousand-franc debt, he considered asking Monsieur Moreau, steward at Presles, for a loan. Then a footman arrived seeking a ride for his master, the Comte de Serizy, traveling incognito after countermanding his carriage. Pierrotin seized the chance, hoping the fare would ease his debts; the footman’s insistence on secrecy and the count’s mysterious purpose heightened Pierrotin’s sense of responsibility to ensure a smooth, discreet journey.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Monsieur Huguet de Serisy descends from the eminent President Huguet, ennobled under François I; the family arms and motto link modesty to the estate’s name.The current count’s father served as president of parliament before the Revolution. The count became a councillor of State at twenty-two, noted for persuasive diplomatic memoranda. He remained at Serizy during the Revolution, protected by his father’s standing, and after his father’s death in 1794 joined the Council of Five Hundred to distract himself from grief. After 18th Brumaire he won the First Consul’s favor, rejoined the Council of State, and was charged with reorganizing a chaotic department—work that helped shape much of Napoleon’s administration. In 1806, at forty, he married the widow of a prominent Republican general, improving his fortune. By 1814 years of labor and ill health compelled his resignation; though Napoleon treated him with some mistrust, he retained rank. Louis XVIII later trusted him, appointing him a cabinet minister. During the Hundred Days he remained loyal to the Bourbons, staying at Serizy. After Napoleon’s final defeat he resumed duties as privy councillor and vice-president of the Council of State, influencing affairs without seeking acclaim. His routine—early to rise, early to bed—reflected relentless, quiet duty; he received many honors, including the grand cross of the Legion of Honor, yet his devotion carried an unspoken sorrow.He had long been passionately attached to his wife; despite strains their bond endured. Work was his refuge from heartache; he treated her with respect and granted her considerable freedom. Though she acknowledged some of his virtues, her ingratitude often wounded him; only occasionally did she soothe his pain.A pressing business matter then occupied him: a farm called Presles was encroaching on his estate. Leased by tenant Léger from a local burgher, Margueron, the lease was expiring and the property would be sold. The count intended to buy it to end disputes and instructed his notary and lawyer to negotiate quickly.His steward, Moreau, had served faithfully for seventeen years; the count’s father had saved him during the Revolution, and the count later spared him from a death sentence. Moreau asked to manage Presles and at first was diligent, but after securing the post he grew lax and corrupt, taking bribes and arranging shady deals—already striking a bargain with Léger. His conduct provoked the retired officer Monsieur de Reybert’s wrath; Madame de Reybert brought the count detailed accusations. Initially skeptical of her motives, the count’s doubts grew and he ordered his notary to accompany Madame de Reybert to Presles. What began as a straightforward purchase thus became an investigation fraught with intrigue and the threat of betrayal by trusted servants.As Pierrotin left the Café de l’Echiquier he saw Madame Clapart and her son at the Lion d’Argent searching for him. In a faded gown she was anxious yet dignified—poverty apparent but devotion clear as she entrusted eighteen-year-old Oscar, on his first trip alone, to Pierrotin’s care. Pierrotin watched with concern and pity. Oscar, in a coat and patched trousers, remained under his mother’s sway and resented her fussing over his gloves and the journey to Moreau.Pierrotin knew them from errands for Moreau, who discreetly patronized the family. Once Madame Husson, she had been impoverished by misfortunes—her first husband’s suicide—and later married Clapart, a meager clerk. Moreau aided them and secured Oscar a scholarship, but the household was strained; Clapart’s coldness worsened Oscar’s faults.Madame Clapart clung to past glories and centered her life on Oscar; her overprotection fed his vanity and entitlement. Blind to his limits, she worried Moreau, who proposed testing Oscar by placing him with the steward. As they left, two well-dressed men mocked Oscar, deepening his humiliation and desire for independence.Boarding intensified his discomfort: a shabby artist and his apprentice Mistigris mocked his self-consciousness. The Comte de Serizy, travelling incognito, changed the coach’s tone; Pierrotin rearranged seats to oblige him, drawing complaints. When the coach set off it presented a cross-section—proud mothers, insecure youths, discreet patrons, bohemians—while Oscar’s struggle with identity and self-worth simmered, setting the journey’s stage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Those who travel by public conveyance usually begin in silence until the most talkative passenger breaks it. National types are sketched briefly: English taciturn, Germans somber, Italians cautious, Spaniards seldom using public transport, Russians hampered by poor roads; France, by contrast, is lively, especially among the lower classes and bourgeois.Georges, a clever, lively young man, quickly sized up his fellow passengers, deemed himself superior, and resolved to amuse himself at their expense. As the coach descended from La Chapelle to Saint-Denis he toyed with identities, finally posing as a disguised Russian prince or a soldier returned from the East. When Mistigris complained about dust, Georges joked and steered conversation toward travels in the Levant, claiming service under Ali Pacha of Janina and inventing a plague scare that alarmed the count enough to have the driver stop. He also asserted he’d fought in the 1813 campaign and at Waterloo, where he was promoted to captain.When the count inquired about decorations, Georges said he did not wear them while traveling; the count produced his own honors, and Georges professed admiration for Napoleon, spinning further tales—of Egypt and a harem supposedly presented to him by Ali Tebelen, though he dismissed harem life as uninteresting. At Saint-Denis the party disembarked at a tavern famed for its cheese-cakes. There the count, growing suspicious, discovered a portfolio that identified Georges as Crottat’s second clerk, quickening doubts about his identity and motives.Despite this exposure, Georges continued to impress—especially Père Léger and Oscar—by paying for treats and ordering drinks, cultivating the pose of an important man. Mistigris, a painter, introduced himself modestly; the count watched the group dynamics, noting Georges’s effort to buy admiration.As the journey resumed, Georges spun more accounts of Eastern oddities: lax policing, strange farming, and heavy taxation under the pacha that left peasants barely surviving. His storytelling culminated in a dramatic Dalmatian romance—Zena, a jealous pirate husband, poisoning, and a narrow escape from execution—that captivated the travelers. Mistigris added humorous anecdotes; laughter, banter, and drink warmed the coach. Georges’s bravado won admiration even as the count stayed alert to contradictions. The journey became a weave of tales, camaraderie, and intermittent skepticism.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pierrotin’s coach stopped at Saint-Brice about half past one to rest the horses; the innkeeper greeted the travelers by name and they disembarked for a brief halt. Georges, ostentatious and boastful, called for a hearty breakfast and twirled his cane. Père Léger, the stout farmer, agreed to “break a crust.” Georges produced a straw case, lit a pale cigar, and offered cigars to Oscar and Schinner. Oscar, flattered then awkward, puffed ineptly and became the butt of teasing: Schinner smoked easily, Georges fumbled, and Mistigris joked about Oscar’s discomfort. Oscar watched Georges’s largesse with thinly veiled resentment, tallying the bills he suspected Georges was burning through.Georges proposed a bottle of Bordeaux with Père Léger, and the innkeeper supplied local gossip. He’d heard François, conductor of Touchard’s coach, say Monsieur Margueron had been invited by the Comte de Serizy to dine at Presles that day. Père Léger laughed and outlined his plan to preempt such patronage: he intended to buy Les Moulineaux cheaply by using newly minted coins and snapping up the farm before the count could act. The innkeeper mused about lending twenty thousand and calculated returns; Les Moulineaux, he said, produced six thousand francs—a sound investment. Père Léger explained he’d made Moreau a partner and, taking advantage of royal favor and the pictures on coin, had secured the estate. The scheme sounded shrewd even if the count tried to intervene.The count—listening—grew uneasy about discretion. When Pierrotin stepped out to water the horses, the count drew him aside and reminded him of a bribe: ten louis to keep the matter secret. Then he raised the inducement dramatically: if Pierrotin would conceal his name, the count would give him a thousand francs the next day for a new coach. He instructed him not to enter the inn, not to speak of the incident, and to stay with the horses. Pierrotin returned curt reassurance and hurried his customers on, insisting he must deliver fish by three to a big dinner in Stors.Père Léger suggested harnessing the horse Pierrotin meant to sell into the cabriolet so they could breakfast calmly and overtake Pierrotin. To everyone’s surprise, Pierrotin rebridled and set off; Schinner and Mistigris walked. Georges kept up talk about the hostess and the landscape, joking about leftover cigars. Oscar, stung by being called “the little young man,” defended his dignity: he was not Mina’s aide-de-camp, had not been to the East, and his family intended him for diplomacy. This invited ridicule: his companions mocked his pretensions, tutors, and “household” language—Oscar proudly noting his mother ran their household—and taunted his social standing and vocabulary.They goaded him into naming his tutor; Oscar named the Abbé Loraux, vicar of Saint-Sulpice, and hinted at family influence that might help the abbé’s ecclesiastical rise. Pressed for acquaintance with Abbé Frayssinous, Oscar said the abbé owed favors to his father. Then, unexpectedly, Oscar declared he was bound for the Château de Presles to visit the Comte de Serizy and claimed intimacy with the count’s son. His confident utterances about the Serizys—outlined to the small party—caught the count’s attention.The count seized the chance to question Oscar, explaining he sought information on Monsieur de Serizy to support a claim he intended to press with the American government. Oscar counseled him to approach the count’s wife rather than the count himself: the count, he said, was madly in love with her, while she secretly despised him. He described the count’s disfiguring skin disease and the humiliating remedies—sulphur and steam baths, even an iron box—portraying a man reduced and ashamed; in Oscar’s telling, such afflictions left the count vulnerable at home. He recommended instead seeking the Marquis d’Aiglemont, an old admirer of Madame de Serizy, for influence.The young man’s ease in speaking of intimate things shocked the company. Schinner scolded him for airing friends’ affairs in public; Mistigris professed indifference to tale-bearing. Georges preached a favored maxim: in a public coach one should know how to talk without saying anything. When the conversation turned to the Comtesse de Serizy, the count interrupted sharply and forbade any disparagement. Asserting his friendship with her brother, the Marquis de Ronquerolles, he vowed that speaking ill of the countess would not go unpunished. The painter agreed: no man should blague (mock) women. The count hinted at influence—knowing ministers, blocking unworthy men from decorations, and even acquaintanceship with the architect of Presles—and asked Pierrotin to stop at the next inn so he could step out.Once they were clear of the others, the count had Pierrotin show him aside in the inn-yard and asked about the talkative young man. Pierrotin explained the boy was the son of a poor woman in the rue de la Cerisaie, Madame Husson, whom he often supplied with fruit and game from Presles. Pierrotin had driven the youngster only once and thought he might be the prince of Maffliers; the boy had told him to leave him near Maffliers, not to go on to Isle-Adam. The count was reassured by this account. When he rejoined the party, Père Léger and the others exchanged awkward glances, feeling exposed; the three young men shared a guilty silence. Mistigris quipped about suffering for loose talk, and Georges reminded Oscar that public conveyances required speaking artfully.They rode on into the Carreau woods in heavy silence; the count noted their quiet as if they had been led to the scaffold. Mistigris muttered a proverb; Georges remarked on the fine weather. Oscar, striving to assert himself, declared he was Oscar Husson and would be famous within ten years. The companions teased him further, and the count refined his name to Husson de la Cerisaie, giving Oscar’s modest boast a veneer of dignity. The coach descended toward the grand château of Presles.Before disembarking, the count delivered formal, almost theatrical farewells: the colonel should make peace with the king; Schinner’s fame was assured; Oscar, the budding diplomat, needed no protection, since he possessed “the secrets of statesmen;” Père Léger was urged to pluck the Comte de Serizy and to do so firmly. Asking Pierrotin to set and fetch him at the same spot the next day, the count left the coach to the others’ bewildered speculation about his identity. Père Léger guessed he was the count who’d leased Franconville; Schinner swore never to blague again in a public coach. Mistigris, unrepentant, shrugged with a proverb.As they neared the château gate, Georges warned Oscar that if the man indeed had been the Comte de Serizy, Oscar should be wary—a chill reminder of his mother’s warnings. Pierrotin called out: they had arrived at Presles. Some of the young men feigned familiarity, but Georges, not wishing to reveal his destination, pretended he would continue to Les Moulineaux. Père Léger then triumphantly disclosed that he was the farmer at Les Moulineaux, forcing Georges to admit it was so.Oscar asked Pierrotin to leave his belongings at the steward’s; he intended to go straight to the château. He plunged forward along a narrow path, uncertain where to go, and Père Léger corrected him: the little gate, not the forest path, led to the château. Guided thus, Oscar entered the courtyard. While Père Léger watched, Georges slipped away upon realizing Père Léger truly possessed Les Moulineaux. Pierrotin, having obeyed the count’s injunction to keep the matter secret for now, drove in with swagger, deposited the painter’s packages and Oscar’s bundles, and handed a small leather portmanteau to the concierge’s wife. Mistigris and the painter took rooms in the château itself—a discovery that stunned Oscar.The scene closed on Pierrotin’s contentment: he had kept the count’s secret and now looked forward to the promised reward. With a sly peasant’s calculation, he anticipated receiving a thousand francs the next day and buying the splendid new coach he long desired. The coach rolled on, the passengers dispersed through the estate, and the social ambitions, pretensions, and private bargains that had surfaced in their talk settled back into the polite surface of conversation, continuing to move unseen beneath it.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Oscar hid behind trees and watched Monsieur Moreau emerge from the guard-room in a blue overcoat, carrying a riding whip. Moreau greeted the painters Mistigris and his master—sent by architect Monsieur Grindot—told the concierge to show them to their rooms, invited them to dinner at five, and mentioned hunting on the estate.As Moreau led Oscar through the park, he sent his son Jacques ahead to tell Madame Moreau of their arrival. Stern in appearance but kindly, he encouraged Oscar to learn to ride and hunt despite his inexperience. Oscar’s mother had allowed only a two-week stay because Madame Moreau was present, yet Moreau seemed intent on extending it.Their steward’s house, built before the Revolution and attached to the chateau, stood in a large garden and contained a salon, dining room, and a modern boudoir furnished to Madame Moreau’s taste. The Moreaus lived comfortably on estate provisions. Madame Moreau, once a maid, kept an air of elegance and worked to be treated as the chateau’s mistress, often patronizing villagers. Tension with the Reyberts dated to a past slight when Madame de Reybert reminded her of her former status. Eager to impress, Madame Moreau hoped the visiting artist would raise her social standing.On the day the painters were due, Madame Moreau dressed elegantly, but the Paris coach brought only Mistigris’s packages, leaving her disappointed. When Mistigris and Bridau arrived, she welcomed them and professed a love of art; the two artists quickly recognized her pretentiousness and decided to humor her with polite conversation about art.Their talk was cut short when a maid announced the count’s arrival, and Madame Moreau grew flustered. Oscar, feeling out of place, was fetched by his younger brother and scolded for his appearance; Mistigris’s joking remark about Oscar’s future increased the awkwardness. The maid then said the count wanted dinner promptly.The count had come unexpectedly and was upset to learn Moreau was absent. Having overheard remarks that suggested Moreau’s disloyalty, he confronted him, angered that private matters had been discussed—especially in front of Oscar. Moreau’s attempts to explain failed; he returned home distraught over the loss of his position and the damage caused by Oscar’s careless words. Estelle, his wife, was furious and grasped the gravity of their predicament. Moreau demanded an explanation from Oscar, who remained silent from fear and shame. In anger Moreau dragged him back to the count to apologize; the count dismissed Oscar’s behavior and doubted his promise. Humiliated, Moreau took Oscar home to face uncertain consequences for their family.Moreau wrote to Madame Clapart, distraught that Oscar had revealed Madame de Serizy’s secrets to an incognito count; the count sent Oscar home, costing Moreau the stewardship at Presles and imperiling the family’s prospects. At the Clapart home Monsieur fretted about Oscar’s character while Madame defended him. When Brochon returned with Oscar and Moreau’s letter, Madame Clapart fainted at the thought of her son’s future.The next day she found Oscar sullen, reminded him of Moreau’s past support and their precarious position without the stewardship, and urged discretion, hard work, and prudence; he remained largely indifferent. As a last hope she appealed to Uncle Cardot, a self-made, modest businessman who generously supported his children and had been unaware of their plight. Cardot received them kindly, praised Oscar’s promise, and—moved by Madame Clapart—offered to fund his legal studies, insisting on integrity, discipline, and effort.Madame Clapart felt renewed hope. Moreau later visited, saying he had begun speculating in land and could place Oscar as a pupil in a law office; he reiterated that disciplined study was essential to repair Oscar’s reputation and secure his future, and Madame Clapart urged Oscar to show gratitude by heeding these admonitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Ten days later Monsieur Moreau brought Oscar to Maître Desroches, a young solicitor in the rue de Béthisy who had recently opened a roomy but inexpensive office. Desroches, twenty-six and raised in poverty, recognized Oscar’s situation; the boy’s gaunt look and curt manners intimidated him. “We work here day and night,” Desroches declared amid a mountain of papers, and he put his head clerk, Monsieur Godeschal, in charge of Oscar’s training so the youth would not lose time on the way to law school and would master the Code. Oscar was lodged in a small attic beside Godeschal’s room; Desroches plainly intended to make him a capable clerk.For two years Oscar lived at the office under a regimen of exacting, petty work. Under Godeschal’s strict oversight he kept a monk-like schedule: rising at five to prepare for the day, running errands, studying the Code under rigorous tests, returning to office tasks after classes, and subsisting on plain meals. His social life was minimal — monthly breakfasts with his uncle Cardot and Sundays with his mother — interrupted only by occasional outings Moreau could arrange. His efforts at elegance met the disapproval of his superiors.Godeschal counseled a modest wardrobe and frugal living — insisting a good clerk should not spend more than seven hundred francs a year — and lived by those principles. He embodied honest, unostentatious leadership: when a colleague failed his duties, Godeschal discreetly guided him to resign rather than face public dismissal, showing practical humanity.By twenty, Oscar had become the third clerk, doing the second’s duties without pay but receiving room and board. His legal knowledge surpassed that of many licensed graduates, and he even began arguing cases in chambers. Yet he felt constrained by a life at odds with his desires: he envied the well-dressed young men he saw on the streets and yearned for freedom and pleasure, feelings he habitually suppressed. Godeschal, aware of these struggles, tried to shield him and sometimes covered the cost of outings to give him some enjoyment; Moreau supported him but could not connect as Godeschal did. Both feared youthful follies that might imperil his future, though they hoped to steer him aright.“How is he getting on?” Moreau asked. “Too much vanity,” Godeschal replied, noting Oscar’s taste for fine clothes and suggesting his talents might suit a barrister rather than a solicitor’s clerkship.In November 1825 a new clerk, Frédéric Marest, arrived. Well-to-do and aiming for the magistracy, Marest claimed a desire for hands-on practice. The clerks enjoyed practical jokes: Godeschal kept a fictitious register of receptions for new clerks filled with absurd entries. When Oscar asked for it, Marest read and laughed, then said he would consult a cousin who was a head clerk about the welcome he should give — a remark that convinced Godeschal of Marest’s inexperience. Despite the office’s camaraderie, Oscar’s inner conflict persisted as he navigated professional pressures and youthful expectations.The next day Georges Marest, now head-clerk to notary Hannequin, greeted Oscar and recalled their past mischief that had led to Georges’ transfer. Georges invited Oscar and the other clerks to the Rocher de Cancale and to an evening with Madame la Marquise de las Florentinas y Cabirolos, promising high society. Desroches, head of the office, interrupted to assign Oscar the task of fetching a crucial legal document from the Palais and warned him to beware of Derville, who might obstruct the errand. Eager to prove himself as second clerk, Oscar accepted.After Georges and Oscar left, Godeschal tried to learn more about the Marquise, but Frédéric maintained a ruse, calling her a wealthy widow from Mexico. The clerks were excited by the promised breakfast and festivities. In truth the Marquise was Mademoiselle Agathe-Florentine Cabirolle, a celebrated danseuse at the Gaîté, supported by Père Cardot, a merchant who had taken her and her mother in, financed her career, and provided a comfortable life while she prepared for a possible engagement at the Opera.With new means, Georges actively pursued Florentine. The clerks dressed in their best; Oscar’s mother, Madame Clapart, voiced hopes for his future and warned him about Georges, whom she blamed for Oscar’s earlier misfortune. On the day of the gathering Oscar felt excitement mixed with apprehension. After a lavish breakfast the intoxicated clerks went to Florentine’s salon and found opulence. Introduced to her, Oscar was charmed and joined a game of vingt-et-un with Florentine and the actress Fanny Beaupré, quickly losing the money he’d been given for the legal document. Florentine offered him a thousand francs, which he accepted—but he lost everything, fell into despair and stupor, and slept in the boudoir while the party continued into the early morning.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning a loud noise woke clerk Oscar Husson. His uncle Cardot scolded him for late-night revelry and the disorderly apartment; Florentine, a danseuse, laughed, revealed Oscar as Cardot’s nephew and urged the uncle to help. Oscar had lost gambling money belonging to his employer, Monsieur Desroches, and ashamed, begged Cardot for aid. Cardot reluctantly lent five hundred francs, warning it would be the last help.Mariette sent a note and money; Godeschal, another clerk, hurried to get the necessary documents for Desroches. Angered, Desroches resolved to dismiss Oscar despite Godeschal’s defense. Godeschal recounted Oscar’s past mistakes and blamed the bad influence of Georges Marest; Desroches half-joked that Oscar might try law, thinking his vanity suited the bar.At home, Clapart tormented his wife with reminders of their son’s failures; Madame Clapart defended Oscar. When Oscar returned he confronted his father, asserting independence, but was ignored. Determined to take responsibility, Oscar faced conscription. Clapart believed military service would instill discipline. Oscar drew a number and, despite his fears, hoped for a favorable outcome. Three days later, through the Comte de Serizy’s influence, Oscar was drafted into the cavalry.Overwhelmed by her son’s misfortunes, Madame Clapart turned to religion. In the army Oscar applied himself and eventually rose to lieutenant-colonel.Years later a decorated Oscar stood with his mother outside the Lion d’Argent, waiting for a coach. Both were altered from their former selves; though haunted by past mistakes, Oscar had achieved success. They encountered Georges Marest, now a shadow of his former self, who failed to recognize him. On the journey, stories of transformed lives circulated; military service had reshaped Oscar, taught hard lessons, steadied his character. He married Georgette Pierrotin, the coach proprietor’s daughter, and his earlier errors became instructive lessons that, with connections and discipline, won him respect and security.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONBalzac dedicates the book to a Polish lady of contrasts—childlike in faith, aged by sorrow, inspiring through love—whose inner poetry he feels unworthy to match but attempts to echo modestly here.CHAPTER I. THE CHALETIn October 1829 the notary Monsieur Simon Babylas Latournelle walks from Le Havre to Ingouville with his wife, son Exupère, and his head clerk Jean Butscha to visit the Mignons. Latournelle coaches Exupère in a ruse: after greeting Mademoiselle Modeste Mignon, Exupère will slip aside to tell Monsieur Dumay “the young man has come,” a signal meant to stir romantic interest. Exupère is bound for Paris to study law; his vain mother and solemn father—notable for green goggles—accompany him. Butscha, a hunchback who adores Modeste, completes the party.Ingouville, a picturesque suburb where merchants escape the city, contains the Chalet, the Mignons’ cottage with a garden and cherished hot-house. Jean François Bernard Dumay, the family’s devoted cashier, holds the lease; the new proprietor, Monsieur Vilquin, resents this and offers money to vacate. Dumay, fiercely loyal, refuses—determined above all to protect Modeste from suitors.CHAPTER II. A PORTRAIT FROM LIFEThe Latournelles find Gobenheim, a pale, serious banker, already visiting; his attentions are worldly rather than affectionate. The household is taut: Dumay prepares to confront any man who might compromise Modeste’s honor. Blind Madame Mignon remains the dignified center while Modeste, embroidering, becomes the focus of unspoken anxieties. Dumay’s vigilance promises an evening that will test Modeste’s feelings.CHAPTER III. PRELIMINARIESOnce a soldier, Dumay became the Mignons’ devoted cashier after their misfortunes. His fidelity to the exiled Colonel Mignon never falters. Dumay’s childless wife treats Modeste as her own; their devotion shapes the household’s protective tone.CHAPTER IV. A SIMPLE STORYA business collapse and the Colonel’s exile to Siberia reduced the family to the Chalet. Dumay and Latournelle work to settle debts; blind Madame Mignon, weakened by grief, depends on them. Modeste’s innocence and growing beauty alarm Dumay, who enforces strict guardianship to preserve her reputation amid poverty.CHAPTER V. THE PROBLEM STILL UNSOLVEDModeste’s attractiveness fuels speculation; the women watch for signs of love, fearing exploitation. Dumay vows no man will approach without his knowledge.CHAPTER VI. A MAIDEN’S FIRST ROMANCEShaped by books and her sister’s tragedy, Modeste lives in imagination and longs to be a poet’s muse. She writes to the poet Canalis, pouring out her heart and seeking connection.CHAPTER VII. A POET OF THE ANGELIC SCHOOLCanalis reads Modeste’s letter with amusement and condescension, recognizing her innocence and wary of being misconstrued. His flattering but cautious reply warns against romanticizing the poet’s life. Disappointed yet determined, Modeste decides to press the correspondence, setting the stage for conflict between her hopes and social limits.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################CHAPTER VIII. BLADE TO BLADETo Monsieur de Canalis:Monsieur, you are a great poet and an honest man. After your frankness to a young girl on the brink of despair, answer me truthfully: would you have replied the same had you known Mademoiselle O. d’Este M. has six million francs and seeks a fool for a husband?Answer frankly. I am wiser than my years; nothing candid can harm you. If you speak openly, I will reply to your first letter.Having admired your talent, I must also praise your integrity. Your humble servant, O. d’Este M.Ernest de La Brière read this and walked the boulevards, conscience and curiosity at war. Parisians might see flirtation; a man of honor felt judged by Truth, Justice, and Honor. Truth accused him of hypocrisy; Justice defended the girl's revolt against mercenary marriage; Honor demanded sincerity. He delayed, then wrote:Mademoiselle, you are not a D’Este. The name is a disguise. Do I owe truth to someone who lies about herself? Are you noble or of the people? Ethics remain, but obligations vary with rank. A daughter of Este with six million may candidly confess a fondness for poetry; a girl of the people would be mocked. What class are you? Answer plainly, and I will reply to your question.Modeste answered:To Monsieur de Canalis, you grow sensible, my dear poet. My father is a count; we have a cardinal among us, and I am the last of our name. We are equal to the Canalis. You will excuse my refusing to send our arms. Answer me as truthfully as I have answered you. I await your response, to know if I may sign myself, Your servant, O. d’Este M.La Brière smiled at her mischief. Four years in Paris had taught him intrigue. He booked a seat to Havre, blamed delay on duties, and obtained a note from the post-office director to ensure secrecy. He watched Francoise Cochet collect letters and followed her discreetly to Ingouville, where he glimpsed Modeste at a chalet window. Struck by her beauty, he learned the estate belonged to Monsieur Vilquin, the richest merchant in Havre. The Vilquins were immensely wealthy; one daughter was married to M. Althor, and rumors hinted at fashionable visitors and a possible match with a young duke.Having seen enough to dream, La Brière resumed the correspondence, though he regretted the reconnaissance. Parisian curiosity seldom rests. He wrote a letter shaped by conscience and passion; Modeste read it at her window on a fine summer day.Mademoiselle, without hypocrisy: had I known your wealth I would have acted differently. Men often confuse happiness with means; money becomes a religion. Faced with such a prize, a young man may pursue it for social gain and flatter, perhaps losing esteem for you and earning yours for me. Honesty compels me to say this.Your letter forced deep reflection—on you and on myself. You stirred base thoughts, from which something generous emerged. I went to Havre, followed Francoise to Ingouville; you are as beautiful as a poet’s dream. I blush at such spying and stopped. You have altered my opinion: you are not merely a woman but a poetic Ideal. Exceptional souls may transcend the crowd’s laws. If your letter sprang from lofty reveries and the wish for friendship beyond ordinary relations, you are an exception.Accept my thanks for forcing me to sound my heart. You corrected my false idea that marriage should be for fortune. I swore to make my own fortune and not choose a wife from cupidity. I reproach myself for the curiosity you provoked. You are not worth six million; such a fortune could not be hidden in Havre. I now fix my rule: seek a partner made by God; do not marry a dunce. If I were rich and you poor, I would lay my heart and fortune at your feet; you seem loyal and rich in soul.Modeste felt delight at touching a noble soul, joy that worth might still meet genius, and triumph at following her instinct. She altered her concealment, giving Francoise envelopes of her own so letters could be slipped back into the box, and resolved to meet the postman herself.Ernest de La Brière received another letter five days later that revealed more than any analysis.CHAPTER IX. THE POWER OF THE UNSEENTo Monsieur de Canalis:My friend, I call you that; you have delighted me. I would not change your image from your first letter—oh, may it not be the last! Who but a poet could so delicately excuse and understand a girl on the brink?I admit with joy I am not the Vilquin nor the withered d’Herouville; my ancestors are respectable and my precautions stand—I will remain veiled. As to my person and belongings, be easy: I am at least as beautiful as the girl whose light you saw, and not poverty-stricken. I have seen the humiliating comedy of heiresses hunted; make no attempt to find me. I am free yet watched—by myself and by resolute people who would not hesitate to stab a man who threatened my retreat. I do not say this to quicken your courage; I merely warn you.Now to your sermon. When I read your distrust, I thought the Muses may have led you astray. You are safe with me: I love poetry but do not affect hypocritical airs. Should I ever bid you come, I promise no old maid or poor wretch will appear. I regret that you came to Havre; you lowered the charm of the secret meeting I dreamed—God alone knew the treasure I reserved for a man noble enough to come. You spoiled it, but I forgive you; Paris breeds a man in every poet.Do you think me gullible? When your first letter reached me I did not toss it aside; my net was carefully cast by a fisherman expecting a miraculous catch. You have my approval in what you say about family. The man who pleases me will have my heart with my parents’ consent; I will neither grieve nor surprise them. I am guarded by devotion and disdain for calculating motives. I worship the ideal without being romantic; but I know social truths.For now we must be friends. Your person may be unknown, but your mind and heart please me; an infinity of thoughts in me needs a man of genius to share them. I do not want my poem wasted; I want it known to you as to God. A true comrade’s confidences are precious; accept these leaflets of a young girl’s thoughts. Later, if our souls match, some white-haired servant may guide you to whatever dwelling fate holds for us—but not now. Come to me when you are unhappy; I have balms for your ills. I am twenty but feel fifty; I have known through another the horrors and delights of love and have no illusions but a religion and beliefs. Whoever I marry—if I choose him—will find me faithful and constant. I will not follow my husband blindly; I will be the divinity of his hearth. But I must test and choose the man who will be life’s companion.Your letter was precious. Yes, it held necessary truths for family life. Act as an honest man, think as a poet, love as women love—that is what I long for. Adieu, my friend. I am poor at present; that is why I hide. I have read your verses in the Revue with delight now that I know your inner loftiness. Will it grieve you that a young girl prays for you, that you are her solitary thought? Send me the counterpart of these pages; your confidences—if full and true—will make me happy.O. d’Este M.La Brière, surprised at his own absorption, wondered if he was already in love. She thought him the great poet; could he sustain the deception? The charm of the unseen—the obscurity that stirs fancy—became an obsession. He answered with a studied letter where passion, pride, and caution mingled.Mademoiselle,—Is it loyal of you to enthrone yourself in a poor poet’s heart while reserving the right to abandon him if he disappoints? Your confidence leaves little of yourself but much charm. I offer humble remonstrances.Do you believe letters—brief, momentary expressions—can replace knowledge gained by daily life? Man is dual: an invisible life of heart and a material life. Letters may suffice for spirit, but cannot reconcile both. A solitary, educated, chaste homage consoles literary men, and I thank you as one equal might thank another.But what next? I am neither Byron nor a colossus. I might have vanity, discordant character, and ambition; I need a career. I have modest means and the external bearing of an ordinary man. I can be neither martyr nor hero. Your project is perilous: if you make yourself the handmaid of a man of genius and worship him till death, you may live forever in his glory; if you attach that ideal to me now, my modest gifts may disappoint you. If we continue, I might love you wildly—but if I pleased you, the tale would likely end in the common fashion: marriage, house, children. Therefore, adieu.CHAPTER X. THE MARRIAGE OF SOULSTo Monsieur de Canalis:My friend, your letter gives me both pain and pleasure; perhaps someday all will be pleasure. I want the soul’s answers you seek from God. Cannot the friendship of Mademoiselle de Gournay and Montaigne be revived in us? Remember Sismondi’s household or the Marquis de Pescaire’s; can two hearts vibrate in unison across distance? Man is harp, musician, and listener. Will you find me jealous like ordinary women? I know you go into the world; I know your beauties and wits. Yet I seek a flower that blooms on Alpine peaks—men of genius—and I wish to cultivate it. I am no commonplacid creature; I would not belong to a ninny or merchant who could ruin us. My father adores my wishes and will not oppose them. If I please my poet and he pleases me, we will build love high and secure.I am an eaglet; I want to be imprisoned by love as I am now by my father’s will. Listen: a young imagination locked in a tower longs to run in its neighbor’s park—your genius. Parents arrange marriages by appearances; a young girl is often denied choice. I want a long marriage of souls before any legal pledge. My mother followed instinct and beauty and prospered; I follow mine. I will not bind us prematurely to vulgar forms. If our correspondence reveals true sympathy, we may be spared common follies and reach graveside hand in hand. I await your orders, monseigneur, and am with all my heart,Your handmaiden, O. d’Este M.To Mademoiselle O. d’Este M.—You are a witch and I love you! Is that what you desire, most original girl? Perhaps you amuse provincial leisure with a folly to make a poet act rashly. If so, you have done wrong. Your letters have mischief. But I am no longer master of myself; my life depends on your answer. If a certainty of unbounded affection that disregards social conventions touches you—if you will let me seek you—say so. If your reply is favorable I change my life; I shall renounce the trivial pleasures we call happiness. For me, happiness is a fusion of feeling, a harmony of souls, an imprint of a noble ideal on daily life—a constancy of heart more precious than fidelity. We may call such devotion sacrifice, but is it not the dream of poets and maidens? I cannot speak more of myself—my past nor my filial affection, which you have altered. Say one word and I will love you until death. Either write no more or give me hope. Let me see you, or let me go. Must I bid you adieu? Will you permit me to sign myself,Your Friend?To Monsieur de Canalis,—What flattery! How quickly the grave man becomes Leander! To what should I attribute such change—my ink, your fancy, or the memory of the girl at the window? Your letter has given me elating pleasures beyond family affection. What are blood ties compared to those forged by mysterious sympathies? Bless you for the happiness. You explain social injustices: glory dazzles men; God gives love to women to soothe them. I have felt my mission and you confirm it.Sometimes I wake in inexpressible sweetness; after your feverish letter I felt heavenly freshness, destined to die for one I love. Your poem “The Maiden’s Song” paints these moments; I think you worthy to be me. Your letter lets me read within you: I have guessed your struggles, curiosity, and plans, but do not yet know you well enough. Shall we make a compact? Write your life briefly—tell me what you do in Paris day by day, as to an old friend—and then I will see you. This is a great step.This involves my life and my parents’—they must find a son in you. To what extent can superb spirits bend to family yokes and endure little miseries? You said Goethe and Byron were egoists; you erred. Great minds are slaves of ideas; their visible egoism often hides abnegation. A man who gives gifts to his epoch is not simply selfish. The poet’s life needs vast organization to bear pleasures and sorrows; consider Molière, whose private suffering darkens his comedy.I place you among those whose generosity borders on the divine. Had I found self-interest where I now find noble flowers, I would have been anguished. I once met disappointment before sixteen; what would have become of me had I learned at twenty that fame is a lie? I should have surrendered to my father and become resigned to a dull life. But I have you; I believe in you. That belief straightens my thoughts and fancies. Sometimes I wish I were already in the middle of the book we are beginning; I feel persistence, strength to love, constancy tempered by reason, a heroism for duty.If you could follow me to the exquisite retreat I fancy, if you knew my plans, you might not call me foolish. I wish to be an inexhaustible spring for the twenty years nature allows me to shine; to vary happiness, put intelligence into tenderness, and give piquancy to fidelity. I wish to be courageous for my friend, to abnegate for the nest like no bird can. Tell me, do you now think me to blame for my first letter? The mysterious wind of will drove me to you. In your letter I hold your cry: “God wills it.”Ah, you will call me a chatterbox; the people around say I am taciturn.O. d’Este M.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The renowned surgeon Desplein’s visit deeply moved Modeste; guests praised the Mignon family for the hope he inspired, and the salon became a debate over Desplein’s “genius.” While the medical community acknowledged his skill, Canalis dismissed it as mere application, not invention. Modeste defended a broader notion of genius, citing practical innovators like Parmentier and Jacquart. The argument grew heated, exposing Canalis’s wounded vanity as he tried to dominate the discussion.Modeste’s flirtation with Canalis provoked La Brière, who left believing he’d lost her; her bold challenge to her father surprised the adults and revealed growing independence. The next day she behaved more provocatively toward Canalis, increasing La Brière’s jealousy. Canalis answered with grand gestures as rivalry mounted, but Modeste remained skeptical of his motives, asserting her independence and resisting mere social expectation.With the grand hunt and the Duc d’Herouville’s arrival, Modeste’s feelings for La Brière deepened while her rapport with Canalis frayed. Her beauty and fortune attracted attention, shifting the suitors’ dynamics; the hunt exposes true natures. Ultimately Modeste moves toward a more complex view of love and ambition, learning the compromises needed to seek happiness within society’s constraints.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Balzac, famed for detailed character studies and depictions of everyday life, rarely portrayed real people. In “The Great Man of the Provinces in Paris” some figures recalled actual persons; in Beatrix he borrows recognizable traits: Mademoiselle des Touches (Camille Maupin) suggests George Sand, while Beatrix, Conti, and Claude Vignon evoke the Comtesse d’Agoult, Liszt, and critic Gustave Planche. The opening scene captures the customs of an old Breton family—a social state largely vanished—signaling a shift from past to modernity and standing out in Balzac’s “Comedy of Human Life.”I. A BRETON TOWN AND MANSIONIn Brittany some towns remain little changed by nineteenth‑century progress because poor links to Paris leave them insulated. They treat new civilization as spectacle and preserve old customs. A traveler seeking bygone human types would find traces of different eras across the provinces—Louis XV in Provence, Louis XIV in Pitou, older traditions in Brittany. Many feudal towns declined and were overlooked by historians, yet their memory and character persist.Guerande is one such town: a preserved feudal life within walls and moats, with intact battlements and gates. The mayor’s planting of poplars along the moat provoked outcry—the promenade had long been public. Houses remain unmodernized; streets recall four centuries past and now lie quiet, allowing a solitary traveler to wander. The atmosphere evokes ancient customs; even a gendarme seems out of place. The public square displays traditional Breton costumes, sharply contrasting bourgeois and noble styles. Social divisions are rigid; revolutionary spirit failed to erase them. Guerande, fervently Catholic and self-contained, resists modern ideas.Overlooking famed salt‑marshes—whose salt locals boast improves their butter and sardines—Guerande connects to France by two slow roads: from Savenay and the more frequented route from Vannes. Slow government and residents’ acceptance of separation reinforce the town’s isolation.Near the church stands the du Guaisnic family mansion, emblematic of past grandeur. Tracing roots to the du Guesclins, the family is ancient and noble; though fortunes declined, they remain significant. The mansion’s crested archway opens to stables and a kitchen; inside, a grand hall with intricate woodwork, a massive fireplace, a dining room hung with fourteenth‑century tapestries, and simple family rooms preserve historical charm. A half‑acre garden with fruit trees, a sundial, Venetian touches and a spiral column testifies to enduring heritage. The du Guaisnics, hosting friends for generations, embody old Brittany’s spirit and the persistence of tradition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in May 1836 the Guenic household consisted of Baron and Baroness du Guenic, the baron’s elder sister Mademoiselle Zéphirine, and their only son Calyste, twenty-one. The baron, Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles, a veteran of the Vendée and Brittany insurrections who fought with Charette and La Rochejaquelein, had prudently sold his property to his sister before the war. After years in resistance he returned to Guérande in 1813, married Fanny O’Brien, an Irish noblewoman, and Calyste was born the day Louis XVIII landed.Now seventy-three, the baron bore the marks of his turbulent life: tall, wiry, deeply wrinkled, with sunken features and large scarred hands. Illiterate except for his prayer book, he dressed plainly—sturdy shoes, ribbed stockings, a coat bearing the cross of Saint-Louis—and combined serenity with somnolent gravity. Household talk centered on the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic faith, and Brittany’s future; Calyste was their sole hope.The baron rigorously trained Calyste and, when the Duchesse de Berry attempted her son’s restoration, took him to join the cause—leaving his wife and sister uninformed. They returned after three months, and the baron observed, “The barons did not all do their duty,” a remark mixing pride and lingering obligation.That evening the baron slept in his armchair while his wife read the newspaper. Fanny O’Brien, forty-two, retained delicate Irish beauty and warmth—refined features, elegant dress, ringleted hair, and a tender, radiant manner that softened her husband’s austerity. Nearby, blind Mademoiselle Zéphirine knitted; proud and cheerful, she refused cataract surgery to avoid burdening her brother and shared a close bond with the baroness. As the room warmed at sunset, Mariotte lit the lamp and Gasselin tended horses and dogs—longtime retainers whose steady loyalty, managed quietly by Zéphirine, exemplified the household’s affectionate, duty-bound cohesion amid trying times.When night fell, Gasselin entered the hall and respectfully asked his master if he was needed. The baron, roused, said Gasselin could go out or to bed after prayers unless Madame or his sister required him. The two ladies consented, and Gasselin knelt with the family while Mademoiselle du Guenic led the prayer. A knock followed; Gasselin opened for Monsieur le curé.Monsieur Grimont, a middle-aged priest in a cassock, entered with the easy civility of his office. His plump, calm face and jovial air suggested a clear conscience; though well regarded in Guérande, he acknowledged the du Guenics’ feudal superiority. He inquired about Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s tardiness and mentioned the young chevalier’s presence at Les Touches that evening. Mariotte cautioned against speaking of the chevalier’s visits in front of Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, who loved town gossip.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel arrived next, led by a page with a lantern. Thin and elderly, she dressed in the fashion of her youth and always carried keys and a silver snuff-box. Of the highest Breton nobility, her family’s influence had waned after a sister’s marriage to a Kergarouet annexed the name; she ran her estate with strict frugality. Her admired avarice drew nieces eager to win her favor.Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel, a friend of Zéphirine du Guenic, intended to marry one niece to Calyste, the young chevalier, to tie the family to the du Guenics. Despite the du Guenics’ wealth, they kept a respectful distance, valuing pride and status. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel treated Calyste with a proprietary air, holding conservative views and mistrusting new influences.She asked whether Monsieur du Halga would join them; the rector had seen him earlier. As they prepared for an evening game, the rector fetched a basket and counters with childlike eagerness. A firm knock announced Chevalier du Halga’s arrival: frail, dressed for his health, once a competent naval officer, now gentle and preoccupied with his little dog. Hardships during the emigration had undermined his health, yet he remained respected in Guérande, having served as mayor. The rector’s cough during the prayer for the king quietly hinted at the political tensions still present in the town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mouche is a card game in which each player receives five cards and one is turned up as trump. Players may play or drop out; dropping costs only the stake. Winning a trick shares the pot; losing makes one a mouche, obliging payment of the full stake and enlarging the pot. Players may exchange cards from the remaining deck, but only in order. The trump card belongs to the dealer, who may swap it for any card in hand. The strongest card is Mistigris, the knave of clubs.Though rules are simple, mouche proves engrossing, exposing greed, cunning, and expression. At the Hôtel du Guénic players begin with twenty counters each—five sous apiece—making the stake five farthings, a notable sum. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël and Mademoiselle Zéphirine treat the game gravely, likening it to a financial venture or stock speculation.A convention from September 1825 allows a player who loses ten sous to quit; the chevalier and the baron, however, subvert this by lending counters to losers to keep play going. The arrival of Demoiselle de Kergarouët and her aunt raises stakes: the young women, enamored of Calyste, care little for such small sums.Evenings at mouche are punctuated by talk of local events and public affairs. Players sometimes forget stakes, provoking accusations of cheating—especially aimed at the rector. Nights can end abruptly, with departures empty-handed and grumbling. Attempts to introduce other games like whist and Boston have failed; the circle prefers mouche, which outlasts newer diversions.One night, as the rector deals, the baroness inquires after the chevalier’s health; he jokes about migrating ailments. Conversation turns to the Duc de Lenoncourt’s death and mortality. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël announces the Comtesse de Kergarouët’s marriage, prompting gossip about Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress of questionable repute.
