My wall would cut away three-quarters of it.“My good Gobain,” the Countess asked, “what sort of man is this florist?”Gobain replied that he disliked women, was the nephew of a Paris cure, and perhaps a little cracked. A harmless lunatic is the only man no woman ever distrusts in matters of sentiment—so the Count had chosen a wise disguise for me.When Gobain informed the Countess that the florist wished to be left in peace, she replied she would speak to him herself. The next day as I walked the path I saw the curtain parted and a face peering out. Gobain announced me as the eccentric neighbor. I had dressed as a countryman—gray flannel trousers, wooden shoes, shabby coat, peaked cap, hands soiled with mold—to strengthen the part. The Countess did not shrink.It was early May and the air was pure; seeing Honorine, I understood Octave’s passion. Her pallor was of a particular white, with pink flushes at emotion. Sunlight through the acacia gave her a pale gold halo. Her brown eyes were tender and lively, framed by long lashes; a glance could enchant. Light-brown hair framed a high brow; her mouth was voluptuous; her head bore a stamp of nobility. Slight but not thin, she embodied mignonne—pliant, ready to be petted, yet proud and imposing. Her walk had a proud carriage, and even humble people made way. Gay and tender, haughty and commanding, she combined contradictions and remained childlike. She could be as implacable as an angel if hurt.The violet perfume she wore made sense of the Count’s adoration: she inspired devotion that demanded chivalrous sacrifice. Many women study their expressions; in her it flowed from a nature that went straight to the heart. If I sketch her it is because her soul and exquisite heart are central to this tale, and you would blame me had I not done so.I was about to forget my ridiculous role as half-crazy rustic when I asked her, with assumed gravity, if she loved flowers. She replied she made artificial flowers—after growing them she imitates them, as a mother paints her children—an occupation that proved she was poor. I pretended to be a gardener with an interest in blue dahlias and blue roses; I argued that blue is the color of superior souls. I proposed open railings to unite our gardens. She, who loved solitude and independence, allowed me the right to enter her garden anytime and urged I be treated as a neighbor with whom she would keep good terms. With a leap I cleared the paling.For a fortnight I seemed indifferent; by late May on a lovely evening we walked on opposite sides of the paling and exchanged civil words. I leaped the fence and joined her. She led me into the house—a charming sanctuary of eighteenth-century taste: frescoed dining-room, tasteful staircase, faded but elegant drawing-room, a bedroom, dressing-room, and workroom used as a library. The kitchen lay below. Everything harmonized with the woman I had come to love.Exquisite simplicity reigned. Her little house was like a box of 18th-century art; but occasionally a bitter smile hinted at deeper sorrow. In days we reached intimacy born of proximity and her conviction of my indifference to women. A look would have spoiled everything; I kept my feelings masked. Honorine saw me as an old friend and treated me with pity rather than coquetry. She soon allowed me into her pretty workshop, full of books and curiosities—a boudoir where elegance softened the vulgarity of the tools of her trade. She had poetized manufacture.Perhaps making artificial flowers allows a woman to remain herself: she can chat, laugh, sing and still work. Honorine had an artist’s sense in arranging petals and shades; white china saucers held colors; a cabinet with drawers held molds; a Japanese bowl held the paste; a Venetian glass held the living model she strove to imitate. She pursued difficult tasks—tiny corollas, wildflowers—moving like an accomplished pianist. Her fingers seemed fairy-like in their deft economy of action.I could not tire of admiring her; she displayed the genius of a painter in bold attempts, copying faded flowers and yellowed leaves to create poetic crowns suitable to women’s moods. She explained a fanciful botany of feelings—flowers for bacchantes, for bigots, pensive flowers for bored women. She employed me to stamp leaves, cut material, and prepare wires. My affected desire for occupation made me useful. We talked as we worked; when idle I read to her to keep my role. My gloomy, misanthropic persona amused and matched her antipathy to love; she was content with a companion on her desert island.I spared you the shame I felt as I feigned wounds in my heart to excite her compassion. Her renunciation of love, her dread of happiness, she proclaimed with vehemence. These happy days convinced me that a woman’s friendship can be superior to her love. She resisted any trap of sentiment; mulish obstinacy is common among women, and she was stubborn.One evening, playing the cynic, I argued that women, once they have inspired passion, hold men sacred forever, while women forget. She rebuked my generalization and denied being like those who give themselves to successive loves. She declared herself a nun, seventy-two years old in spirit, not a woman. Her disgust for life made her invulnerable. She often spoke of the angel Gabriel singing in her heart; if rich she would work still to prevent herself from soaring on “many-tinted wings.” She found some solace in her flowers but sometimes they failed to occupy her. She felt some great event impending; her life seemed about to change.After three months of this diplomatic struggle between my assumed melancholy and her proud indifference, I told the Count I could not coax her out of herself; the shell must be broken. The Count left me free to act.One Saturday in July, she gleefully showed me five gold pieces she had earned for her flowers and caps. It was late, a moonlit evening. The room smelled of perfumes; the Countess jingled the coins that Octave’s accomplices had provided as part of the ruse. She said she earned her living by amusing herself and felt transports of pride every Saturday. I suggested the image of a pretty child running among the flowers calling her “mother,” and her reaction was immediate and extreme: she suffered an attack—what she called “a cramp of the heart.” Madame Gobain tended her; I walked the path weeping and doubting whether I should abandon the bird-catcher’s role I had assumed. Gobain returned saying I cried like a child, and the Countess, roused by the danger of misinterpretation, wore a wrapper and came down to me.“You are not the cause of this attack,” she said, and when I asked about her troubles she declared her heart a poem laid before God. I threatened, lightly, that I could know all her secrets by tomorrow. She defiantly challenged me to be curious and told me to reveal what I discovered and the means by which I discovered it, for the preservation of the small happiness she so dearly bought depended on my discretion.“I defy you,” she said; she wanted me to find out who she really was. I warned that if I learned anything carelessly, others would be informed, and then—she would fly “on wings” to the New World. I interrupted that Paris is a place where those who must work can hide. She persisted: find out who I am. I promised to tell her what I might discover the next day and pleaded she not hate me. She replied coldly, almost imperiously, that only two words would suffice to destroy her peaceful fraud and she must be allowed to keep her carefully arranged retreat. After a few more words, she dismissed me abruptly.Thus the Count’s scheme had reached a crucial point: I was to be the Count’s instrument, the gardener-spy and companion, someone who could win Honorine’s confidence without revealing his identity. The house’s delicate balance—Honorine’s love of flowers, her pride, her vow of solitude, and the secret charity that sustained her—would be the stage for whether pity, cunning, or patience could redeem what force and authority could not.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“The battle is to-morrow, then,” I said, forcing a smile; the phrase kept returning as I thought of the coming confrontation of hearts. Octave shared Honorine’s agitation. The Count and I walked until two in the morning like generals before a decisive engagement, both conscious that victory might turn on an instant. We lay awake—one hopeful, the other dreading reunion. For Octave and Honorine the true drama was interior, in a spiritual world of lofty souls.The next evening I arrived at ten and entered Honorine’s white-and-blue bedroom, the sanctuary of this wounded dove. The Comtesse seemed about to speak but held back at my respectful manner. I told her plainly she was not living in her own house but in Count Octave’s: M. Lenormand, the usher, was only a front; the seclusion, the money, the protection all came from him. Her disappearance had been plausibly explained; her husband even hoped she had been saved from a shipwreck. He had hidden her as she hid herself, obeying her through seven years of magnanimity.The Countess trembled, defiant, eyes dry and fierce that later yielded helpless tears. She had believed herself free; marriage weighed on her like a prison. “I will go!” she cried. “He forces me to it; I will go where no one will come after me.” I asked if she meant suicide. She insisted she had powerful reasons to refuse returning to Comte Octave.I urged her to tell me and my uncle—both devoted advisers. My uncle was a priest but not a pedant; we would listen and try to find a solution. If she were the victim of a misunderstanding we could clear it; if guilty, expiation could be sought. I promised friendship and refuge from Octave’s tyranny if she proved she could not and ought not return. She spoke of the convent as last hope, but I warned that as a Minister of State the Count could block many convents; nonetheless I pledged to save her if she showed she had no other life to lead. I offered her peace, solitude, independence, and vowed not to see her without her consent.She answered that no man could understand her conscience, “like circles of Dante’s hell.” Another man, though unworthy, had had all her love; for her there could never be two loves. The cup of happiness once broken could not be refilled. She would not be a prostitute to Octave—she esteemed his generosity and respected him but could never love him. Expecting tenderness from him would be impossible; every time her eyes met his she would see her sin reflected. To return would be a snare: she feared killing him with coldness or provoking suspicion, and could not bear to kneel while he stood. She clung to the memory of a child conceived in rapture and nursed for seven months; other children would draw tears in their milk. For her there was no situation in the life he offered that did not hide a precipice. Religion offered solace, but she lacked the strength to face that suffering. She had chosen between this hell, where God did not forbid blessing Him, and the hell awaiting her in Count Octave’s house.I asked where she would go. Could a woman at thirty, beautiful and with undiscovered powers, live unprotected in the wilderness where I could hide her? I reassured her the Count, who had not shown himself at her house for nine years, would not go anywhere without her permission; she had his devotion, a guarantee for her tranquillity. I urged her to discuss the future in confidence with my uncle, a man whose moral influence equaled a minister’s. He would be tender before her flowers.I left at midnight; Honorine seemed calm but depressed, harboring a secret purpose I could not divine. I found Octave a few paces away in the Rue Saint-Maur—drawn by irresistible attraction, he had left our agreed spot. On hearing of my visit he fretted: “What a night my poor child will go through! Suppose I were to go to her! What if she saw me suddenly?” I warned that at that moment she might throw herself out the window; she was one of those Lucretias who could not survive such violence. He countered that passions change like a tempest on a lake; tonight she might as well rush into his arms as she might throw herself out. He joked of opium to sleep away the torment.Next day Gobain brought a letter informing me the Countess had gone to bed at six, taken a chemist’s draught, and fallen asleep. Gobain read Honorine’s anguished letter aloud. She acknowledged conscience condemned her: conscience is God’s interpreter to man. If she did not reconcile with Octave she would be damned; civil law condemned her to obey; if her husband did not reject her the world would deem her pure. Legally, religiously, and socially she ought to return; it would be cruel to deprive him of happiness, to deprive him of children, to blot his name. Her sufferings and repugnance she admitted were selfish and ought to be sacrificed to family. She should be a mother; a child’s caresses might wipe many tears. God, law, and society were in accord.Yet she declared she could not live false to herself. She would become “white and innocent again” only in death. It was not obstinacy, she said, but a nature that could not renew passion once consummated. “A young bride is like a plucked flower; a guilty wife is a flower trodden underfoot,” she wrote. She could not love Octave. Every time their eyes met she would see her sin reflected; his generosity would measure her crime. Neither esteem nor sacrifice could survive. She feared she would by coldness kill him or provoke suspicion. She could not bear to be treated as chattel, to kneel while he stood. She clung to the memory of a child conceived in rapture; other children would draw tears from her in their milk. Religion offered solace but she lacked energy for that particular suffering. She had chosen between a hell in which God did not forbid blessing Him and a hell awaiting her under Count Octave’s roof. She closed asking me to tell her where to find the solitude, peace, and silence I had promised.After copying the letter I went to the Rue Payenne. Opium’s power had been overcome by anxiety; Octave paced his garden like a madman. I gave him Honorine’s letter and urged him to answer; he must reassure modesty with experience, for it is harder to restore conviction than to seduce ignorance. He cried with joy on reading her words: “She is mine!” Ecstasy and torment possessed him; he would act. I visited other friends and, comparing Mademoiselle de Courteville with Honorine, reflected that certain women inspire perfect, inevitable fidelity while others, like Honorine, demand extraordinary generosity. Such comparisons, fatal at marriage’s threshold, are wrong. Life teaches that marriage excludes passion and family cannot be founded on tempests of love. Having dreamed impossible passion and tasted its torment, I saw modest reality and took a manful resolution.When I returned to the Count to announce his relations’ arrival I saw him grow young with hope. He asked what ailed me; I replied with a crucial condition: if he succeeded in bringing the Countess back, she must never see me again, must never know Maurice was his secretary. “Never mention my name to her, or all will be undone.” I told Octave to get me a diplomatic post abroad instead of the position he had obtained for me at home; I asked not to marry Amélie and to be sent away. He pressed my hand, understanding. We agreed on evening arrangements at Honorine’s.That August evening the Countess sat under an arbor in a loose white muslin dress with blue bows. She did not rise but signaled me to sit. “Is not life deadlocked for me?” she asked. I told her it need not be if she chose differently. She asked how. I told her her letter was in the Count’s hands; she started like a frightened deer and accused me of being a traitor, a spy sent by her husband. I explained I had forwarded her letter through my uncle and would bring the Count’s reply. There is a law, divine and human, that even hatred obeys: do not condemn without hearing the defense. She must read the Count’s answer in the presence of my uncle and me, or she would be mere wilful child. Make this sacrifice to the world, to the law, and to God.She consented. Months of effort had led to this instant. The Count had pinned all hopes on it and he had reached it. My uncle’s entrance into the little Pompadour drawing-room at ten was formidable: his fine head, silver hair against black, and divinely calm face soothed the Countess. My uncle, the Cure of the White Friars, handed Honorine Octave’s letter.In that letter Octave said that if she had trusted him and read a letter he had written five years earlier she would have spared five years of privations; he admitted faults and ignorance at the marriage’s start; an angel had been in the house and he felt punished for his audacity. He begged mercy, saying he had so appreciated her susceptibilities he would not bring her back to Rue Payenne where he could not bear to see her. He was decorating another house in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré and hoped to conduct not a wife secured by ignorance and law but a sister who would allow him to press a fatherly kiss on her brow. He promised not to force passion on her until sure of his strength to leave her free. He argued she could live as a sister or daughter presiding: unapproachable, alone, occupied as she pleased, under legitimate protection and with fortune for good works. If she longed for absolution it would wait on her pride; he would not use ecclesiastical threats or legal force; he wanted her happiness and would not take peace except from her consent. If she persisted in condemning him to loneliness he would yield; she would be troubled no more.Honorine folded the letter, pressed it to her bosom, and, soothed, said she would avail herself of the Count’s permission to remain here. My involuntary cry betrayed my feeling and revealed to her that I was more than a spy; she gave me a mischievous glance and took my hand at the door, pressing it affectionately. I replied that I would leave tomorrow—“You love your husband,” I said after a squeezing farewell. I rushed away, leaving my uncle to explain my agitation as a touch of madness.Six days later I left with an appointment as vice-consul in Spain. After I established myself, Count Octave wrote to me: if I were happy he would not write; he had entered a new life of suffering, grown young again in desire but governed by a diplomat’s prudence. When I left, Octave had not yet been admitted to the pavilion in Rue Saint-Maur, though a letter promised permission. He waited more than a month and finally called. Gobain stalled, saying “Madame had to dress,” flattering Octave’s hopes. Once admitted, he spoke with trembling: he told her he had no crime but being in love with his wife and proposed she no longer work, offering her certificates worth twelve thousand francs each—an offer which was freedom more than money. She took them, languidly, and understood the gift. She conceded and said she would be seen as often as he liked.Two months of habit brought out her true character; it was a delicious springtime of love that gave Octave ineffable bliss. Yet when he proposed she go to England to resume rank and life in their new residence she feared to leave their experiment. “Why not live always as we are?” she asked, and he submitted, wondering if she were making an experiment. He painted his case in a letter and begged her answer; she made none and seemed sad. When she finally received him in her blue-and-white room she was bewitching in dress, but her face was grave, fires beneath ice. She told him she would return as his wife when he wished, but warned that submission had dangers: she could be resigned but could not give the love he wanted. She loved his soul enough to die in his service—an expiation—and knelt, sobbing she might not die.For two months he struggled. His heart was too full; he sought a friend and cried, “What shall I do?” I did not answer that letter. Two months later newspapers announced the Comtesse Octave had returned on an English vessel, restored to her family after contrived adventures. When I moved to Genoa I received formal notice of the birth of a son to the Count and Countess; I held the letter for two hours on the terrace bench. Two months later, urged by Octave and friends and broken by my uncle’s death, I agreed to marry.Six months after the Revolution of July I received a final letter concluding the story. The Countess wrote that she was dying though already a mother—perhaps because she was a mother. She had played her part as a wife well; she had deceived her husband; she had had happiness as genuine as an actress’s tears. She was dying for society, family, marriage, as early Christians died for God. She did not know of what she was dying; she tried to find out but could not. She was not perverse; she had been bent on explaining her malady to me—the man who had brought the heavenly physician, my uncle, at whose word she surrendered. He was her director; she had nursed him in his last illness; he had shown her the way to heaven, bidding her persevere in duty.She had done her duty. She did not blame those who forget; she admired them as strong natures. But she had the malady of memory. She could not twice feel that love which identifies a woman with a man. To the last moment she cried in the confessional and to her husband, “Have mercy!” but there was none. She died with stupendous courage, playing the actress’s cheerful mask while the invisible rival—her dead child—came every day to claim her. She was rent and smiled; two children existed, but the elder, the dead one, would triumph. She told me she had said this before: the dead child calls her. The intimacy of marriage without love degraded her soul every hour. She could only weep or dream when alone. Social duties and care of her child left no moment to renew herself as she could in solitude. The constant need for watchfulness starved her heart. She lacked tender lips to cool her burning eyes; her soul acted a part and so she died. The doctors, who discovered her secret, allowed a plausible complaint as cause. Octave believed in her apparent adoration; she feared he might follow her. She begged me to be her little Count’s guardian; she had placed a codicil expressing her wish and told me not to produce it unless needed. She asked me not to mourn; she had been dead these many days if Saint Bernard was right that where there is no love there is no life.The Consul closed the reading by putting away the letters and locking his pocket-book. The guests asked about the Count. The Consul-General recalled seeing him a white-haired man, an old man of forty-five, passing through Genoa in search of health and amusement; the man had placed his will in the Consul’s hands and appointed him guardian of his son. He suspected the truth of Honorine’s death and that suspicion was killing him. At the steamboat Octave, poor friend and patron, had entrusted his will and looked down at the Mediterranean, pondering the mystery of why human nature sacrifices exquisite beings to fugitive pleasures. He confessed he was consumed by remorse. In the Rue Payenne he had been dying of joys not had; now in Italy he would die of joys he had had. He wondered where the discord lay between two equally noble natures.A profound silence followed. The Consul then asked the two women present, “Was she virtuous?” Mademoiselle des Touches took his arm and answered by turning the question to men: are they not equally wrong when they take a young girl to wife while cherishing an angelic image and then compare us to unknown rivals? She argued that if marriage were based on passion her point would be valid—that kind of marriage would be Paradise. Others remembered such passions devastating men and making women like nuns or suicides. Camille Maupin and others reflected that such exceptional souls find peace only in solitude or in religious life. As the guests dispersed along the seashore at eleven, the conversation lingered on the rarity of Honorine: perhaps the most extraordinary intellect, a pearl whose tragedy was that life alternates pain and pleasure and that to ask Dante’s Paradise of life is to demand a luxury nature resists. Those great souls still existed, they agreed, though to live by such intensity often meant sacrifice beyond the world’s comprehension.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!” cried the lawyer’s clerk Simonnin, leaning from a window and tossing a breadcrumb at a stranger crossing Maître Derville’s courtyard.“Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks, or I will turn you out. However poor a client is, he is still a man!” admonished Boucard, head clerk.Simonnin, thirteen or fourteen, and others including Godeschal taunted the shabby visitor as “old Box-coat.” Godeschal suggested a prank, claiming M. Derville received clients only at odd hours. The office buzzed with laughter; the cluttered, musty room’s disorder was routine.When the weary man entered and politely asked for M. Derville, Boucard brusquely dismissed him, telling him to return at one. The clerks jeered—nobleman or porter?—and laughed when he insisted he was Colonel Chabert, reported dead at Eylau. Amid chatter, Boucard finally silenced them, irreverence stark against the old man’s somber presence.At about one in the morning Colonel Chabert knocked at Maître Derville’s door. The porter said Derville had not returned, but the head clerk—arranging papers—explained Derville preferred to work at night and that the Colonel was only the third late caller.When Derville arrived he was struck by the man’s thin, pale appearance and scars suggesting past trauma. The stranger claimed to be the Colonel presumed killed at Eylau, prompting Derville and the clerk to suspect madness, but the Colonel insisted on telling his story.Derville, used to varied clients, urged brevity. Chabert recounted Eylau: gravely wounded and buried among the corpses, he had miraculously survived because his horse’s body shielded him. He described awakening in a grave, struggling free, and being rescued by a woman who took him to a hospital. After months of recovery he discovered he had lost everything—especially proof of identity. Without documents, attempts to reclaim his life met disbelief and ridicule; he wandered as a beggar, was often treated as mad, and spent two years confined in a mental institution. Upon release he learned his wife, the Countess Ferraud, had remarried and borne children.Derville, sensing the gravity though skeptical, was willing to help. Chabert sought to reclaim rights and fortune he believed unjustly taken and lamented that no lawyer had taken him seriously. Moved, Derville offered to obtain necessary papers from Germany, gave a small sum for subsistence, and provided a letter to a notary for further assistance.Overwhelmed with gratitude, Chabert felt hope for the first time in years, voiced a longing for vengeance, and declared he would rather die than live in obscurity. Derville acknowledged the case’s complexity but promised to treat it seriously; the meeting restored Chabert’s purpose and gave him a glimmer of hope.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About three months after the interview, in Derville’s room, the notary Crottat—tasked with advancing half-pay to Derville’s eccentric client—demanded the six hundred francs the old soldier had received be refunded.“Are you amusing yourself with pensioning the old army?” Crottat laughed; a young man who had taken the office after his chief fled in bankruptcy.“Thank you for reminding me of that affair,” Derville replied wryly. “My philanthropy will not extend beyond twenty-five louis; I fear I have already been duped by my patriotism.”Noticing German-stamped papers on his desk, Derville ordered his head clerk Boucard to translate. A Berlin notary reported the requested documents would arrive and were legally stamped; witnesses to the recorded facts still lived in Eylau, Prussia, and the woman who had saved Comte Chabert was alive in Heilsberg. “This looks like business,” Derville observed.Questioning Crottat about Chabert’s estate and the rogue Roguin, Derville learned the facts. Roguin—called by Crottat “the unfortunate Roguin” with a laugh—had indeed absconded with eight hundred thousand francs of clients’ money. Crottat, who had copied the papers as third clerk, recounted that Rose Chapotel, wife of Hyacinthe (called Chabert, Count of the Empire), had married without settlement so they held property in common. The personalty was about six hundred thousand francs. Before marriage Colonel Chabert made a will leaving one-quarter to hospitals and assigning another quarter to the State; the will was contested, forcing a sale and leaving the widow with the portion bequeathed to the treasury. Thus Chabert’s personal fortune had been reduced to roughly three hundred thousand francs.Derville then sought out the Colonel. Chabert lodged in the Faubourg Saint-Marceau with an old quartermaster named Vergniaud; Derville had to walk because his coachman refused the unpaved street. The house was dismal—mismatched windows, trodden-earth floor, a single chair and a straw bed, damp walls—testimony to poverty. Yet when Chabert emerged smoking a pipe he retained the military calm of a man unshaken by hardship.“Why did you not write to me?” Chabert asked, giving practical directions to avoid manure.“Here, we are not distinguished for luxury,” Chabert added when asked about his lodgings. “But I have done no one wrong, and I sleep in peace.” Derville hesitated about the money he had advanced and instead asked why Chabert had not moved to Paris to live better.“The good folks here have taken me in and fed me for a year,” Chabert replied. “I cannot leave them now that I have a little money.”Derville told him the Heilsberg papers were expected and that the woman who saved him was alive; Chabert, frustrated at the thought of money he did not possess, threw down his pipe. Derville then outlined the legal complexities: proving Chabert’s identity would require lengthy, costly proceedings. The case could be prolonged by the bigamy issue—Comtesse Ferraud had remarried—and opponents would demand inquiries, translations, and foreign legal formalities that might take years and drain resources.“You will face many challenges,” Derville cautioned. “Your opponents will demand inquiries that could take years.” Overwhelmed by the prospect of a drawn-out lawsuit, Chabert expressed despair. Derville reassured him the outcome could be favorable and proposed a friendly suit to annul both the death certificate and the subsequent marriage. He offered to send a power of attorney for Chabert to sign and to provide financial help. Chabert, feeling hopeless, agreed to trust Derville completely.As Derville left he met Louis Vergniaud, who asked for a loan to improve the Colonel’s living conditions. Derville agreed but insisted Chabert should be the one to help his friends. He then planned to visit Comtesse Ferraud to assess her position and potential leverage.Monsieur le Comte Ferraud, the son of a former Councillor, had returned to France after the Revolution and married Madame Chabert, who had turned her share of her husband’s fortune into a substantial income. Their marriage was socially accepted; Ferraud was politically ambitious but had begun to regret his union. The Countess had become comfortable and influential, a sharp contrast to Chabert’s squalor.Derville arrived at the Hotel Ferraud and found her surrounded by luxury. “Good-morning, Monsieur Derville,” she greeted him, but he quickly shifted to serious matters. “I have come to speak with you on a very serious matter,” he stated, revealing that Colonel Chabert was alive. The Countess laughed dismissively until Derville’s serious tone silenced her. He warned her of the danger she faced if Chabert’s identity were proven, hinting at legal and social ramifications. She insisted her second marriage was secure because of her children, but Derville countered that the law might not favor her and that Ferraud might not support her if his name and fortune were at risk.Derville’s words struck a nerve; the Countess began to weigh the implications. He proposed a compromise, sensing her vulnerability. “Does he still love me?” she asked, contemplating how to use Chabert’s affection to her advantage. Derville left her with the suggestion to consider a settlement, confident he had gained the upper hand.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A week after Derville's visits, Colonel Chabert and his wife, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet at their lawyer's office. With Derville's help Chabert arrived restored: dignified in a blue suit and wearing the Legion of Honor ribbon, his soldierly bearing renewed.When Chabert entered, Madame Ferraud soon followed, made up to appear youthful. Clerks noticed his change; Derville, sensing the couple's tension, separated them to present a memorandum: Chabert would formally acknowledge his identity in exchange for financial support from his wife. The Countess, aghast at public exposure, resisted the obligation.Back together, Chabert confronted her about their past, laying bare the pain of their separation. She feigned surprise and tried to leave, but Derville urged her to stay. A heated exchange ensued; despite cautions, Chabert pressed for justice. The Countess then took him to her carriage and brought him to her country house in Groslay. During the ride she confessed turmoil: she loved another man, Comte Ferraud, and wanted to preserve her dignity and avoid scandal. Hurt, Chabert offered measured understanding, aware of the social consequences she feared.At the country house she treated him kindly and they reminisced; Chabert found temporary solace even as resentment lingered. Meanwhile the Countess acted to secure her position, writing to Delbecq to neutralize his claim. When Chabert discovered this betrayal he resolved to confront her but ultimately sacrificed his identity to protect her and their children, renouncing his name and withdrawing from society.Months later Derville found him reduced to a vagabond in a courtroom. Despite poverty, Chabert retained dignity and scorn for society's indifference, refusing charity. His story traces loss and resilience, love, betrayal, and the struggle for identity in an indifferent world.In June 1840 Godeschal, an attorney, accompanied Derville to Ris. Near Bicêtre they found an old pauper under an elm, drying his handkerchief. In a shabby wrapper-coat he had a carved, grotesque look; Godeschal compared him to German carvings, while Derville recognized him as Comte Chabert, husband of the pious Comtesse Ferraud, who had likely consigned him to the workhouse because he reminded her of their past.Curious, Godeschal urged Derville to tell Chabert’s story. Two days later they returned and found the old man drawing in the sand. When spoken to he insisted he was no longer Chabert but “Hyacinthe,” merely a number in the institution; he even envied a condemned man for not being married. Godeschal gave him money for snuff; Chabert accepted eagerly, saluted them as “brave troopers,” waved his stick and cried “Vive Napoleon!” Another pauper noted his odd blend of philosophy and childishness and recalled how Chabert once impressed a Prussian officer with military recollections.Derville reflected on Chabert’s tragic arc—from foundling to Napoleonic soldier to workhouse inmate—and on the disillusionment facing priest, doctor, and lawyer, all exposed to human baseness: betrayal, greed, moral decay, fathers abandoned by daughters, mothers exploiting children. Godeschal admitted he’d seen similar horrors and longed to flee Parisian corruption for a quiet country life with his wife. An addendum lists characters from Balzac’s Human Comedy and their other appearances, including Napoleon and Comtesse Ferraud.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Bianchon, trained in surgery under the celebrated French surgeon Desplein, became a theoretical physiologist. Desplein’s surgical skill was admired, though he left no school; his avowed atheism—hardened by dissecting corpses without perceiving a soul—shaped him. He combined practical brilliance with skeptical paradox.Critics noted contradictions: eccentric dress, moods swinging between kindness and harshness, and contempt for humanity’s follies. Among his students, Horace Bianchon—who endured poverty and showed integrity and cheerfulness—won Desplein’s mentorship, launching his career and Paris practice.When Bianchon mentioned a destitute water‑carrier with a debilitating illness, Desplein hurried to help, showing compassion for Auvergnats. Bianchon later saw him enter a church—odd for an atheist—and learned he had attended mass for twenty years and founded it. These episodes complicate Desplein’s beliefs, portraying a man of skill, skepticism, compassion, paradox.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Some time passed before Dr. Bianchon, despite their friendship, found an opening to ask Desplein about a pivotal episode in his life. Their meetings rarely allowed private talk, but seven years later, after the Revolution of 1830, Bianchon saw Desplein entering Saint-Sulpice and knelt beside him; they attended mass together.“Can you explain your sudden piety?” Bianchon asked as they left. “You, who do not believe in God, at mass? You owe me an explanation.”Desplein parried that many are outwardly religious but inwardly skeptical, then criticized politicians, but Bianchon pressed: why had he founded this mass? Desplein answered simply: he was nearing the end of his life and could trace its beginnings. Standing in the Rue des Quatre-Vents, he pointed to the house where he had lived two desperate years and recalled hunger, poverty, isolation, and an irritable temper that made friendship difficult. He had no money for books or medical training and struggled alone to rise from destitution.He spoke of meager meals and tireless labor without sympathy or support. Those born to wealth could not imagine his plight and asked as if debt were a choice. Early hardships shaped his view of humanity: he had witnessed selfishness and envy and expected mediocrity to try to pull down those who strove for excellence.A turning point came when, at the depth of his poverty, he contemplated enlisting. He had hoped for a box of linen from a relative, but when the porter demanded too much to release it, despair set in. Then his neighbor Bourgeat—a humble water-carrier—appeared and changed his fate.Bourgeat, though poor himself, paid Desplein’s debts and secured new lodgings. He devoted his savings to Desplein’s future, understood his mission, and supported him selflessly. Desplein described Bourgeat as nurturing, almost maternal, who took pride in his protege yet sought no recognition. When Desplein became a house surgeon, leaving Bourgeat pained him; Bourgeat was proud nonetheless.Later, Desplein repaid him by buying him a horse and a barrel, fulfilling a long-held dream. Bourgeat was angry that Desplein had spent his money on him, yet delighted; he never claimed credit for Desplein’s rise, though Desplein felt he owed him everything.When Bourgeat fell ill, Desplein tended him devotedly; Bourgeat died in his arms, leaving him all he had. He had strong faith, and Desplein honored his wishes by having masses said for him after his death. Desplein explained that the mass he founded was a tribute to Bourgeat’s faith; he hoped, if there is a paradise, that Bourgeat had found peace there. Desplein admitted he would give anything for the faith Bourgeat possessed.Bianchon, who had witnessed Desplein’s last days, could not say definitively whether the great surgeon died an atheist. He wondered whether Bourgeat might have opened the gates of Heaven for Desplein, just as he had opened the doors to the earthly temple they had shared.PARIS, January 1836.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy: Bianchon, Horace; Father Goriot; César Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty; The Country Parson. In addition, M. Bianchon narrated Another Study of Woman and La Grande Bretèche. Desplein appears in Cousin Pons; Lost Illusions; The Thirteen; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Seamy Side of History; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At one in the morning Dr. Horace Bianchon and Baron de Rastignac walked, speaking of the Marquise d’Espard. Bianchon distrusted her; Rastignac, dazzled by her rank and beauty, thought she could advance him and settle his debts. Bianchon warned it was a mistake, likening her to a “one-eyed horse”—cosmetics hide neither age nor temper. As a physician he scorned fashionable women as insincere; the Marquise seemed a polished façade. Rastignac dismissed him, insisting a woman of fashion could be a useful ally. Bianchon nevertheless agreed to introduce him to his uncle, Judge Jean-Jules Popinot, though he doubted the rigid judge would help.The next morning Bianchon visited Popinot’s house in a decayed quarter, the building hinting at former grandeur. Popinot was unkempt and unambitious, reduced to a supernumerary judge. Devoted to justice, he combined kindness and an ability to elicit confessions without force with firm integrity, yet was frustrated by the system. Bianchon reflected how hard it would be to persuade such an uncompromising man.Twelve years earlier, during the famine of 1816, Popinot was appointed President of the Commission Extraordinary to distribute food in his neighborhood. A prominent lawyer and criminal judge, he had long noted legal outcomes without understanding their causes; direct contact with the destitute awakened a deep compassion that gradually made him a protector of the poor, likened to a modern Saint Vincent de Paul.The change was incremental: one act of charity led to another, and Popinot became central to local benevolence. He worked to prevent crime, find employment for the vulnerable, and offer discreet assistance, living a largely private life unknown to most Parisians. Gratitude from those he helped was often unspoken—too burdened for thanks.After two years he converted a storeroom into a modest parlor for the needy, furnished with wooden benches and plain chairs. He kept meticulous records of donations and requests to allocate resources fairly; his aide Lavienne managed pawn tickets and visited the hardest-hit districts. From dawn until late morning the parlor filled with women, children, and the destitute; their numbers made heating unnecessary, and even criminals respected the household. Popinot devoted mornings to the poor, afternoons to his legal practice, and evenings to his judicial duties.This close observation sharpened his judicial instinct: he learned to discern hidden virtues in the poor and detect subtle signs of wrongdoing. His modest inherited fortune, supplemented by his late wife’s savings and a restrained judge’s salary, required frugality, especially given his charitable outlays. Despite his public service, he remained one of the few judges of the Court of the Seine without the Legion of Honor.His routine was interrupted when the President of the Second Chamber, at the Marquise d’Espard’s request, commissioned Popinot to examine the Marquis d’Espard for a Lunacy Commission. Bianchon went to the Rue du Fouarre—alive with the needy early and deserted by nine—expecting a contrast between Popinot’s rough domesticity and the Marquise’s courtly expectations.The parlor was a tableau of hardship: worn faces, anxious lines, and quiet resilience. Popinot, in a threadbare nightcap and dressing gown, sat absorbed in his ledger; Lavienne kept the petitions in order. Bianchon brought an urgent matter, but Popinot paused to help a woman needing funds to sustain her grocery while her husband was imprisoned for debt, recording the transaction. By midmorning Bianchon learned Popinot had attended to more than two hundred people, with others waiting. Sent on an errand, Bianchon returned to find the parlor emptied—the last of the needy helped.Popinot’s home reflected his divided life: dust, scattered papers, his late wife’s belongings, and tokens of gratitude. As they prepared for breakfast, Bianchon urged Popinot to dress suitably for the Marquise’s meeting. Popinot hesitated—magistrates should not accept hospitality from parties in cases they oversee—but, persuaded that a visit would be revealing and kinder, he agreed and began to consider the Marquis d’Espard’s mental state and its implications for the family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next evening at nine Bianchon climbed his uncle’s dusty staircase and found him bent over a complicated judgment. Because the tailor had not sent the coat Lavienne ordered, Popinot wore his old stained coat; Bianchon straightened his cravat and buttoned it, but the judge’s habitual movements revealed a gap between waistcoat and trousers. Bianchon noted the absurdity just as his uncle entered the Marquise d’Espard’s room.A brief account of Madame d’Espard frames the interview. For seven years she had been a Parisian fashion-figure. After separating from her husband in 1815, the Marquise—about thirty-three and mother of two boys, fifteen and thirteen—maintained her vogue by elaborate hair, cold baths, and strict diet; emotionally detached, she admitted neither love nor hatred and parted from M. d’Espard with satisfaction when he took the children, whom she saw as obstacles to her ambitions.With an income of twenty-six thousand francs a year, she lived quietly at first but by 1820 re-emerged socially, hosting gatherings and supplanting other prominent women. She cultivated ties with duchesses and countesses, turning her drawing-room into a discreet hub for political discussion. Adroit at navigating social complexities, she was admired by ambitious young men but vain and instrumental—using intimacy as influence while avoiding personal compromise.Rastignac, seeking an alliance, viewed her as a useful, cunning power. Popinot’s arrival confirmed the household’s opulence; she received him with feigned weakness while the Chevalier d’Espard observed silently. Perceptive and probing, Popinot questioned the husband’s supposed mental change and their separation. The Marquise offered a crafted story, but Popinot’s inquiries—about the children’s welfare and practical arrangements—exposed inconsistencies: lavish living clashed with claims of hardship, suggesting she might be maneuvering to control her husband’s affairs for self-serving reasons.When she tried to deflect, Popinot warned that the court would scrutinize motives and transparency and that her façade might collapse. Afterwards Bianchon and Rastignac discussed the case’s scandal potential and Popinot’s character; Bianchon feared the Marquise’s influence. Popinot resolved to investigate further and summon Madame Jeanrenaud.The next day Madame Jeanrenaud arrived: stout, unrefined, a sharp contrast to the Marquise. She denied wrongdoing and expressed simple concern for M. d’Espard’s well-being. Her straightforward honesty intrigued Popinot. As he began tracing M. d’Espard’s financial dealings, he realized the situation was more complex than it first appeared; untangling the truth would require careful examination of all involved.Those who have matured know how trivial incidents can shape major events: Popinot’s minor illness delayed a crucial meeting. The judge, feeling unwell, stayed home instead of visiting the Marquis d’Espard, losing a day whose absence echoed larger historic delays.Before following the lawyer and his clerk to the Marquis’s residence, consider the father’s home life, which his wife’s petition branded as mad. In old Paris some buildings still display a taste for permanence. The house on Rue de la Montagne-Sainte-Genevieve where M. d’Espard lived was one such stone structure: once grand, now weathered and wounded by time and revolution, formerly housing dignitaries, later partitioned and partly occupied by a printing establishment. M. d’Espard took the ground floor, likely for his children’s sake, because the garden was unusually large for the area. He paid a modest rent given the building’s decay and the depressed market. The house dated from Henry III–Louis XIII and still bore traces of former glory—its perron and garden among them.The Marquis furnished his rooms in rich browns, creating a quiet, harmonious atmosphere with carefully chosen pieces. Few visitors were admitted; family life remained secluded. The household comprised the Marquis, his two sons, and a small staff—a long-serving cook and a man-servant—whose discipline maintained the house’s orderly calm. To shield his children from outside influence, M. d’Espard avoided socializing with tenants, and that isolation bred gossip: neighbors branded him mad and his servants untrustworthy. Tenants believed him financially ruined, a view reinforced by his extreme frugality and habit of paying cash.Noble by birth, the family embodied increasingly rare ideals, their dignity clashing with egalitarian neighbors. The Marquis, about fifty, had a distinguished, somewhat cold appearance—his slightly crooked nose and prominent forehead lending an eccentric air. Elegant in manner but frugal in dress, he wore the same coat for years. His sons, Comte Clément de Négrepelisse, nearly sixteen and fashionable, and Vicomte Camille d’Espard, were handsome. The Marquis concentrated on a broad education for them—mathematics, Eastern languages, and history—eschewing what he considered unproductive studies.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Popinot planned to question M. d’Espard fell on a Thursday holiday. Before their father rose, the two brothers were in the garden; the younger begged to visit the shooting-gallery and urged Clément to agree. The Viscount often took advantage of Clément’s weakness and delighted in wrestling with him. Their noisy quarrel roused their father, who watched amused from the window. Two neighbors joked that the Marquis seemed mad to make his children fight. Noticing them, the Marquis called the boys; they leapt into his room and, after a moment’s hesitation, Clément obtained permission from Camille.Through the house people whispered about the Marquis’ supposed new madness. When Popinot arrived near noon with his clerk, the portress conducted him to the third floor, remarking that M. d’Espard had made his children fight that morning and had laughed when the younger bit the elder until he bled; she added that the Marquis rarely showed himself. The landing’s muddy floor and grimy banisters lent some credibility to her gossip and made Popinot, though impartial, receptive to the tale.They entered an outer office where an old man, likely the clerk, sat amid shelves of published numbers; a grating separated a private room, and a cash-desk showed the enterprise’s commercial aspect. A shopman opened the inner room to reveal the Marquis, a venerable, simply dressed old man wearing the Cross of Saint-Louis, seated at a desk. Popinot explained they wished to speak on private business. After formalities the Marquis asked their purpose. Popinot identified himself as a judge on the Inferior Court of Appeal for the Department of the Seine, charged with examining M. d’Espard regarding a petition for a Commission in Lunacy filed by the Marquise d’Espard. The old man withdrew; the clerk sat to take notes. The Marquis reddened and trembled, then steadied himself.“How is it,” he asked, “that I have had no notice of such a petition?” Popinot explained that notice is unnecessary because those deemed subject to such a commission are presumed to lack reason; the Court must verify the petitioner’s claims. The Marquis accepted this and asked what he must do. Popinot urged him to answer fully and confidentially, especially concerning delicate accusations Madame d’Espard might make. The Marquis feared that his explanations could blame his wife. Popinot promised discretion and the customary courtesy should the inquiry reveal a misunderstanding.Then Popinot confronted the major allegation: that the Marquis had dissipated his fortune for the benefit of Madame Jeanrenaud, widow of a bargemaster, or her son Colonel Jeanrenaud, for whom he allegedly procured an appointment and exhausted influence with the King. The Marquis flushed; tears came, but pride returned quickly. He said he was placed in a strange dilemma: revealing his motives would mean disclosing secret wounds and trusting the family’s honor to strangers. Popinot assured him of confidentiality. Freed from his usual reserve, the Marquis spoke with clarity and passion.After his marriage his wife had incurred heavy expenses that forced him to borrow. He could not afford a steward; most of his title-deeds had been taken to Paris by his father, who feared scrutiny. Their family name was Negrepelisse; the title d’Espard had been acquired through marriage in the time of Henri IV. An ancestor had lost the town of Negrepelisse during the religious wars and been ruined by property destruction. Though a later ancestor secured favor with Louis XIV and fortune, a stain of blood and disgrace remained, and the Marquis was determined to erase it.He discovered that his grandfather had obtained the lands of Negrepelisse by means tied to the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes: a Protestant merchant was arrested and hanged, and the land passed to his ancestor by iniquitous means. The merchant’s family had the rightful claim; the victim’s name was Jeanrenaud. The Marquis could not bear the disgrace and resolved to find the heirs. Eventually he located M. Jeanrenaud, reduced to poverty and serving as a lieutenant under Napoleon. Believing the heir’s rights incontestable and their lack of means to sue, he offered to pay them the lands’ value at the time they were taken—eleven hundred thousand francs—payable at his convenience and without interest.To effect restitution he lived on little for a long time. When he proposed to Madame d’Espard that they move to the country to live on half her income, she treated him as mad; her coldness convinced him she would have condoned his ancestors’ crimes. Terrified by her attitude, he resolved to leave her in control of her fortune after settling their debts, and to take charge of educating their children himself, determined to raise them as gentlemen. He invested prudently, repaid the debt sooner than expected, and had recently completed the payment of the eleven hundred thousand francs, making restitution without harming his children's prospects. These were the reasons for payments to Madame Jeanrenaud and her son.Popinot asked whether Madame d’Espard knew his motives for retiring from public life. “Yes, monsieur,” the Marquis replied. Popinot, visibly moved, excused his clerk. He then inquired about other allegations, such as that the Marquis was engaged in a business unbecoming his rank. The Marquis declined to discuss it there and summoned Comte de Nouvion, an old family friend, explaining that he used the rooms as offices for the publication of the Picturesque History of China by a reputable firm, not as his private residence.He showed Popinot his ground-floor apartment—a place of simple, touching harmony that struck the judge. The pleasant weather and garden convinced Popinot that a madman would hardly produce such beauty. The Marquis said he intended to remain until his younger son finished his studies, then travel with the children to European capitals to further their education and languages. He explained his China project: Comte de Nouvion, returned to France without fortune, was his partner; the Abbé Grozier, Keeper of the Books at the Arsenal, had been his tutor and taught him about China. Though enamored of China’s history, he remained a French gentleman. Financially, they had two thousand five hundred subscribers—twelve hundred in France—the book would cost about three hundred francs, and the Comte would earn six to seven thousand francs a year from it; the hundred thousand francs he hoped to make would cover his children’s education and pleasures. Twelve years of retirement to educate his children had cost him his memory at Court and political prospects, but he believed he had purified his family’s past and secured a glorious future for his children—a noble, if secret, task.At that moment horses clattered in the courtyard. The two boys entered, robust and sunburnt, dressed for riding. They greeted their father and the lawyer. Popinot needed no further evidence of the Marquis’ bond with his sons. The boys reported shooting success and that they had ridden in the Bois, where they had seen their mother. “Why didn’t you speak to her?” the Marquis asked. “I think I noticed, father, that she doesn’t want us to speak to her in public,” Clément whispered. A cloud passed over the Marquis’ brow; Popinot felt deep sympathy at the scene of fatherly devotion. “Justice may look in here at any time,” the Marquis said. “If anyone is mad, it can only be the children, who are a little crazy about their father, and the father who is very crazy about his children—but that kind of madness rings true.”Madame Jeanrenaud then entered, disregarding protests from the man-servant. “I take no roundabout ways!” she exclaimed. Bowing to those present, she announced she had come too late because the criminal judge was there. She declared that she and her son were of one mind to return everything, for their honor was at stake; they preferred to give back everything rather than cause trouble. “They must be as stupid as pans without handles to call you a lunatic,” she added. The boys clung to their father, alarmed.“Silence, madame,” Popinot interposed. The Marquis sent his children to the garden. Popinot addressed Madame Jeanrenaud: the payments made by the Marquis were legally due, though prompted by an expansive sense of honesty. If every possessor of confiscated goods were compelled to make restitution every century and a half, few legitimate owners would remain in France. The law allowed M. d’Espard to dispose of his income without accounting for it. A Commission in Lunacy is appropriate only when actions lack reason; here, the remittances were driven by the highest motives. She could keep the money without remorse and let the world misinterpret a noble action. Popinot declared he would rather see the Marquis crowned than subjected to a Commission. Madame Jeanrenaud applauded his defense, calling it judge-like and warmly praising his words.The Marquis extended his hand to Popinot, who pressed it gently; a private understanding passed between the humane judge and the noble, sublime man. “Monsieur le Marquis,” Popinot added, “from the first words of this inquiry, I regarded my clerk as unnecessary.” Leading M. d’Espard to the window, he said it was time to return home and that Madame la Marquise had likely acted under an influence the Marquis should counteract. Popinot left, the scene lingering with him; he even thought the rooms would suit him if the Marquis left them.The next morning, having written his report the night before, Popinot went to the Palais de Justice to pursue prompt justice. As he prepared to put on his gown, the usher said the President wished to see him privately. “Good morning, my dear Popinot,” the President greeted. “I’ve been waiting.” Popinot asked if something was wrong. The President called it a silly trifle: the Keeper of the Seals, with whom he had dined, had suggested it would be best if Popinot did not sit on the case. Despite Popinot’s protest that he had left Madame d’Espard’s house before tea, the President cited propriety—“Caesar’s wife must not be suspected”—and the need to avoid any appearance of conflict that might expose the Bench to journalistic malice.It was to be a matter of propriety, not discipline. Popinot was to signal his retirement from the case as a personal favor; in recompense, the President would secure the Cross of the Legion of Honor for him, long deserved. M. Camusot, a newly appointed judge from a provincial court, had been named in Popinot’s place. Popinot noted with ironic amusement Camusot’s pale ambition. He bowed and withdrew, choosing not to contest the implication.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Dedicated to Lieutenant-Colonel Periollas, as a testimony of the author's esteem.Have you felt unease and sadness in the crowded galleries since the revolution of 1830? Balzac contends the true Salon has been overwhelmed by quantity. Where a strict selection once showcased the finest works and provoked debate—helping form reputations like Delacroix and Ingres—exhibitions now drown in numbers. Before 1817 only a few paintings were shown; today galleries overflow, individual works lose impact, shows close quickly, and the Salon’s role as arbiter of taste has become a tumultuous bazaar. Great artists are obscured by mediocrity, and talk of unrecognized genius grows when the Academy’s discerning judgment is absent.Amid this swelling catalogue stands Pierre Grassou, nicknamed “Fougeres.” In 1832 he lives in a cramped rue de Navarin apartment with a modest studio overlooking Montmartre. Tidy and careful, Grassou is a genre painter who avoids grand historical subjects, painting easel-sized works suited to his limited means.One December morning the dealer Elie Magus visits. Habitually buying Grassou’s pictures cheaply, Magus proposes a commission: paint portraits of the bourgeois Vervelle family, who wish to enter the art world. Their daughter brings a substantial dowry, and Magus hints that marriage might secure Grassou’s future. Startled by the suggestion, Grassou nevertheless sees in the commission a chance to establish himself as a portraitist and improve his status.Balzac traces Grassou’s past: he studied under masters like Servin and Schinner but never mastered their techniques; his education brought little recognition and frequent ridicule for mediocrity. Gentle and diligent, he failed to stand out. His first Salon attempt—a village-wedding scene in 1819—was rejected; Schinner told him to give up. Persisting, Grassou refined the piece and sold it to Magus for a meager sum, beginning a long career of perseverance.Grassou’s story embodies many contemporaries’ plight: ambition checked by limited talent, hopes of recognition, and the reality that circumstance and market forces often outweigh true merit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For several days Pierre Fougeres waited near Elie Magus's shop hoping his painting would attract notice, but it was ignored. After a week it disappeared; when Fougeres asked, Elie said he had sold it and would buy more if Fougeres produced Flemish interiors, anatomy lessons, and landscapes. Overjoyed, Fougeres felt validated and missed the ironic tone a more cynical artist might have noticed.He met his friend Joseph Bridau but, consumed with painting, could not enjoy the evening. Back home he sketched, bought supplies, hired models, and devoted himself to his craft. After two months he produced four paintings. Schinner and Bridau called them derivative and urged literature, but Fougeres retouched the canvases and sold them to Elie for twenty-five francs apiece, barely breaking even. He noticed a strange mist over his paintings in the shop but dismissed it.Over seven years he developed competent, if unremarkable, work. Elie sold the pieces, and Fougeres earned a modest living. At the 1829 Exhibition, with friends' help, he entered a striking piece—a young man in prison inspired by Gerard Dou—which drew attention even from Charles X. Madame bought the painting and commissioned more.Fougeres’s success rested more on imitation than innovation, yet his modesty, perseverance, punctuality, and steadiness won supporters. He furnished a studio, completed commissions reliably, and by thirty-seven had produced around two hundred paintings in a style that appealed to the bourgeoisie. Friends often preferred his character to his art and regretted his career choice, though he offered sound advice and maintained a keen appreciation for beauty.After the July Revolution he kept submitting to the Salon, lived frugally, and managed finances with his notary Cardot. He dreamed of a comfortable artistic life and aspired to join the Institute. One day the wealthy Vervelle family came for portraits; the father admired Fougeres’ Legion of Honor cross, the family liked his work, Pierre was charmed by their daughter Virginie, and they began to see him as a suitable match, blending personal hopes with professional ambitions.When Fougeres began painting Mademoiselle Virginie, Grassou had become part of the Vervelle household, treating their neat studio as a second home. Near the end of the sitting Joseph Bridau burst in, disheveled, begged five hundred francs to meet creditors, and criticized Grassou’s style, urging acceptance of nature’s imperfections. Bridau left; Madame Vervelle feared he had spoiled the portrait, but Grassou assured her he had improved it.Flattered, Grassou accepted an invitation to the Vervelles’ country house at Ville d’Avray. They lavished hospitality—admiring grounds, a grand dinner, and fawning praise as if he were celebrated—then proudly showed their gallery, certain it rivaled the king’s. Examining the canvases, Grassou was shocked to find many were his own works, long since sold to the dealer Elie Magus. Vervelle boasted the collection’s value at three hundred thousand francs; Grassou dryly noted he had sold those same pictures for far less. Vervelle promised to double his daughter’s dowry if Grassou could prove the claim. The revelation raised Grassou’s standing; when he later completed their portraits he gifted them, securing their favor.Today Pierre Grassou is a competent portrait painter, earning about twenty thousand francs a year, living on his wife’s six-thousand-franc dowry, painting bourgeois portraits for five hundred francs apiece, and even holding the Legion of Honor. Yet he remains insecure—sure other artists mock him and critics ignore him—still craving Academy recognition and secretly swapping weaker works in the Ville d’Avray gallery for those by celebrated painters. His mediocrity, tempered by kindness and generosity, renders him complex and oddly sympathetic.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers; translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley. Dedication: To Maria.In some provincial towns a cloistered melancholy hangs over certain houses; silence seems to freeze life, broken only by a solitary figure at a window. Such an air brooded over a house in Saumur, down a steep, little-used street of pebble pavement and three-century-old buildings. These wooden houses, prized by antiquarians, show great carved oaken beams, slate façades, worn window-sills with neglected flower-pots, and heavy studded doors bearing traces of history—Protestant marks or forgotten bourgeois insignia—so that the history of France seems recorded in wood and stone.Beside a dilapidated dwelling stands a country gentleman’s mansion; its stone arch faintly shows armorial bearings eroded by revolutions since 1789. The ground floors are not modern shops but plain dark rooms where goods lie exposed; a girl knitting will call her parents to serve customers. The local climate governs commerce: merchants, wine-growers, and coopers watch the weather anxiously, fearing frost or rain that could ruin them. On Saturdays vineyards call their owners away, streets fall silent, and neighbors observe one another’s doings.The most melancholy mansion belonged to Monsieur Grandet. To understand the house one must know the man. Grandet began life as a master cooper in 1789 and married the daughter of a rich wood merchant. With savings and his wife’s dowry he bought prime vineyard land during the sale of church property and gained a reputation as protector of local nobility and supplier to Republican armies. By shrewd investments in vineyards and estates—and by 1806 serving as mayor—he steadily increased his fortune. That year he also inherited three fortunes, adding vast vineyards, farms, and an abbey to his holdings, making him one of the region’s wealthiest men.Townspeople speculated about the extent of his riches; he had a knack for timing sales to secure maximum profit, and his financial cunning made him both admired and feared. By 1816 his fortune was estimated at nearly four million francs, and many believed he held as much again in cash. Former ridicule faded as his acts took on the weight of community law. Frugality governed his life; he spent only for necessities.Grandet’s manner was plain and unpretentious. He spoke little and stuttered in prolonged conversation—an awkwardness that concealed calculation. He never committed without consulting his wife, whom he kept dependent. Stout, round-faced, and crafty in appearance, he dressed simply.Only a handful entered his house, among them the young magistrate C. de Bonfons, who hoped to win Grandet’s daughter Eugénie. The town’s social life turned on competition for her—chiefly between the powerful Cruchot family, who sought her for their nephew, and the des Grassins, who wanted her for their son Adolphe. In early 1811 the Cruchots gained advantage: when the estate of Froidfond was sold they negotiated its purchase for Grandet, who paid in gold—an ostentatious display that confirmed his wealth and influence throughout Saumur.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It is clear what is meant by “the house of Monsieur Grandet”: a cold, silent dwelling above the town, sheltered by ruined ramparts. The entrance, with two pillars and a tufa arch—soft white stone that barely lasts two centuries—shows weathered holes that give the façade a jail-like look. Above the arch is a crumbling bas-relief of the four seasons, topped by a plinth where plants, even a small cherry tree, have taken root.The solid oak door is split and bolted with iron. A small rusted grating in the middle panel lets curious visitors peer into a dark vault leading to a garden enclosed by thick damp walls, remnants of the ramparts bordering neighboring plots.The principal ground-floor room serves as antechamber, salon, office, boudoir and dining room. Wood-paneled and gray with exposed beams, it has two street-facing windows, a white stone chimney topped by an old brass clock and a greenish mirror, and antique chairs in faded tapestry. In each corner stand closets or buffets with dirty shelves. Between the windows sits an old card table with a barometer and two pastel portraits of Madame Grandet’s ancestors. Red gros de Tours curtains—a rare luxury—contradict Monsieur Grandet’s stinginess. A straw chair by the window lets Madame Grandet watch passersby; a work table and Eugenie’s armchair complete the room.For fifteen years Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet’s lives have been governed by strict economy: fires lit only on specified dates, the foot-warmer used through the cold months. La Grande Nanon, the family servant for thirty-five years, earned only sixty francs a year yet managed to invest in an annuity. Unattractive but indispensable, she cooked, washed and ran domestic affairs with tireless devotion; Grandet’s rare favors—an old watch, a pair of stockings—were treated as monumental largesse.During the difficult 1811 grape harvest, Grandet’s relationship with Nanon combined dependence and domination: he relied on her labor while maintaining strict authority, and she, grateful for the employment and meager kindnesses, remained devoted. Their bond was one of power and dependence rather than affection.Nanon’s domain contrasted with the rest of the house. Her kitchen was a model of cleanliness and order; she worked late and slept in a small closet-like space, while the upper floors remained bare and unadorned compared with the hall’s modest comforts.On a November evening in 1819, Nanon lit the fire for the first time that season, coinciding with Eugenie’s birthday. The household prepared for a gathering as local notables arrived. Grandet preserved the ritual of giving Eugenie a gold coin; Madame Grandet presented a new dress. Grandet urged Eugenie to save for her future marriage, underscoring the family’s thrift and fixed habits.Guests, including the des Grassins family, arrived. Madame des Grassins, lively and pushy, and her son Adolphe presented gifts that provoked jealousy. Compliments and attention masked rivalry and calculation; beneath laughter, attendees subtly vied for a share of Grandet’s wealth. Grandet watched with pride and possessiveness over his daughter, manipulating relationships for his benefit. The evening blended familial warmth with cold calculation—until a loud knock at the door signaled an unexpected interruption that would change the night.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nanon lit a candle and opened the door; Monsieur Grandet followed. His wife called anxiously, the players exchanged uneasy looks, and Monsieur des Grassins suggested leaving. A young man arrived with a porter and trunks. Grandet brusquely told his wife to return to the game and went out to speak with the newcomer.The players sat but did not resume. Madame des Grassins asked if he was from Saumur; her husband said Paris. The notary checked the hour; Abbé Cruchot inquired if the gentleman was young. Eugénie noticed Nanon’s absence; others guessed the newcomer might be a relative. Madame Grandet urged continuation, aware of her husband’s irritation. Adolphe hinted the young man could be a cousin but was silenced.Grandet returned alone; the traveler entered and received polite bows and curtsies. Madame Grandet offered warmth, but Grandet, absorbed in a letter, dismissed concern. The stranger, Charles Grandet, thanked Eugénie but said he was not tired. Adjusting his eyeglass, Charles surveyed the room and the loto game. At twenty-two, handsome and confident, he intended to impress provincial relatives with Parisian polish. Sent by his father to stay with his uncle in Saumur, he displayed fashionable dress and urban ease: a hunting costume, several waistcoats, a gold toilet set—and, despite instructions to travel modestly, he had taken a private coupé to flaunt his means.His elegance sharply contrasted with the provincials. The shabby Cruchots were taken aback; locals met his nonchalance with simultaneous admiration and disdain. Eugénie was captivated, longing for the world he represented; enchanted by his appearance and delicate possessions, she yearned to connect.The loto ended when Nanon returned for sheets for Charles’s bed; Madame Grandet followed. Guests, sensing the evening’s close, began to leave. Eugénie, seized by impulse, excused herself to help prepare his room, suggesting small refinements—a warming pan, a wax candle—using her birthday as an excuse to persuade Nanon.While Eugénie fussed, Madame des Grassins engaged Charles, vying for his attention and hinting at local prospects. Abbé Cruchot made sly remarks exposing ambitions. Grandet, who had read a letter from his brother about impending bankruptcy and asking him to protect his son, masked his emotions. When guests departed, Grandet offered Charles a modest room, downplaying his wealth. The evening closed with speculation: Charles’s presence might unsettle Saumur. The Cruchots and des Grassins, seeing a common rival, resolved to unite against any attachment between Eugénie and her cousin and to manipulate the situation to their advantage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the four relatives were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew Charles it was too late for business and set the household hours: breakfast at eight, a light lunch at noon, dinner at five. He professed poverty despite appearances, insisting his fortune came from hard work. He told Nanon to bring candles; Charles at first refused but accepted one so worn it looked like tallow.Grandet led him through the house, avoiding the cold stairwell; as they climbed Charles’s expectations fell—the staircase and decor were shabby, and he wondered why his father had sent him. On the landing Charles noticed the doors, especially one walled up that led to Grandet’s office — the secret room where Grandet did business and hoarded wealth. Across from it, Eugenie, Grandet’s daughter, sat in her room; after a brief goodnight she and her mother retired. Grandet showed Charles to his attic room, warning him to call Nanon if needed because the dog could be dangerous. Nanon returned with a warming pan, which Grandet dismissed with a grumble as he left.Alone, Charles surveyed the shabby surroundings and questioned his father’s intentions; he felt out of place and uncertain of his purpose. Meanwhile Eugenie rose early with a new sense of beauty and longing. Dressing, she admired herself but felt inadequate beside her cousin.
