The garden and courtyard, once dull, now seemed charming; her heart swelled with unfamiliar emotions toward Charles and she longed to impress him.Eugenie hurried to Nanon, asking her to prepare cream for Charles’s breakfast. Nanon reminded her they needed Grandet’s permission for provisions. When Grandet entered, he and Nanon quarreled over supplies, exposing his miserly nature; reluctantly he agreed to give some flour and butter for a galette.After breakfast Grandet took Eugenie to the fields, where they met Cruchot, the notary. Grandet spoke of his land and the recent sale of poplars, revealing his calculating side. Cruchot mentioned gossip that Charles might be a suitor for Eugenie. Grandet reacted vehemently, declaring he would rather throw his daughter into the Loire than marry her to her cousin — words that shattered Eugenie’s hopes.Back at the house, news arrived of Grandet’s brother’s suicide — a development with dire consequences for Charles. The subsequent meal was heavy with unspoken grief. Eugenie, moved and tearful, felt deep sympathy; Grandet remained indifferent. Despite the risk of provoking him, Eugenie and Nanon conspired to prepare Charles a better breakfast, and her affection for him deepened as she worked.After two hours’ work Eugenie prepared a simple breakfast that broke the family’s custom of a standing midday meal of bread, fruit, and wine. She set the table and placed an armchair for her cousin Charles, anxious about her father’s reaction; Madame Grandet promised to take responsibility if he returned.Charles arrived in Parisian fashion, cheerful despite a difficult journey. He said he seldom ate before midday but welcomed the meal and joked about wanting a partridge. As they sat, his charm captivated Eugenie; she admired his elegance and felt a flutter of affection. Their conversation emphasized the contrast between his life of luxury and her modest upbringing. When Nanon brought eggs, Charles praised Eugenie, making her blush.He showed Eugenie his ring and their fingers briefly touched, deepening the spark. The mood shifted when Nanon brought coffee: Charles called it outdated, and the women exchanged anxious glances, fearing Monsieur Grandet’s return.When Grandet entered he surveyed the scene with suspicion, noting the breakfast and especially the sugar, and demanded its source. Charles asked for sugar for his coffee, but Grandet rebuffed him, suggesting more milk instead. Eugenie, quietly defiant, returned the sugar to the table.Grandet’s demeanor stiffened as he spoke to Charles, then revealed devastating news: Charles’s father had died by suicide, leaving him bankrupt and penniless. The revelation shattered Charles; he broke down in tears. Eugenie and her mother felt deep compassion, while Grandet remained detached and immediately focused on the financial consequences, lecturing Eugenie about the dishonor of bankruptcy.Grandet declared Charles must go to the West Indies to seek fortune. Eugenie offered to help; Grandet reluctantly agreed to fund the journey. The house remained heavy with grief as Charles mourned; Eugenie urged him to preserve his honor and resolved to share his sorrow—her life irrevocably changed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About four o’clock a sharp knock at the door startled Madame Grandet.Grandet burst in, rubbing his hands. He announced that he had “trapped them all”: the wine was sold to the Dutch and Belgians at two hundred francs a puncheon, half paid down. He showed six louis for his wife and predicted that prices would fall in three months. News of the sale spread panic through Saumur. Eugenie, excited, asked if he had a thousand puncheons. He confirmed it; she quickly calculated the sum in small coins and asked, “Then, father, you can easily help Charles.” The neglected nephew’s name stung Grandet. He scolded Eugenie for presuming to advise him, insisting that any duty to Charles would be discharged on his terms and threatening to send her to the Abbaye des Noyers with Nanon if she meddled.When Madame Grandet said Charles had not come down, Grandet asked why. Eugenie answered that he was weeping for his father. Momentarily bewildered, Grandet withdrew to his private room to consider investing the proceeds in public funds. His thinning forest had already brought six hundred thousand francs; with this sale his total reached nine hundred thousand. Though tempted by the idea of twenty percent returns, he rationalized a more modest eight percent, imagining that in two years he might withdraw fifteen hundred thousand in gold. Nanon’s call to dinner interrupted his calculations; he accepted the eight percent plan, murmuring that hunger would bring his nephew from grief.At table he learned Charles refused to eat; he commented curtly, and Nanon approved—“So much saved.” When Madame Grandet suggested mourning, Grandet mocked the expense and told her to buy her mourning out of her six louis: a hat-band would suffice. Eugenie sewed in silence while he ran through arithmetic. The town buzzed about the sale; Nanon kept spinning, and Grandet repeated his maxim, “Nothing should be wasted,” as he pursued more schemes.That night Grandet, preoccupied with present gain and dismissive of any future life, revealed how money governed his laws, politics, and morals. He bargained with conscience and religion, ordered Madame Grandet to her prayers, and finally growled, “The devil take your good God!” He visited Charles’s dark room to berate him gently in his own miserly fashion: he allowed the nephew to weep as natural, insisted he was a “good uncle,” offered a little glass of wine, and scoffed at the theft of a small wax candle. Madame Grandet and Eugenie, overhearing, fled in fear.Alone, Madame Grandet prayed for Grandet and fretted over the punishment he had threatened for Eugenie should she interfere. Eugenie, meanwhile, lay restless; her concern for Charles swelled into a personal disturbance. In the early hours she rose and found him asleep in an armchair, exhausted by grief; his face, smoothed by sorrow, touched her in a way she barely understood. She watched him with a feeling that made her both ashamed and secretly happy. He woke, apologized; she urged him to bed and fled, fearful of what he might think. This innocent boldness marked a turning point: she feared his judgment yet hoped he would see love in her visit.The next morning she and her mother waited anxiously for Grandet. He arrived distracted but outwardly affectionate, kissed Eugenie, and sat at breakfast as if forgetting his harsh threats. “What has become of my nephew? The lad gives no trouble,” he said; Nanon replied he was asleep. “So much the better; he won’t want a wax candle,” Grandet mocked. His unexpected softness surprised Madame Grandet, but it was only surface; Grandet, a poor sleeper, had spent the night calculating schemes to enlarge his fortune and dominate others, equating imposition with power.That day he contrived a way to restore his brother’s honor without spending a franc: to save Charles by manipulating public opinion in Paris, a sport of prudence for him. In his absence, Eugenie found tender pleasure in caring for her cousin. She prepared his breakfast with self-forgetting devotion, deciding to bring it to his room to spare him the shame of eating before her father. Her anticipation and care lit her day; the warmth and attention of the provincial household—so different from the indifference Charles had known in Paris—moved him. Eugenie’s innocence and genuine affection drew him closer; they shared an unspoken understanding.Life went on around this quiet intimacy: Grandet continued his calculations, Nanon spun, and later Grandet invited the Cruchots to dinner. Yet beneath the household routine, Eugenie’s tenderness and Charles’s gratitude suggested changes that money alone could not govern, hinting at the complex obligations that would shape their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Saumur was more stirred by the dinner Grandet gave the Cruchots than by the previous night’s sale of his vintage, which the wine-growers had seen as a betrayal. Grandet, who fancied himself superior to those he deceived, paid little heed to their opinion.When the des Grassins learned of Guillaume Grandet’s failure and death, they came that evening to offer sympathy and to learn why he had invited the Cruchots. At five o’clock Monsieur C. de Bonfons and his uncle, the notary, arrived in their Sunday best. The dinner was sombre: Grandet grave, Charles silent, Eugenie mute, Madame Grandet speaking little—a fitting meal of condolence. Afterwards Charles asked to retire to write a letter.Once the women left, Grandet displayed the craftiness that earned him the nickname “the old dog.” Using a practiced stutter as an affectation, he controlled and provoked his guests while steering conversation toward bankruptcy. The president of the commercial court explained that a respected merchant facing insolvency might have a receiver appointed, distinguishing liquidation from absolute ruin; sometimes liquidation could be managed without the courts: if the merchant did not file his schedule and no creditor appeared, the family or heirs could liquidate the business. Debts could also be bought at a discount so that, if titles to all debts were acquired, the heirs would owe nothing.The notary urged this course as a way to save Grandet’s reputation. Mindful of family standing and his nephew’s prospects, Grandet weighed the advantages and professed ignorance of Parisian tricks, saying he tended only to vineyards and local affairs. The president offered to go to Paris to deal with creditors and suggested Grandet might provide some assets to facilitate negotiations.Before he committed, the des Grassins declared they intended to liquidate the brother’s debts honorably; the banker applauded, and Madame des Grassins even offered to go to Paris, competing with the president. Favoring the banker, Grandet pulled him aside to discuss a separate investment in government bonds and asked him to say nothing of these plans to the Cruchots—discretion to avoid annoyance. They agreed to meet the next day for final instructions.As word of his supposed generosity spread, the town quickly forgave the vintage sale and admired Grandet again. Once the house was closed, he ordered Nanon to keep silent and prepared a secret trip to sell gold in Angers while the market was favorable. In the early hours, Eugenie, worried about Charles, thought she heard a cry but was distracted by her father and Nanon carrying a heavy keg. Grandet told Nanon to tell his wife he had gone to the country and hurried off in the carriage, determined to reach Angers before morning.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“My father has gone,” Eugenie thought as she listened from the top of the stairs. The house lay hushed; the carriage’s rumble faded and a cry from her cousin’s room cut the silence. A thin thread of light from the doorway lit the old staircase.“He suffers!” she cried, running up. Charles sat in an armchair, head bowed, a pen fallen from his hand; his breathing was labored. On the table she saw sealed letters to tradesmen—signs he was settling affairs to leave France—and two open letters. The address “My dear Annette” struck her to the core. Rooted, she felt shame and curiosity; curiosity won. She adjusted his head, kissed his hair, and read.Charles’s letter to Annette explained that misfortune had ruined him: his father had killed himself, their fortune was gone, and he was penniless. He could not remain in Paris where the scorn for a ruined man would be intolerable. He would not ask Annette to sacrifice comforts to keep him; better to part. He planned to try his fortune in the Indies or America—perhaps to go to sea—and might return aged and altered; he urged Annette to treasure their happiness but accepted that he could not demand fidelity, for marriage or new ties might become necessities. The letter ended abruptly, as if his hand had left the page.Eugenie excused the coldness in the words, convincing herself a girl in love might not see its selfishness. Young and pure, she read passion into his phrases; “My dear Annette” became music to her. She mourned his misfortune and thought, “I have gold; I will give it to him.”The second letter, to his friend Alphonse, asked him to settle affairs: sell possessions, collect whatever could be recovered, keep his horse Briton if possible, press builders to delay delivery of an unpaid carriage, and pay off debts, including six louis lost at cards. It showed a practical, calculating mind intent on avoiding dishonor and salvaging what remained.Overwhelmed with compassion and admiration, Eugenie ran to her room and opened the old oak cabinet where she had hidden a red velvet purse—a grandmother’s relic. Inside were coins: various foreign gold pieces, rare rupees—about five thousand eight hundred francs in all. Joy and resolve filled her. Secret love for her cousin and pity for his plight dissolved propriety. She returned upstairs at once, candle and purse in hand.Charles was awake, stunned at seeing her. Eugenie, trembling, confessed she had read the letters and then, without hesitation, offered him her savings. She called herself a poor girl who wanted nothing, a sister needing to help a cousin. Kneeling, she implored him to accept her gold and, by taking it, to show that he respected her and was generous. Tears fell from Charles onto her hands; moved, she poured the coins onto the table. “You will be rich,” she cried; “this gold will bring you happiness; someday you shall return it to me—are we not partners?”Charles could not refuse. He rose, kissed her, and finally revealed he would reciprocate not with money but with an object he held dear: a leather dressing-case containing, in a hidden drawer, two portraits by Madame Mirbel, set with pearls—his mother and his father, who were Eugenie’s aunt and uncle. He declared the case more precious to him than life, and begged Eugenie to be its guardian. If she guarded it and, in the worst event his fortune vanished, the pearls and gold in the case would repay her; yet he demanded that it ultimately be destroyed to prevent its passing into other hands. This exchange of trust and sacred confidence marked a turning point; Eugenie looked at him with her first deep, womanly affection. He kissed her hand, calling her an “angel of purity,” and vowed that feeling and sentiment must be everything between them henceforth.They exchanged gentle words and good-nights. Eugenie, practical and blushing, suggested Charles keep the treasures in her old cabinet. He sighed in his mourning but kissed her hand and, as she closed the door, he murmured the ruin of his fortunes. “What matter? My father is rich; I think so,” she answered naively. He scoffed; she retorted about Froidfond and Noyers, and he dismissed them as poor farms. The exchange was sweet and foolish: from that moment, beneath his black, Charles began to wear roses.Next morning, before breakfast, Madame Grandet found Eugenie and Charles together in the garden. He remained sorrowful; the three women of the house, respectful of his grief, allowed him the morning in the hall while they tended chores and awaited tradesmen for neglected business. Around five, Monsieur Grandet returned from Angers, unexpectedly jubilant, having made fourteen thousand francs on an exchange of gold. He had left his groom at Angers to look after the exhausted horses.“I am hungry,” he proclaimed upon entering. Nanon scolded him for not eating. Shortly Des Grassins arrived to take Grandet’s orders. Grandet barely registered his nephew’s presence, absorbed in his financial success and amused to baffle others. Des Grassins, the banker, informed him that gold fetched high prices in Angers and that people had come from Nantes; he talked of sending theirs. Grandet curtly refused, hinting at giving his old friend respite from wasted exertion. A startled consultation between the two men followed; they glanced often at Charles. Des Grassins learned that Grandet intended to invest a sum that might yield—so it was suggested—an enormous return; talk even floated of producing a hundred thousand francs a year, though the deputies’ figures and Grandet’s actual plans remained shrouded in his dry, cautious talk.Des Grassins took the moment to offer to go to Paris and asked Charles if he had any commissions. Charles declined stiffly. Des Grassins rebuked the nephew for the curt reply; he reminded him of family ties and honor. Charles, pale, kissed his uncle and withdrew. Eugenie watched her father with secretly swelling admiration for his mastery over affairs.Grandet dismissed Des Grassins with a jocular “decoy those people; lead them by the nose,” and the two men shook hands. Grandet resumed his armchair and asked Nanon for black-current ratafia. His excitement made him fidget at first and then sing snatches of a ditty, pacing in what Nanon called “his dancing steps.” The women exchanged uneasy glances at his towering contentment; his gaiety frightened them when it rose to a peak. He chose to go to bed early, expecting others to follow his lead. Madame Grandet, caught in his current, lived according to his will.During the two hours before retiring, Grandet grew more talkative and philosophical about money, offering peculiar maxims and showing the mindset that governed his life: “You have no sooner put your lips to a glass than it is empty! Such is life. You can’t have and hold. Gold won’t circulate and stay in your purse. If it were not for that, life would be too fine.” His cheerfulness came with small generosity—offering Nanon a share of ratafia—and an undertone of the calculation that made him formidable. The evening passed under a mood blending domestic routine, latent anxieties about affairs, and the quiet bloom of a new intimacy between Eugenie and Charles, sealed by her sacrifice and his trust.Thus the house resumed its ordinary hum: tradesmen, household tasks, and the unspoken commerce of feelings and fortunes. Eugenie’s secret gift and Charles’s confided treasures had altered their relation; Grandet’s profit from the Angers exchange had reawakened his habit of managing and tightening the household’s finances. Between these threads—Eugenie’s selfless devotion and Grandet’s thrifty triumph—Balzac sketches the beginnings of passions, calculations, and the mingling of sentiment with the sober arithmetic of money that will shape the family’s future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning the family breakfasted together in a private, intimate mood. Grief had drawn Madame Grandet, Eugenie, and Charles closer; even Nanon felt sympathy. The old man, absorbed in thrift and intent on sending Charles away to avoid expense, stayed aloof, busy with farm work and leaving Madame Grandet to watch over Eugenie and Charles as their relationship quietly deepened.For Eugenie, love grew from their shared secret. Their bond took on the simplicity and tenderness of childhood: soft glances, gentle words, and small consolations. Eugenie delighted in comforting Charles, soothing his pain with the sweetness of new love; Charles, hardened by worldly passions, found in her a pure, sincere attachment and prized the modest, unpretentious life they shared. While Grandet worked in the fields after breakfast, Charles savored the company of Eugenie and her mother, charmed by virtues he had once thought limited to Germany. Eugenie became his ideal; she surrendered to their love without foreseeing the looming separation.Three days after the des Grassins left, Grandet took Charles to the civil courts to renounce his claim to his father’s estate—a legal act that felt like a painful betrayal. Charles signed powers of attorney, obtained a passport, and sold his belongings to prepare for departure. Grandet admired Charles’s new appearance in mourning and regarded him as ready to seek his fortune. When Charles gathered valuables to sell, Grandet offered to buy them, valuing them at nearly a thousand francs; Charles accepted with gratitude and gave his mother’s thimble to Madame Grandet. Grandet then insisted on paying for Charles’s passage to the Indies, a generosity that paradoxically cost him little.As Charles prepared to leave, Eugenie confessed her sorrow; he acknowledged his obligations and his coming-of-age. The family wore mourning for a Requiem Mass for Guillaume Grandet, and Charles received letters from Paris about his affairs, which he shared with Eugenie. In the garden he assured her his affairs were in order, that his effects would be sent to Nantes, and urged her not to tie her future to his uncertain fate. Still, their love held firm and they kissed passionately.On the day of departure Grandet unexpectedly packed Charles’s things for Nantes. The family saw him off at the diligence; after he left the house felt empty. Eugenie, determined to preserve his memory, kept his room unchanged and conspired with Nanon. Her beauty grew more radiant and dignified as she followed his voyage on a map and confided her constant love only to Nanon, who, though resigned, deeply empathized.Two months later the once-monotonous life of Eugénie and her mother was consumed by the secret that bound them: Charles filled their thoughts, and Eugénie even looked for him in her aunt’s portrait. One Sunday Madame Grandet discovered the truth about the gold Eugénie had given Charles, and panic followed.“What will you say to your father on New Year’s Day when he asks to see your gold?” Madame cried. They spent a morning in dread, missed high Mass, and feared the coming confrontation. Madame, unprepared for winter and chilled by her husband’s impending wrath, suggested consulting Monsieur des Grassins in Paris, but Eugénie insisted it was too late.On the cold morning of January 1, 1820, Madame asked for a fire in her room to avoid the confrontation. Grandet, unexpectedly cheerful, agreed and revealed he had received a large sum from investments. Boasting over breakfast and unaware of their anxiety, his polite New Year wishes only deepened their unease.After breakfast he demanded to see Eugénie’s gold and ordered its return. Cornered, she said she no longer had it. His anger flared; he accused her of deceit and pressed to know what she’d done with the money. Eugénie defended herself and asserted her independence; he threatened confinement on bread and water. Heartbroken, she fled to her mother, who pleaded for her, but Grandet remained obstinate. Nanon, fearing for Eugénie, vowed support and that evening secretly brought a hare pâté, strengthening their bond as they faced Grandet’s harshness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For months the old wine-grower tended his ailing wife while never mentioning their daughter Eugénie, who remained confined to her room at his orders. Madame Grandet’s health declined as her husband grew harsher and even began to err in business. Townspeople learned Eugénie was given only bread and water, with Nanon secretly bringing extra food; the community condemned Grandet, and Eugénie’s gentle attendance at Mass drew sympathy and curiosity.Eugénie’s chief sorrow was her mother’s deterioration, for which she often blamed herself. Madame Grandet tried to comfort her and urged reconciliation, begging her husband to restore their daughter to the family, but he remained indifferent as his wife weakened. In spring Madame confided in the Cruchots, who were appalled and discussed possible legal consequences of Eugénie’s confinement. Eugénie overheard but, fearing scandal, begged them to stay silent and resolved to obey her father for the family’s honor.One day the notary Cruchot warned Grandet that if Madame died Eugénie’s inheritance might force sale of property and threaten his control. Pressed by this, Grandet began to soften. Returning home to reconcile, he found Eugénie and her mother admiring a gold dressing-case from Charles. Possessive at the sight, he confronted her; Eugénie demanded it be returned and, in despair, threatened self-harm. Shocked, Grandet relented, allowed her to keep the case, scattered coins on the bed, and promised kindness. Despite this brief gentleness, Madame Grandet died peacefully in October 1822, urging Eugénie to seek happiness in heaven and fearing for her in a selfish world.The day after her father’s death Eugenie felt a renewed attachment to her childhood home, where she had endured much suffering, including her mother’s death; familiar objects moved her to tears. Her father’s sudden tenderness—taking her to breakfast and watching her with an almost kind gaze—made the household suspect his mind was failing. But at the mourning dinner he revealed his plan.“Maitre” Cruchot explained Grandet did not want to divide property or pay taxes on supposed funds; to avoid this he needed Eugenie to renounce her rights so he could retain control. Confused, Eugenie asked for the deed to sign. Grandet, visibly moved, instead proposed she trust him and accept a monthly allowance of a hundred francs. She agreed; he clasped her hand and declared they were quits. The next morning she signed away her inheritance. A year later he had not paid the promised money; when she reminded him he awkwardly offered jewels.Over the next two years Grandet trained Eugenie in running the household and managing finances, instilling his frugality. By the third year she became mistress of the house, and their monotonous life continued for five years. Eugenie’s sadness was well known though she never spoke of it. Her only company was Nanon and the Cruchots with their circle, who played whist with her father.In 1827 Grandet, now eighty-two, suffered a stroke. As he declined Eugenie devoted herself to his care, feeling increasingly isolated. His death was uneventful; to the end he remained fixated on hidden wealth. When the priest came for last rites, Grandet’s avarice surfaced in his final words, urging Eugenie to take care of his riches.After his death Eugenie was left alone with only Nanon, her loyal servant and confidante. Maitre Cruchot then informed her she had inherited seventeen million francs. Despite this fortune, her thoughts centered on finding her cousin Charles. In the empty house she and Nanon lamented their isolation while news of her wealth spread through the province.Eugenie’s first act was to secure an annuity for Nanon, who soon married Antoine Cornoiller and became Eugenie’s housekeeper. Cornoiller managed the estate; life for the farmers continued unchanged. At thirty Eugenie had never known joy; her childhood had been shadowed by her mother’s suffering and an unfulfilled love for Charles, who had left her with only memories. Mourning delayed thoughts of marriage. The Cruchots flattered her; Monsieur de Bonfons ingratiated himself, Madame des Grassins pushed a match with the Marquis de Froidfond, and Eugenie’s indifference was misread as interest—all while Charles had not contacted her in seven years.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While events unfolded in Saumur, Charles prospered in the Indies. His early ventures yielded six thousand dollars, and he soon learned the slave trade was the most profitable. He went to Africa, purchased enslaved people, and combined that traffic with other lucrative goods, intent on returning to Paris rich and restoring his social standing.Experience hardened him. He became skeptical and amoral, convinced one society’s condemnation might be another’s praise. He grew ruthless—engaging in exploitative trades, customs fraud, and reselling cheaply bought pirate goods. Memories of Eugénie faded; he viewed his family with contempt and regarded her as little more than a creditor. To conceal his identity he adopted the alias “Shepherd.” His efforts paid: by 1827 he returned to Bordeaux aboard the Marie Caroline with nearly two million francs in gold-dust, planning further profit at the Paris mint.On the ship he met Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman with royal connections, returning with his family. Once prominent, the d’Aubrions were now strained financially. Madame d’Aubrion, anxious to secure her family’s future, sought a wealthy husband for her plain daughter. She saw Charles as suitable and promised to help him gain a title and a comfortable income through marriage, elevating both families into Parisian comfort.Intoxicated by ambition, Charles entertained the match. Annette encouraged him, noting the advantage for her friend of an influential, affluent husband. Des Grassins, aware of Charles’s return and pending marriage, asked him to settle his father’s debts; newly wealthy, Charles refused, saying his father’s failures were no longer his problem.In August, Eugénie, sitting where they once pledged their love, received a trembling letter from Charles recounting his success, announcing the engagement as a pragmatic step to status, and declaring love in marriage a delusion. Devastated but resigned, she recalled her mother’s warning and resolved to a life of prayer and acceptance.Eugenie returned from the garden cautiously, avoiding the corridor haunted by memories of her cousin. The day was pivotal: the parish priest—connected to the Cruchots and allied with Monsieur de Bonfons—had been urged to speak to her about marriage. Consumed by unhappiness, Eugenie confessed a desire to withdraw from the world and live only for God. The priest warned against such a drastic step, reminding her of duties to society and the poor.Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by vengeance and despair, to reveal that Charles Grandet had returned from the Indies and was engaged to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Eugenie masked her shock. Madame des Grassins produced a letter from her husband outlining Charles’s situation, including his father’s bankruptcy and the marriage’s consequences. Contempt and sorrow filled Eugenie; she returned the letter unread.Tormented, Eugenie asked the priest whether remaining a virgin after marriage would be a sin, exposing her inner conflict. She secluded herself in her father’s secret room, skipped dinner, and avoided guests. Later, curiosity charged the evening when she unexpectedly asked Monsieur de Bonfons to stay—an act taken as a pledge of marriage.Privately, Eugenie told the president she could give only friendship and asked him to settle her uncle’s debts in exchange for her hand. The eager president accepted. He hurried to Paris, met creditors, and arranged payment with interest. Carrying a letter from Eugenie to Charles explaining her decision and its conditions, he informed Charles, who was initially excited but then shocked by the implications when he learned of Eugenie’s fortune. Madame d’Aubrion, unaware of the president’s interventions, remained confident of the marriage.The president returned to Saumur and announced his marriage to Eugenie. Though wealthy, she lived simply and devoted herself to charity. Her husband entered politics but died soon after being elected deputy. Widowed, Eugenie lived piously, dedicating her fortune to the community and rejecting worldly desires. Rumors linked her to the Marquis de Froidfond, but she remained independent, supported by loyal servants Nanon and Cornoiller. Her story reveals the tension between wealth and personal fulfillment, showing her struggle to meet social expectations while staying true to her values.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:Dear Child,—You, the joy of the household, whose pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of Wierzschovnia, how can I dedicate to you a story full of melancholy? Yet should not sorrows be spoken of to a young girl like you, since the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is difficult to find a subject worthy of your eyes, so I have no choice; perhaps you will feel fortunate when you read this story sent to you by your old friend, De Balzac.PIERRETTEI. THE LORRAINSOn an October morning in 1827 a sixteen-year-old boy in modest clothes stood in a small square of Lower Provins, taking in working mills and simple bourgeois houses framed by linden trees. He recognized the house he sought: a recently restored white-stone dwelling with gray blinds and yellow iron balconies, its neatness betraying the pride of a retired petty shopkeeper.As the sun lit a calico curtain in a garret window, the boy began a Breton ditty for newlyweds, a song that stirred memories of Brittany and life’s bittersweet turns. A girl, Pierrette, signaled from the window; he offered a yellow broom-flower. He was Brigaut, her childhood companion, and he spoke of settling nearby. Pierrette, alarmed, urged him to flee when a grotesque old maid below—sharp-eyed and ready to imagine scandal—appeared at a window.Overwhelmed by Brigaut’s visit, Pierrette reflected on her past. Seated in a shabby armchair, frail and thoughtful, she recalled childhood joys now tinged by illness and uncertainty. Her mother, born a Demoiselle Auffray, came from a tangled family—her father had married twice and left a complicated inheritance. Her mother later married Major Lorrain, a Breton officer who died in battle, leaving the family impoverished; the Lorrains could not care for Pierrette.Ultimately she was sent to live with her uncle and aunt, the Rogrons, in Provins. Unaware of the full intricacies of her lineage and the consequences of her parents’ fortunes and losses, Pierrette finds her fate increasingly shaped by these relatives. The Rogrons’ control sets the stage for the drama to follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Père Rogron, an innkeeper in Provins, was a ruddy-faced, indulgent short man. He and his unattractive, selfish wife lived roughly on modest proceeds from their land and inn, then moved into a dilapidated house inherited from old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother. Their children, Sylvie and Jérôme-Denis, were neglected and ill-bred, their features coarsened and voices harsh. Rogron sent them to Paris to work in shops with little support.Sylvie apprenticed on rue Saint-Denis and became a forewoman; Jérôme rose to head-clerk. They met rarely and pooled savings to buy the retail “Family Sister.” They had some success, but competition and severity made them notorious.Sylvie, shrewd though plain, ran the business effectively; Jérôme was unsuited to broader trade. Their lives revolved around the shop, leaving them oblivious beyond it. Their stinginess and poor treatment drove high clerk turnover. Sylvie was a pleasant saleswoman to customers and a sour spinster at home.While mourning their father, they received a letter about cousin Pierrette’s inheritance. They considered selling and returning to Provins but refused to take Pierrette in to protect their wealth, delayed replying, and left for Provins four years before Brigaut arrived.A petty shopkeeper returns from Paris to his provincial town with a few Parisian airs that soon fade as provincial life reasserts itself. The transition to the bourgeois feels like an illness: Paris’s vibrancy yields to local stillness. With newfound wealth, the Rogrons—brother and sister—indulge desires like travel, politics, and hunting, and remodel their house to mimic wealthier families.Their renovations advertise ambition; the contractor even consults them on elite details. Their first year is consumed by talk of the house and efforts to join the town’s social circle, headed by the established Julliards, Guepins, and Guenées. Madame Tiphaine, the local belle, advances her husband toward deputyship by hosting influential gatherings. The Rogrons lack polish: Sylvie studies card games while Rogron stays awkward and silent.Lavish dinners fail to win acceptance; invitations dwindle and ridicule spreads. Sylvie’s faith that money guarantees status collapses; she goes frugal, abandons entertainments, and the siblings sink into ennui, nostalgic for business days and resentful of isolation. Bringing in cousin Pierrette Lorrain backfires as neglect becomes public. Bitter and sidelined, the Rogrons court the lower bourgeoisie—figures like Colonel Gouraud and lawyer Vinet who exploit their wealth and naiveté. The story shows provincial hierarchies and subtle social codes trump money.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After her grandparents were hospitalized, Pierrette—ashamed to live on charity—felt relieved when wealthy relatives were found. Brigaut, a childhood friend and her mother’s friend’s son, gave her sixty francs for the trip to Paris; she accepted without thanks, a sign of their close bond. He often visited to console her and ran after the coach in tears when she left. Penniless in Paris, a conductor briefly paid for her hotel and treated her like a package; four days later a kinder conductor brought her to Mademoiselle Rogron’s, where her cousins received her with astonishment.She arrived with a small bundle, dressed in Breton clothes. Sylvie and Rogron were startled and questioned her, but Pierrette, shy and overwhelmed, spoke little. Sylvie and the maid Adèle showed her a modest room that seemed luxurious to Pierrette, and Sylvie immediately fretted over her lack of clothing, resolving to buy her a new wardrobe.In Brittany Pierrette had known affection and freedom; with the Rogrons she found coldness and criticism. Exploring the house and garden the next morning, she was reproved for dirty shoes. Her cousins’ concern for possessions over her well‑being and their constant fault‑finding sharply contrasted with the love she’d known. Pierrette’s innocence made their disdain more painful; she began to suppress her joy and became passive for fear of scorn.The Rogrons obsessed over her appearance, treating her as a burden and stifling her spirit. When she tore a dress while playing, she was devastated, expecting stern rebukes. Life in Provins worsened as Sylvie forbade visits to friends and pursued social ambitions encouraged by Vinet and the colonel. As her cousins plotted for status, Pierrette was increasingly confined—transformed from a lively child into a shadow of her former self, enduring harsh, self‑interested care that was not love.At the start of a new phase with her wealthy cousins, Pierrette went to say goodnight and Sylvie turned away coldly, making her cry. When asked why, Pierrette said her rich cousin was not as kind as her poor grandmother; Sylvie retorted the grandmother had taken money while the cousin would leave her some—Pierrette wanted affection, not wealth.Sylvie then accused Pierrette of mischief—breaking a watch, disobeying orders—and Pierrette left ashamed. Rogron demanded punishment, Madame Vinet suggested sending her to school, and the colonel thought instruction might keep her quiet. Vinet called Pierrette a rough diamond needing polishing and a writing master was hired. His lessons produced ink stains and scattered papers, blamed on Pierrette, and she was sharply told she must earn her keep. Rogron’s severity and Sylvie’s indifference deepened her isolation; her curiosity and spirit were stifled and she began to feel ill, complaining of pains the cousins dismissed.Only Adèle, the servant, showed kindness, secretly warming Pierrette’s bed. Sylvie believed in hardening children; the colonel and Vinet attributed Pierrette’s behavior to a stubborn Breton nature. Meanwhile Vinet and Rogron invested in a local newspaper to boost their standing, while Pierrette’s situation worsened.She considered escaping to her grandparents in Brittany, but a death and Rogron’s appointment as her guardian prevented it. Vinet hinted at an inheritance; Rogron secured from her grandmother a promise of eight thousand francs upon the old woman’s death. Preparations for Pierrette’s first communion shifted household dynamics. Sylvie insisted on the zealous priest Monsieur Habert, who profoundly influenced Pierrette. Finding solace in faith, she abandoned escape plans and embraced the Church’s teachings, a change that even made Sylvie adopt new piety.Habert’s sister offered to take Pierrette to her school, but the Rogrons refused, fearing a convent education. Later Vinet introduced the Chargeboeufs; Bathilde attracted Rogron, and Sylvie forced Pierrette into domestic tasks. Without Adèle’s protection she was treated worse than staff—scolded for trifles, denied praise—and over the years her health and spirit faded into a silent shadow while her cousins remained oblivious.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Before recounting the domestic drama Jacques Brigaut's arrival would incite in the Rogron family, it is necessary to explain how he came to Provins, since he plays a significant yet passive role in the events.Brigaut fled the Rogron house, shaken by Pierrette’s distressing change; her once-vibrant spirit had become pale and sorrowful. From a high point in the Upper town he watched her home, consumed by questions about her welfare and the depth of his feelings. Their rare, deep bond led him to try to improve his prospects: he briefly went to Paris to learn a trade, then returned to Provins and found work as a journeyman cabinetmaker so he could stay near Pierrette.He buried his feelings while learning more about the Rogrons. Madame Frappier revealed their dubious acquisition of Pierrette’s grandfather’s property. Brigaut often saw Pierrette at the market, where her frailty distressed him. Sundays at church brought them near, though she signaled him to remain hidden, sensing danger. Determined to discover the cause of her pallor, he feared for her health.Pierrette, meanwhile, fretted about changes since Brigaut’s return but was unaware of the Rogrons’ internal strife, especially between Sylvie and Mademoiselle Habert (Céleste), both angling for advantageous marriage. Sylvie, forty-two, feared growing old alone; Céleste, ten years younger and encouraged by her brother the priest, actively sought a husband. A recent political shift emboldened local Liberals and intensified competition for social standing in Provins.Sylvie’s anxiety drove her to consult a doctor about marrying at her age; his discouraging prognosis heightened her fear. Céleste, seeing an opening, urged delay to remove a rival. Sylvie’s unease turned to jealousy when she noticed the colonel’s increasing attention to Pierrette. Vinet, a lawyer with designs on Bathilde, exploited Sylvie’s vulnerability: he plotted to separate the colonel from Pierrette and steer the Rogron fortune toward Bathilde.Vinet persuaded the colonel to court Pierrette, painting a picture of happiness while secretly maneuvering to benefit Bathilde. The colonel, a mix of affection and reserve, began seeking Pierrette more, which inflamed Sylvie’s fears. Her suspicions sharpened after overhearing Brigaut serenade Pierrette; she concluded the colonel was involved. On a tense morning she accused Pierrette of harboring a secret admirer, mocked her innocence, and demanded answers. Pierrette, frightened and bewildered, stayed silent, which intensified Sylvie’s wrath and threats.As Pierrette endured the oppressive Rogron household, her health continued to decline. Her innocence sheltered her from some realities, but emotional strain and heavy labor eroded her strength. Brigaut’s presence offered brief comfort, yet Sylvie’s relentless scrutiny deepened Pierrette’s isolation. Amid this turmoil, love, jealousy, and ambition became entangled, setting the stage for a tragic sequence that would affect everyone involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For a month Rogron had stopped bringing the Constitutionnel to Gouraud, so Gouraud came himself and lingered to gossip and walk with Sylvie. Eager to see him, Sylvie dressed in her best; when Gouraud arrived Rogron excused himself, leaving them alone.Noting her solemnity, Gouraud asked after her cousin; she replied the cousin was unwell. He grew uneasy: Vinet’s rising influence with the Rogrons made him feel sidelined. A half-pay officer, he feared Vinet’s control of their political plans and Rogron’s infatuation with Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, which threatened his prospects. He even suspected Vinet might be manoeuvring to steer him toward Pierrette as a ploy to favor Vinet and Bathilde.Alone with Sylvie he sensed her unease; when she tentatively mentioned Pierrette he dismissed any affection for the girl, saying he preferred a more mature partner. Relieved, Sylvie concealed her intentions and hinted her brother had once considered Pierrette a match. Sensing her interest, Gouraud declared he would marry Sylvie, delighting Rogron. Sylvie began planning their home but remained wary of Pierrette’s interactions with the colonel.As dinner neared Sylvie scolded Pierrette for a stain yet ordered her to dress nicely—suspecting collusion. The Chargeboeuf ladies arrived; Bathilde, spectacularly dressed under Vinet’s guidance, treated marriage to Rogron as a stepping stone to power, using beauty and connections for independence.The evening grew tense: Bathilde’s condescension and Vinet’s remarks sharpened rivalries. At cards Pierrette’s innocent comments provoked Sylvie, and Pierrette left in tears. That night Sylvie brooded on men’s duplicity and her precarious position. The next day Pierrette suffered a painful fall; Sylvie blamed disobedience and showed no sympathy, revealing a widening rift between the cousins.The week ended in relentless torment for Pierrette under Sylvie’s escalating cruelty. She concealed Brigaut’s arrival to avoid anger, but her longing and fear for him endured. Determined to communicate, Brigaut arranged a nightly note exchange: one morning he slipped a message to her while pretending to buy fruit and asked her to lower a string from her window.Pierrette returned with his heartfelt letter, full of longing and plans to escape. Despite the difficulty of obtaining writing materials without waking Sylvie, she resolved to reply, pouring out her love and describing her relatives’ abuse. At midnight she sent the letter down; their correspondence continued, hopeful and intimate, while Sylvie’s jealousy and vigilance grew.Under Sylvie’s scrutiny Pierrette’s health declined; one evening she fainted, drawing concern from the colonel and others, though Sylvie dismissed it as a ruse. Desperate, Pierrette cried for help, provoking a violent confrontation when Sylvie tried to seize her letters. In the struggle Pierrette was injured, including a hurt hand, and left distressed.At the height of Sylvie’s fury Madame Lorrain, Pierrette’s grandmother, arrived after Brigaut’s urgent appeals to come to Provins. She witnessed the violence, rushed to Pierrette’s aid, and sought medical care. As Pierrette recovered, her bond with Madame Lorrain deepened; the promise of protection and a future with her grandmother and Brigaut offered a hopeful glimmer. Pierrette remained determined to reclaim her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At nine o’clock Monsieur Martener told Monsieur Tiphaine about Pierrette’s mistreatment by the Rogrons. Tiphaine brought in Auffray, a notary and relative, and long‑standing enmities between the Vinet and Tiphaine factions—inflamed by damaging truths about Madame Tiphaine’s family—gave the dispute political force.Seeing an opportunity to weaken the influential royalist Rogrons, Tiphaine consulted the public prosecutor. He, Auffray, and Martener arranged to petition the courts to make Auffray Pierrette’s guardian so a Family Council, backed by medical testimony, could remove her from the Rogrons’ control.By midday the town buzzed: neighbors had heard Pierrette’s cries and rumors spread. Doctors Bianchon and Brigaut examined her and confirmed abuse. Vinet, the Rogrons’ lawyer, warned of criminal exposure; Sylvie, initially evasive, later admitted jealousy and the truth. Exploiting fear, Vinet proposed a marriage announcement to quiet the scandal. Under pressure the Rogrons signed a marriage contract that furthered their political aims. Vinet then filed a petition accusing Brigaut and the widow Lorrain of abducting Pierrette, attempting to recast his clients as victims.The hearing was postponed, heightening interest. The Tiphaines rallied support for Pierrette, who, unaware of the turmoil, was cared for by her grandmother and Brigaut. The doctors, appalled by her condition, unanimously condemned the Rogrons’ treatment.When the court reconvened, Auffray presented the physicians’ report to justify protecting Pierrette. Vinet countered, but the judge ordered she be kept from the Rogrons until the Family Council could meet—a public rebuke. The Council of relatives and allies voted to remove Rogron as guardian and appoint Auffray. Despite Vinet’s defense, the evidence led to a civil suit for neglect and abuse.As the legal battle continued, Pierrette’s health declined. Martener and the Auffray family grew fond of her; her grandmother’s care and Brigaut’s visits comforted her. A critical operation in March 1828 offered little hope; she forgave her cousins and soon died. Her funeral filled the town; the Rogrons, fearing outrage, stayed away. Brigaut made her coffin. Though community support hampered the Rogrons’ maneuvers and Vinet faced backlash, the Rogrons and Vinet largely retained political standing; only a few—Martener, Brigaut, and like‑minded friends—remembered the reality of Pierrette’s suffering. The account closes on justice, accountability, and the lasting impact of her life and death.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Dedication: To David, Sculptor—Balzac expresses gratitude for the permanence of sculpture compared with the precariousness of other fame.THE VICAR OF TOURSIn the autumn of 1826 Abbé Birotteau, short, corpulent, about sixty and subject to gout, was caught in a rainstorm returning from a friend's house. He hurried across the deserted Cloister behind Saint‑Gatien cathedral, careless of wet shoes because he was in high spirits after winning at cards with Madame de Listomère and buoyed by hope of obtaining a long‑desired canonry. That ambition consumed him so completely he even neglected his umbrella.The Cloister, once home to Chapter dignitaries, had become the narrow Rue de la Psalette. On its left stood a single house long occupied by priests and owned by the devout, discreet old maid Mademoiselle Gamard, who had preserved the property through the Restoration by taking priests as boarders. She could accommodate only two lodgers; for twelve years she had sheltered Abbé Troubert and the late Abbé Chapeloud. When Chapeloud died, Birotteau—who had long admired the apartment’s furnishings during visits—succeeded him.Chapeloud had been a close friend; his death left Birotteau both bereaved and relieved. He inherited Chapeloud’s tasteful library and furniture, rewards he had envied and seen as part of the comforts and status he craved. Becoming Gamard’s boarder and securing the canonry seemed to promise those comforts. Installing himself in the rooms he had coveted, he enjoyed imagined attentions and domestic ease; but gratitude soon gave way to renewed obsession with preferment.One rainy evening he returned to find the house door locked and had to ring repeatedly; his servant Marianne explained Gamard assumed he was already in. He noticed other petty omissions—no slippers waiting, no fire—and felt slighted. These trivial neglects pricked his vanity and suggested intentional disrespect. Innocent and lacking worldly tact, he could not see that the lapses were deliberate.Mademoiselle Gamard’s discontent had been growing during Birotteau’s eighteen months of tenancy. She prized social recognition and dreamed of cultivating a salon—ambitions frustrated by his indifference and frequent evenings with friends. As he distanced himself, she felt her plans slipping away and began to nurse a quiet vengeance. Her coolness—locked doors, omitted attentions—was the first sign.Absorbed by hopes of preferment, Birotteau confronted symptoms, not causes, convinced he must somehow have displeased her. The contrast between Chapeloud’s worldly tact and Birotteau’s guilelessness turns small domestic grievances into a deeper conflict of pride, naiveté, and provincial intrigue—a prologue to the wider tale to follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Birotteau woke absorbed in thoughts of a possible canonry, briefly forgetting the previous night’s ill omens. He rang for Marianne, who did not appear; before he could ring again Abbé Troubert entered after knocking. This monthly visit was expected. Troubert noted the absent fire, called for Marianne, and asked after Birotteau’s health and the canonry. Unaware that Troubert resented Madame de Listomère for not inviting him to her house, Birotteau described his efforts to obtain the position.The two abbés contrasted sharply. Troubert was tall, thin, serious, with a wrinkled face suggesting sarcasm and contempt; Birotteau was plump, round-faced, and cheerful. Troubert’s calm exterior masked a dulled intellect some attributed to subservience to Mademoiselle Gamard; Birotteau’s joviality made him popular. Neither seemed likely to rise far—Troubert because, at fifty, failing health and a pious conservatism might only win him a vicar-generalship; Birotteau because amiability kept him comfortable. Abbé Chapeloud quietly opposed Troubert’s promotion, but Troubert treated him respectfully despite Chapeloud’s skepticism.Troubert asked to borrow the Register of Bishops, which Birotteau lent. Carrying heavy books downstairs, they met Mademoiselle Gamard’s sharp disapproval; she scolded Birotteau for cluttering her dining room and stung him with her ill temper, a jarring contrast to his usual cheerfulness.Breakfast was tense. Gamard complained of being disturbed the previous night; weighed down by her displeasure, Birotteau could not engage. Her bitter demeanor—shaped by the resentment and social frustration of an unmarried woman—showed in sharp features and abrupt movements. Feeling oppressed, Birotteau left for the garden, found Gamard and Troubert watching him, escaped into church duties, and on returning discovered he had missed dinner—Gamard had set the clock ahead to avoid waiting, a petty manipulation protecting her propriety at his expense.Eight days after the events began, Abbé Birotteau discovered that Mademoiselle Gamard’s hostility—visible since the candlestick-and-clock incident—was a six-month plot against him. Previously oblivious, he now felt suffocated by her malice as she savored revenge while he, absorbed by minor worries, confided in no one.His life became torment. Anxiety about confronting Gamard worsened his health, so he sought Abbé Troubert’s help. Embarrassed to complain, he described Gamard’s provocations; Troubert listened with restrained amusement and advised understanding her, blaming ill health for her behavior.Desperate, Birotteau hoped a temporary stay at Madame de Listomère’s country house would ease tensions, not realizing Gamard could exploit his absence. After ten days, Monsieur Caron, Gamard’s lawyer, reported she was alarmed by his prolonged leave and wanted clarity to avoid disputes. Back among friends, Birotteau recounted his troubles and speculation followed. Madame de Listomère suggested Troubert coveted Birotteau’s apartment. An old landowner warned this was only the start. Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix brought news that the vicar-general was ill and Troubert might be appointed, potentially giving him appointment power.Madame de Listomère urged Birotteau to consider the canonry rather than endure Gamard. Friends proposed yielding to Gamard and Troubert if it aided his advancement. But the struggle intensified. Caron returned asking Birotteau to sign a document relinquishing his residence; pressed by friends, he signed.Despair deepened when he learned he had signed away possessions Gamard claimed as compensation for board differences. Confronted, she coldly cited the agreement. Devastated, he fled to Madame de Listomère and found his belongings packed. Mademoiselle Salomon, fearing for his sanity, took him back to the Alouette, where he confided in friends.Monsieur de Bourbonne examined the deed, found it exploitative, and urged legal action. The group debated suing to recover property versus compromising to avoid scandal; they resolved to support Birotteau, though Bourbonne warned many would desert him if the fight grew bitter. Gossip, political divisions, and public suspicion of the clergy complicated matters. An expert appraisal revealed the true value of his goods, exposing Gamard’s fraud. As legal proceedings opened, town gossip intensified; the stakes were high and the outcome uncertain against the alliance of Gamard and Troubert.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few days after Birotteau’s quarrel with the old maid began, Baron de Listomere—expecting promotion to corvette captain—received a letter warning he might be retired. Alarmed, he hurried to Paris; the naval minister dismissed his fears, but inquiries uncovered a document confirming the retirement pending the director’s signature.Desperate, the baron consulted his deputy uncle, who said the minister believed he had sided with Radicals in Tours and had offended the influential Abbé Troubert, the vicar-general. His uncle urged reconciliation with Troubert, insisting good relations with the clergy were vital to the baron’s career. Seeing the danger, the baron resolved to avoid further conflict.Three days later, fearing political fallout from supporting Birotteau, the family followed Monsieur de Bourbonne’s advice to appease Troubert and distance themselves from Birotteau as a liability. The baroness confronted Birotteau, asked him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and leave her house. Distressed, he agreed and requested Chapeloud’s portrait; the baroness promised to negotiate.Mademoiselle Gamard fell ill and gossip blamed Birotteau’s suit. The baroness sought Troubert; their meeting was a duel of polite insinuations. She presented Birotteau’s withdrawal; Troubert feigned indifference but was privately pleased. He later returned with Gamard’s refusal to surrender the portrait; the baroness accepted it to keep the peace.Gamard died, leaving her fortune to Troubert, further worsening the Listomeres’ plight. At her funeral Troubert delivered a grand eulogy while Birotteau mourned alone. Troubert became Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere’s death left Birotteau an annuity, but Troubert’s influence continued to threaten the baron and to contest Birotteau’s legacy. Ultimately Birotteau was sent to a distant parish, ill and grieving—Troubert’s ascent exposing the moral costs of ambition and clerical power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy.Dear Nodier, this book exposes actions hidden behind domestic doors where divine intervention often replaces human justice. Its moral lessons center on family and motherhood and on the harms that follow when paternal authority—the traditional tribunal for domestic wrongs—is weakened. A loving mother cannot substitute for patriarchal power. The story defends indissoluble marriage and warns against unchecked selfishness in a society that exalts wealth and success while the law is impotent. I hope such a society will return to Catholic moral guidance through a different education.In “Scenes from Military Life” I will portray noble self-sacrifice and also show war’s corrupting influence on those at home who live as if on a battlefield. You have observed our times keenly; I entrust this tale to your name as a shield from criticism.Your sincere admirer,  De Balzac  Paris, November 1842.THE TWO BROTHERSCHAPTER IIn 1792 the townspeople of Issoudun disliked Dr. Rouget; gossip blamed him for his beautiful wife’s unhappiness, though the household’s true nature remained secret. Madame Rouget, née Descoings, had been frail since youth. They had a son, Jean-Jacques, and, ten years later, a daughter, Agathe.Rouget’s conviction that men must display strength caused much female suffering. The Descoings family—wealthy wool merchants turned investors in forfeited estates—were rich and miserly. Descoings, Rouget’s brother-in-law, had left Issoudun for Paris, failed as a grocer, and later been driven by love for a beautiful widow. Rouget, expecting his wife’s death, sent Agathe to Paris to secure Descoings’ favor and a share of the inheritance.Agathe, the most beautiful girl in Issoudun and like her mother in looks, provoked a quarrel between Rouget and their friend Lousteau, who then left town. Madame Rouget, learning her daughter had gone, predicted she would never see Agathe again—a prophecy that proved true as her health declined, worsened by her son’s neglect; Jean-Jacques offered little comfort.Agathe’s arrival in Paris coincided with Descoings’ imprisonment for imprudent political remarks during the famine and his subsequent execution alongside the poet André Chénier—an event that silenced local grocers and left a sharper immediate mark on the community than history would later reckon.Afterward Agathe married Bridau, a ministry secretary, with Rouget’s approval; Rouget had hoped to profit from any Descoings inheritance. Bridau, captivated by Agathe’s beauty, supported Napoleon, won promotion in 1804, and worked relentlessly. In 1808 he died of overwork just as another promotion was imminent—an honor the Emperor had meant to confer but belatedly.Following Bridau’s death, Agathe received no letters from Issoudun. Her mother died shortly before the birth of her second son; her father died around the Emperor’s coronation, an event that distracted Bridau. Jean-Jacques remained absent. Madame Hochon, Agathe’s godmother, warned that Agathe would inherit nothing unless she granted power of attorney to her brother; Agathe hesitated. Bridau’s prudent investments had secured only a modest income; after his death Agathe and Madame Descoings pooled resources and lived on a joint twelve thousand francs.By 1809 Madame Descoings, sixty-five but still fond of the theater and fine food, was secretly gambling on lotteries and concealing mounting debts. She also kept secrets about her son Bixiou, a major whose child had been educated alongside Agathe’s. Devoted to her children and refusing to remarry, Agathe undertook both maternal and household duties. Her limited judgment and blind trust in Madame Descoings proved disastrous when the lottery debts were revealed; Agathe, without reproach, sold possessions and liquidated investments to settle them—a decisive turning point in their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the bleakest stretches of Paris was rue Mazarin between rue Guenegard and rue de Seine, shaded by the high gray walls of the college and library given by Cardinal Mazarin. There, on the third floor of a damp, cold house, lived a poor widow, Madame Bridau (Agathe). Opposite were the Institute buildings, where aspiring artists, the rapins, worked amid the tumult of examinations; from her windows Agathe could see their cages and the students’ uproar. To brighten her room she defied police regulations and set boxes of earth on the sill to make little gardens.The shallow house, reached by a winding staircase, contained three rooms: a dining room, a small salon, and a bedroom, plus a kitchen and two single rooms. Agathe had chosen the flat because it was cheap—four hundred francs a year—near her sons’ school and in a neighborhood she knew. The interior matched the building’s dreariness: yellowed wallpaper, unglazed tiles, and only essential furniture. The salon held an Aubusson carpet given to her and a mahogany sofa bearing a pastel portrait of her late husband, Bridau, which captured his noble essence; a vivid Napoleon hung above a card table.After Bridau’s death Agathe’s life narrowed. She filled the room with two large birdcages—canaries and Java sparrows—a hobby that left her space littered with millet, chickweed, and cat hair, signs of grief and neglected personal care. She preserved everything belonging to her husband, even wrapping his last pen with a note. Seized by sorrow, she seemed to lose her sense of womanhood and devoted herself entirely to her children.Agathe lived for her sons and worried ceaselessly over their finances. The Emperor provided three hundred francs a year for each child, and scholarships supplemented this. Her aunt, Madame Descoings, on the second floor, assigned three thousand francs from her annuity to help Agathe; the Descoings themselves now lived modestly on twelve hundred francs a year, employed a woman for morning chores, and shared dinners with the two widows. Madame Descoings doted on Philippe and Joseph, indulging them with treats and small sums. Distrustful of the future, Agathe scrimped and saved—she set aside three thousand francs from her pension—hoping to secure some inheritance for her boys. Thus both widows had passed from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty, each for different reasons.The house, the students’ uproar, and her husband’s portrait formed the setting that would shape her youngest son, Joseph Bridau, into a notable painter of the modern French school. Philippe, the elder, resembled his mother in appearance but had a bold demeanor often mistaken for courage; Agathe hoped his looks promised similar moral qualities. At fifteen his charm reinforced her hopes. Joseph, three years younger, took after his father in less favorable ways: untidy, often scolded, and plainly less favored by his mother.Joseph’s artistic bent was sparked by a visit to the Tuileries, where a caricature on an Institute wall captivated him and made him want to draw. He began sneaking into the Institute courtyard, enduring teasing from older students until his persistence won their respect. He later visited the sculptor Chaudet’s atelier; Chaudet recognized and encouraged his talent. Agathe feared art meant hardship and tried to dissuade him, but Chaudet defended the vocation as a divine calling, insisting true talent must be followed.Meanwhile Philippe, enchanted by military life, secretly petitioned the Emperor to enlist. He was sent to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr and rose rapidly, becoming a captain by nineteen. Joseph, after his scholarship was withdrawn, tried to earn a living by art, encouraged by Madame Descoings. As politics shifted, Philippe returned home a decorated officer while Joseph faced an uncertain career. Agathe’s preference for Philippe became more apparent as she admired his success; yet Joseph remained devoted to his mother. Despite divergent paths, both brothers pursued their callings, with Agathe’s constant love and anxiety marking every step of their futures.In 1816 Joseph persuaded his mother to let him convert the adjoining garret into an atelier, partly financed by Madame Descoings. Unlike the two widows, who treated painting as a trade, Joseph embraced it passionately, installing a skylight and chocolate-colored walls and continuing studies under Gros and Schinner.Political unrest simmered in France; Philippe was implicated in conspiracies, briefly arrested, then released for lack of evidence, although his military pay was cut and Agathe feared for him. Friends urged the widows to send him abroad for safety; though she resisted the idea of him serving a foreign power, Philippe joined General Lallemand in the United States to help found the Champ d’Asile—an enterprise that failed. Agathe equipped him with ten thousand francs, and by late 1817 the household had adjusted to reduced means. Joseph lived frugally to support the family while his talent developed.In 1818 the notary Roguin absconded with funds intended for Madame Descoings. Soon after, Philippe sent a bill for a thousand francs, having lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. Agathe, distraught, blamed herself for urging his departure. Despite her own difficulties, Madame Descoings continued to support them. Desperate, Joseph appealed to Gérard, who arranged for him to paint copies of a portrait of Louis XVIII; Joseph worked tirelessly, selling panels to cover Philippe’s debt.When Philippe returned, Agathe welcomed him warmly but failed to notice how America had hardened him. He became self-centered, drinking, gambling, and adopting a swaggering arrogance. Agathe excused his faults as results of circumstance. The family’s finances deteriorated; friends discussed selling possessions to help him. Philippe refused to serve the Bourbons, lived off others, disappeared for days in cafés and gambling halls, and neglected family duties. He sought to impress Mariette; Giroudeau proposed journalism to exploit his experiences. A cashier’s post at a newspaper, secured by Agathe and Madame Descoings, brought temporary relief but did little to reform him. Joseph felt increasingly overshadowed and unappreciated. Philippe’s return and altered behavior strained relationships, exposing the consequences of choices, the limits of maternal devotion, and the fragile balance between ambition and family duty.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Three months later the colonel, who ate enough for four, still criticized the food while the widows—hesitant to press him—spent lavishly on meals though he had not paid a penny. His mother and Madame Descoings avoided reminding him of his promise; months passed without any of the “tigers with five claws,” as Leon Gozlan joked, migrating from Philippe’s pocket into the household purse. The colonel quieted his conscience by seldom dining at home. “Well, he is happy,” his mother said, “he has a place.”Urged by a feuilleton edited by Vernou, Mariette debuted at the Porte-Saint-Martin and triumphed with the famous Begrand. A wealthy general, enchanted and acquainted with Philippe and Giroudeau, became her impresario and arranged her first appearance—an example of the solidarity among those taken by folly.Bixiou soon told his grandmother and Agathe that Philippe, the cashier, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. This appalled the widows; Agathe’s piety cast all stage women as morally suspect, and both feared such women lived extravagantly. “Do you think my brother will spend on Mariette? Such women ruin rich men,” Joseph protested. Bixiou suggested Mariette might be taken from Philippe by higher-ranking admirers; he’d even heard an ambassador was infatuated with her.Partly from financial motive, Philippe proposed; Mariette declined, perhaps suspecting his intent or valuing her freedom, and hoped instead for an engagement at the Grand Opera. Over the next year Philippe visited his mother only twice a month, spending days at the office, the theatre, or with Mariette, enjoying pleasures and mixing with Tullia and other entertainers.The carnival lasted eighteen months but grew stormy. After her Opera debut in January 1821 Mariette attracted a distinguished duke. Philippe, driven by passion, began dipping into the funds he managed—taking eleven hundred francs by May. Mariette left for London to seek patrons while the temporary opera house was prepared. Philippe, despite her infidelities, remained deeply in love; to her he was merely a stepping stone.Forced to relinquish Mariette, Philippe returned to his mother’s apartment, unable to imagine life without recent pleasures. Suicide crossed his mind—not from the deficit alone but because he could not bear life without Mariette’s company. He entered Joseph’s studio where Joseph was copying a picture for a dealer and explaining his earnings. Pale and subdued, Philippe asked how long it would take to paint his portrait, saying he wanted to leave his mother a likeness. When Joseph asked if he was leaving again, Philippe replied, “I am going never to return,” and, pressed, confessed he had taken money from the funds and could not live with the dishonor.Joseph tried to reassure him that repayment would save his honor, but Philippe, despairing, fled downstairs, leaving Joseph anxious. Joseph informed Madame Descoings, who was horrified. Agathe was devastated, crying at the thought of Philippe failing in honor. Madame Descoings urged hope, attributing his ruin to a bad woman; Agathe lived in terror for her son.That day Philippe calmly submitted his accounts, inventing a story that he’d retained the money for safekeeping. He then went to a gambling house—winning at first, then losing everything. Returning home, he found concern and affection from his family, which briefly comforted him. “Promise not to kill yourself, and all shall be forgotten,” his mother pleaded. Philippe, realizing the threat had worked, feigned gratitude and affection, and they shared dinner; he felt momentarily relieved.The next day Agathe appeared older and more anxious, aware the family’s finances were precarious. Madame Descoings suggested consulting Desroches to manage the affair and preserve honor. Desroches, in his diplomatic way, advised Agathe to sign a transfer of funds and petition for Philippe’s reinstatement in the army, assuring them Philippe would soon regain his rank and easing some fear. Yet Agathe’s faith was shaken and she lived in constant dread of his downfall.Philippe returned to a life of leisure—gambling, drinking, appearing disheveled and indifferent to the family’s plight. His love for Mariette had hardened him; he treated women with contempt. Joseph, concentrating on painting, gained recognition and Madame Descoings’s support, who pinned her hopes on his future. As Madame Descoings pursued lottery dreams, she remained oblivious to growing tensions. Joseph, noticing discrepancies in his finances, began to suspect Philippe and confided in Madame Descoings. They planned to catch him. When Joseph discovered that Philippe had indeed taken money from him, he felt betrayed; despite anger, he still struggled to confront his brother. Madame Descoings urged sympathy for Philippe’s struggles, but Joseph remained wary of his influence on the family’s future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite Philippe’s careful approach to nightly gambling, he was sometimes “cleaned out.” Needing his usual ten francs, he began stealing from his family—pilfering from his brother and whatever Madame Descoings or Agathe left about. After a dream in which Philippe robbed her, Agathe recognized his decline and begged him to ask for money instead of stealing. Within two weeks her savings were gone; she took worsted work for twenty sous a day, spent sleepless nights waiting for Philippe, and watched his increasing drunkenness and mood swings.When Madame Descoings suggested Philippe get new clothes, he retorted that his mother had no money and that he had mortgaged his pension. He admitted borrowing from Giroudeau, calling it a “debt of honor.” Agathe excused him as out of his element, while Joseph criticized his neglect of appearance and duty. Philippe sank further into debauchery and gambling, losing the steadiness and good looks he once had.One day Philippe asked Joseph for money to pay a tobacconist; Joseph refused. Philippe then guessed where Madame kept her savings and, expecting she’d be out buying Christmas treats, broke into her room. He found twenty napoleons hidden in the mattress and took the money to gamble.At the gambling house Philippe won substantial sums over several sessions and, intoxicated by success, amassed over one hundred fifty thousand francs by night’s end. Pressed by greed, he continued and lost everything. The next morning he awoke in despair, knowing he had ruined his family.Agathe and Madame Descoings waited for Philippe at Christmas dinner; when he failed to show they worried. Madame had pinned hopes on a lottery stake, but upon checking her savings she found the money gone and fainted. The concierge confirmed Philippe had been in the apartment. Agathe returned pale and shaken; the family confronted Philippe. He dismissed their accusations, claiming he’d taken what was owed him. A fight erupted between Joseph and Philippe, and Madame Descoings suffered a stroke amid the chaos.Joseph offered his mother money afterward, but she refused, clinging to lottery hope. When the winning numbers were announced it was clear she’d missed her chance; she died shortly after. Philippe, recovering from illness, was told by Agathe that their finances had collapsed and urged to rejoin the army. Indifferent and feeling abandoned, he accepted the money she reluctantly offered and left to seek the company of those he called true friends. Agathe, heartbroken, prayed for her son and resigned herself to the loss of the child she once loved.By February 1822 Madame Bridau had been moved into the attic above the kitchen of her former apartment, recently occupied by her son Philippe. Seeing his mother reduced, Joseph set out to make her comfortable: he rearranged the garret, added better furnishings, and took Agathe daily to a table d’hôte that cost him nearly a hundred francs a month. Alarmed by the expense and urged by her confessor, Agathe accepted a seven-hundred-franc-a-year post at a lottery office that demanded long hours.She adapted to the routine, preparing Joseph’s breakfasts, mending his clothes, keeping the studio tidy, and aiding his work despite having no artistic training. Their bond deepened through these small acts of tenderness.
