They spoke often of Philippe—Agathe feared his reckless life, but Joseph dismissed her worries, convinced Philippe was prospering in New York.One afternoon Joseph visited Philippe, who worked at a newspaper. Philippe invited Joseph to dine; Joseph declined to stay with his mother. When he described Philippe afterward, Agathe felt hopeful. She decorated Joseph’s studio and prepared an elaborate breakfast for a sitting for Philippe’s portrait; overwhelmed, she hid and watched the two sons together. After the sitting Philippe invited them to a sumptuous dinner, which Agathe eagerly accepted while Joseph felt uneasy at the ostentation. At another sitting she learned more of Philippe’s circle—journalists and actresses—and heard boastful plans, though he remained vague about money.Later Joseph postponed a session for a commission. Philippe, mistaking a valuable copy of a Rubens for an original, took it and sold it for a fraction of its worth. Discovering the theft devastated Joseph; Agathe was heartbroken, feeling she had lost her son to vice.Two months after, an old officer told Agathe Philippe was ill and needed money. Working at the lottery, she had little to spare and at first refused to help the son she felt she had banished from her heart; Joseph insisted Philippe go to the hospital. When she later found Philippe destitute on the street, she gave what she could and despaired.Agathe then learned Philippe had been implicated in a conspiracy and was arrested; she fainted. Joseph consulted Desroches, who urged them to secure the family inheritance and gather evidence of undue influence; he planned to visit Issoudun at the holidays to help. Agathe’s letter to her godmother brought a discouraging reply about an unhelpful brother. Agathe and Joseph set off for Issoudun, leaving Philippe to face the consequences and Agathe to confront their deep estrangement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Issoudun, one of France’s oldest cities noted by Caesar and Rigord, yielded in recent excavations by M. Armand Peremet a fifth-century basilica—likely unique in France—built from stones of a Roman temple to Isis. The town’s name, from “Isis” with “dun” indicating a religious and military settlement, points to ancient Celtic origins.In the Middle Ages Issoudun was a center of power: Richard Coeur-de-lion built a tower above the basilica as part of its fortifications, and the town later served as a stronghold for Routiers and Cottereaux mercenaries sent by Henry II against his son Richard. Its prominence has since faded. After the 1830 insurrection vine-growers resisted government intervention, burned tax offices, and asserted autonomy—demonstrating the force of about eight thousand vine-growers.In the fourteenth century Issoudun held sixteen to seventeen thousand people, prospering on the wool trade and producing blankets and gloves; by Louis XIV’s time it enjoyed some elegance. Today the population is about twelve thousand, including vine-growers from nearby suburbs. The local economy, especially wine, suffers from outdated practices: vine-growers resist modernization and produce poor-quality wine that undercuts potential prosperity.This decline reflects a broader spirit of stagnation. When a proposed new road linking Paris and Toulouse through Issoudun arose, local leaders opposed it, fearing higher food prices—an example of provincial preference for tradition over progress.Issoudun’s social fabric has also deteriorated. The disappearance of the nobility removed a source of competition and dynamism. The bourgeoisie, content with routine social forms, have grown complacent, leading to stagnant governance. Administration is dominated by intermarried local families whose ties produce ineffectual leadership and weak accountability; the clergy exert little influence, further diminishing civic vitality.Against this background the town’s youth, feeling trapped, formed the “Knights of Idleness,” led by Maxence Gilet. Rumored an illegitimate son of a local official, Max rose from poverty, distinguished himself in the military, and returned to clash with the bourgeoisie. The Knights’ disruptive but creative pranks, sheltered by a tavern run by Père and La Cognette, embody youthful frustration against Issoudun’s modern decline.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Joseph and Madame Bridau traveled from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness played a prank. An old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war, left his empty cart at the foot of the Issoudun tower. Maxence, arriving at the Knights’ rendezvous, proposed, “Here’s Père Fario’s cart. Let’s get it up on the embankment first, and then decide.” In an hour they disassembled the cart and hoisted it piece by piece to the embankment like artillery; once reassembled, the Knights, hungry and thirsty, returned to Mère Cognette’s to await the Spaniard’s astonishment.Their amusements varied; they did not prank nightly. The twenty youths met for hunts, autumn vintages, and winter skating. Some were especially close to Max, now their idol. François Hochon and Baruch Borniche, Madame Hochon’s grandsons, treated Max as a cousin and relied on him for money and the adventures their grandfather denied them.That night, after twelve to fifteen bottles of wine, François and Baruch sat beside Max at a dim table. Baruch warned, “You are threatened in your stronghold.”“What do you mean?” Max asked.“My grandmother got a letter from Madame Bridau—her goddaughter—saying she and her son are coming here. She’s preparing two rooms for them.”Max, thirty-four and soldierly in appearance, was unsettled despite his outward confidence. From the other end of the table someone remarked, “Rouget’s sister will secure the property when he dies—and goodbye to your harvest!”Max bristled. “What’s that to me?” When François suggested Rouget might revoke a will, Max snapped, “I’ve silenced those who meddle with you, François. Is this how you repay me? By using a contemptuous nickname for a woman I care about?”For the first time Max spoke openly about Flore Brazier, Rouget’s servant-mistress whom society disparaged. His friends knew to tread carefully on the subject. With Max in command, François fell silent and Goddet reminded the Order of their freedom of speech. Max steered the conversation.“You believe Flore, the Rabouilleuse, supports me since I returned. That I throw away three hundred francs a month and treat you to dinners because of her money? Yes—Flore aims for Rouget’s property,” he said.“You think I plan to marry Flore when Rouget dies, and that these Parisians threaten my future?”“That’s it,” François admitted.“Don’t worry,” Max went on. “If I need help to deal with these Parisians, will you assist me—within the limits of our fooleries, of course. I’m not asking you to kill or poison them. If the Bridaus succeed and Flore has nothing but what she stands in, I’d be satisfied. I love her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet, if she would have me.”Mademoiselle Fichet, the richest heiress in Issoudun, was being courted by Goddet. Moved by Max’s frankness, the Knights cheered: “You’re a fine fellow, Max!” “We’ll stand by you!” “A fig for the Bridaus!” Their relaxed morality and loyalty reflected Max’s influence. He raised his glass of claret.“As a mark of ill-will I wish you all a mistress as fine as Flore! As for this influx of relatives, I feel no immediate unease; we’ll see what the future holds—”“Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”“That’s safe enough!” Goddet replied.“I’ll handle that,” Max assured them. “Be in the market early, and let me know when the old fellow comes for his cart.”At half-past three the Knights slipped away in their soft shoes. Max returned to place Saint-Jean, masking his worries; his time in Cabrera had taught him concealment. The forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s property anchored his affection for Flore. Despite his bluster, news of legitimate heirs unnerved him: Rouget’s savings, amassed over seventeen years and amounting to large sums, remained in the doctor’s name; even if Flore could claim a will, those funds might be secured elsewhere.“That fool of a girl never mentioned her sister and nephews in all these seven years!” Max muttered entering rue l’Avenier. “Seven hundred and fifty thousand francs with various notaries can’t be liquidated quickly without everyone knowing in this gossiping town! The priority is to get rid of these relatives; once they’re gone, we can secure the property. I need to think this through.”Exhausted, Max used a pass-key to enter Père Rouget’s house and went to bed, telling himself, “Tomorrow, my thoughts will be clear.”The narrative then traces how Flore Brazier became known as the “Rabouilleuse” and the quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s household. As Doctor Rouget aged he saw his son’s ineptitude and tried to impose a routine, only to see Jean-Jacques submit to the first authority that came along. One day in September 1799, returning from his rounds, the doctor found a captivating little girl by a brook, emerging from the water in a tattered petticoat and makeshift hat. The child said she was from Vatan and was catching crabs for her uncle Brazier, who joked about republican liberty from the brook.Struck by the child’s looks, the doctor offered to take her in, promising a better life and wages to her uncle. Flore’s life changed: she was fed, clothed, and given gifts to encourage study, though her peasant habits made her dislike books and the doctor eventually stopped her schooling. Town gossip soon colored perceptions of impropriety around the doctor’s attention; yet for five years he enjoyed educating her without scandalous complications.Born in 1787 and raised amid the Revolution’s upheavals, Flore grew up with shaken traditional values and a pragmatic self-interest rather than strict virtue. As Rouget neared death, his notary urged him to make provisions for her; Rouget’s cynical refusal to leave her anything revealed bitterness and a desire to punish what he saw as an unreciprocated attachment. That refusal set the stage for the inheritance struggle that now threatened Max’s position and Flore’s hopes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father’s death, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son miserable, especially after Jean-Jacques came of age, yet he had provided Flore with the material comforts prized in the country. After the funeral, when Fanchette asked Flore what would become of her now that Monsieur was dead, Jean-Jacques showed animation for the first time.At seventeen Flore still had the beauty that had attracted the doctor, though she was growing plump like other countrywomen. Jean-Jacques hesitated to speak; the memory of his father’s death and benefactions weighed on him, and Flore left him puzzled by his silence.When his father died Jean-Jacques was thirty-seven: timid, submissive, and childlike. His torpor left him passionate but lacking confidence, awkward and unattractive, preferring solitude. Too late the doctor recognized how his son’s temperament had suffered. He wanted Jean-Jacques to marry but feared losing control of his property, so he sought a daughter-in-law and tried to instill avarice in his son to give him some financial instinct.Despite his father’s efforts, Jean-Jacques was secretly in love with Flore, the only woman whose education and presence he could comfortably observe. He took pride in the instruction his father gave her, thinking it would make her easier to win. Flore, unaware of his feelings, waited for him to declare himself, but he remained silent.Three weeks of uncertainty passed. One night Flore awoke to find Jean-Jacques crouched outside her door and took it as proof of his love; the next day she found him less repellent. Their conversations were halting: Jean-Jacques asked if she wanted to return to the fields, and Flore, curious about his intentions, assured him of her honesty. These exchanges revealed his growing affection even as he struggled to express it.After ten months Jean-Jacques brightened and became attentive to his appearance, much to Flore’s satisfaction. The townspeople noticed and speculated. Fanchette, the cook who had disapproved of Flore’s place in the household, eventually left; Flore had anticipated this and learned to cook and manage the house, surpassing the former cook and taking pride in keeping things neat.Over the next nine years Flore gained complete control over Jean-Jacques. She treated him with a blend of familiarity and respect and gradually became the dominant figure; he, childlike and dependent, relied on her for everything, and she indulged his whims.By 1815 Flore was at the height of her beauty; in 1816 she fell in love with Maxence Gilet, a penniless officer. Her new passion altered the household. Feeling neglected, she pressed Jean-Jacques about his wealth and the empty rooms and suggested inviting Max. Fearing to lose her, Jean-Jacques reluctantly agreed. With Max in the home Jean-Jacques found the companionship and conversation he had lacked, while Flore shirked household duties, hired a new cook, refined her appearance to impress Max, bought him a horse, and took on a servant. The household shifted: Flore and Max became central and Jean-Jacques receded into the background, increasingly dependent on them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim her threatened inheritance, Jean-Jacques Rouget had become semi-vegetative. After Max’s financial aid, Flore raised Rouget’s standard of living, feeding him from Vedie’s kitchen. Despite better food, he grew weaker and utterly submissive; his devotion to Flore was his sole motive and made him seem much older than fifty-seven.Max’s apartment reflected his devotion: provincial comforts, colored tiles, elegant wallpapers and mahogany furniture. A rush matting on the stairs muffled footsteps so Max could come home late without waking anyone — not even Rouget, who remained ignorant of Max’s nocturnal activities.One morning Flore, in a cotton gown, found Max asleep and noticed his late return. He told her a serious matter: Rouget’s family were coming to Issoudun, likely to evict them. Flore vowed confrontation; Max urged caution, asking for coffee and suggesting they plan later. As she began to make coffee, Baruch interrupted: Fario was searching for his cart.Max joined the crowd. Fario, a small peculiar man, claimed his cart had been stolen. Max led him to the tower embankment where, to everyone’s surprise, the cart was found at the top. Amid laughter Max theatrically tossed the cart down, humiliating Fario and turning the episode into town gossip.The incident heightened concern. François informed Max that the Bridaus would arrive that night. Max resolved they should not appear hostile; he proposed letting the Hochons handle driving them out. Meanwhile Flore, unable to contain her anger at the news that Rouget’s sister and nephew were coming, punished Rouget by withdrawing her affection, leaving him bewildered and distressed. Vedie urged him to placate Flore. Rouget denied inviting them and blamed Madame Hochon for the visit.Max argued Rouget should welcome his kin for appearances. Rouget thanked him; Max counseled him to keep his will while being polite to his sister. After breakfast they discussed finances and plans to secure Rouget’s wealth, scheming so the Hochons would help deal with the Bridaus.Opposite Rouget’s, the Hochon household was modest. Monsieur Hochon, a retired tax collector, was famed for frugality. Madame Hochon prepared to receive her goddaughter Agathe, and their granddaughter Adolphine secretly hoped to win Joseph Bridau’s affection. News of the Bridaus’ arrival split Issoudun: cautious bourgeoisie offered measured support, while the Knights of Idleness remained vehemently loyal to Max. The Fario incident and the town’s division underscored how fragile reputations and power were, and how a few calculated actions could shape the looming confrontation over inheritance and influence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales in Issoudun at three. Though tired, Madame Bridau felt revived by familiar sights and memories. News spread quickly; Madame Hochon greeted Agathe like a daughter, clinging to that affection after years of loss.Agathe met the uninviting Monsieur Hochon and the household: the young men Baruch and François, and shy Adolphine, who noticed Joseph. The miserly Hochon remarked on Joseph’s recent hospital stay and hunger; the youths were struck by his looks. “This is my son, the painter; my good Joseph,” Agathe said proudly. Joseph’s blunt remark about resembling his father was misread by Madame Hochon as affection for Agathe. Baruch hurried off to spread news, highlighting Joseph’s striking appearance.Townspeople quickly gossiped, likening Joseph to a brigand and airing disdain for artists; many planned to call on the Hochons that evening to satisfy curiosity about the Parisians.Installed in modest attic rooms, Joseph contrasted his Parisian artistic life with the drab provincial existence. Dinner revealed the Hochons’ frugality—plain bouilli, hard eggs, and meager accompaniments. Accustomed to better fare, Joseph found it disappointing but tried to adapt. Agathe learned her childhood home had been sold to the Borniches, and Madame Hochon braced for an influx of visitors. Joseph observed the family’s strict ways and limited hospitality.After dinner the women gathered in the salon. Agathe and Madame Hochon exchanged life stories—Agathe recounting hardships, Madame Hochon offering hard-won advice about endurance—continually interrupted by a stream of callers eager to meet the Parisians. Joseph remained detached, studying visitors with a critical eye that made them uneasy. Later, as the house quieted, the two women resumed their heartfelt exchange about the burdens they carried.Elsewhere the Knights of Idleness convened to plot mischief against the Parisians. Max, their leader, proposed a rat prank to sabotage the grain supply of a disliked merchant, Fario; the gang welcomed the scheme and sought a way to drive the Bridaus away without harming the Hochons.Next morning at breakfast Joseph glimpsed his brother Jean-Jacques Rouget, unrecognizable and disheveled, with the conspicuous Flore Brazier. Joseph admired Flore’s beauty; Agathe feared her influence. A letter from Jean-Jacques inviting Joseph to dine arrived, which the Hochons received with mixed feelings, suspecting Flore’s involvement. Monsieur Hochon advised Joseph to observe carefully and speak frankly with his brother about his nephews. With tensions simmering and rival schemes in motion, a fraught family reunion was imminent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At four o’clock Joseph crossed from the Rouget to the Hochon house, where Kouski announced him. The table was set in the large hall; Joseph greeted his uncle Rouget, Flore, and Maxence Gilet with light banter, admired Flore and the house, and noted Max’s good looks. Baruch and Francois arrived and Flore suggested a walk before dinner. While she prepared, Joseph eagerly examined the house’s paintings, which Rouget said had come from the Descoings and had been bought during the Revolution. Ecstatic, Joseph inspected more upstairs with Max. Flore urged Rouget to give the paintings to Joseph, arguing Rouget didn’t care for them.Rouget offered the pictures to Joseph—without frames—and Flore proposed paying him four thousand francs to make copies. Overwhelmed, Joseph accepted. The news spread through Issoudun and enhanced Flore’s standing. Dinner that night was splendid, with local dignitaries. Joseph, somewhat tipsy, went home at eleven; Rouget, having drunk heavily, was carried to bed. Left alone, Max and Flore agreed it was better to befriend the Bridaus than antagonize them and planned to send the pictures to Joseph the next day.The next morning the paintings were propped against Rouget’s wall. Agathe, Joseph’s mother, visited Rouget to thank him after Hochon urged her—he feared the Bridaus’ influence and warned that Joseph had been fooled. Hochon urged Agathe to cultivate Flore to gain access to Rouget. Agathe was warmly received; Rouget was ill from the previous night. Flore feigned servitude and Agathe hinted Rouget should marry her, but Flore insisted Rouget would remember her in his will.In the following days the Knights of Idleness—local troublemakers—released rats and mice into Fario’s grain store, causing havoc. The Bridaus’ hold on Rouget seemed to strengthen: Flore claimed Rouget was too weak for walks and needed a carriage; they bought a shabby vehicle that drew town attention. Rouget signed a power of attorney granting Max authority over his investments, while Flore handled local transactions. Fario returned to find his store devastated and suspected Max’s involvement; he began gathering evidence against the Knights.Agathe believed Rouget had not made a will and intended to ask him directly, while Hochon urged her to stay longer and use religious influence to counter Max and Flore—advice she reluctantly considered. Progress for the Bridaus was slow; Hochon stressed persistence.Meanwhile Joseph, sensing the paintings’ real value, sent them to Desroches in Paris for appraisal, convinced they were worth far more than the 4,000-franc copying job. Agathe warned him to keep their worth quiet, but word leaked and searches for old artworks spread through Issoudun. Max regretted aspects of their scheme, now fearful of the Church’s influence if the paintings’ value became public.Letters from Desroches and Schinner advised keeping Agathe in Issoudun, warned about Max, and suggested Rouget might already have a will—so the Bridaus’ prospects depended on a struggle between Flore and the Church. Disturbed, Joseph decided to return to Paris. He bade Rouget farewell, resolved to rely on his talent, and planned later to return the pictures to Rouget. The Hochon family suspected Max and Flore had known the paintings’ value and manipulated Joseph; Agathe defended her son, but Hochon warned that returning the paintings might jeopardize any chance at inheritance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between midnight and one the Knights of Idleness fed the town’s dogs; their expedition lasted until three. After supper at Cognette’s, Max, turning from rue l’Avenier into Grande rue at half-past four, was stabbed by Fario, who had lurked in a recess, fled toward Vilatte, and wiped the knife on his handkerchief. Fario then washed the handkerchief in the Rivière forcée and returned to his lodgings at Saint-Paterne, where his new watchman later found him in bed.Max fell, crying out and alarming Lousteau-Prangin and young Goddet, who ran up the street calling for help; the townspeople, used to false alarms, stayed in bed. Max had fainted. He recognized Fario but, on regaining consciousness and seeing the wound was not fatal, decided to exploit the incident: “I think I recognized that cursed painter!”Lousteau-Prangin hurried to his father, the judge. Max was carried home by Cognette, Goddet, and others. Goddet, suspicious of Max’s words, asked Gritte if Joseph Bridau was in bed; she replied that Joseph had left at half-past four, restless all night. The revelation shocked the group, and they entered Rouget’s house to find Max in a bloody shirt.Joseph had spent the night anxious about how the townspeople would view him, fearing they might think he had robbed his uncle. He longed to flee to Paris and confront Max; at dawn he walked to the poplar avenue toward Tivoli to steady his nerves.Max plotted revenge. After Goddet examined the wound and found it serious but not fatal, news spread and a crowd gathered at the place Saint-Jean. Max whispered to Flore that they could use the incident to rid themselves of the Parisians: pretend he was dying and have Joseph arrested. Flore, feigning tears, spread the story that Joseph had attacked Max out of jealousy over paintings. Incited by her, the crowd demanded Joseph’s capture; the mob grew hostile.Monsieur Hochon, warned by Gritte, locked himself in, fearing for his safety and wealth. He learned Joseph had left early; Madame Hochon, anxious for their daughter Agathe, hoped she would sleep through the shock. Agathe, however, woke, learned of the accusations, and fainted at the sight of the gendarmes. Monsieur Hochon urged the magistrates to act rapidly; the lieutenant reported a large crowd demanding justice.Joseph was spotted returning home and nearly seized by the mob. Gendarmes intervened and, despite his protests of innocence, took him to the Palais for his safety. There Joseph calmly insisted on his innocence and provided an alibi, recounting his morning’s movements. The magistrates were somewhat swayed by his composure but kept him in custody while they verified his claims. Joseph wrote to his mother, reassuring her he would soon be free.Max, recovering, remembered a crucial detail: the attacker had worn list shoes, while Joseph had left in boots. He wrote to the magistrate urging Joseph’s release. As the investigation progressed, witnesses confirmed Joseph’s alibi and he was freed. He returned to his mother’s warm relief; the ordeal strengthened their bond and Joseph felt grateful for the support.The townspeople celebrated the departure of the Parisians as a victory; Max and Flore were pleased. Joseph and Agathe returned to Paris; Joseph’s spirits improved, while Agathe fretted about her son Philippe, whose trial loomed. To escape stress, Joseph went to the Comte de Sérizy’s chateau to work.Philippe, punished for actions during the unrest, was sentenced to five years’ surveillance and exiled to Autun. Desroches intervened, securing a change of residence to Issoudun and urging Philippe to reclaim his inheritance from Uncle Rouget. Motivated to restore the family’s fortunes and confront Max and the Rabouilleuse, Philippe prepared to return to Issoudun and, after a brief dinner, departed to face the challenge.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On All-Souls’ Day, November 2, Philippe Bridau registered at the Issoudun commissary and was told to stay on rue l’Avenier. News that a banished officer linked to a painter falsely accused in the affair had arrived unsettled the town. Maxence Gilet, recently recovered, had converted all of Père Rouget’s mortgages into cash, alarming many. Monsieur Hochon, after consulting notary Héron—who thought Rouget’s heirs should thank him for preventing Gilet’s profit—resolved to protect the Bridaus, long resentful of Gilet and fearful of losing property.Gilet and Flore Brazier received Philippe, confident they could manipulate Rouget to secure his funds. Philippe presented himself to his uncle in tattered clothes; his disordered appearance betrayed hardship, but he refused to beg. His blunt pride impressed Flore and Gilet. Gilet sensed a latent threat but avoided open conflict.Philippe then visited the Hochons, meeting curiosity and fear—Madame Hochon especially linked him to past tragedies. Candid about living on little, he won some household members with charm and honesty. Requesting secrecy, he asked Monsieur Hochon for names of officers hostile to Gilet—Mignonnet and Carpentier. Befriending them, Philippe impressed them with knowledge of the recent conspiracy against the Bourbons and, by careful behavior, integrated into the local military circle while concealing his aims.He dined regularly with Mignonnet and Carpentier, cultivated a respectable image, took fencing lessons and practiced shooting. His disdain for Gilet was evident, yet Gilet remained cautious.Philippe’s inquiries led him to Fario, who gave information about the Knights of Idleness and their schemes. Suspecting Gilet and Flore planned to secure Rouget’s income and flee, Philippe urged Rouget to take walks to loosen Flore’s influence. He told Hochon that Gilet sought power of attorney to access Rouget’s funds; they agreed to watch Rouget’s movements. When Gilet proposed going to Vatan to secure the money and marry—Flore, fearful of Philippe’s influence, reluctantly consented. Tensions rose as Philippe’s resolve to protect his family and restore their honor hardened, setting the stage for a dramatic town showdown.As Philippe walked arm in arm with his uncle Rouget along the boulevard Baron, he contemplated the looming confrontation between Colonel Bridau and Captain Maxence Gilet—the town hoped it would settle the struggle over Rouget’s fortune. Monsieur Hochon listened as Philippe outlined a ruthless plan to regain control of Flore, the Rabouilleuse.Philippe told Rouget bluntly that Flore loved Maxence and wanted Rouget out of the way so she could marry him. Though Rouget still loved her, Philippe insisted he could make her obedient again—by killing Maxence—if Rouget would refuse to sign the power of attorney they demanded before December third. That document, Philippe warned, would let them sell Rouget’s assets and flee to Paris. Rouget recoiled at Philippe taking Maxence’s place; Philippe answered that fear and force were the only ways to handle women.They met Mignonnet and Carpentier, whom Philippe instructed to arrange the banquet seating so he could force a public confrontation with Gilet. They agreed but warned of Gilet’s skill as a duelist. Philippe reassured Rouget that after proper mourning he and Flore would soon be together.At home Rouget found Kouski in tears: Flore had left with Vedie. Rouget’s frantic search failed. Gilet, sensing growing hostility over his conduct, felt pressure to act before a duel; Captain Potel urged him to face Bridau. Yet Gilet remained focused on securing Rouget’s fortune first, plotting to transfer assets to Flore to keep them from Philippe—believing the power of attorney would let him control events.Aware of these moves, Philippe prepared to counter them, suspecting Gilet would seek reconciliation with Flore. He planned to use Baruch to secure the power of attorney on Rouget’s behalf while distracting or keeping Gilet at bay, determined to protect his uncle’s interests and return Flore to Rouget.Rouget drafted a letter threatening to revoke his will in Flore’s favor unless she returned; Philippe encouraged it to force her back. Town gossip swelled about the impending duel and the estate’s fate. When Flore returned, Philippe asserted control and warned her of consequences if she disobeyed. Rouget, emotionally fragile, clung to her; by morning the town speculated on the duel, and Philippe, confident in his banquet plan, believed it would decide both Rouget’s fate and his own.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Around four the next afternoon officers of the old army gathered at the place du Marché in Issoudun, waiting for Colonel Philippe Bridau at a coronation banquet. Conversation turned to Max’s recent dismissal from old Rouget’s house; only Potel and Renard defended him. An old sub‑lieutenant said Max should have resisted. When Max arrived he was coldly received, a sharp contrast to Philippe’s warm welcome, which stiffened Max’s resolve to confront him.At dinner Philippe toasted the Grand Army and was echoed by several soldiers. Mignonnet toasted those who had tried to restore the Emperor’s son, and Max, whose reputation gave weight to his words, rose with an ironic wish to meet again next year. Tension mounted as Philippe and Max exchanged barbs: Philippe remained composed while Max struggled to control himself. The meal ended tensely; the duel was set for the next morning with sabres, Goddet, a former army surgeon, to attend.That night Monsieur and Madame Hochon urged Philippe to pray; Madame Hochon offered a relic, which he declined, though she vowed to pray for him. The next morning both men appeared composed, but Max felt intimidated by Philippe’s superior stance. When the duel began Max attacked but was quickly disarmed; Philippe paused to let him recover his weapon, increasing the strain. When they resumed they exchanged fierce blows; Philippe was wounded but delivered a fatal stroke. Max died, his ambitions and the Order of Idleness ended, much to the town’s relief.Flore Brazier, devastated, fell ill with fever and inflammation. Philippe, recovering from his own wounds, wrote letters outlining plans to secure his inheritance and manage Flore’s situation, noting her illness prevented her from fleeing and intending to use her to his advantage.By February 1823, urged by others and influenced by Philippe’s suggestion that marriage would secure her future, Flore, recovering, was persuaded to accept a match he had orchestrated. The contract granted her a substantial dower and a life annuity. Agathe, Philippe’s mother, delighted in her son’s apparent reform and the town’s approval, but the family lawyer Desroches warned that Philippe’s cunning would likely prevent her and Joseph from inheriting Rouget’s property.Once married, Philippe asserted control: demanding a power of attorney from Rouget, using Flore to manage her uncle’s affairs while keeping her under close watch, and seeking to reestablish himself in the army and in Paris. Backed by local endorsements and letters to the minister of war, he was reinstated as lieutenant‑colonel.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lieutenant-Colonel Philippe Bridau returned to Paris with his aunt and the ailing Rouget, whom he took to the Treasury to transfer his uncle’s income to himself. The old man and the Rabouilleuse were soon absorbed into extravagant Parisian life; Rouget overindulged after a lavish supper with the dancer Lolotte and died, reportedly of indigestion.Madame Rouget prospered in Paris, though Philippe had Mariette watch her to prevent serious lapses. In October 1823 Philippe went to Issoudun to settle his uncle’s estate, quickly amassing 1,600,000 francs (excluding valuable paintings held by Monsieur Hochon), and entrusted the funds to Mongenod and Sons.Philippe married and legally protected his wife’s fortune if she died childless. He bought her a furnished apartment and secretly acquired a grand hôtel on rue de Clichy, spending lavishly on renovations and furnishings. The rise of Charles X aided the Duc de Chaulieu’s circle; because Philippe supplied information about past conspiracies he was appointed lieutenant-colonel in the Duc de Maufrigneuse’s regiment and, through court connections, ennobled as Comte de Brambourg.Ambitious and insatiable, Philippe sought further honors—an aide-de-camp to the Dauphin and ultimately a general’s rank—distancing himself from old friends who might jeopardize his ascent. He planned to marry Mademoiselle Amélie de Soulanges and acquire military distinctions to cement his status.Meanwhile Joseph Bridau struggled as an artist, supported by his mother Agathe, who ran a lottery office. Despite talent, Joseph endured poverty and criticism while Philippe’s success overshadowed him. Deceived by appearances, Agathe accepted Philippe’s rise as justification for his neglect. Desperate, she wrote to Philippe for help; his cold reply devastated her and hastened her decline. Near death she learned painfully of the contrast between her sons, realized she had misplaced her affections, and died; Joseph buried her alone, heartbroken, while Philippe remained indifferent.Joseph found solace among friends. Later Flore, Philippe’s wife, wrote pleading for help in destitution. Joseph and Bixiou visited, arranged care, and with friends plotted to expose Philippe’s abandonment and secure justice. Philippe continued to rise until his fortunes collapsed in the July Revolution of 1830. Ultimately Joseph inherited Philippe’s estate and achieved success in art, becoming Comte de Brambourg; he remained humble, often reflecting on his family’s tragedy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Duchesse de Castries.CHAPTER IThe commercial traveller, a modern figure, signals the shift from mere material enterprise to intellectual influence: he links local power to universal markets, equalizes products, and transmits metropolitan ideas to the provinces. A self-taught actor and observer, he knows the world superficially, reads people quickly, and must judge habits and solvency at a glance. Travel taught him the haunts of vice and virtue throughout France; he persuades with polished speech, seizes opportunities, and often shows more practical diplomacy than ambassadors, negotiating deals in taverns rather than salons. By sheer persuasiveness he imports Parisian innovations into provincial life.One exemplar is Gaudissart: an acrobat of commerce extracting wealth from the provinces with a torrent of phrases. Marked by an olive-green overcoat and striped shirt, he is persuasive, disarming, and adept at courting attention. Having made his fortune selling hats—enchanting vendors and winning social favor—he is jovial and festive but businesslike when required, tailoring his approach to each audience.The Revolution of 1830 turned ideas into commodities; writers began selling thought, speculators followed, and Gaudissart adapted. He took a commission selling life-insurance policies, learned quickly, earned praise from employers, and even sold newspaper subscriptions. Confident in his talents, he agreed to market both insurance and periodicals.CHAPTER IIAfter a week immersed in Saint-Simonism and finance, Gaudissart prepared to travel in mid-April. Tempting hat offers tried to lure him back, but he set off, joking with Jenny, a florist, about refurnishing her room from his travels. She doubted his boasts, prompting playful banter about his prospects. He assured her he would earn handsomely from insurance and the profitable "Children’s Journal," measuring himself against successful politicians and merchants. The next morning he left for his assigned towns, stopping in Blois and writing to Jenny, boasting of his sales.CHAPTER IIIArriving in Vouvray, Gaudissart expected his persuasive gifts to work but met provincial resistance. The Tourangians were witty, satirical, and hostile to Saint-Simonism. At the Soleil d’Or inn he encountered Monsieur Vernier, skeptical of commercial travellers and aware of Gaudissart’s reputation. Vernier resolved to humiliate him and sent Gaudissart to Margaritis, a local reputed lunatic.Margaritis, obsessed with wine and newspapers, began a conversation that quickly turned absurd. Despite Gaudissart’s efforts to explain life insurance, the madman’s eccentricities produced nonsensical replies. Gaudissart tried to maintain composure and sell his product, but the man’s erratic behavior and the watching townspeople thwarted him.CHAPTERS IV–VSeated at home with wine, Margaritis spoke delusions while two women listened amused and Vernier watched, intent on spectacle. The talk devolved into misunderstandings and incoherence. When the innkeeper revealed Vernier had engineered the encounter, Gaudissart, enraged at the insult, demanded satisfaction. The townspeople enjoyed the scene; Vernier’s refusal escalated toward a duel until the innkeeper proposed mediation over breakfast. They patched up the quarrel—acknowledging its absurdity—and Gaudissart left Vouvray, resolved to return to Paris and his usual work, reminded that eloquence alone cannot overcome discernment about when persuasion is futile.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################To Monsieur le Comte Ferdinand de Gramont.MY DEAR FERDINAND,—If these lines endure in literature, they will be a small token of gratitude for your efforts. You, Hozier, Cherin, the King-at-Arms of these Studies of Life, have enriched the “Human Comedy” with your heraldic designs. Your manual of French Heraldry, showing deep knowledge of the feudal spirit and learned symbolism, has greatly influenced me.Your old friend,  DE BALZAC.On the skirts of Le Berry lies Sancerre, a charming town perched on a hill above the Loire flats. It benefits from fertile alluvium left by occasional floods, though some areas suffer drifting sand. Its distance from the river let the nearby port of Saint-Thibault prosper, shipping wine and other goods. By the time this story begins, suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault have replaced ferries, marking progress since the upheaval of 1830 when the House of Orleans funded improvements.Sancerre’s steep, narrow streets within the Great Ramparts form its main thoroughfares. Wine is the chief industry, producing vintages comparable to Burgundy and supplying Paris; lower villages like Fontenoy and Saint-Satur recall picturesque vineyards. Once of military consequence, the town declined as trade shifted to the Loire’s right bank. Its population of about 3,500 is now eclipsed by Cosne’s 6,000, a reversal of fifty years. Still, Sancerre retains historic charm, fine views, pure air, and a civic community that is friendly but watchful—predominantly Calvinist without strict Puritanism.Notable natives include Horace Bianchon, a luminary in modern medicine, and Etienne Lousteau, a successful journalist. Politically, a few large landowners dominate municipal life and manipulate elections. A local plot to break their hold—fueled by vanity—collapsed from internal jealousy. A proposed remedy was to unite behind one champion for the next election, one of Sancerre’s two representatives in Paris—a progressive provincial plan advanced by the borough’s Superior Woman, whose personal interests and past explain her ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Around 1836 Sancerre revealed a locally notable figure known as the Superior Woman, newly noticed amid a Parisian cultural influence—Etienne Lousteau and Bianchon had prospered there, and George Sand’s sentimental “Sandism” helped bring previously hidden superior women into view.The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town-and-country house near Saint-Satur belonging to a diminished branch of an illustrious family. The tale traces back to a Milaud sheriff who, a former Calvinist, converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes and was rewarded under Louis XIV, though later anti-heretic measures brought misfortune. Under Louis XV a Milaud de la Baudraye secured a military commission for his son, who died in battle, leaving a child later favored by Louis XVI.In 1800 an ex–farmer-general, returning from exile, bought La Baudraye out of vanity. His only son, Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, was frail—blamed on his father’s excesses—and was devotedly raised by his noble mother until her death recalled his father to France. On his father’s death Polydore inherited the fief, some money, and a collection of autographs. He lived frugally in Sancerre’s healthy air, enlarged the vineyards between 1802 and 1815, but hesitated to press Parisian debts during the Restoration. After Napoleon’s fall he tried to monetize his father’s collection, failed, and returned home.In 1823, at forty-three, Polydore sought a wife to secure an heir. He chose Dinah Piedefer, a beautiful, educated woman from a once-Calvinist family reduced to poverty. Educated in a reputable school, Dinah hoped to rise socially and converted to Catholicism. The Archbishop of Bourges, seeing her promise, tried to arrange a match, but families feared her beauty and intellect. When Polydore arrived in Bourges to seek the Cardinal’s support while recovering debts, his bluntness offended the Minister; nevertheless he married Dinah, provoking local speculation.Polydore’s Paris dealings were fraught: pressing debtors including the Duc de Navarreins until a bargain—forgiving the Duc’s debts in exchange for a tax-collector post—was proposed. He accepted, briefly became a tax collector, then obtained lucrative appointments and rose to Royal Commissioner. By 1827 he bought the estate of Anzy.Dinah transformed La Baudraye—gardens, improved interiors, refined company—while keeping her husband’s financial maneuvers private. Her beauty, intellect, Paris correspondence, and demand for conversation set her apart; provincial women resented and isolated her even as local men admired her. Madame Piedefer rejoiced in her daughter’s success, and Polydore in having a Superior Woman. Their story shows how exceptional qualities can inspire both admiration and resentment, ultimately isolating those who exceed local norms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1825 Dinah de la Baudraye faced accusations of socializing only with men and became a target of criticism. In defending her reputation she once made a rash remark to a false friend about her isolation—“I would rather have my bowl empty than with anything in it!”—which backfired. Childless after five years of marriage, she invited further ridicule.Monsieur de la Baudraye was a frail man with thin legs who sometimes wore false calves and favored peculiar dress. Gentle and seldom contradicting Dinah, he allowed her to dominate conversation. Admired for beauty and intelligence, Dinah drew gentlemen from nearby towns, relishing attention and delivering prepared speeches drawn from wide reading on varied subjects.Her charm created a protective aura that shielded her from much censure; Monsieur often excused himself from her long expositions, preferring walks or business. As she established herself, she championed the romantic arts, collected antiques, and filled their home with Renaissance treasures. Her husband supported these interests but focused chiefly on his land.Over the years her notable collection impressed visitors and supplied material for her talks on art and history. She attempted to form a literary circle in Sancerre, but it degenerated into social gatherings lacking literary focus—an evident disappointment for Dinah, who hoped to elevate local culture. Admirers, including magistrates and a priest, were often diverted from intellectual pursuits; one young man who yawned during a lecture fell from her favor, revealing her strict expectations.When Dinah turned twenty in 1826 suitors still courted her. Devoted to her role as wife and sheltered by her mother and the visible presence of admirers, she kept slander at bay while Monsieur used the marriage to secure favors. Local women speculated, and a slow domestic drama—fed by the era’s appetite for gossip—shaped her seemingly virtuous early married life.A young woman whose academic success sprang from pride was unlikely to halt a promising trajectory. Monsieur de la Baudraye, though appearing frail, proved an unexpected match for Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer; she nevertheless wondered why he married a seventeen-year-old and what she might gain.At first he surprised her: he let her use five acres for pleasure and financed house improvements so she could buy furniture and redecorate in varied styles. Dinah struggled to reconcile his stingy reputation with this apparent generosity, but the illusion dissolved after his second trip to Paris. When she asked for supplies she learned he strictly allotted a monthly allowance and paid her mother a yearly sum, stressing caution because their winegrower income was uncertain.Dinah’s attempts to provoke jealousy produced only indifference; he remained steady and unyielding. Frustration grew as she felt trapped in La Baudraye and Sancerre, longing for a more ambitious life. She briefly believed he might share such ambitions when he bought the estate of Anzy and applied for a patent of entail; for a time she hoped for a brighter future. But their short reconciliation exposed deeper contrasts: Dinah’s energy and potential clashed with Monsieur de la Baudraye’s lack of ambition and rigid routine.Over six years Dinah grew increasingly provincial, losing individuality and spirit. Paris and the provinces diverged: Paris offered varied social life and opportunities, while the provinces confined women to a single, often uninspiring identity. Her beauty and charm faded as she adapted—wardrobe reduced, she made her own clothes, elegance giving way to conformity. A meeting with her former school friend Anna, now a sophisticated Parisian, left Dinah despondent. She turned to poetry; her "Paquita la Sevillane" drew provincial attention and revealed talent but provoked anxiety about exposure. Despite literary effort, Dinah remained trapped, oscillating between dreams of fame and a suffocating routine that bred lasting resentment.After the Revolution of 1830, George Sand’s fame encouraged many French towns to celebrate local female talent, inspiring aspiring women writers. Monsieur de Clagny exploited this trend by publishing a collection by "Jan Diaz," a poet who had died young. Two hundred copies were printed; many sold in the departments where the poet had lived, while some sent to Paris were lost in editorial offices. It emerged that Jan Diaz was a pseudonym for Madame de la Baudraye—Dinah—who quickly became celebrated and was cast by some provincials as a rival to George Sand. Local, often polarized praise elevated work that might otherwise have been overlooked.Dinah had long felt unrecognized and sought fame to fill unmet ambitions. The late Abbé Duret had warned her about her husband’s character, but she resisted until she discovered his incapacity for love and his vengefulness; the Abbé’s warning proved accurate. As her reputation grew, domestic life felt ever more constraining and public sympathy intensified her despair. After a confrontation she vowed to stop writing and withdrew into desolation at Sancerre, where her absence from the literary scene was keenly felt.Despite fame, Dinah faced jealousy and opposition among local rivals. She had hoped to support Monsieur de Clagny’s political ambitions, but his electoral defeat left her disillusioned. These troubles concealed a deeper dissatisfaction and intensified her longing for freedom from marriage.At the château d’Anzy, Dinah contemplated her future and the possibility of meeting influential figures. She admired Lousteau, a journalist known for his charm with women, and tried to attract him to Sancerre; her efforts to engage Bianchon at first met indifference. When Lousteau and Bianchon finally arrived—initially unrecognized—Dinah invited them to her château and played the gracious hostess. She voiced frustrations with provincial life: monotony, the stifling of creativity and individuality, and the centralization of culture in Paris. Her frankness intrigued her guests; Lousteau was especially captivated, suggesting a possible deeper connection. In a lighter moment she asked for autographs—Bianchon and Lousteau obliged—leaving tangible links to the literary world and the recognition she craved. Dinah’s story thus reveals the tension between ambition and domesticity and a woman’s struggle for identity and fulfillment in a restrictive provincial environment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At five the next morning the household at the Chateau d’Anzy stirred for a day's sport arranged by the young Monsieur de la Baudraye, an ostentatious plan to reclaim twelve hundred acres. The Parisians in his company were awake for the shoot. Gatien Boirouge asked Monsieur Gravier why the Public Prosecutor, Monsieur de Clagny, had not joined them; Gravier said he had court duty, but Gatien suspected otherwise, believing rumor that Clagny had taken a place on the bench. Conversation slid into mockery and suspicion: Lousteau and Bianchon derided provincial life and Gatien’s anxieties, teasing that the affair was vanity and gossip.At a copse Gravier left the men with a keeper to pick a ravine path, while Bianchon and Lousteau lingered. They debated waiting for Gravier, but Gatien, convinced Gravier was already at breakfast with Madame de la Baudraye (Dinah) and Clagny, fretted: if the two were together might they watch one another until an opportunity arose? He feared Clagny’s seductive eloquence and Madame de la Baudraye’s Parisian ambitions. Lousteau and Bianchon treated such provincial intrigues with amused disdain, but Gatien insisted he could read the town’s women—there were too few of them for secrets to survive.Lousteau proposed a prank: at dinner they would tell lurid tales of betrayed wives and vengeful husbands to watch Dinah and Clagny for any telltale emotion. Bianchon agreed: theatrical stories might reveal expressions. Gatien favored entrapment, recalling a Parisian editor’s wife who frightened her husband with tales of lovers boiled alive; striking shocks, he believed, could unmask a relation. They resolved to keep diplomatic faces by day and be provocative by night.At dinner Lousteau was in his element. He produced an album of melancholic verses signed by himself—an elegy that seemed freshly written and won Madame de la Baudraye’s sympathy. Clagny, seeing her expression, reacted with thinly veiled dislike and took refuge in backgammon. Madame de la Baudraye, busy with embroidery, declared she stitched for her husband’s charity box; Lousteau taunted gently and she replied she hoped her needlework would still be for him if she ever married. Wry jests about adultery and morality flew: the Public Prosecutor criticized Parisian writers’ treatment of illicit love; Lousteau defended literature by invoking classic poets and symbols, provoking Clagny.The conversation widened into paradoxes: judged by stern courts, history seems to condemn pioneers—Socrates, Jesus—and institutions can persecute what they cannot assimilate. Lousteau teased that religion and state are fragile before new ideas; Clagny angrily called this blasphemy. The company touched nerves about justice, passion, and revenge. Monsieur Gravier half-joked he’d side with a betrayed husband if he were a writer; Lousteau mused on the sublimity of a trusting husband and questioned the balance between trust and jealousy. Madame de la Baudraye’s quiet sympathy for victims tormented into crime stirred unease: human justice poorly judges private tragedies.Lousteau then spun an anecdote from Charles Nodier about a Chevalier who feigned escape from a jealous commandant—harmless in itself—and received no reaction from Clagny or Dinah. Bianchon followed with the Spanish tale La Grande Bretèche—of a grandee walled into a cupboard to starve by a jealous husband—stunning the guests. Monsieur Gravier added a Spanish adventure of illicit deliveries, betrayals, and a murdered surgeon-major. The cumulative effect was chilling; Clagny reminded them that the Gazette des Tribunaux regularly recounted horrors comparable to romance.The Parisians—Lousteau, Bianchon, Etienne the doctor, the tax receiver—watched Dinah and Clagny closely, hypothesizing that if one were guilty of passion these grim tales would elicit a reaction. The evening closed with the bachelors holding a council in the corridors: Gravier briefed them on probing Dinah’s virtue; Lousteau warned serenity can mask cunning. Gravier suggested a country trick: affixing hairs in wax on the doors of Madame de la Baudraye and the Public Prosecutor to detect nocturnal comings and goings. That night the doctor, Lousteau, the receiver, and Gatien crept barefoot to press hairs into wax seals on the doors and agreed to inspect them at five.At dawn they found the seals unbroken—same wax, same hairs. Lousteau declared they had been chasing a will-o’-the-wisp and admitted the matter was more complex than they’d supposed. The discovery puzzled Gravier and Gatien. Lousteau privately told Bianchon that if Dinah were truly virtuous she deserved the effort it required and compared the situation to storming a fortress. With tone softened from mockery to admiration, Lousteau sought Dinah in the garden.Their conversation was partly contrived: she had asked him to study provincial life at La Baudraye; he cut to the point, asserting that Gatien’s gossip had misrepresented him as adoring her and that her silence at dinner had been an unguarded indiscretion. He confessed he had plotted the evening’s stories to rouse emotion and test both her and Clagny. He reassured her that her innocence had been proved—if she had yielded to Clagny, he would lose respect for her—and professed a taste for a certain perfection and disdain for cold, taciturn men. Dinah, embodying an ideal of superior womanhood, replied she believed Lousteau sought pleasure but not love, and that true love often comes late; she showed sympathy for his vulnerability, and for an instant the Parisian’s bravado softened.Lousteau’s frankness and their exchange delighted Dinah. He and Bianchon spent the day entertaining her with anecdotes and witty sayings—an intellectual feast she savored—while Lousteau flattered her by belittling a local celebrity and suggesting she could rival him. Flattered, Dinah warmed to the Parisians’ company; Clagny, Gravier, and Gatien looked on and perceived a more affectionate bearing from her.Word of the evening's promise spread; by sundown sixteen people had assembled at Anzy—coaches and hired horses bringing curious neighbors. Dinah prepared the drawing-room with a hostess’s pride. Lousteau, Bianchon, and Dinah exchanged knowing looks as provincial notables arrived in ancestral finery and local airs. A footman brought letters and a packet of proofs; Lousteau left a sheaf for Bianchon and, opening a wrapping, found an inferior romance used as packing: “Olympia, or Roman Revenge.” Spotting comic potential, he read fragments—brigands, a Duchess’s glove, Cardinal Borborigano—and with mock erudition invented commentary about the author’s pretensions and printing contrivances.Bianchon joined, and their improvised critique turned the tattered romance into an amusing spectacle. The audience—from old grandees to curious young women—drank in the performance. Dinah, pleased by the attention and wit, enjoyed the interplay; the room buzzed with amusement and social electricity. Thus the evening, intended as both entertainment and a test of character, became a stage where Parisian cleverness dazzled provincial complacency, while the mystery of the sealed doors remained unresolved for the men who had come to spy on hearts and reputations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For three days Dinah’s imagination was stirred by the talk of her two Parisian visitors. Lousteau watched her distraction, eager to captivate her. Dinah wished to show La Baudraye to her guests; when Bianchon’s papers were said to have been left in Anzy, a pretext arose: Gatien rode off to fetch them while Madame Piedefer went shopping, leaving Dinah alone with Lousteau and Bianchon. Lousteau sat close and bantered; Bianchon adopted a professorial air. They mocked the local men and probed the unhappy circumstances of her life, putting Dinah on the defensive.Crossing the Loire, Bianchon told her bluntly that she had shut herself against love but now needed it; Dinah, startled, asked if he prescribed love. He warned that her present habits would make her unattractive. Lousteau half-jokingly defended him as offering sound counsel. At Cosne, after a crowd had gathered by an old carriage, Dinah insisted they disembark and walked rapidly with Bianchon along the Loire. There Bianchon suggested that Lousteau loved her but was frivolous and hard to keep; he advised making a friend of him, since Lousteau could bring beauty and happiness. Under Bianchon’s scrutiny Dinah felt vulnerable and unsure how she might compete with Lousteau’s past lovers.Their coachman then announced Gatien’s return. Bianchon blessed them as they entered town. Lousteau immediately took Dinah’s hand and declared his love; she resisted, asking whether she should give herself to a man who treated her as a passing pleasure. Gatien’s arrival complicated matters. Lousteau whispered that she should feign resistance to preserve his honor and, in a reckless instant, crushed her muslin dress, leaving her disordered and humiliated. Gatien, oblivious, rode up while Lousteau tried to distract him; Dinah urged Gatien to fetch Bianchon’s papers, and he complied.Back at La Baudraye, Madame Piedefer appeared anxious; hearing Gatien’s horse, Dinah fled to her room. Outside, Lousteau and Gatien confronted one another—Gatien accused trickery, Lousteau shrugged it off as Dinah’s mockery. Dinah changed into a velvet gown; when she rejoined them, conversation skirted Bianchon while she remained silent, weighed down by recent events.Intent on winning her, Lousteau declared he would stay in Sancerre to be near her and swore he had renounced Parisian vices. On the road back to Anzy his charm began to dissolve Dinah’s reserve; she laughed, a sign her feelings were shifting. After a stolen intimacy in the gardens and a pledge exchanged before his return to Paris, Dinah accompanied him to Cosne, openly flouting propriety. At La Baudraye she praised him for showing what true love felt like. Her three admirers, including Monsieur de Clagny, were left to reckon with their feelings; Dinah took Clagny as a confidant, lavishing affection while he remained devoted despite her new happiness.Back in Paris, Lousteau soon forgot the happy days at the Chateau d’Anzy. He lived by his pen in an age when writing—after the rise of the bourgeoisie—had become near servitude: constant production or the appearance of it. He wrote daily for a newspaper and several literary magazines without artistic pretensions, ranking among Paris’s literary hacks. Deprived of advancement, he had become a journalist.Still, his life was not unpleasant. He mixed with blue‑stockings, aspiring actresses, publishers, and authors, enjoying a lifestyle that required little beyond paying rent. He kept theatre boxes, and the sales of books he reviewed covered minor expenses. His social life was busy, and his writing bought that ease. Ten years on, however, he remained as financially constrained as when he arrived, still imagining a monthly income of five hundred francs would make him rich.Lousteau lived in well‑furnished ground‑floor rooms in the Rue des Martyrs rented for twelve hundred francs. His finances peaked and dipped—January, April, July, and October were especially tight—because he spent heavily on cabs, meals, and gifts for lovers, leaving him perpetually broke despite earning seven to eight hundred francs a month.Occasionally weary of this literary life, he joked with friends like Nathan and Bixiou about marriage and romance. He had a mistress, Madame Schontz, proud to have a writer as her lover; she lived nearby and liked teasing friends about their relationships. This bohemian existence would later affect Dinah: he could laugh about his entanglements, including Dinah, whom he had almost forgotten.When Bixiou asked about Sancerre, Lousteau told of three timid provincial men who’d failed to pursue a local beauty. He received a long, affectionate letter from Dinah, which he read and burned, dismissing her feelings; a week later another arrived and he discarded it unread. Madame Schontz noticed the scented paper, opened hampers Dinah had sent—game and flowers—and suggested they host a dinner. Lousteau sent a clever but insincere letter he believed would charm Dinah. By December her letters were piling unread in a drawer.Then Madame Schontz told him of a marriage opportunity. A young woman’s previous lover had died, and her father, a notary, wanted to marry her quickly, offering a substantial dowry. The notary sought a respectable man to restore the daughter’s position. Madame Schontz urged Lousteau to consider it, stressing the financial benefits and a comfortable life while warning about a strict mother‑in‑law who would scrutinize his past.Hesitant at first, Lousteau agreed to meet the notary and family. He flattered the notary, recalling his own father and presenting himself as ambitious. The notary liked him, and at his first dinner Lousteau noticed Félicie, the shy, grateful daughter; Madame Cardot regarded him as a man of promise. Buoyed by this reception, Lousteau felt a new purpose—stability, respectability, and the financial security the marriage promised.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Cardot, having borrowed the illustrated Gil Blas to inspect prints, unexpectedly went with her daughter Félicie to Lousteau’s bachelor rooms to judge her future son‑in‑law. Her sister‑in‑law Madame Camusot had already been uneasy about the match after unflattering reports from her husband, a Supreme Court Councillor. On a January day in 1837 they took a cab; the porter was away, but his daughter handed them the key after Madame Cardot slipped her a gold coin.Around noon Lousteau, returning from breakfast, was startled when Dinah, his former lover, arrived by coach and revealed she was a mother. Overwhelmed, he tried to dismiss her, but she insisted on staying. The child, Pamela, sensing the awkwardness, announced that his mother‑in‑law was inside; Lousteau discovered Madame Cardot and Félicie hiding. Dinah declared her love; Lousteau, fearing the arranged marriage he faced, began to imagine using her to escape it.Madame Cardot openly disapproved and left with Félicie, while Dinah, trusting Lousteau’s reassurances and high‑minded air, remained. She admired the apparent elegance of his rooms, not their underlying poverty. Pamela had borrowed dinnerware from Madame Schontz, who learned of Lousteau’s predicament and warned him Cardot would not pursue the match. Lousteau, however, focused on his new intimacy with Dinah and the prospect of avoiding a wealthy marriage.When his friend Bixiou challenged him—arguing provincial women often fail to adapt to Paris and that marriage should secure legitimacy—Lousteau dismissed him. Later Madame Schontz told him Cardot had arranged Félicie’s marriage to another, extinguishing his respectable prospects. Having lost a stable future, Lousteau grew more entangled with Dinah; their domestic life, sustained by his entertainments, slid into the provincial romance he once mocked, leaving both trapped in dependency and illusion.This winter in Paris did for Madame de la Baudraye what October had done in Sancerre. Etienne, eager to introduce Dinah to Parisian life, took her to the theater, where she at first felt self-conscious. Lousteau, vain and ambitious, stripped away her provincial modesty by taking her to fashionable dressmakers and introducing her to society. Dinah adapted quickly, immersing herself in the city’s culture and entertainments.A newspaper item about Monsieur le Baron de Clagny’s promotion recalled Sancerre to Dinah, and she teased Lousteau about lingering attachments. Their passion remained intense: they exchanged extravagant vows even as Lousteau privately entertained the idea of deceiving her when his fancy flagged, treating her as a useful asset. Dinah, however, retained dignity and ambition. More than a charming companion, she was intelligent and well read, able to hold her own among sophisticated women; Lousteau came to regard her as both muse and partner.When Dinah became pregnant she prepared to withdraw from public life but first attended the premiere of a Nathan play with mixed excitement and anxiety. The evening soured: seated beside Anna Grossetete, an acquaintance, she was exposed to scrutiny when Lousteau abandoned her to mingle. Humiliated and uneasy, Dinah received a theater visit from Clagny, who warned that her liaison with Lousteau threatened her reputation. Though resolute about her sacrifices for love, Clagny’s words provoked nostalgia and regret.After the child’s birth Dinah devoted herself to motherhood and running the household, determined to support Lousteau’s career. She pawned jewelry to help finances, believing her sacrifices would secure a better life. Lousteau’s financial irresponsibility and laziness soon emerged: he failed to write steadily, accumulated debts, and grew indifferent and dependent. A second child increased Dinah’s burdens while his detachment deepened; he sought comfort with friends and other women and withdrew from domestic duties.Her love compelled her to sustain him, but the sacrifices became one-sided. Jealousy and insecurity led to conflicts. The turning point came when her estranged husband, Monsieur de la Baudraye, reappeared seeking a power of attorney to manage an inheritance. Clagny—now Attorney‑General—aided Dinah, negotiating an allowance in exchange for her cooperation and protecting her interests. Confronted with betrayal and loss, Dinah reached a crossroads: Lousteau’s lack of ambition proved her sacrifices might not be returned, and she resolved to reclaim her identity and seek a future defined by mutual respect and commitment rather than mere passion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day Etienne lived in luxury and Dinah could hold her own among Paris’s best-dressed women. Lousteau, however, affected ruin before friends, blamed Madame de la Baudraye, and feigned helplessness to exploit Dinah’s supposed desperate love—while remaining unfaithful. Monsieur de Clagny, upset by Dinah’s disgrace, warned her of betrayal; she replied, “I know it.” When Clagny asked if she still loved Lousteau, she cried, “I would lose my soul for you!”—a confession that left him speechless and teary.Slowly Dinah recognized Lousteau’s nature. She told her mother he was a poet: idle and pleasure-seeking, but someone she could not hate because she felt responsible, convinced she was his only support. Her mother deplored the hell she endured; Dinah answered, “I will be a mother to him!” Accepting humiliations to keep him, she compromised her dignity, hoping to win back his affection by emulating Madame de Pompadour. His insincere, theatrical devotion eroded her self-respect, yet she clung to the warped consolation of being his plaything.Dinah rehearsed the tragedy Le Dernier Jour d’un condamné, imagining their separation, but every word or gesture from Lousteau pulled her back. She even contemplated suicide while walking in her garden, yet her devotion persisted. Influenced by the novel Adolphe, she vowed not to become like Ellenore—refusing to display her pain in tears or reproaches.Meanwhile Monsieur de la Baudraye plotted to restore his fortune by manipulating his wife’s inheritance—underreporting its value, keeping the difference, and investing in land. He convinced himself he could defer obligations to Dinah, planning to restore her fortune later. Ambitious for status, he envisioned a noble life in Sancerre, seeking a title and greater social prominence.By early 1842 Dinah felt reduced to Lousteau’s convenience, sacrificing herself for his comfort. She dressed in mourning as a sign of remorse; her mother worried and sought to intervene. The reality, far from dramatic literature, was fatigue and disillusionment. With Clagny’s help, Madame Piedefer urged Dinah to consider life apart from Lousteau, suggesting her husband might allow her to keep the children and live in Paris. Dinah let Clagny negotiate; he assured her her husband would support a respectable return to society.Unknown to Dinah, Monsieur de la Baudraye had been investing in a grand Paris residence to polish his image, aiming for a peerage and a well-furnished home. He invited her to live with him in Paris and promised financial support for their children’s education.Now a Countess, Dinah felt the weight of title and expectation, preparing for social prominence that contrasted with earlier struggles. In May 1842 she paid off her debts and invited Lousteau to dinner—an event that marked a decisive change. When he arrived he was struck by her transformation. Dinah told him of her new status and declared their separation: she still cared but insisted on a calm parting. Lousteau, feeling betrayed, accused her of ruining his life; she stood firm, refusing blame. Their last exchange mixed sorrow and defiance. Dinah left both liberated and heartbroken.In her new life she transformed her home into a model of comfort, spending more than her husband expected. Monsieur de la Baudraye, back in Paris, admired her efforts but remained unaware of the emotional turmoil beneath her composed exterior. Though charming and fashionable, Dinah missed the passion and excitement of the past and struggled to connect with other women. Clagny tried to introduce her to influential figures, but she remained unfulfilled.After a theater evening Dinah confided to Clagny about lingering feelings; he then revealed Lousteau was living with another woman. This provoked conflicting emotions and drove her deeper into society, seeking success and recognition to fill the gap. In March 1843 she was appointed to collect donations for charity—a turning point that restored her public image and social standing, though emotional scars remained. Summers found her in Sancerre, where her husband doted on their children; in June 1844 he proudly presented them to a cousin, showcasing the family’s new status.Dinah maintained composure through these shifts, balancing roles as mother, Countess, and a woman shaped by sacrifice. Yet she remained torn between love and ambition, public success and private longing—an unresolved tension that defines her journey.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Across France there were many Chevaliers de Valois; the one in Alençon is this tale’s focus. Though sharing the name, these chevaliers mostly did not know one another; they accepted the Bourbon monarchy because the Valois line had no male heir. The Alençon chevalier was an elderly, tall, thin gentleman whose youth in Paris and romantic pursuits had been interrupted by the Revolution.Well regarded among the local aristocracy, he was noted for his manners, wit, and anecdotes from Louis XV’s reign. A regular at social gatherings, his charm and discretion made him a favorite. At fifty-eight he claimed to be fifty, preserving a youthful air despite age. He had a prominent nose and a pale complexion that reddened on one side—his excuse for the flush, and for a voracious appetite, was a “hot liver.”Though seemingly frail, he remained vigorous and passionate; his face bore the marks of a lived life. He groomed himself carefully and kept his clothes clean though worn, maintaining aristocratic standards. Living modestly in a rented room above Madame Lardot’s laundry, he dined out daily on a small annuity. Beloved in Alençon for card-playing and amiability, he was a master of conversation who mingled with all ranks and whose presence was sought. Yet curiosity attended his private life, including persistent rumors of a secret marriage to a woman named Césarine. Financially precarious, he managed provincial life with dignity.One spring morning Suzanne, a young girl from Madame Lardot’s laundry, interrupted him with news that might bring pleasure or pain; she feigned indifference to his rumored link with Césarine. Ambitious and clever, Suzanne aspired to leave Alençon for Paris, convinced she could better herself. The chevalier, struck by her charm and drive, encouraged her in a paternal tone and advised courting Monsieur du Bousquier, a wealthy bachelor who had shown interest.Du Bousquier, by contrast, bore a dubious reputation: once wealthy and influential, his role as a purveyor during the Revolution had tarnished him. Returned to Alençon, he hoped to marry Mademoiselle Cormon, a rich old maid, to restore his fortunes. His brusque opportunism contrasts with the chevalier’s elegance; their rivalry over Mademoiselle Cormon, and Suzanne’s ambitions, set in motion an intricate dance of desire, ambition, and societal expectation in a changing world.Madame Granson, a widow on a modest pension and a small property, struggled to support her son Athanase, who only added a meager salary from the mayor’s office. Their simple, provincial home shaped their lives and morals. Athanase, a pale, introspective twenty-three-year-old, secretly loved his cousin Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he believed to be wealthy. His passion was tangled with social expectations and the possible ridicule of loving an older woman.What began as a practical hope to secure a better life for himself and his mother grew into genuine affection. He admired Mademoiselle Cormon from afar, constrained by circumstances that smothered his talent and ambition. Madame Granson, aware of his feelings, saw the match’s advantages despite Athanase’s lack of fortune and the cousin’s age; she waited patiently for the right moment while Athanase dreamed of a future together despite daunting barriers.The arrival of Suzanne, an even poorer young woman, changed the dynamics. She felt an immediate bond with Athanase and grasped his struggles, yet her ambition pushed her toward deception as a way out of poverty. After a brief meeting with Madame Granson, Suzanne left town with money and new clothes; the community lamented her departure.As Athanase prepared for a dinner with Mademoiselle Cormon—encouraged by his mother—their fates began to diverge: Suzanne headed for Paris to remake her life, while Athanase remained in Alençon, weighed down by unfulfilled dreams and provincial pressures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In many second-class French prefectures a salon gathered influential—but not elite—locals. Hosted by prominent residents, it included lesser nobility, clergy, and magistrates who were invited to events but did not mix with the highest echelons; the latter kept their distance and communicated formally. The salon had considerable local influence: members prized practicality over luxury, focused on land and local concerns, resisted Parisian fashions, and used gossip as a form of judgment.
