Lady Dudley embodied that materialism: compelling physically but leaving an emptiness I did not feel with Madame de Mortsauf, whose love was spiritual and boundless.Lady Dudley’s jealousy was masked by charm; she sought to erase Madame de Mortsauf from my heart. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf, but my letters went unanswered, filling me with dread. When I finally returned to Clochegourde, a coldness revealed the harm my infidelity had caused.Madame de Mortsauf’s demeanor had altered; she was distant and sorrowful, her beauty dimmed by grief. I felt the weight of my betrayal as I watched her withdraw, pride and pain evident. The count, unaware of the turmoil that had provoked such change, welcomed me warmly, while Madame de Mortsauf remained aloof.Witnessing her, I realized the depth of my wrongdoing. The count, whose health was fragile, confessed his fears and the emotional toll he bore; Madame de Mortsauf’s health seemed delicate, and I was tormented by the thought that I was its cause. Devoted to her children, she sought solace in them, yet shadows of despair crept in.In desperation I sought Madame de Mortsauf’s forgiveness, but she rebuffed me, insisting I had caused her too much pain. Left to grapple with guilt, I felt the weight of my actions pressing down on me. Torn between two women who represented different aspects of love—Lady Dudley’s passionate, material allure and Madame de Mortsauf’s nurturing devotion—I was forced to confront the true nature of love and sacrifice; my desires carried duties, and the harm I caused those I cared for weighed heavily on me.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I know nothing more destructive than the wit of an Englishwoman; it carries the pompous certainty with which the British mask their prejudices. French wit is lace, adorning life’s joys and quarrels; English wit is corrosive, stripping flesh until only bone remains. Arabella wielded her power like a sultan, cutting down rivals with her words.“My angel,” she said, “I can speak morality too. I’ve questioned whether my love for you is a crime, and I find it both natural and pious. God created beauty to inspire adoration. The real crime would be not to love you. That other woman insults you by comparing you to men; the laws of morality do not apply to you, for you are above them. Am I not drawing closer to divine love by loving you? Will God punish a woman for seeking the divine? My love is a weakness, while that woman’s is strength. Don’t frown; I do not wish her ill. I adore the morality that has freed you for me—because you are mine forever, aren’t you?”“Yes.”“Forever and ever?”“Yes.”“Ah! I have found favor in my lord! I alone understand his worth! You say she knows how to cultivate her estate. I leave that to farmers; I cultivate your heart.”I try to recall this intoxicating babble to convey Arabella and the events that followed. Her passionate speech, mingled with flattery, sought to obliterate the impressions left by Henriette’s dignified love with impetuous affection. Arabella’s fierce attack revealed fear and admiration for her rival. The next morning she was tearful, lamenting a sleepless night.“What troubles you?” I asked.“I fear my love will ruin me,” she replied. “I have given all. That woman possesses something you still desire. If you prefer her, forget me; I will not trouble you. I will go far and die, like a plant deprived of sunlight.”She forced pledges from me and, reassured, dried her eyes. “Go back to her; I do not want her to owe you to my force, but to your will. If you return, I shall know you love me as I love you, which I always doubted.”Under Arabella’s influence I returned to Clochegourde. The falsity of my position did not strike me then; had I refused, Lady Dudley would have found in Henriette a triumph. Arabella, certain of me, would have taken me to Paris; but going now to Clochegourde insulted Madame de Mortsauf. Would any loving woman forgive crimes against love? Unless an angel, a loving woman would rather see her lover die than know him happy with another. Thus my leaving Clochegourde for the Grenadiere was fatal to my true love and favored the transient affection that held me. Lady Dudley had calculated a skilful plan.When I met the countess that morning she was pale and depressed, like one who had not slept. Perception made me see actions the world ignores but lofty spirits call criminal. Like a child playing near a precipice, I felt we were separated by a universe. Henriette’s illusions were shattered; her heart had endured its passion. She refused to look at me, denying me the light that had shone on my life for six years. I felt the wrong of bringing into this calm roof, where pleasure was unknown, a face that had known joy. Had I let Lady Dudley depart alone and returned to Clochegourde, perhaps Henriette would not have resolved to treat me as a brother. But now she paid me ostentatious attentions—humiliating, like caring for a sick man.“You were out walking early,” said the count. “I hope you have brought back a good appetite, you whose stomach is not yet destroyed.”That remark, which made Henriette smile, completed my sense of ridicule. Day and night I could not be at Clochegourde and Saint-Cyr. By day, the transition from lover to brother seemed like an illness. I was ashamed; I cursed the pleasure Lady Dudley gave me; I wished Henriette would demand my blood. I could not tear her rival apart before Henriette, for she avoided speaking of Arabella. Had I mentioned her, Henriette would have despised me. After five years of intimate intercourse, we had nothing to say; our words had no connection with our thoughts; we hid intolerable pain from each other.Henriette feigned cheerfulness, but she was sad. She called herself my sister, and we had no common ground, remaining in constrained silence. She increased my misery by pretending to be the only victim.“I suffer more than you,” I said once.“How so?” she asked haughtily.“Because I am the one to blame.”Her manner grew so cold that I resolved to leave Clochegourde. That evening on the terrace I bade farewell. As I took my horse, she said, “Let us walk down the avenue together, alone.” At the grove marking the boundary, she stopped.“Farewell, my friend,” she said, resting her head on my chest and wrapping her arms around me. “Farewell, we shall never meet again. God has given me the sad power to see the future. Do you remember the terror when you first returned, so young, so handsome? I saw you turn your back on me as you do now. Once again, I have seen into the future. Friend, we speak for the last time. I can hardly say a few words; part of me speaks. Death has seized something in me. You have taken the mother from her children; I ask you to take her place. You can; Jacques and Madeleine love you, as if you had always made them suffer.”“Die!” I cried, terrified by the fire in her eyes. “Henriette, I command you to live. Swear to me you will send for Origet and obey him in everything.”“Would you oppose God’s mercy?” she cried, misreading me.“You do not love me enough to obey blindly, as that miserable Lady Dudley does?”“Yes, yes, I will do all you ask,” she cried, goaded by jealousy.“Then I stay,” I said, kissing her eyelids.Frightened, she escaped my arms and walked home without looking back. I followed; she was weeping and praying. We reached the lawn; I respectfully kissed her hand. This submission touched her.“I am yours—forever, and as you will,” I said; “for I love you as your aunt loved you.”She trembled and wrung my hand.“One look,” I said, “one last of our old looks! The woman who gives herself wholly,” I cried, “gives less of life and soul than I have now received. Henriette, thou art my best-beloved—my only love.”“I shall live!” she said; “but cure yourself as well.”That look erased Arabella’s memory. Thus I was the plaything of two irreconcilable passions, influenced alternately. I loved an angel and a demon; two women equally beautiful—one adorned with virtues we decry through hatred of our imperfections, the other with vices we deify through selfishness. Returning down the avenue, seeing Madame de Mortsauf surrounded by her children waving handkerchiefs, I felt a transient pride in being arbiter of two destinies; proud of inspiring such passion that death must come to whichever I abandoned. That conceit punished me doubly.I do not know what urged me to remain with Arabella and await the moment the count’s death might give me Henriette, for she would ever love me. Her harshness, tears, remorse, and Christian resignation testified to a feeling that could not be erased. Walking down that avenue, I was no longer twenty-five; I was fifty. A man passes quickly from youth to middle age. Perhaps evil thoughts came from the Tuileries, from the king’s cabinet. Who could resist the polluting spirit of Louis XVIII?Back in Paris, Arabella and I grew intimate. We soon abandoned conventional restrictions I had tried to keep—the comedies and concealments that renew desire. Young passions, like youth, raze their forests instead of cutting timber. Arabella adopted none of the bourgeois strategies; she wished to exhibit me to all Paris as her “sposo.” She used her powers of seduction to keep me under her roof, not content with whispered scandal. Seeing her happy in an imprudence that frankly admitted her position, how could I not believe her love?Once plunged into the comforts of our illegal union, despair seized me; I saw my life bound to a course defying Henriette’s ideas and advice. From then on I lived in a rage like consumptives who, knowing death near, cannot bear scrutiny. There was no corner of my heart where I could escape suffering; an avenging spirit filled me with forbidden thoughts. My letters to Henriette depicted this moral malady and did her infinite harm. “At the cost of so many treasures lost, I wished you to be at least happy,” she wrote in the only answer I received. But I was not happy. Happiness is absolute; it admits no comparisons. Once the first ardor fades, comparisons arise. All great passions first check character’s asperities; later, when lovers are accustomed, moral physiognomies reappear and natural antipathies surface. This period began with me. Less blinded, I undertook a critical examination of Lady Dudley that harmed her.First, she lacked the qualities of mind that distinguish Frenchwomen. When a Frenchwoman loves, she metamorphoses: coquetry adorns love; she abandons dangerous vanity and claims no merit but loving well. She espouses her lover’s interests and acquires experience in a day; heedless and prodigal by nature, she manages with grace. She becomes mother, adviser, doctor, giving transformations a grace that reveals infinite love. She combines other nations’ special qualities and unifies them with wit that enlivens and varies love, relieving monotony. The Frenchwoman loves always, in public or solitude; in public she speaks by silence or looks; if speech and look fail, she finds other means. The Englishwoman, by contrast, makes her love bend to the world. Educated to icy manners, she opens and shuts her heart with a British mechanism. Passionate when no eye sees her, she is cold before the world. A lover doubts his empire seeing her immobility, her aloof countenance, the calm voice with which she leaves her boudoir. Hypocrisy becomes indifference; she has forgotten all.A woman who can lay aside her love like a garment may be thought capable of changing it. What tempests in a man’s heart when he sees a woman take and drop her love like embroidery. These women, too completely mistress of themselves, never wholly belong to you; society’s influence prevents you reigning supreme. Where a Frenchwoman comforts with a look, an Englishwoman’s silence irritates and frets. They are so on their dignity that fashion often reigns over pleasures. An Englishwoman forces everything into form; Protestantism’s doubt and reasoning kill belief; it is the death of art and love. Passionate hearts flee worldliness.You can understand my shock when I found Lady Dudley could not live without the world, and the English system of two lives was natural to her. When she loved, she loved madly—no woman could compare; but when the curtain fell, she banished memory of it. In public she never answered a look or smile; she was ambassadress-like, folding her phrases and elbows; her composure outraged my heart. She degraded love to a need rather than raising it to an ideal. She expressed neither fear nor regret nor desire; then, at a given hour, tenderness reappeared like a sudden fire.Which of the two should I believe? By a thousand pin-pricks I felt the infinite differences between Henriette and Arabella. Madame de Mortsauf’s smallest movements—the sound of her gown, her lowered eyelids, her musical voice—were a continual caress, a consistent thought. She did not halve her soul for two atmospheres. Arabella’s mind existed for the world and sarcasm; she loved to rend and bite to satisfy craving. Madame de Mortsauf would have hidden her happiness; Lady Dudley chose to exhibit hers to all Paris yet kept an impenetrable English mask in public. This mixture of ostentation and dignity, love and coldness, wounded me. When I complained, she replied with triple sting, mingling boasts of love with cutting English sarcasms. In opposition she made amusement of hurting me, kneading me like dough. She caricatured my ideas, even daring to ask if I wished her to kiss me at the opera before all Paris. Despite passion, she was never meditative, self-contained, or reverent like Henriette; she was insatiable for excitement, noise, and celebrity; everything concerned herself, not me.Henriette’s letter you have read—proof of how this virtuous woman obeyed the genius of a Frenchwoman—shows how she had studied my prospects, political relations, moral conquests, and with what ardor she took hold of my life in permissible directions. Lady Dudley affected the reticence of an acquaintance. She never inquired into my affairs as a man. Prodigal for herself without being generous, she separated self-interest and love too decidedly. I knew, without proving it, that to save me a pang, Henriette would have sought for me what she would never seek for herself. In any great misfortune I would have gone to Henriette for counsel; I would sooner have been dragged to prison than say a word to Lady Dudley.The contrast extended to outward things. In France luxury expresses the man; it reproduces his ideas and gives attention value between lovers. English luxury, at first alluring, proved mechanical. The thousand attentions at Clochegourde Arabella considered servants’ business; each had a duty as if horses. She never attached to servants; the death of the best would not affect her; money could replace him. Her selfishness was candid and created laughter. Crimson draperies covered an iron nature. The siren of amorous folly could make a young man forget the hard Englishwoman; only gradually I discovered the stony rock where my seeds were wasted. Madame de Mortsauf had seen this at a glance. She was right; Arabella’s love became intolerable to me. I have since remarked that many women who ride well have little tenderness. Like Amazons, they lack a breast; their hearts are hard in some direction.When weariness took possession of me, when I comprehended the sanctity true feeling imparts to love, when memories of Clochegourde brought the fragrance of roses, the warmth of the terrace, and the warble of nightingales, I received a blow whose echo still wakes in my heart.I was working in the king’s cabinet when the Duc de Lenoncourt entered. The king asked news of the countess. I raised my head hastily; the king, offended, gave me the look that precedes harsh words.“Sire, my poor daughter is dying,” replied the duke.“Will the king deign to grant me leave of absence?” I cried, tears in my eyes.“Go, my lord,” he answered, withholding reprimand in satire.More courtier than father, the duke asked no leave but got into the carriage with the king. I left without bidding Lady Dudley goodbye, leaving a note that I was sent on a mission by the king. At the Croix de Berny I met his Majesty returning. He threw me an ironic look seeming to say: “If you mean to be anything in politics, come back; don’t parley with the dead.” The pompous equipages rolled away to cries of “Vive le Roi!” It seemed the court had driven over the dead body of Madame de Mortsauf with utter insensibility. Though the duke was an excellent man, he would no doubt play whist with Monsieur after the king retired. The duchess had long ago given her daughter the first stab by writing of Lady Dudley.My hurried journey was like a ruined gambler’s dream; I despaired at receiving no news. Had the confessor excluded me? I accused everyone. Beyond Tours, on the poplar-bordered road to Poncher, I met Monsieur Origet. He guessed I was going to Clochegourde and told me: “I doubt if you find her living. She is dying a frightful death—of inanition. When she called me last June, no medical power could control the disease; she had the symptoms Monsieur de Mortsauf knows, for he thinks he has them. Madame la comtesse was not in a transient condition; her disease had reached a point where science is useless; it is the incurable result of grief. Her physical condition is produced by inertia of an organ as necessary as the heart. Grief has done the work of a dagger. Don’t deceive yourself; she is dying of hidden grief.”“Hidden!” I exclaimed.“No,” he said, looking at me, “and since she was so seriously attacked, Monsieur de Mortsauf ceased to torment her. I am no longer needed; Monsieur Deslandes is all-sufficient; nothing can be done; her sufferings are dreadful. Young, beautiful, and rich, to die emaciated—for she dies of hunger! During the last forty days the stomach has rejected all nourishment.”Origet pressed my hand with respect. “Courage, monsieur,” he said, lifting his eyes.These words were like poisoned arrows. I sprang into the carriage and promised a reward if I arrived in time.Despite impatience, the distance seemed short as bitter reflections crowded my soul. Dying of grief, yet her children were well? Then she died through me! My conscience arraigned me. What weakness in human justice, which avenges open deeds but forgives murderers of the soul! How many victims—Madame de Beauseant, the Duchesse de Langeais, Lady Brandon—our period teems with such events. Who does not remember the young woman who poisoned herself from jealousy? Society and science are accomplices in crimes without assizes. The world declares no one dies of grief; our scientific nomenclature provides labels that serve as passports to the coffin. Is there some law we do not know? Must the century pitilessly strew earth with corpses? My God! do I belong to the race of tigers?Remorse gripped me as I entered Clochegourde’s avenue on a damp October morning. Jacques, Madeleine, and the Abbe Dominis knelt at the foot of a wooden cross. I sprang from the carriage toward them, my heart aching. The old huntsman stood apart.I kissed Jacques and Madeleine, who gave a cold look. The abbe rose; I took him by the arm.“Is she still alive?” I implored. He bowed sadly and said it might be better to wait; she was greatly changed. “It is necessary to prepare her for this interview, or it might increase her suffering—death would be a blessed release.”He told me how for six months the countess had suffered more despite Origet’s care. The doctor had come every evening for two months. “To heal the body, the heart must first be healed,” the doctor had said. As the illness increased, the countess’s words grew bitter. She called on earth to keep her, then repented. Such alternations rend the heart. “She falls, but each time she stumbles she rises higher toward heaven,” he said.I cried, “Surely this pure lily will flower in heaven?”“You left her still a flower,” he answered. “You will find her consumed, purified, pure as a diamond buried in ashes.”The count, white-headed, hurried out, declaring, “She was right! He is here! ‘Felix, Felix, Felix has come!’ she kept crying. My dear friend, death is here. Why did it not take a poor madman like me?”I walked toward the house but the Abbe Birotteau stopped me. “Madame la comtesse begs you will not enter yet.” Inside, servants moved, all dumb with grief. Manette searched for Madeleine; we paced the lawn. I saw the dear “mignonne” gathering autumn flowers. Overcome, I sat, broken.“Poor Felix,” said the count, “she forbade me to write to you. She knew how much you loved her.”All was gloom. The servants wept; work was left undone; the place seemed uninhabited. The count, speaking in bursts, blamed and then wept for his wife, mixing despair with accusations. Madeleine told me her mother was ready; the Abbe Birotteau accompanied me. He warned, “I have done all to prevent this meeting. The salvation of this saint required it. I shall be present to protect you against yourself and perhaps against her. Respect her weakness. She is dying of hunger and thirst. Since morning she suffers feverish irritation. Pray God she die not in a moment of doubt.”We reached the chamber. Henriette sat in white, before the fireplace, flowers about her. The abbe’s amazement at the room’s restored tenderness showed she had prepared to receive me. Her shrunken face had the greenish pallor of a magnolia; hollow eyes shone; her forehead kept proportion; inward fires issued despite waxy features. The smile on her whitened lips resembled death’s grin. Her robe showed emaciation. She knew she was changed; thought filled her with bitterness. No longer the sublime Madame de Mortsauf, she was a nameless thing struggling against annihilation. I took her dry, burning hand to kiss. Her discolored lips tried a smile—the forced smile of one seeking to hide irony, expectation, or fury.“Ah, my poor Felix, this is death,” she said, “and you do not like death; odious death. This is the end of love; I knew it would be so. Lady Dudley will never see you thus surprised. Ah! why have I so longed for you, Felix? You have come at last, and I reward your devotion by the same horrible sight that made the Comte de Rance a Trappist. I, who hoped to remain beautiful and noble in your memory, destroy your illusions! True love cannot calculate. But stay; do not go, stay. Monsieur Origet said I was better this morning; I shall recover. Your looks will bring me back to life. When I regain strength and nourishment, I shall be beautiful again. I am scarcely thirty-five; there are many years of happiness before me—we will leave Clochegourde and go to Italy.”Tears filled my eyes; I turned to the window. She put her burning lips to my ear: “My anguish has been in not seeing thee! Did you not bid me live? I will live; I will ride on horseback; I will know life, Paris, fêtes, pleasures!”Those tones pictured a lifetime’s battles, the anguish of true love lost. The countess rose with the motion of a child. The confessor fell on his knees and prayed. She flung an arm around my neck, kissed me violently, and said, “You shall not escape me now!” She fainted; I laid her on the bed. “You carried me thus before,” she murmured.Deslandes entered, surprised at the decorations. “We must suffer much to die,” she said faintly. The doctor felt a pulse and murmured that it was the forty-second day since she had eaten or drunk. The Abbe Birotteau took me to the garden. “Let us leave her to the doctor,” he said; “they will wrap her in opium. Well, you have heard her—if indeed it is she.”“No,” I said, “it is not she.”Stupefied, I hid in a punt to be alone. I tried to detach myself from my life—torture like that which afflicts adulterers. My life was a failure. Despair suggested to die beside her or bury myself at Meilleraye among Trappists. I looked at the windows where Henriette was dying, fancying I saw the light burning the night I betrothed my soul to hers. Ought I not to have followed the simple life she created, keeping faithful while I worked in the world? Had she not bidden me become great to be saved from shameful passions? Chastity! Love as Arabella understood it disgusted me.Madeleine walked slowly and sat on a bench. When I approached, she rose and returned to the house, hating me; she fled from her mother’s murderer. At the portico Jacques sat, expressionless. Young persons carrying death are often unmoved at funerals. I longed to question Jacques.“You know, Jacques,” I said, “that in me you have a most devoted brother.”“Your friendship is useless; I shall follow my mother,” he replied with a sullen look.He showed me his handkerchief stained with blood.They each had a fatal secret. The brother and sister avoided each other. Henriette lay low; all was ruins at Clochegourde.“Madame is asleep,” Manette said, happy that the countess was out of pain. Small joys matter then. “Monsieur Deslandes ordered the flowers taken away; they excited Madame’s nerves,” Manette added. She thought the flowers caused delirium.I returned as the sunset gilded Azay’s roofs. All was calm and pure. A soft light lit the bed where my Henriette lay wrapped in opium. The body seemed annihilated; the soul reigned. Blanche and Henriette, two sublime faces of one woman, reappeared. The abbes sat at the bed. The count stood stupefied. I took a seat on the sofa. We exchanged looks of admiration mingled with tears. The Abbe Dominis and I communicated in signs: yes, angels were watching. Her face cleared; the green of suffering gave way to pure white pallor; she seemed to embrace the divine.Madeleine knelt, adoring, crying “My mother!” Jacques smiled; he would follow her. “She is entering the haven,” said the Abbe Birotteau. The Angelus rang; Nature’s poetry joined the sacred hour. We burst into tears. Even servants knelt and prayed. The doctor signaled that this sleep was the hour without pain; it was time for the last sacraments. At nine o’clock she awoke quietly with gentle eyes, and we beheld her in former beauty.“Mother! you are too beautiful to die—life and health are coming back to you!” cried Madeleine.“Dear daughter, I shall live—in thee,” she answered, smiling.They embraced. The count kissed his wife. She colored when she saw me.“Dear Felix,” she said, “this is, I think, the only grief I have ever caused you. Forget all I may have said—I, a poor creature beside myself.” She held out her hand; I took and kissed it. “As in the old days, Felix?”We left the room during the last confession. I approached Madeleine. In the presence of others she could not escape civility; but, like her mother, she looked at no one.“Dear Madeleine,” I said softly, “what have you against me? Why such coldness before death, which should be reconcile us?”“I hear in my heart what my mother says,” she replied, with a look like Ingres’ “Mother of God.”“And you condemn me when your mother absolves me—if indeed I am guilty.”“You, you—always yourself!” Her voice revealed determined hatred, implacable as Corsican judgment.An hour passed in silence. The Abbe Birotteau returned after receiving the countess’s confession. We found her sitting up, dressed in the long white garment to be her shroud. She had had her letters burned—sacrifice she had delayed until this hour. She smiled with eyes moist and inward lucidity; she saw the promised land.“Dear Felix,” she said, pressing my hand, “stay with us. You must be present at my last scene; be not absent from the least painful among many things in which you are concerned.”She made a sign and the door closed. She knelt before the astonished count, then laid her head on his knees.“Though I have been a faithful wife to you,” she said faintly, “I have sometimes failed in my duty. I have given a friendship outside my family more affectionate care than to you. Perhaps I have irritated you by comparisons. I have had a deep friendship which no one—not even he who has been its object—has fully known. Though virtuous by human laws and an irreproachable wife, other thoughts, voluntary or involuntary, have crossed my mind, and in this hour I fear I welcomed them too warmly. But having loved you and continued submissive, I now pray for your blessing with a clean heart. I shall die without a bitter thought if I can hear a tender word for your Blanche, for the mother of your children—if you forgive her those things she did not forgive herself till reassured by the great tribunal which pardons all.”“Blanche, Blanche!” cried the broken man, shedding tears on her head. “Would you kill me?” He raised and kissed her solemnly. “Have I no forgiveness to ask? Have I never been harsh?”“Perhaps,” she said. “But dear friend, indulge a dying woman’s weakness; tranquillize me. Will you grant that I leave to our friend here that pledge of my affection?” She showed a letter on the mantel. “He is now my adopted son, and that is all. The heart makes bequests; my last wishes impose a duty on that dear Felix. If you do not wish to marry Madeleine, at least seek my soul’s repose by making Monsieur de Mortsauf as happy as he can be. Farewell, long farewell like that I gave last night to our valley, where I shall rest and where you will often—will you not?—return.Henriette.”I fell into abyssal reflections as the depths of her life were lighted by this expiring flame. My clouds of egotism rolled away. She had suffered as much as I—more, for she was dead. She believed others would be kind to her friend; she was blind to her daughter’s enmity. That last proof of tenderness pained me. Poor Henriette wished to give me Clochegourde and her daughter.From that dread day when I first followed my noble Henriette to the grave, the sun has been less warm, nights more gloomy, movement less agile, thought more dull. Some departed we bury in earth; others are buried within us, their memory mingling with our heartbeats; they are in us by a metempsychosis of love. A soul is within my soul. When some good thing is done by me, that soul acts and speaks. All that is good within me issues from that grave; sarcasm and bitterness are mine. Natalie, when next my eyes are darkened or my lips silent, do not ask, “Of what are you thinking?”I ceased writing for days; these memories were too bitter. I owe you an account of events that followed; they need few words. Henriette’s letter set a star of hope before me. To live at Clochegourde with Madeleine, consecrating my life to her, seemed a rescue. But it was necessary to know her true feelings. As I bade the count farewell, we walked the terrace. He spoke of his loss but soon of the future; he feared his daughter, who lacked her mother’s gentleness. Madeleine’s firm character alarmed him; he foresaw a struggle between father and daughter that would end his days in bitterness. His son was to leave; his daughter marry; what son-in-law might she have? Though talking of dying, he feared being alone without sympathy.Madeleine could see me from the terrace but would not come down. I seized a moment to beg the count to call her, saying I had a last wish of her mother to convey. The count fetched her and left us on the terrace alone.“Dear Madeleine,” I said, “if I speak to you, it should be here where your mother listened to me. I know your thoughts; but are you not condemning me without knowledge of facts? My life and happiness are bound up in this place; you know that, yet you seek to banish me by coldness instead of brotherly affection that death should have strengthened. If my mother’s name still has power, in her name I beg you never to return me to Clochegourde so long as you are in it. The sight of me causes a repugnance I cannot express, which I shall never overcome.”She stopped me.“Monsieur,” she said, voice trembling, “I know all your thoughts; but I shall not change my feelings. I would rather fling myself into the Indre than ally myself to you. If my mother’s name has power, in her name I beg you never to return while I am here. The sight of you causes repugnance I cannot overcome.”She bowed with dignity and returned to the house without looking back. The searching eye of that young girl had discovered her mother’s secrets; her hatred of the man she fancied fatal to her mother may have been increased by a sense of complicity.All before me was chaos. Madeleine hated me without considering cause or victim. The edifice of my happiness fell in ruins. I alone knew the life of that unknown woman; none had fathomed her heart. Strange truth: how many families have their Henriette! How many noble feelings leave earth with no historian to measure their depths! Mothers know their children little; so with husbands, lovers, brothers. When Madeleine disappeared, I left with a broken heart. Bidding farewell to my host at Sache, I started for Paris, following the right bank of the Indre.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At the story’s outset the Stanhope press and ink-distributing roller were not yet common in provincial shops. In Angoulême, tied to Parisian typography by its paper mills, only wooden presses were used: “the press groans” is literal—leather ink-balls still applied the ink and the pressman inked by hand. This old mechanism, cherished by Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, is central to the tale.Séchard, once a journeyman pressman, survived the Requisition of 1793 and became sole worker after his master’s death. Illiterate, he nonetheless obtained a master-printer’s license from a Representative of the People and bought the business cheaply. He partnered with the noble M. le Comte de Maucombe, who helped print decrees while avoiding official attention. By 1795, after the Terror, Séchard needed a compositor and hired an Abbé who later became a bishop. Though uneducated, he learned to run the business, mastering costs and profits; his avarice grew as he bought paper when prices fell, became his own landlord, and acquired the old printing house.He had one son, David, sent to grammar school as a hoped-for successor. David excelled; despite Séchard’s scorn for book learning, he economized while studying in Paris and finished without burdening his father. The printing house held a regional monopoly on official printing, but the Cointet Brothers sought a second license, threatening Séchard’s position. Confident in David, Séchard anticipated retirement.Now in his seventies and drinking since his wife’s death, Séchard’s bloated face, cunning gaze, three-cornered hat and old-fashioned clothes matched his habits. When David returned, Séchard feigned warmth but pushed business matters, seeing him as both heir and possible rival. The long-established shop—a large room with a glass door to the workshop—drew customers who watched the process. The vine-grown yard, kitchen where inky runoff mixed with household waste, and the cramped, unchanged living quarters above reflect the old printer’s stinginess and relentless pursuit of profit.The sitting-room, partly modernized by the late Mme. Séchard with powder‑blue wainscoting and Oriental paper, was sparsely furnished—lyre‑backed chairs, bare arched windows, and unfinished plans: no clock, sconces, or mirror, since Mme. Séchard had died before completing them. Jérôme‑Nicolas Séchard, practical and profit‑minded, left it as it was.He brought his son David in, pointed to a valuation of the printing plant, and had him read the inventory aloud. David scoffed at the old presses and tossed the paper; Séchard, affronted, led him downstairs and briskly demonstrated the presses’ efficiency. He challenged David to name an English press to match his, bragged they could earn enough without a foreman, and dismissed newer machinery as ruinous to type. David doubted the high price placed on the type, which Séchard defended—insisting it had been cast at M. Vaflard’s foundry and, after twelve years, was nearly new. The inventory totaled thirty thousand francs, including license and goodwill. David, remembering his father had once bought the business for ten thousand francs, found the asking price exorbitant and hesitated about whether he could afford it.Séchard, who preferred argument to silence, pressed the point: the license alone brought income from advertisements and work, and attached to the valuation was a partnership deed—Séchard would rent the premises to David for twelve hundred francs a year and reserve a room for himself. Cornered, David calculated the license and goodwill’s worth and, thinking he might manage the debt, accepted the deal, to his father’s surprise.The next day Séchard had the apprentice move David’s belongings to the attic, leaving him three empty rooms and no funds to pay wages. When David asked for help, Séchard refused, saying he had already paid his share. Humiliated, David saw himself alone; his father intended to profit. He mentioned his mother’s fortune, but Séchard dismissed it as merely beauty and intelligence and warned any legal claim would be long and costly. Resolving to work hard, David accepted his situation.Séchard spoke then of a “treasure” he left behind—Marion, a capable country girl who ran many tasks in the shop—and departed for his Marsac vineyard, fretting over the thirty thousand francs and making periodic visits, suspecting David’s mismanagement as the Cointet Brothers gained ground.David, indifferent to politics, struggled for work. The Cointets, aligned with the clergy, monopolized the market while David’s business dwindled; they smeared him as a Liberal and untrustworthy because of his father. As they prospered, the Cointets offered to buy the printing house. Séchard, fearing the loss of their link to Liberal clientele, at first urged David to keep the paper and even negotiated what he framed as a protective price of sixty thousand francs. Ultimately David was forced to sell, marking the Séchard establishment’s decline. Séchard grew more detached, visiting less often though still attached to the old shop.David’s fortunes worsened when Lucien Chardon, an old school friend reduced to poverty after his father’s death, sought work. David made him a printer’s reader. Their friendship deepened: Lucien dreamed of being a poet; David, of studying natural science. Lucien recounted his father’s schemes for refining sugar and making paper, which intrigued David and suggested other ventures.But these ambitions distracted them from the shop’s immediate needs. While they indulged plans and conversation, the Cointet Brothers consolidated their advantage. Séchard & Son scraped by, the building and workshop fell into disrepair, and the household’s strained fortunes showed in the neglected, grimy premises.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day in May 1821, David and Lucien waited by the window until the men went to dinner. Uncomfortable in the office, David led Lucien outside to sit under the vines, where sunlight sharpened their contrasts. David was robust, fighter-built, swarthy and weathered—shaped by humble origins and a melancholy reserve. Lucien was graceful and beautiful, with a refined profile and striking blue eyes; delicate in appearance but ambitious and shrewd, willing to view society pragmatically and even consider dubious means to rise.Both men were low in social standing but nourished higher ideals, which made them bitter when reality intruded. Lucien, impetuous and adventurous, usually led; David, stronger but more introspective and cautious, admired Lucien and often yielded to his charm, content to be the more ordinary companion. For three years their friendship thrived on literature and art: they read voraciously, worked hard, and dreamed of fame as escape from hardship.One day David brought a new volume of André de Chénier’s poetry. The verses moved them, offering solace and kinship with the poet’s struggles and hope of transcendence. Their reverie was broken by Cérizet, an apprentice who brought a stranger seeking to print a monograph. Lucien eagerly engaged the author, who mentioned Mme. de Bargeton—a woman Lucien admired. He blushed, thanked the author, and confessed to David his passionate love. David cautioned him about social barriers, but Lucien hoped love could bridge them and vowed not to visit Mme. de Bargeton without David’s welcome—a pledge that revealed loyalty and pride.Walking through Angoulême, Lucien reflected on the gulf between his origin and Mme. de Bargeton’s world. The city, perched on a crag, was split between the upper town of nobility and the lower town of commerce. The upper class clung to old values, resisted outsiders and reform, and looked down on merchants; the bourgeoisie resented this disdain. Despite modernization efforts, the hierarchy stayed rigid, breeding tension and the prospect of social upheaval as the middle class pressed for recognition.In that climate, Lucien’s ambition to enter Mme. de Bargeton’s circle was daunting. His background as a druggist’s son marked him an outsider, yet his literary talent, charm, and Mme. de Bargeton’s artistic tastes gave him hope. David’s mixture of admiration and concern captured their friendship: he understood Lucien’s depth but saw the obstacles. Lucien’s determination and his pledge to include David in any future visits encapsulate their bond as love and ambition unfold against entrenched divisions and shifting fortunes.M. de Bargeton descended from a Bordeaux alderman ennobled under Louis XIII, but the family declined after his grandfather, “the Waster,” squandered its wealth. By 1789 he possessed only entailed lands and a house in Angoulême with modest rents. In 1805 he married Marie-Louise-Anaïs (Naïs), from a once-prominent family whose father lived frugally on his wife’s estate, farming and distilling brandy.During the Revolution the Abbé Niollant taught Naïs languages and music, fostering independence and a certain haughty superiority. Her father’s thrift and provincial seclusion reinforced this outlook; she resisted marriage, valuing autonomy over submission. Seeking a husband who would not challenge her intellect, her father chose M. de Bargeton: well bred but limited in mind. Naïs accepted, intending to pursue social ambitions within the marriage despite their age gap (she thirty-six, he fifty-eight).Settled in Angoulême, they awaited Naïs’s inheritance and a hoped move to Paris. Instead a modest income and petty provincial life stifled her intellectual yearnings and produced theatrical affectations, turning noble feelings into exaggeration. Longing for love, she was moved by the Empire’s heroes and loved a young officer whose death left her heartbroken.Years of solitude hardened dignity into affectation—until M. du Châtelet arrived. Charming, ambitious, and disruptive, he used music to ingratiate himself and eyed Naïs as a potential wealthy widow. His presence scandalized the local elite, who defended her salon; she welcomed the excitement, which exposed tensions of ambition, love, and provincial expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion was loud and exaggerated: the Quotidienne showed tepid loyalty and Louis XVIII was mocked as a Jacobin. Society women were awkward and ill-spoken; in the countryside a lingering nobility and old breeding masked many defects, and loyalty to the Bourbons remained a proud provincial badge.Châtelet, seeking solace in society because of his interest in Mme. de Bargeton, flattered many women and embodied what their husbands lacked; all coveted his proximity. To keep his place he endured insolence, courted the clergy, catered to the queen of Angoulême’s petty tastes, and shared new literature. Mme. de Bargeton—Naïs—enchanted by the Bourbon revival yet craving Parisian genius, learned from Châtelet of a promising young poet in Angoulême.The young poet Lucien was unaware of the stir. Praised by his headmaster, he expected a warm reception at the Hôtel de Bargeton. His sister Éve secretly supported him—saving for shoes and clothes and coaching him in aristocratic manners—leaving him proud and anxious about his prospects.At the Hôtel de Bargeton Lucien found the house less grand than imagined, but Naïs embodied the poetry he loved. She admired his beauty and diffidence; their meeting brimmed with mutual attraction, and Lucien overlooked her faded airs, sensing intimacy.Sensing a rival, Châtelet asserted influence over Lucien, offering unsolicited advice about society and claiming Naïs. Despite Châtelet’s intimidation, Lucien continued to visit—first flattered by noble condescension, later resentful.Naïs cultivated Lucien as a project—patroness and confidante. His first love deepened as she addressed him intimately, encouraged his literary ambitions, introduced him to music and books, and revealed vulnerabilities. A portrait of a dead lover provoked jealousy, yet Lucien declared his feelings and their relationship advanced.When their liaison became public the town buzzed. Châtelet tried to undermine Lucien; Naïs defended him, arguing genius can rise from humble origins. To launch him she hosted salons and urged him to adopt the noble name Rubempré, promising courtly patronage. Exhilarated, Lucien’s aspirations widened; earlier egalitarian ideals gave way to social ambition. In a passionate letter he asked Naïs’s help for a friend, David, revealing his inner conflict between love, artistic sincerity, and the pursuit of recognition.As Lucien descended from Angoulême he worried that Mme. de Bargeton might be angry and whether she would receive David. He perceived the vast social gulf between a queen and her favorite and knew David could not easily cross it; he did not yet see that receiving David could ruin her by associating her with commoners, an unforgivable breach of social law even for the powerful.Lucien’s aristocratic instinct sensed danger: noble bearing did not equal noble feeling, and many notable men lacked social polish. David lacked the graces Lucien possessed, and Lucien feared the ridicule his friend would suffer. After a poetic reverie he returned to worldly wisdom, regretting his impulsive letter to Mme. de Bargeton and anticipating the sacrifices success demanded. He preferred to win by his own efforts, inspired by Napoleon’s rise from humble origins, though he felt ashamed of his father’s shop as an obstacle to advancement.At home M. Postel greeted him, praising Lucien’s grandfather and pricking Lucien’s resentment. Searching the shop, Lucien found Mme. de Bargeton’s reply: she offered to help David but revealed prejudice toward lower social standing. Lucien read it as a triumph, convinced David would shine in her circle.Eve noticed his elation and hoped for his happiness; Lucien reassured her, though Eve sensed his priorities shifting. David, feeling unworthy of the elevation Lucien sought, decided to decline Mme. de Bargeton’s invitation. He affirmed his commitment to being a printer and emphasized the social distance between them. His humility and loyalty moved Lucien, yet ambition began to eclipse gratitude, drawing Lucien toward advancement more than friendship.Preparing for his first public reading, Lucien and David agreed to choose a different poem. David and Eve shared a quiet moment hinting at mutual affection. At the reading Lucien met the awkward, ineffectual M. de Bargeton, whose limited intellect and shallow pleasantries underscored the social dynamics Lucien must navigate. The evening, marked by Lucien’s ambition and strained relationships, set the stage for future conflicts and aspirations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of nominating the mute gentleman for deputy, but Lucien, who had only briefly visited, had not yet fathomed him. M. de Bargeton, reclining and silent, observed everything with a cool reserve that awed Lucien; imaginative, Lucien fancied him a sphinx and treated him deferentially.“I am the first comer,” Lucien said, bowing more than usual.“That is natural enough,” Bargeton replied, a remark Lucien took as a sly barb and which made him self-conscious about his appearance. When Lucien asked why those from farther away arrived earlier, Bargeton shrugged and lapsed into silence. Lucien tried to converse—guessing Mme. de Bargeton was dressing, fretting over the room’s preparations and his own attire, even lingering by a Japanese jar for a topic—but Bargeton’s curt answers and intent scrutiny unnerved him.The old servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered with easy confidence, inspecting Lucien’s clothes and deepening his discomfort. Sixte du Châtelet, with the faded air of a once-stylish beau, dismissed Lucien’s poetry as vaudeville. Mme. de Bargeton soon appeared, elaborately dressed and theatrically charming; Châtelet’s compliments delighted her, while she scarcely acknowledged Lucien.Guests arrived in a parade of types: the dignified Bishop and his Vicar-General; Mme. de Chandour with her eccentric husband—Amélie posing like a rival queen; the solemn M. de Saintot (Astolphe) and his austere wife Élisa, affecting scientific airs; M. de Bartas, craving praise for his singing; the artist Alexandre de Brébian and his wife; fashion-obsessed Lolotte and Fifine with their dowdy husbands. The Comte de Senonches came with Zéphirine, tall and striking despite ill health; Francis, a devoted family friend who ran their household, appeared unusually attentive to Zéphirine and provoked speculation. The wealthy Marquis de Pimentel and his wife arrived with the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac, drawing envy as Mme. de Bargeton lavished them with attention.The Prefect and the General came last, followed by a nervous country gentleman who awkwardly brought a treatise on silkworms seeking listeners. Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard, noble but impoverished, hovered about, pressing social connections with thinly veiled desperation; M. de Séverac, a childless widower, spoke of silkworm nurseries while Mme. du Brossard pushed her daughter’s prospects.Throughout the evening Lucien felt increasingly scrutinized and diminished. Most called him M. Chardon rather than M. de Rubempré, a slight that stung his pride. The assembly seemed absorbed in its own interplay of flattery and rivalry rather than his art, leaving him isolated.When he read, excitement mixed with anxiety. Announcing poems by the newly discovered André de Chénier, he was misunderstood as affectation. He began with Le Malade to mild applause, but his reading of L’Aveugle fell flat—impatience and inattention showed. Sweating under pressure, Lucien drew courage from Louise’s supportive glance and proceeded to Chénier’s Iambes, which finally captured some attention though many missed the meaning.During the break Zéphirine sent Francis to inquire after the poems, and the women whispered about Lucien’s origins. Amélie’s remarks about his noble name and printing background sparked gossip that complicated his social standing. Absorbed in his poet’s beauty, Lucien remained largely unaware of the mounting tension and misunderstanding around his reading.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien tried to immortalize his love for Mme. de Bargeton with an ode he fancied rivaled Chénier’s. Presenting it proudly under her protective gaze, he felt secure while she privately fretted for him. The audience, however, was unimpressed: the Baron dismissed the poem as youthful—ornate but empty—while the women feigned admiration, their indifference and sarcastic whispers about Lucien’s ambitions obvious.Amid the mockery, Francis, a shrewd observer, sought to undermine him by spreading rumors that his mother had shaped his verse. The Bishop, unwittingly complicit, praised Lucien; the compliment only intensified the aristocrats’ scorn. Overwhelmed and alienated, Lucien misread people and bore the full weight of their disdain.As the evening shifted to games and music, Mme. de Bargeton, stung by her peers, withdrew with Lucien to her boudoir. She consoled him, urging endurance for future greatness—romanticizing suffering as the path to immortality and encouraging him to transform pain into art.Meanwhile Lucien’s sister Eve and her lover David debated his ambitions and the danger of the liaison. David feared Lucien was unsuited to high society and might be ruined; Eve hoped they could sustain him until success. When David proposed, Eve hesitated over their unequal standings but accepted, moved by love and a wish to help Lucien. David vowed to provide so Lucien wouldn’t be alone. Frustrated, Lucien resolved to win Mme. de Bargeton’s love and rise above the aristocrats, torn between aspiration and harsh social realities.In Angoulême, the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s rejoinder made Lucien a local celebrity. Walking through Beaulieu he was envied and flattered. He told Louise of his sister Eve’s impending marriage; she greeted him passionately, praised his reading, and promised a poetic refuge despite the envy.Buoyed by family and friends, Lucien’s self-regard grew. He teasingly asked Louise to be his Beatrice; she promised future love and urged him toward greatness. He confided about David and Eve’s attachment; Louise reassured him their bond transcended family ties. When M. de Bargeton arrived, Louise invited Lucien to dinner and a reading, reinforcing his sense of belonging.Calling himself M. de Rubempré, Lucien enjoyed Louise’s intimacies and M. de Bargeton’s patronage and expected a literary breakthrough. Eve and David postponed their wedding to support him—charmed by his persuasion, though David feared Lucien might change with status. Their closeness drew Châtelet’s attention; he watched for anything to exploit, intent on exposing them. In the small town, even innocent interactions sparked gossip, and Louise felt the weight of convention.Determined to declare his feelings, Lucien was repeatedly thwarted by provincial constraints; Louise considered fleeing. Châtelet manipulated perception: an outburst seen by Stanislas was misread and seeded rumor. Gossip spread, Châtelet seized the moment, and Stanislas reveled in the drama. Alarmed, Mme. de Bargeton defended her honor and urged her husband to act. Though insecure, M. de Bargeton resolved to challenge Stanislas; approaching modestly, he won respect at the tense gathering, setting the stage for a confrontation that would entangle lives and expose the town’s fraught mix of love, honor, and social expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet grasped the gravity of a late-night visit to the bedbound M. de Bargeton and suspected Naïs’s involvement. As household authority he asked Bargeton if he wanted to see Stanislas; relieved to have a mediator, Bargeton was brought to Amélie’s room, ready for a confrontation that could make Mme. de Bargeton a widow and implicate Lucien.Châtelet warned Stanislas that Bargeton would challenge him about remarks concerning Naïs and urged decorum. In the room, Bargeton demanded a retraction of remarks about Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré. Cornered, Stanislas accepted a duel and chose pistols.News of the duel altered the guests’ mood and divided blame. Stanislas was shaken; Amélie, sensing the tension, fell ill and was taken away. The men debated consequences, some recalling Bargeton’s former skill with firearms.At breakfast the next morning Mme. Chardon arrived alarmed: Bargeton had nearly killed Stanislas, who’d accused him of compromising behavior with Mme. de Bargeton. The grim fact—Stanislas shot in the neck—left him injured. Lucien, hearing the news, defended Mme. de Bargeton’s innocence.Lucien then received a note from Louise warning him to avoid her home because of the duel’s fallout and urging him to spend the day with her. David, preparing for his own marriage, suggested Lucien limit his visits to Mme. de Bargeton.Visiting Mme. de Bargeton, Lucien found her at breakfast with her husband and M. de Nègrepelisse, who watched him closely. Afterwards Louise revealed she planned to go to Paris to secure a political post for Bargeton; leaving Angoulême would contain the duel’s consequences.Louise pressed Lucien to join her in Paris, praising the capital’s opportunities over provincial stagnation. Drawn to that life and to Louise, Lucien hesitated because of his sister Eve’s imminent wedding and family duties. After a heated exchange she fainted; Lucien apologized and, desperate, promised discretion and to be ready to leave with her the next night.At home Eve and their mother lamented his likely departure and the wedding costs. David overheard, offered help and agreed to endorse a loan, deepening Lucien’s guilt. They arranged to send his belongings to Paris covertly. Mistrustful of Mme. de Bargeton’s departure, Châtelet sent someone to follow her. Early next morning Lucien left with David, who feigned business, as Mme. de Bargeton’s carriage approached—Lucien torn between anticipation and dread.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################
</story>
